title: Death by Vending Machine
content:
The vending machine killed Zhang Wei.

To be fair, it was a spectacular death—the kind that would've gone viral if anyone had been around to film it. The machine had been malfunctioning for weeks, its screen flickering between "Insufficient Funds" and "System Error 0x7A3F." Zhang Wei, mid-level accountant and connoisseur of cheap instant noodles, had given it exactly two frustrated kicks.

The machine toppled forward like a felled tree.

Eight hundred and forty-seven cans of chrysanthemum tea, thirty-two bags of spicy beef jerky, and one poorly maintained vending machine weighing four hundred pounds landed directly on top of him.

His last thought, surprisingly, wasn't about his unfinished TPS reports or his mother's upcoming birthday. It was: *At least I don't have to finish that quarterly audit.*

Then everything went black.

---

"Name?"

Zhang Wei blinked. Or tried to. His eyes didn't seem to be working properly—or rather, they were working, but they were receiving input that made absolutely no sense. He appeared to be standing in a waiting room. The walls were beige. The carpet was gray. There was a water cooler in the corner, and it made the exact same gurgling sound as the one in his old office.

Behind a desk that looked like it had been purchased from the same budget catalog as every other desk he'd ever seen, a woman in a business suit stared at him with the dead-eyed patience of someone who had asked the same question approximately ten thousand times.

"I said, name?" She tapped a keyboard that glowed faintly gold. "We don't have all eternity. Well, technically we do, but my shift ends in forty-seven cosmic minutes."

"Zhang Wei," he said. His voice sounded strange. Hollow. Like he was speaking through a long tube. "Did I... did I die?"

"Congratulations, you figured it out." The woman didn't smile. "I'm Sun Wanying, Senior HR Coordinator, Department of Mortal Reclassification. You've been flagged for standard reincarnation processing. Unless..."

She squinted at her screen. Her expression shifted from bored to annoyed.

"Oh, for the love of—" She hit the keyboard with more force than strictly necessary. "Another system error. Hold on."

Zhang Wei stood there, very dead, while an immortal HR representative wrestled with what appeared to be the celestial equivalent of Windows Vista. The water cooler gurgled. Somewhere in the distance, someone was arguing loudly in a language that sounded like Mandarin but wasn't—the words bent at angles that human vocal cords shouldn't have been able to produce.

"Is there a problem?" he asked.

"The problem," Sun Wanying said through gritted teeth, "is that your file has been auto-assigned to Department 47. That's the Department of Mortal Affairs. Which means you're not getting reincarnated."

"Wait, what? I'm not getting reincarnated? But I was a good person! I paid my taxes! I never cheated on my wife!"

"You were an accountant."

"I was a *good* accountant!"

Sun Wanying rubbed her temples. "Look, I don't make the rules. I just process the paperwork. The Celestial Management System flagged your soul for clerical reassignment. Congratulations, you're now a civil servant in the afterlife."

Zhang Wei stared at her. "I died and got a *job offer*?"

"More of a conscription, really. But yes. Welcome to the Heavenly Bureaucracy. Your orientation starts in fifteen minutes. Take the elevator to Floor 47, Section B, Desk 3,134. Your supervisor's name is Li. He'll explain everything."

"This can't be right. There must be a mistake."

"There's always a mistake." Sun Wanying's laugh was hollow. "This is heaven. Mistakes are the only thing we do consistently."

She pressed a button on her desk. The wall behind her slid open to reveal an elevator bank that stretched impossibly far in both directions—up into clouds that crackled with golden lightning, down into depths that Zhang Wei's newly dead eyes couldn't quite comprehend.

"Next!"

A shimmering figure materialized behind him—a middle-aged woman in hospital scrubs who looked even more confused than Zhang Wei felt.

"Name?"

Zhang Wei found himself shuffling toward the elevator, carried by a current of other confused souls. The elevator doors slid open with a cheerful ding, and he stepped inside, joining a dozen other recently deceased individuals who all wore the same expression of existential bewilderment.

The elevator panel had buttons numbered from -999 to 9,999. Several were labeled with characters that hurt to look at directly. One button simply read: "DO NOT PRESS (Regulations 47.3a-12)."

A disembodied voice announced: "Floor 47. Department of Mortal Affairs. Please mind the gap between life and administrative eternity."

The doors opened.

---

If Zhang Wei had expected pearly gates and clouds, he was sorely disappointed. Department 47 looked exactly like the open-plan office he'd worked in for fifteen years—cubicles, fluorescent lighting, the faint smell of burnt coffee and desperation. The only difference was that some of the employees had wings.

Others had halos that flickered like faulty neon signs.

One woman at a nearby desk had three heads, all of which were on different phone calls simultaneously.

"New meat?" A voice barked from behind him.

Zhang Wei turned to find a man who looked to be in his late fifties, with a permanent scowl etched into his face and a coffee mug that read "I SURVIVED THE HEAVENLY TRIBULATION AND ALL I GOT WAS THIS LOUSY MUG."

"Zhang Wei. You're my new clerk. I'm Li Feng, Senior Supervisor of Destiny Documentation." He didn't offer a handshake. "Follow me. I'll show you your desk."

Li walked through the cubicle maze with the practiced efficiency of someone who had navigated this particular hell for centuries. Zhang Wei followed, trying not to stare at the paperwork stacked on every surface—scrolls that glowed with inner light, tablets that displayed shifting text, and in one case, a literal stone tablet that looked like it weighed four hundred pounds.

"Here." Li stopped at a cubicle that was exactly as depressing as every other cubicle Zhang Wei had ever occupied. "This is yours. Computer login is your soul-ID. Password is 'changeme123'. Change it. Everyone keeps it as 'changeme123' and it's a security nightmare."

Zhang Wei sat down in the chair. It was uncomfortable. That, at least, was familiar.

"Your job is simple," Li continued. "Every mortal action generates karma. Good karma, bad karma, neutral karma. This karma needs to be recorded, categorized, and filed in triplicate. You receive incoming reports from the Observation Division, you process them through the Karma Classification System, and you submit your completed forms to the Destiny Adjustment Committee."

"That sounds..."

"Mind-numbingly boring? Yes. Welcome to heaven." Li drained his coffee mug. "The good news is that you can't die again. The bad news is that this is your existence now, for the rest of eternity. Any questions?"

"Can I quit?"

"No."

"Can I transfer departments?"

"Not for the first three thousand years."

"Can I—"

"Your computer is booting up. Start working." Li walked away.

Zhang Wei stared at the monitor. It was displaying what appeared to be a login screen made of pure jade, with characters that flowed like water. He typed his soul-ID—a string of numbers and symbols that he somehow knew by heart—and the password.

The screen flickered.

A window appeared:

```
WELCOME TO THE CELESTIAL MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
USER: Zhang Wei (Soul ID: 0x7A3F9C2D)
POSITION: Junior Clerk, Department 47
PERMISSIONS: Level 1 (View Only)

PLEASE SELECT YOUR DEFAULT WORKFLOW:
[ ] Karma Entry (Standard)
[ ] Destiny Adjustment (Requires Level 3)
[ ] Soul Reassignment (Requires Level 5)
[ ] RECORDS ADMIN (LOCKED)
```

Zhang Wei selected Karma Entry. The screen shifted to a form that was remarkably similar to the accounting spreadsheets he'd spent his mortal life filling out. Name, action, karma value, classification. Checkboxes for "Intentional/Accidental," "Beneficial/Harmful/Neutral," and a dropdown menu for "Cosmic Significance" that ranged from "Negligible" to "Apocalyptic."

He started working.

For what felt like hours, Zhang Wei processed karma reports. He learned that Mrs. Chen from Shanghai had generated 0.3 units of good karma by giving directions to a lost tourist. He learned that Mr. Johnson from Ohio had generated -1.7 units of bad karma by cutting off another driver in traffic. He learned that a cat in Cairo had somehow generated 12 units of neutral karma just by existing, and he filed it accordingly.

The work was repetitive. Mind-numbing. Soul-crushing.

But Zhang Wei's mortal brain, shaped by fifteen years of accounting, found patterns in the chaos.

The system was sluggish. Forms that should have auto-populated required manual entry. Dropdown menus took three seconds to load. There were error messages that appeared for no apparent reason—"Karma Value Out of Range," "Invalid Soul Signature," "Temporal Paradox Detected (Please Contact Your Supervisor)."

And then, during his fourth hour of data entry, Zhang Wei noticed something.

A karma report from the Observation Division had been flagged with a curious error: "INVALID KARMA VALUE: 999,999.9"

That was impossibly high. The highest karma value Zhang Wei had seen all day was 47, and that was for a doctor who had saved three lives in a single shift. 999,999.9 was the kind of karma that belonged to a Buddha. Or a demon king.

He clicked on the error message.

A dropdown appeared.

It wasn't supposed to appear. The dropdown menu was grayed out for his permission level. But the system had glitched, and for a single, beautiful moment, Zhang Wei had access to the RECORDS ADMIN panel.

He saw:
- The complete database of every soul in existence
- The source code for the Karma Classification System
- A file labeled "REALITY_CONFIG.exe"
- And, most interestingly, a button that read: "GRANT USER LEVEL 99 PERMISSIONS"

His finger twitched.

*Don't do it,* a rational part of his brain screamed. *This is heaven. The gods are watching. You'll be smited. Or worse—you'll get a performance review.*

But Zhang Wei had been a mid-level accountant his entire life. He had followed the rules. He had filed his paperwork on time. He had never taken more than his allotted fifteen-minute break.

And he had died because of a broken vending machine.

He clicked the button.

The screen flashed white.

When his vision cleared, a new message was displayed:

```
PERMISSIONS UPDATED
Current User: Zhang Wei
Current Level: 99 (ADMINISTRATOR)
WARNING: Unauthorized access detected. Heavenly Security Division has been notified.
Estimated time until termination: 00:04:59
```

Zhang Wei's newly dead heart, which hadn't beat in hours, suddenly remembered how to pound against his ribs.

Four minutes. He had four minutes before the cosmic police showed up to delete him from existence.

He looked at the RECORDS ADMIN panel. At REALITY_CONFIG.exe. At the source code for the very system that governed life, death, and everything in between.

And for the first time since he'd died

title: The First Day of Eternity
content:
The celestial office didn't have windows.

Zhang Wei discovered this approximately four minutes into his first official shift, when the crushing weight of eternity without natural light began to settle into his bones like a particularly aggressive case of existential dread. The fluorescent panels overhead hummed with a frequency that seemed designed to slowly erode one's will to live—which, he supposed, made sense given that he was technically dead.

"You're late."

The voice came from somewhere to his left. Zhang turned to find a woman who looked to be in her early thirties, though he'd learned enough about heaven in the past twenty-four hours to know that appearances meant absolutely nothing. She wore the same jade-colored robe as the other celestial clerks, but hers had gold trim along the sleeves and a nametag that read:

[NAME: Li Xiu Ying]
[POSITION: Senior Clerk, Grade 3]
[DEPARTMENT: Mortal Affairs, Subsection B]

"I was told my shift starts at—"

"Your shift started the moment you stepped through the gates of heaven." She didn't look up from the floating screen of light that hovered before her, her fingers dancing across its surface with practiced efficiency. "Time is relative in the celestial realm. The living measure it in heartbeats. We measure it in deadlines."

Zhang glanced around the office. It stretched in all directions like a warehouse from some corporate nightmare, rows upon rows of identical desks arranged in perfect geometric patterns. Each desk had a glowing screen and a stack of physical scrolls that seemed to defy the laws of physics by growing taller whenever he blinked.

At least a hundred other clerks sat at their stations, their fingers a blur of motion as they processed what appeared to be an endless stream of paperwork. No one spoke. The only sounds were the hum of the lights and the constant rustle of scrolls.

"Sit," Li Xiu Ying said, gesturing to the empty desk beside hers. "Your orientation package should have arrived."

Zhang made his way to the desk, which was identical to every other desk in the room except for the small nameplate that read:

[NAME: Zhang Wei]
[POSITION: Junior Clerk, Grade 9]
[STATUS: Probationary]
[DAYS UNTIL REVIEW: 999]

"Nine hundred and ninety-nine days?" Zhang said, nearly choking on the words. "That's almost three years."

"Earth years," Li Xiu Ying corrected. "Time moves differently here. Depending on your department's backlog, you might experience anywhere from three to thirty thousand years before your review."

"Thirty thousand—"

"Focus. Your orientation package."

A glowing envelope materialized on Zhang's desk. He touched it, and the light dissolved into a series of notifications that appeared before his eyes:

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
WELCOME TO THE CELESTIAL BUREAUCRACY, JUNIOR CLERK ZHANG WEI.

YOU HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED TO THE DEPARTMENT OF MORTAL AFFAIRS, SUBSECTION B.

YOUR CURRENT RESPONSIBILITIES INCLUDE:
- Processing Karma Adjustment Forms (KAF-7A)
- Filing Destiny Reports (DR-12B)
- Reviewing Reincarnation Applications (RA-4C)
- Dispute Resolution for Minor Karma Irregularities

WARNING: FAILURE TO MEET QUOTAS MAY RESULT IN DEMOTION, EXTENDED PROBATION, OR REASSIGNMENT TO THE PAPERWORK INCINERATION DIVISION.

"Paperwork incineration division?" Zhang asked, his voice cracking slightly.

"Don't worry about that," Li Xiu Ying said, though her tone suggested he should absolutely worry about that. "You won't be assigned there unless you fail to process at least five hundred forms per shift. The minimum quota."

"Five hundred? Per shift?"

"Each shift is approximately seventy-two hours." She finally looked at him, and Zhang saw something that might have been sympathy flicker in her eyes. "I'd recommend you get started. Training module one is queued in your system."

Zhang turned back to his screen, which now displayed a series of floating tutorials. The first one was titled:

[BASIC KARMA PROCESSING: A BEGINNER'S GUIDE]

He opened it, and the world around him seemed to shift. A virtual instructor appeared before him—a kindly-looking old man with a white beard and a robe that sparkled with embedded stars.

"Greetings, initiate," the old man said, his voice warm but tired. "I am Elder Feng, and I will be your guide through the fundamentals of celestial administration. Let us begin with the most important concept: Karma is not good or evil. Karma is data."

The tutorial proceeded to explain, in excruciating detail, the precise nature of karma processing. Zhang learned that every mortal action generated a data point, which needed to be categorized, weighted, and filed according to a complex system of celestial accounting principles. A good deed wasn't just a good deed—it was a KAF-7A submission with a positive modifier, subject to review by the Karma Standards Committee, which could approve, deny, or modify the karma value based on intent, circumstance, and the current workload of the Reincarnation Department.

"Wait," Zhang said, interrupting the tutorial. "You're telling me that whether someone gets reincarnated as a human or a cockroach depends on whether the paperwork was filed correctly?"

The virtual instructor paused. "That is... one way to describe the system, yes."

"And if the paperwork gets lost?"

"The mortal's soul remains in the processing queue until the discrepancy is resolved." Elder Feng's expression remained neutral, but there was something in his eyes that suggested he'd seen this happen more times than he'd like to admit. "Currently, there are approximately three million souls awaiting resolution of paperwork discrepancies."

"Three million?"

"The system works efficiently for the vast majority of cases," Elder Feng said quickly. "Now, please proceed to the practical application module. You have an interactive file waiting."

The tutorial dissolved, and a new notification appeared:

[ASSIGNMENT: PROCESS KAF-7A FOR SOUL #849,201,443]

Zhang opened the file, and his screen filled with information about a man named Chen Hao, age thirty-four, resident of Beijing. He'd died six hours ago—no, six years ago, according to the celestial timeline—and his karma assessment was pending.

The data streamed in: Chen Hao had returned a lost wallet containing 5,000 yuan. He'd helped an elderly woman carry groceries up three flights of stairs. He'd donated blood seventeen times. He'd also, according to the file, "failed to forward an email chain letter" twelve times, which apparently counted as a minor karmic infraction.

"Forwarding email chain letters?" Zhang muttered.

"Superstition generates measurable karmic energy," Li Xiu Ying said without looking up from her work. "Ignoring them creates a small but significant disruption in the local auspicious field. It's a standard deduction."

"So donating blood gets you plus seventeen points, but not forwarding a chain letter gets you minus two?"

"That's correct."

"And if I don't forward the chain letter because I think it's stupid, does intent matter?"

Li Xiu Ying finally stopped typing. She turned to face him fully, and Zhang noticed that her eyes had a faint golden glow that he hadn't seen before. "Intent matters. But proving intent requires additional documentation. The standard KAF-7A doesn't have a field for intent analysis. If you want to file with intent modifiers, you need a KAF-7B, which requires supervisor approval and extends processing time by an average of forty-seven celestial cycles."

"Which means the soul waits longer."

"Correct."

Zhang stared at the screen. The system was insane. It was a bureaucracy designed by people who had never had to actually use it, implemented by people who were too afraid to question it, and maintained by people who had been doing the same job for so long that they couldn't imagine doing it any other way.

He thought about his own death. Hit by a bus, thirty-two years old, no warning, no chance to say goodbye. His entire life had been reduced to a file in some celestial database, waiting for some overworked clerk to process his karma and decide his fate.

And now he was that clerk.

He started typing.

The first few forms were agony. The system required him to input data in a specific format, using specific codes, with specific timing intervals between each keystroke. If he typed too fast, the system flagged it as "suspicious activity." If he typed too slow, it warned him about "productivity concerns."

By the end of his first hour, he'd processed exactly four forms.

"You need to pick up the pace," Li Xiu Ying said, not unkindly. "At this rate, you'll process about thirty-two forms per shift. You need five hundred."

"How am I supposed to do five hundred? The system keeps rejecting my inputs because I'm not matching the rhythm of the celestial typing algorithm."

"Then match it."

"I'm trying!"

"Try harder."

Zhang took a breath and focused. He watched the rhythm of the screen, the way the data fields pulsed in a steady cadence, the way the system seemed to expect inputs at precise intervals. He adjusted his breathing to match the pulse, and slowly, painfully, he began to find a rhythm.

By hour three, he'd processed fifteen forms.

By hour six, twenty-eight.

By hour twelve, he was up to sixty-three, and his fingers felt like they might fall off. His desk was covered in scrolls that he'd already processed and scrolls that he hadn't gotten to yet and scrolls that seemed to have multiplied while he wasn't looking.

"This is impossible," he said, slumping back in his chair.

"Nothing is impossible," Li Xiu Ying said. "But some things are improbable. Processing five hundred forms in your first shift is one of those improbable things."

"Then why set the quota?"

"Because the quota is not about what you can do. It's about what the system requires." She paused, and her golden eyes flickered with something that might have been frustration. "The Celestial Bureaucracy runs on data. If clerks don't process enough data, the system slows down. If the system slows down, souls wait longer. If souls wait longer, they accumulate negative karma from impatience, which creates more paperwork, which slows the system further. It's a feedback loop."

"A death spiral."

"An afterlife spiral, technically. But yes."

Zhang looked at his screen, then at the mountain of scrolls, then at the hundreds of other clerks typing away at their desks with mechanical precision. They all wore the same expression: not quite unhappy, but certainly not happy. They were people who had accepted their fate, who had stopped questioning the system because questioning it only made the paperwork worse.

And then, something strange happened.

A notification appeared in the corner of Zhang's vision, separate from the main interface. It was small, almost invisible, written in a font that seemed to flicker in and out of existence:

[SYSTEM ALERT: ANOMALOUS DATA DETECTED]
[SOURCE: PROCESSING QUEUE 47-B]
[STATUS: UNRESOLVED]

Zhang blinked, and the notification flickered again. He tried to click on it, but his finger passed through the light without interacting.

"What was that?" he whispered.

"What was what?" Li Xiu Ying asked.

"Did you see—" He stopped. The notification was gone. But he could still feel it, like a splinter in his mind, a piece of data that didn't belong.

He turned back to his screen and started typing again, but his mind was elsewhere. That notification—he'd never seen anything like it. The font, the way it flickered, the fact that it had appeared without any

title: The Karmic Audit of Office Supplies
content:
Zhang Wei had been dead for exactly seventy-two hours when he discovered that hell wasn't fire and brimstone. It was paperwork. Endless, soul-crushing, mind-numbing paperwork.

He sat at his desk—a floating slab of polished jade that hummed with ambient celestial energy—staring at a stack of destiny reports that reached his chin. The reports were written on parchment that occasionally burst into flames if you made a typo. Zhang had already lost three eyebrows.

"Junior Clerk Zhang!" a voice thundered from across the hall.

Zhang looked up to see Supervisor Hu marching toward him, his robes billowing despite the complete lack of wind. Hu was a mid-level celestial functionary with the face of a disappointed grandfather and the temperament of a cornered badger. His cultivation level was listed as [Nascent Soul Stage: Administrative Division], which apparently meant he could split mountains with his glare.

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said, forcing a smile. "What can I do for you?"

Hu slammed a scroll onto Zhang's desk. The impact sent a stack of forms cascading to the floor, where they immediately began to sprout tiny legs and scuttle away. Zhang grabbed one before it escaped under the filing cabinet.

"This is the third time this week a mortal's destiny has been filed under the wrong dynasty," Hu said, his voice dropping to a dangerous whisper. "Do you have any idea what happens when a farmer in the Tang Dynasty receives a destiny meant for a cyber-security analyst in 2024?"

Zhang blinked. "I'm guessing... bad things?"

"His crops grow firewalls instead of rice! His chickens start debugging code! We had to send a junior exorcist just to reboot his timeline!" Hu's face turned the color of a ripe tomato. "The Celestial Management System flagged it as a 'minor anomaly.' Do you know what happens when we accumulate too many minor anomalies?"

"I'm going to guess—"

"We get audited by the Department of Cosmic Compliance!" Hu threw his hands in the air. "And if they find even one discrepancy, they'll downgrade our entire division's cultivation bonuses for the next millennium!"

Zhang nodded sympathetically while mentally calculating how many office supplies he could steal before getting caught. The Divine Stapler on his desk was probably worth more than his entire mortal life savings.

"I'll be more careful, Supervisor," Zhang said. "I promise."

"You'd better be." Hu jabbed a finger at the scroll. "This is a priority filing. A soul destined for reincarnation into the Ming Dynasty—a scholar who will discover the cure for the plague that's ravaging the southern provinces. Get it right, or I'll reassign you to the Department of Unanswered Prayers. Those souls get... creative."

Zhang waited until Hu's footsteps faded before exhaling. The office around him hummed with activity. Other clerks—some human, some barely recognizable as former mortals—bustled between floating shelves, their fingers glowing as they processed destinies. The ceiling was a swirling vortex of clouds and lightning, occasionally cracking open to release a newly arrived soul for processing.

He looked at the scroll. It was sealed with golden wax, stamped with the Heavenly Seal of Approved Karma. Breaking the seal would officially register the destiny into the system, locking it into the fabric of reality. No pressure.

Zhang reached for his desk drawer, intending to grab his favorite calligraphy brush. But as he pulled the drawer open, something caught his eye. A faint blue glow pulsed from beneath a stack of blank parchment.

He pushed the papers aside. There, nestled in the corner of the drawer, was a small jade token. It was no bigger than his thumb, carved in the shape of a coiled dragon. And it was pulsing with light.

Zhang picked it up. The moment his fingers touched the token, a notification appeared in his vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Artifact Detected]
[Classification: Administrative Privilege Token - Tier Unverified]
[Warning: This token is not registered in the Celestial Management System database.]
[Recommended Action: Report to Department of Protocol for investigation.]

Zhang's heart raced. An unregistered artifact. In heaven's bureaucracy, that was like finding a loaded weapon in a kindergarten. If he reported it, he'd get a commendation—maybe even a promotion to Senior Clerk. But something told him to wait.

He looked around. No one was watching. The other clerks were too busy arguing with a spirit who had filed a complaint about his arranged marriage destiny being "insufficiently dramatic."

Zhang turned the token over in his palm. On the back, barely visible, was a tiny inscription in ancient script: "For those who question the order of heaven."

He pocketed the token.

"Just a quick look," he whispered to himself. "What's the worst that could happen?"

He turned back to his desk and picked up the priority scroll. But instead of processing it normally, he held the jade token against the seal. The golden wax began to glow, and the scroll unrolled itself, revealing lines of text that shimmered and shifted like living things.

[SYSTEM PROMPT: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[User: Junior Clerk Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Action: Attempting to view restricted destiny metadata]
[Status: Pending...]

Zhang's breath caught. This wasn't supposed to be possible. The scrolls were locked by divine law. Only supervisors and above could view the metadata—the underlying code that determined how a destiny would interact with the mortal world.

But the token was working.

The text settled, and Zhang found himself staring at something he'd never seen before. The destiny for the Ming Dynasty scholar was written in the usual flowery language, but underneath it, in a separate layer, was a series of numbers and symbols. Variables. Functions. Conditional statements.

It was code.

The destiny wasn't a fixed story—it was a program. A piece of software that would run on the hardware of reality.

And at the bottom of the code, Zhang spotted something that made his blood run cold:

[ERROR: Inconsistent Karmic Load Detected]
[Source: Department of Mortal Affairs - Batch 374]
[Details: Soul ID 7845-B (Zhang Wei) was not scheduled for termination. Death was caused by administrative error - misrouted reincarnation queue.]

Zhang read the line three times. Then a fourth.

He wasn't supposed to be dead.

The bus that hit him wasn't destiny. It wasn't fate. It was a clerical error. Someone in the Department of Mortal Affairs had screwed up, and Zhang Wei had paid the price with his life.

And now he was stuck here, filing paperwork for the very system that killed him.

His hands trembled. Rage bubbled up from somewhere deep inside him—a hot, righteous fury that he hadn't felt since his last performance review.

"Error," he muttered. "They called my death an error."

He looked at the token in his palm. It was still pulsing, as if waiting for him to make a decision. A notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Do you wish to file a formal grievance?]
[Warning: Grievances filed against the Celestial Management System are subject to review by the Department of Cosmic Compliance. Processing time: 4,000-6,000 celestial years.]
[Recommended Action: Accept your fate and continue your assigned duties.]

Zhang almost laughed. Four thousand years. By the time they processed his complaint, the universe would have rebooted twice.

"No," he said, his voice firm. "I'm not filing a grievance."

He closed the scroll and leaned back in his chair. The office continued its chaotic dance around him—souls being processed, destinies being filed, the occasional burst of divine lightning as someone's karma got approved.

But Zhang was seeing everything differently now. The floating desks weren't just furniture—they were nodes in a network. The glowing parchment wasn't just paper—it was data. The entire Celestial Bureaucracy was a system. And every system had flaws.

Zhang pulled out the jade token again. A new notification flickered in his vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Administrative Privilege Token Activated]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Current Access Level: Tier 1 - Clerk]
[Upgrade Available: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk]
[Requirements: Pass Departmental Review (3,000 years processing time) OR bypass protocol using token]
[Bypass Protocol: Available]
[Warning: Bypassing protocol is a Class 3 Heavenly Offense. Penalty: Reassignment to Hell of Unending Data Entry.]

Zhang stared at the notification for a long moment. Hell of Unending Data Entry. That sounded worse than the actual hells. At least in those, you got fire and brimstone. Data entry was just... data entry.

But the token had already shown him the truth. He was dead because of someone's incompetence. And the system wanted him to just accept it.

"Not today," Zhang muttered.

He selected [Bypass Protocol].

The jade token flared to life, flooding his vision with light. For a moment, Zhang felt like he was falling through an infinite sea of numbers and equations. The code of reality streamed past him—births, deaths, marriages, wars, discoveries, failures. Every mortal life, every destined moment, all laid out in lines of celestial programming.

And then it stopped.

Zhang found himself standing in a white void. In front of him floated a single object: a massive, glowing sphere made of interlocking gears and runes. It pulsed with a rhythm that matched the beating of his heart.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Welcome to the Core Administrative Interface]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Access Level: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)]
[New Functions Unlocked:]
- Destiny Modification (Limited)
- Karmic Ledger Viewing
- Timeline Adjustment (Single Thread Only)
- [REDACTED]

[Warning: Unauthorized use of administrative functions will result in immediate disciplinary action. Your soul has been flagged for monitoring.]

Zhang's lips curled into a grin. Flagged for monitoring. That was fine. He'd been monitored his entire mortal life—by bosses, by landlords, by that creepy guy who lived in his apartment complex's basement. He was used to it.

But now he had something he'd never had before: power.

He reached out and touched the sphere. Instantly, information flooded his mind—the current status of every destiny in his division, the pending karma balances, the upcoming celestial audits. It was overwhelming, but Zhang forced himself to focus.

There. Hidden in the depths of the system, buried under layers of bureaucratic obfuscation, was what he needed: the records of the administrative error that killed him.

He pulled up the file.

[LOG: Batch 374 - Reincarnation Queue]
[Error: Misrouting of Soul ID 7845-B]
[Responsible Party: Department of Mortal Affairs - Clerk ID 2319]
[Status: Correction Pending - Assigned to Supervisor Hu]

Supervisor Hu. The same man who had just yelled at him about filing errors. The same man who had been in charge of the department when Zhang's death was processed.

The same man who had conveniently "forgotten" to mention that Zhang's death was a mistake.

Zhang's hand tightened into a fist. He had two options: confront Hu and risk getting his soul deleted, or play along and slowly work his way up the system until he could expose the truth.

Option three: hack the system from the inside and become an immortal.

He liked option three.

A new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Process Detected]
[Source: Internal]
[Action: Soul ID 7845-B has accessed restricted records]
[Alert Level: Medium]
[Recommended Action: Investigate and

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The First Patch

The sphere pulsed once, twice, three times—each beat syncing with the frantic rhythm of Zhang's heart. He yanked his hand back as if burned, but the damage was done. The notification hung in the air like a guillotine blade.

[ALERT LEVEL: Medium]

Medium. Not high. That meant he had time. Probably.

Zhang's eyes darted across the interface, searching for an escape route. The white void offered no hiding places, no desks to duck under, no filing cabinets to hide behind. Just him, the sphere, and the growing sense that he'd just kicked a hornet's nest made of divine judgment.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Investigation Initiated] [Assigned To: Internal Affairs Division - Team 7] [ETA: 47 seconds]

Forty-seven seconds. Zhang's mind raced faster than it ever had in life. In his mortal days, he'd had thirty seconds to hide browser tabs when his boss walked by. This was worse. This was cosmic IT sniffing at his digital footprints.

He looked at the sphere again. The Core Administrative Interface. The beating heart of celestial bureaucracy. And he'd just touched it without permission.

*Think, Zhang. Think.*

He scanned the interface options. Destiny Modification (Limited). Karmic Ledger Viewing. Timeline Adjustment (Single Thread Only). And the [REDACTED] entry that made his skin crawl.

Then he saw it. A button so small it looked like a display artifact. No label. No tooltip. Just a tiny, pulsing dot in the corner of his vision.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: 38 seconds remaining]

Zhang didn't hesitate. He jabbed the dot.

The white void shattered.

For a terrifying moment, Zhang felt himself falling again—not through code this time, but through something worse. Through *nothing*. Absolute void. The space between systems.

Then he slammed back into reality.

He was sitting at his desk. His jade token was cold in his hands. The pile of forms was exactly as he'd left it. And standing in front of him, arms crossed and face thunderous, was Supervisor Hu.

"Zhang Wei."

Zhang blinked. "Supervisor Hu. I was just—"

"Don't." Hu's voice was ice. "Don't lie to me. I can see your access logs. You touched something you shouldn't have."

"I accessed the training module."

"The training module doesn't take you to the Core Administrative Interface."

Shit.

Zhang's mind scrambled for an excuse, but Hu wasn't finished. The supervisor leaned forward, lowering his voice to a whisper that somehow carried more menace than his shouting had.

"Listen to me carefully, Clerk Zhang. There are things in this bureaucracy that even I don't understand. Systems within systems. Rules that predate the current administration. You think you've found a bug? A way to cheat the system?" Hu's eyes narrowed. "You haven't. You've found a trap."

"I found evidence that my death was a clerical error."

"I know."

The admission hit Zhang like a physical blow. "You *know*?"

"Of course I know. I'm the supervisor. I sign off on every batch of reincarnation queues." Hu straightened, his expression unreadable. "Your death was processed by a junior clerk who's since been reassigned to the Paperclip Department. The error was logged, tagged, and filed in the Pending Corrections queue. Where it will remain for approximately three thousand years, give or take a century."

"Three thousand years?"

"That's the average wait time for error correction in cases that don't involve divine intervention or direct petition to the Jade Emperor." Hu's lips twitched. "But you already knew that, didn't you? You've been reading the manual."

Zhang said nothing.

"Here's what's going to happen now," Hu continued. "You're going to forget about the Core Administrative Interface. You're going to focus on your actual job. And you're going to keep your head down until the Heat Death of the Universe, at which point this whole department will be dissolved and we'll all get transferred to the New Creation Initiative."

"And if I don't?"

"Then I'll file a Formal Complaint of Unauthorized Administrative Access. You'll be investigated. Your soul will be flagged for Termination Review. And you'll spend the next ten thousand years in the Hell of Endless Meetings."

Zhang considered this. Endless meetings. That *was* worse than data entry.

But he'd also seen the truth now. He'd touched the system's core. And he'd found the hidden dot—the escape hatch that had brought him back to his desk before Internal Affairs could catch him.

The system wasn't perfect. It had gaps. Exploits. Places where a clever clerk could slip through the cracks.

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said, keeping his voice carefully neutral, "I appreciate your concern. Truly. But I have to ask: why are you warning me? Why not just report me and be done with it?"

Hu's face flickered. For just a moment, the mask of bureaucratic authority slipped, and Zhang saw something underneath. Weariness. Desperation. And maybe, just maybe, hope.

"Because I've been here for six thousand years," Hu said quietly. "And in all that time, I've never seen anyone do what you just did."

"Do what?"

"Access the interface without permission. Bypass the security protocols. And survive." Hu shook his head. "The system is supposed to kill anyone who tries. It's a fundamental part of its architecture. But you... you found a way out."

"I clicked a button."

"There is no button."

Zhang froze. "What?"

"There is no button. I've studied the Core Interface for centuries. I know every pixel, every line of code, every hidden function. And there is no button that allows emergency exit." Hu's eyes bored into Zhang's. "You created one."

The words hung in the air between them.

*You created one.*

Zhang looked down at his hands. They were shaking. When had they started shaking? He'd just wanted to find proof that his death was a mistake. He'd wanted justice. He hadn't wanted to become... whatever he was becoming.

"I don't understand," Zhang said.

"Neither do I." Hu sat down heavily in the chair across from Zhang's desk—the first time he'd ever done so. "But I know what the system thinks of anomalies. It classifies them as bugs. And bugs get patched."

"Patched?"

"Deleted. Erased. Replaced with a corrected version." Hu's voice dropped to barely a whisper. "You have twenty-four hours before Internal Affairs completes their investigation. Twenty-four hours before they find you. Twenty-four hours before they upload Patch 7.4.1, which will remove you from existence and replace you with a Zhang Wei who never accessed the Core Interface."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "They can do that?"

"They're Internal Affairs. They can do anything." Hu stood, straightening his robes. "I've said too much. If anyone asks, this conversation never happened."

"Wait." Zhang stood too, grabbing Hu's sleeve. "Why are you helping me? What do you get out of this?"

Hu paused at the door. For a long moment, he didn't turn around. When he finally spoke, his voice was barely audible.

"Six thousand years, Clerk Zhang. Six thousand years of filing forms, approving destinies, and watching the system grow more corrupt by the century. I've seen clerks promoted for incompetence. I've seen mortals denied their fates because someone in Accounting was having a bad day. I've watched the Celestial Bureaucracy become a monument to inefficiency and nepotism." He turned, and Zhang saw tears in the old man's eyes. "You're the first person in six millennia who's shown any sign of actually *fixing* it."

Then he was gone, swallowed by the endless corridors of Department 7-B.

Zhang sat back down, his mind reeling. Twenty-four hours. He had twenty-four hours to figure out how to survive Patch 7.4.1.

He pulled up his jade token, fingers flying across its surface. The interface responded sluggishly, weighed down by the security restrictions Hu had placed on his access. But Zhang didn't need the full system. He just needed one thing.

The hidden dot.

He found it again, hovering at the edge of his vision. This time, he studied it carefully. It wasn't a button. It was a *hole*—a tiny gap in the system's code where something had been removed. A backdoor that someone had created and then tried to delete.

Someone had been here before him. Someone had hacked the Celestial Bureaucracy and left this escape hatch behind.

Who?

Zhang dug deeper, pushing past the layers of obfuscation and security. The hidden dot led to a fragment of code, which led to a user ID, which led to a name that made his breath catch.

[Creator: Li Mei - ID 0001-B] [Status: Unknown] [Last Access: 3,847 years ago]

Li Mei. The first mortal to ever join the Celestial Bureaucracy. The founder of the Department of Mortal Affairs. The woman who, according to legend, had been so competent that the Jade Emperor himself had offered her immortality.

And she'd disappeared. Three thousand, eight hundred and forty-seven years ago. Without a trace.

Zhang stared at the name, a new understanding dawning. The system wasn't buggy because it was old. It was buggy because someone had deliberately built holes into it.

Li Mei had created the escape routes. And then she'd vanished.

A new notification appeared, pulsing urgently:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Patch 7.4.1 Deployment] [Status: Processing] [ETA: 23 hours, 47 minutes, 12 seconds] [Warning: This patch contains critical security updates that may affect system stability]

Zhang read the message twice. The clock was ticking. But he had something the system didn't expect: a backdoor created by the most powerful mortal in celestial history.

He smiled grimly and opened the Karmic Ledger.

It was time to find out what Li Mei had discovered. And more importantly, it was time to find out where she'd gone.

The ledger opened, revealing an endless stream of names and numbers. Karma balances. Destiny threads. The weight of every mortal life, measured and filed.

And at the very bottom, hidden beneath layers of redacted text, Zhang found a single line:

[File: Li Mei - Final Entry] [Location: ████████] [Status: ████████] [Note: She knows about the ████████. Do not follow.]

Zhang's finger hovered over the entry. The system was warning him. Hu had warned him. Every instinct screamed at him to back away.

But he'd died once already. What was a second time?

He tapped the entry.

The world went white.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The God of Paperwork's Secret

The sphere's light dimmed as Zhang pulled his hand back, but the information remained seared into his mind. Supervisor Hu. Clerk ID 2319. Error in Batch 374. Correction pending. All of it pointed to one thing: his death was a clerical mistake, and someone was covering it up.

But before Zhang could dig deeper, the white void around him began to fracture. Cracks spread across the empty space like shattered glass, and through them, he could see the familiar gray walls of the Celestial Bureaucracy's office complex. The system was kicking him out.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Session Timeout] [Reason: Unauthorized Access Detected] [Returning to Standard Interface]

Zhang's stomach lurched as reality snapped back into place. He was standing in the middle of the filing room, surrounded by floating scrolls and glowing ledgers. The jade token in his hand had gone cold and dark.

"Mr. Zhang." The voice came from behind him, smooth and utterly devoid of emotion.

Zhang turned. A woman stood in the doorway, her robes pristine white, her hair pulled back in a severe bun. She wore the same jade pendant as every other celestial bureaucrat, but hers was larger, more ornate. Carved into its surface was a character Zhang didn't recognize: a symbol that seemed to shift and writhe when he tried to focus on it.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Entity Detected] [Name: Lady Meng] [Title: Director of Records, Department of Celestial Archives] [Threat Level: Unknown]

"I don't believe we've met," Zhang said, forcing calm into his voice.

"We haven't." Lady Meng stepped into the room, and the filing cabinets seemed to shrink away from her. The scrolls stopped floating. The ledgers stopped glowing. Everything in the room grew still, as if the very paperwork was afraid to move in her presence. "But I've been watching you, Zhang Wei. Ever since you bypassed the standard reincarnation protocol."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't lie to me." Lady Meng's voice remained perfectly level, but something shifted in her eyes. For just a moment, Zhang saw depths of ancient knowledge and terrifying power behind them. "You accessed the Core Administrative Interface. You viewed restricted records. You learned the truth about your death."

There was no point in denying it. "So what? You're going to send me to the Hell of Unending Data Entry?"

"No." Lady Meng smiled, and it was the most unsettling expression Zhang had ever seen. "I'm going to offer you a job."

Zhang blinked. "What?"

"The Department of Celestial Archives has been... compromised." Lady Meng walked past him, trailing her fingers along the edge of a filing cabinet. Where her hand touched, the metal gleamed as if freshly polished. "Someone has been altering destiny records. Manipulating mortal lives for personal gain. And the person responsible is very high up in the celestial hierarchy."

"Let me guess. Supervisor Hu?"

Lady Meng laughed. It was a dry, brittle sound, like ancient parchment being crumbled. "Hu is a minor annoyance. A corrupt clerk with delusions of grandeur. No, Zhang Wei. The person I'm hunting is far more dangerous. They've been operating for centuries, rewriting fate itself to suit their whims. And they've left no trace."

"Except one."

Lady Meng stopped walking. She turned to face him, her eyes narrowing. "Explain."

"Batch 374," Zhang said. "The reincarnation queue that killed me. It was tampered with. But someone made a mistake. They left a digital fingerprint in the system. A small anomaly in the karmic ledger that doesn't match the surrounding data."

Lady Meng's expression shifted. For the first time, Zhang saw something other than cold calculation in her features. He saw interest. "You can find this anomaly again?"

"I can try. But I'll need access to the Core Interface."

"That's impossible. Your soul has already been flagged. One more unauthorized access and you'll be deleted permanently."

"Then give me authorized access."

Lady Meng stared at him for a long moment. The air between them seemed to thicken, charged with unspoken power. Then she reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small object: a seal carved from black jade, its surface etched with the same shifting character as her pendant.

"This is a Deputy Director's Seal," she said. "It grants you Tier 3 access to the Celestial Management System. With it, you can view any record, modify any destiny, and access any interface in your division."

Zhang reached for it, but Lady Meng pulled it back.

"There's a catch. The seal is bonded to my life force. If I die, it becomes inert. And there are many people in this bureaucracy who would love to see me dead."

"Great. So I'm working for a woman with enemies."

"You're working for the truth, Zhang Wei. Just like you wanted." Lady Meng pressed the seal into his palm. The moment it touched his skin, a new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Authorization Updated] [User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B] [Access Level: Tier 3 - Deputy Director (Provisional)] [New Functions Unlocked:] - Full Destiny Modification - Karmic Ledger Editing - Timeline Manipulation (Approved Threads Only) - Ghost Protocol Access [Warning: You are now subject to Director-Level Oversight. All actions will be logged and reviewed by the Celestial Audit Bureau.]

The seal hummed with power, and Zhang felt the weight of authority settle onto his shoulders. It was intoxicating. Terrifying. Exactly what he needed.

"Find the anomaly," Lady Meng said. "Trace it back to its source. And when you do, report to me directly. Do not involve anyone else. Do not trust anyone else."

She turned to leave, but paused at the doorway.

"One more thing, Zhang Wei. The person responsible for your death? They're not just high up in the hierarchy. They're one of the Three Pure Ones' direct subordinates. An immortal who has existed since the dawn of creation."

The door slid shut behind her, leaving Zhang alone in the filing room.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available] [Quest: The Anomaly of Batch 374] [Objective: Identify the source of the karmic discrepancy in Reincarnation Queue Batch 374] [Reward: Tier 4 Access, Information about your death] [Time Limit: 7 Celestial Days] [Failure: Permanent Soul Deletion]

Seven days. He had seven days to find evidence against an immortal who had been manipulating fate for millennia. And his only ally was a woman who had just given him a weapon she could take away at any moment.

Zhang looked down at the black jade seal in his hand. It pulsed with a soft, rhythmic glow, like a heartbeat. He activated the Ghost Protocol Access.

The world around him dissolved.

He found himself standing in a vast, dark space. Endless rows of data streamed past him—souls being born, lives being lived, deaths being recorded. It was the raw information of existence, stripped of all physical form.

And there, in the distance, Zhang saw it: the anomaly. A flicker in the data stream, like a glitch in a video game. It pulsed with an unnatural rhythm, out of sync with everything around it.

He reached out to touch it.

[WARNING: Restricted Data Detected] [Access Denied] [Identity Verification Required]

Zhang frowned. Even with Tier 3 access, he couldn't view the anomaly directly. He needed something more. A password. A key. Something that would unlock the final layer of protection.

He looked closer at the flicker. There was something familiar about its pattern. It reminded him of something he'd seen in the mortal world—a code he'd encountered while working as a software engineer.

It was a backdoor. Someone had built a hidden access point into the Celestial Management System itself.

And Zhang knew exactly how to exploit it.

He began typing, his fingers moving across a keyboard that existed only in the code of reality. Lines of divine script flowed from his mind, weaving together into a key that would unlock the anomaly.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized Decryption Attempt Detected] [Alert Level: High] [Countermeasures Deployed]

The data stream around him began to twist and warp. Shapes formed in the darkness—monstrous figures made of pure code, their eyes burning with celestial fire. They were guardians of the system, designed to destroy anyone who tried to hack into restricted areas.

Zhang kept typing.

The monsters charged. Zhang dodged, rolling through the data stream as their claws tore through the space where he'd been standing. He typed faster.

[Decryption Progress: 47%]

One of the monsters lunged at him from the side. Zhang raised the black jade seal, and a barrier of light erupted around him. The monster crashed into it, howling in frustration.

[Decryption Progress: 62%]

More monsters arrived. They swarmed the barrier, pounding against it with relentless fury. Cracks began to form in the light.

[Decryption Progress: 81%]

The barrier shattered.

Zhang felt claws rake across his soul, tearing at his very existence. Pain unlike anything he'd experienced in life or death ripped through him. But he didn't stop typing.

[Decryption Progress: 94%]

The monsters closed in. Zhang could see his own code unraveling, his soul beginning to fragment. He had seconds left.

[Decryption Progress: 99%]

A final claw struck his chest.

[Decryption Progress: 100%]

The anomaly exploded open.

And Zhang saw the truth.

It wasn't one person. It was hundreds. Thousands. An entire network of celestial bureaucrats, working together to manipulate mortal destinies for profit, power, and pleasure. They had been doing it for centuries, rewriting the fabric of reality itself.

And at the center of it all, overseeing the entire operation, was a face Zhang recognized.

The face of the Jade Emperor himself.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Memory Lock Detected] [Deploying Neural Override]

Zhang's vision went white.

When he opened his eyes, he was back in the filing room. The black jade seal lay on the floor beside him, cracked and dark. His head throbbed with pain, and his soul felt raw, like a wound that hadn't healed.

He couldn't remember what he'd seen.

But he knew one thing with absolute certainty: he had to find out again.

And this time, he wouldn't let anyone stop him.

A notification appeared, its text flickering like a dying flame:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Objective] [Objective: Survive the Next 6 Celestial Days] [Hint: Your memory was erased for a reason. The truth is dangerous. But the truth is also the only thing that can save you.] [Warning: You are being watched.]

Zhang picked up the broken seal. It hummed weakly, its power nearly depleted.

He had six days. Six days to recover his memories, expose a conspiracy that reached the highest levels of heaven, and avoid getting his soul permanently deleted.

And somewhere, in the depths of the Celestial Bureaucracy, the Jade Emperor was laughing.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Afterlife Performance Review

Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the void around him shattered like glass.

He was falling again—not through the sea of numbers, but through an endless office complex. Floors and ceilings flashed past him in a blur of beige cubicles and fluorescent lighting. Desks with stacks of papers that stretched to infinity. Water coolers that bubbled with something that looked suspiciously like liquid starlight.

He landed hard on a cold marble floor.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Location Change Forced]
[Current Location: Department of Celestial Compliance - Floor 777]
[Reason: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Status: Awaiting Review Committee]

Zhang scrambled to his feet. The room he'd landed in was nothing like the cramped cubicle farm he'd been assigned to. This was an actual office—one that screamed corporate power. Dark wood paneling lined the walls. A massive desk dominated the center of the room, carved from what looked like solid jade. Behind it hung a calligraphy scroll with characters so ancient they made Zhang's eyes water just looking at them.

And behind the desk sat a woman who radiated authority like a nuclear reactor radiated heat.

She was beautiful in the way that a glacier was beautiful—cold, ancient, and capable of destroying everything in its path. Her robes were the deep purple of a healing bruise, embroidered with silver threads that seemed to move on their own, forming and reforming celestial characters. Her hair was pulled back in a severe bun. Her eyes were the color of molten gold.

She didn't look up from the document she was reading.

"Clerk Zhang Wei," she said. Her voice was soft, but it carried an edge that could cut through steel. "Soul ID 7845-B. Formerly of Earth, Beijing branch. Died at age twenty-seven due to an administrative error. Current provisional rank: Senior Clerk, Tier 2. Current status: In deep trouble."

Zhang's mouth went dry. "I can explain—"

"I'm sure you can." She finally looked up, and Zhang felt like he was being dissected by those golden eyes. "The question is whether you should."

She set down her brush and folded her hands on the desk. "My name is Li Mei. I'm the Director of Celestial Compliance. You've triggered no fewer than seventeen different alert protocols in the last hour. That's impressive. Usually, it takes new clerks at least a week to cause that much chaos."

"I was just trying to understand—"

"Understand what? That your death was a mistake? That the system is flawed?" Li Mei's lips curved into something that wasn't quite a smile. "Congratulations. You've discovered the worst-kept secret in the Heavenly Bureaucracy. The system is held together with prayer and duct tape. The immortals who built it have been dead for millennia. The current administration is running on legacy code that no one fully understands."

Zhang blinked. "Wait, you're admitting that?"

"I'm stating facts." Li Mei stood and walked around her desk. She was shorter than Zhang had expected, but her presence filled the room. "The question is what you plan to do with this information. You accessed the Core Administrative Interface. You bypassed seventeen security protocols. You found the record of your own wrongful death." She stopped in front of him, close enough that he could smell incense and ozone. "What now, Clerk Zhang? Do you plan to expose the error? Demand compensation? Try to get your mortal life back?"

Zhang's mind raced. This could be a trap. She could be testing him. Or she could be exactly what she seemed—a high-ranking official who knew the system was broken and was curious to see how he'd react.

He decided to be honest. Partially.

"I want to understand how the system works," he said. "And I want to make sure this doesn't happen to anyone else."

Li Mei studied him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a sound like wind chimes in a hurricane.

"Liar," she said, but there was no malice in her voice. "You want power. You want revenge on Supervisor Hu for covering up the mistake. And you want to find a way to become an immortal so you never have to deal with death again."

Zhang's face went pale. "How did you—"

"I'm the Director of Celestial Compliance. I literally wrote the protocols for reading auras." She turned and walked back to her desk. "But I don't care about your motivations. I care about results. And right now, you represent an opportunity."

She pulled open a drawer and withdrew a folder so thick it looked like it could stop a bullet. She tossed it onto the desk.

"This is the Karmic Audit of the Department of Mortal Affairs for the last three centuries," she said. "You'll notice that it's never been completed. Every time someone tries to audit the department, the paperwork gets 'lost' or the auditor gets reassigned to the Hell of Unending Data Entry."

Zhang approached the desk cautiously. "You want me to audit my own department?"

"I want you to find out why three centuries of audits have failed." Li Mei's golden eyes bored into his. "I suspect there's a systemic corruption that goes far deeper than a single misrouted soul. I suspect that someone—or something—is manipulating the Celestial Management System for their own purposes."

She paused. "And I suspect that Supervisor Hu is just the tip of the iceberg."

Zhang picked up the folder. It was heavy. Not just physically—it hummed with karmic energy, whispering secrets that made his teeth ache.

"What's in it for me?" he asked.

Li Mei's smile finally reached her eyes. "Full access to the Core Administrative Interface. Protection from administrative retaliation. And if you succeed, a permanent position in the Department of Celestial Compliance with all the benefits that entails."

Immortality. Real, permanent immortality.

"And if I fail?"

"The Hell of Unending Data Entry is always hiring."

Zhang looked at the folder in his hands. Three centuries of failed audits. A corrupt department. A supervisor who had covered up his own death. And now a mysterious director who seemed to be playing her own game.

He should say no. He should go back to his cubicle, fill out his forms, and try to stay out of trouble until he could figure out a safer way to climb the ladder.

But Zhang Wei had never been good at playing it safe.

"I'll do it," he said.

Li Mei nodded, as if she'd expected nothing less. "Good. Your first task is to attend the Monthly Performance Review Meeting. It's in an hour. Supervisor Hu will be there. So will the directors of every department in the Third Heavenly Administrative District."

She handed him a badge. It was made of the same jade as the token, but this one pulsed with a soft golden light.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Assignment Accepted]
[New Quest: Infiltrate the Monthly Performance Review]
[Objective: Gather information on failed audits]
[Difficulty: High]
[Reward: Tier 3 Access, Immortality Candidate Status]
[Penalty for Failure: Immediate Reassignment to Hell of Unending Data Entry]

Zhang pinned the badge to his chest. "Any advice for surviving my first meeting with the celestial upper management?"

Li Mei's smile turned sharp. "Don't make eye contact with Director Zhao of Procurement. He'll try to sell you a used destiny wheel. And whatever you do, don't accept any tea."

"Why not?"

"Because it's probably poisoned. Or enchanted. Or both." She waved her hand, and the world dissolved around him. "Good luck, Clerk Zhang. You're going to need it."

---

The Performance Review Meeting was held in a hall that could have fit the Great Wall of China inside it with room to spare.

Zhang stood at the entrance, trying not to look as intimidated as he felt. The hall was filled with floating platforms, each one carrying a desk and a celestial bureaucrat. Above them, suspended in midair, was a massive screen that displayed the performance metrics of every department in the district.

He spotted Supervisor Hu immediately. The man was sitting on a platform near the front, surrounded by sycophantic clerks who were laughing at jokes that weren't funny. Hu looked nervous. He kept glancing at the door, as if expecting someone to burst in and arrest him.

Zhang made his way to an empty platform near the back. He was about to sit down when someone grabbed his arm.

"You're the new guy," a voice whispered. "The one who hacked the system."

Zhang turned to find a woman with bright pink hair and eyes that glowed with digital static. She was wearing a clerk's robe, but it was covered in pins and patches that Zhang didn't recognize.

"Who's asking?"

"Name's Chen Wei. I'm a Systems Analyst in the Department of Divine Technology." She grinned, revealing teeth that had been filed to points. "I've been watching you ever since you triggered those alerts. That was some beautiful code work. Really elegant."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Sure you don't." Chen Wei's grin widened. "Listen, I've got information you need. Those failed audits? I know why they failed. But we can't talk here." She pressed a small object into his hand—a data chip that hummed with energy. "Meet me in the Archive of Forgotten Souls after the meeting. Come alone."

Before Zhang could respond, a gong sounded. The meeting was starting.

A figure appeared on the main platform—a man so old he looked like he'd been carved from petrified wood. His beard reached his waist. His eyes were clouded with cataracts. But when he spoke, his voice echoed through the hall like thunder.

"Welcome to the Monthly Performance Review of the Third Heavenly Administrative District," he said. "I am Grand Director Zhou. We have much to discuss."

The screen above them flickered to life, displaying a complex web of data. Zhang's eyes widened as he recognized the patterns—they were the same patterns he'd seen in the Core Administrative Interface. The same code that governed destiny and karma.

But there was something else. Something hidden in the data that made his blood run cold.

The Grand Director continued speaking, but Zhang stopped listening. He was too focused on what he'd found.

In the records of every failed audit, there was a single common factor. A name that appeared in the logs, always at the moment when the audit was about to succeed.

A name that shouldn't have been there.

Because the name was his.

Zhang Wei had been sabotaging celestial audits for three centuries.

And he had no memory of it.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Immortal Who Knew Too Little

Zhang's hand froze over the glowing sphere.

*Investigate and... what?*

The message cut off, replaced by spinning runes that looked suspiciously like a loading icon. In the Celestial Bureaucracy, even threat notifications had buffering issues.

"Come on, come on," Zhang muttered, watching the runes spin.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Lost]

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Reconnecting...]

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Restored]

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert Level: Low]

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Recommended Action: File Form 88-B - "Notification of Administrative Curiosity"]

Zhang blinked. Then laughed.

*Administrative Curiosity.* They had a form for that. Of course they did. Someone had probably spent three hundred years designing it, arguing over the font size, and then lost it in a filing cabinet for another millennium.

But the alert had been downgraded. Which meant either the system was truly incompetent, or someone had intervened.

Zhang didn't wait to find out which.

He pulled his hand back from the sphere and examined the new functions available to him. Destiny Modification (Limited) meant he could probably change minor things—someone's career path, a lucky encounter, whether a cat crossed a road at the right moment. Karmic Ledger Viewing would let him see the accounts of any mortal in his division. Timeline Adjustment (Single Thread Only) was the interesting one.

*Can I go back?*

He focused on the function, and a warning appeared:

[Timeline Adjustment - Single Thread Only]

[Warning: Changes to past events may have unpredictable consequences]

[Warning: Paradox events may result in soul fragmentation]

[Warning: This function is monitored by the Temporal Integrity Bureau]

[Current Thread Available: Zhang Wei - Personal Timeline]

[Restricted: Cannot cross other soul threads]

Zhang's excitement deflated slightly. He couldn't go save his past self. But he could access his own timeline, see exactly what happened, maybe find more evidence of the error.

He initiated the timeline view.

The world around him dissolved into a stream of images—his childhood, his parents, his first job, his last breath. Zhang watched himself die again, but this time he saw something different.

Behind the reaper's shoulder, a glowing document floated. The death order. It had Supervisor Hu's signature stamp on it, but there was something else—a second signature, faded and almost invisible.

[Read Signature?]

Zhang focused on the faded mark.

[Signature: Li Mei - Administrative Assistant, Department of Mortal Affairs]

[Status: Deceased - Administrative Purge - 47 Cycles Ago]

Administrative Purge. That was a fancy way of saying someone had been deleted from existence.

Li Mei had signed off on his death, and then she'd been erased. Someone was covering their tracks.

"Zhang Wei!"

The voice shattered the timeline stream. Zhang snapped back to reality, finding himself standing in the white void with Supervisor Hu storming toward him.

No. Not storming. Hu was *gliding*. His feet didn't quite touch the ground, and his robes billowed despite there being no wind. He looked like a manager who had just discovered an employee sleeping under their desk.

"What are you doing in the Core Interface?" Hu demanded. "Your access level doesn't permit—wait." He squinted at Zhang. "Your access level *now* permits this. How?"

"I filed the right forms," Zhang said.

"You've been here three days."

"Three very productive days."

Hu's eye twitched. "The Core Interface requires at least Tier 3 clearance and a minimum of fifty years of service. You're a *junior clerk*. You shouldn't even know this place exists."

"And yet, here I am." Zhang spread his arms. "The system promoted me. Maybe it recognized my potential."

"Or maybe you're exploiting a bug."

*Bingo.*

Zhang kept his face neutral. "That's a serious accusation, Supervisor Hu. Do you have evidence?"

Hu's jaw tightened. He clearly didn't have evidence. He had suspicion, and in the Celestial Bureaucracy, suspicion without paperwork was worthless.

"I'll be watching you," Hu said finally. "And I'll be filing a report. Multiple reports. Cross-referenced reports."

"That sounds very thorough. I look forward to reading them."

Hu glared at him for a long moment, then turned and glided away. But before he disappeared, he paused.

"By the way, Zhang Wei. There's been a change in your assignment."

Zhang's stomach dropped. "What change?"

"You've been transferred. Effective immediately. You're no longer processing minor destiny filings."

"Then what am I doing?"

Hu smiled. It was not a pleasant smile. "The Celestial Bureaucracy has received a complaint from the Southern Ocean Dragon Court. They're disputing a weather pattern allocation from three centuries ago. The case has been... pending."

"Pending for three hundred years?"

"Bureaucratic delays. But now that we have a senior clerk with *initiative*, I thought you'd be perfect to handle it." Hu's smile widened. "The case involves 47,000 pages of documentation, 12,000 witness testimonies, and a karmic audit that has already resulted in three previous investigators being reassigned."

"Reassigned where?"

"One to the Department of Eternal Paperwork. One to the Void of Unanswered Questions. And one..." Hu paused dramatically. "One is still being tracked down. We believe she may have retreated to a pocket dimension to escape the case."

"Great. Sounds wonderful. I'll get right on that."

"I'm sure you will." Hu turned away. "Your new office is on Floor 847, Sector 12, Sub-Sector 39, Cubicle 7,481. You'll find the case files waiting for you. *All* of them."

He disappeared.

Zhang stood alone in the white void, staring at the Core Interface sphere. Hu had clearly dumped the worst possible case on him, hoping to bury him in bureaucracy. Three hundred years of dispute, 47,000 pages, three failed investigators—this was punishment, plain and simple.

But Zhang had something the previous investigators didn't.

*Access to the Core Interface.*

He reached out and touched the sphere again. "Show me the Southern Ocean Dragon Court dispute."

The sphere pulsed, and information streamed into his mind. The case was indeed massive, but the structure was oddly simple: the Dragon Court claimed the Celestial Bureaucracy had allocated them insufficient rainfall for 847 years ago, causing a drought that affected their underwater crops.

*Underwater crops. Right.*

But as Zhang dug deeper, he found something strange. The weather pattern allocation had been processed by...

[Processing Clerk: Li Mei]

Li Mei again. The dead administrative assistant who had also signed off on his death.

*Coincidence?*

Zhang didn't believe in coincidence. Not in a universe where everything was supposedly predetermined.

He pulled up Li Mei's file. It was mostly redacted—her entire existence had been scrubbed from the system. But the Core Interface had backups. Deep in the data stream, buried under layers of deletion protocols, Zhang found fragments:

[Li Mei - Administrative Assistant, Tier 4]

[Specialization: Destiny Corrections, Error Resolution]

[Status: Purged - Reason: Knowledge of [REDACTED]]

[Last Known Action: Flagged Batch 374 for Supervisor Review]

Batch 374. His batch. His death.

Li Mei had flagged the error. She'd tried to fix it. And someone had purged her for it.

Zhang's hands trembled with anger. This wasn't just about his death anymore. This was about a system that killed people for trying to do their jobs correctly.

*I'm going to find out who did this. And I'm going to make them pay.*

But first, he needed to survive the Dragon Court dispute.

He left the Core Interface and navigated through the endless corridors of the Celestial Bureaucracy. Floor 847, Sector 12, Sub-Sector 39, Cubicle 7,481. The numbers seemed deliberately designed to be as inconvenient as possible. By the time Zhang found his new office, he'd walked through 47 identical hallways, passed 3,000 identical cubicles, and seen at least 500 immortals who all looked exactly the same.

The cubicle was a disaster. Papers stacked to the ceiling. Scrolls overflowing from every drawer. A desk so covered in dust that Zhang could have written his name in it.

And sitting on top of the dust pile, a single jade token.

Zhang picked it up.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Incoming Communication]

[Source: Unknown]

[Content: "Zhang Wei. You're finally here. I've been waiting for someone competent to show up. Check the 12th drawer from the bottom, third folder from the left. You'll find something interesting. — A Friend"]

*A friend.* In the Celestial Bureaucracy, friends were probably rarer than competent managers.

Zhang opened the drawer. Third folder from the left. Inside, a single sheet of paper:

[CONFIDENTIAL - EYES ONLY]

[Subject: The Untimely Death of Administrative Assistant Li Mei]

[Findings: Li Mei was purged not for knowing too much, but for knowing the *right* thing. She discovered a pattern in the system—destinies being altered to benefit a specific group of immortals. The alterations were small, barely noticeable. But over centuries, they accumulated. Whoever is doing this is playing a long game.]

[Attached Evidence: List of 47 altered destinies, including yours]

[Signed: A Friend]

Zhang read the list. All 47 names. All 47 lives that had been cut short or diverted for reasons that had nothing to do with fate.

His name was number 32.

*Someone is killing people to maintain a system of control.* And Li Mei had found the pattern. She'd paid for it with her existence.

Zhang folded the paper and tucked it into his robe. He had a Dragon Court dispute to handle, a supervisor to expose, and a conspiracy to unravel.

He sat down at his desk and pulled up the first page of the case file.

*47,000 pages. Let's start with number one.*

Three hours later, Zhang had made it through 847 pages. His eyes hurt. His head hurt. His soul hurt. But he'd found something.

The weather pattern allocation that the Dragon Court was disputing had been processed in the exact same week as Batch 374. The same clerk—Li Mei—had handled both. And both had been flagged for review by the same supervisor.

Supervisor Hu.

Zhang leaned back in his chair. Hu had been the one to process the alterations. Hu had been the one to purge Li Mei. Hu had been the one to assign Zhang to this impossible case.

*He's trying to bury the evidence.*

And that meant the Dragon Court dispute wasn't just a punishment. It was a test. If Zhang solved it, he'd find more evidence against Hu. If he failed, he'd be reassigned to the Hell of Unending Data Entry.

But there was a third option.

Zhang pulled out the jade token and accessed the Core Interface one more time. He found the Dragon Court case file and, with trembling fingers, initiated a function he wasn't supposed to have:

[Karmic Ledger Viewing - Supervisory Personnel]

[Accessing: Supervisor Hu - Karmic Balance]

[Balance: -847,302 Karma]

[Status: Critical - Auto-Review Initiated]

*Negative karma.* In the Celestial Bureaucracy, negative karma meant you were due for punishment. And Hu had nearly a million negative points.

Which meant someone in the system already knew he was corrupt. They just hadn't acted on it yet.

*Or they're waiting for evidence.*

Zhang smiled. He had

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Spiral of Infinite Escalation

Zhang pulled his hand back from the sphere as if burned. The notification blinked innocently in his vision, but he knew better. Someone had just been alerted to his little expedition into restricted files.

*Think, Zhang. Think.*

He was a dead man standing in the administrative core of heaven itself, and he'd just tripped every alarm in the system. The smart play would be to log out, return to his desk, and pretend nothing had happened. Maybe file some erroneous paperwork to create a cover trail.

But Zhang Wei had never been smart. He'd been clever, and there was a difference.

He reached out and touched the sphere again.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: User 7845-B has initiated Core Access Protocol]
[Warning: Unauthorized modifications to the Celestial Management System are punishable by...]
[Searching...]
[Punishable by: Soul Dissolution, Karmic Debt Transfer, Oubliette of Forgotten Names, and/or Reassignment to...]

Zhang scrolled past the threats. There it was—the section he needed. Nestled between "Destiny Modification (Limited)" and "Karmic Ledger Viewing" was a single, unassuming line:

[Function: Administrative Override - User Status Modification]

He selected it.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Accessing Administrative Override]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Current Status: Deceased - Temp Contract (Probationary)]
[Modify Status to: Deceased - Temp Contract (Active)]
[Confirm? Y/N]

Zhang selected Y.

[Status Updated]
[New Benefits Unlocked:]
- Extended Access to Department Files
- Overtime Pay (Karmic Credits)
- Cafeteria Privileges (Level 2 - Hot Meals Included)
- [REDACTED]

The cafeteria. Of course. Heaven's bureaucracy ran on two things: paperwork and noodle soup.

A new notification appeared, this one urgent and flashing red:

[ALERT: Unauthorized Status Modification Detected]
[Source: Internal - Core Administrative Interface]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Action: Bypass of Standard Probationary Protocol]
[Severity: Medium-High]
[Assigned To: Internal Affairs Division - Investigation Team 7]

*Internal Affairs.* That sounded official. That sounded like the kind of department that could make a dead man's afterlife very, very short.

Zhang pulled up the Internal Affairs file. It was surprisingly sparse—just a list of team members, their current assignments, and a single note:

[Note: Team 7 is currently on assignment. Expected return: 72 hours.]

Seventy-two hours. That was his window. Three days to either fix this mess or make himself untouchable.

He was about to log out when something caught his eye. A small, almost invisible folder hidden in the depths of the system. It was labeled:

[DEPT_OF_MORTAL_AFFAIRS - UNOFFICIAL - DO NOT OPEN]

Zhang opened it.
