Inside was a single document. A list. Names, dates, and a single column labeled "Disposition." Zhang scanned the list and felt his blood run cold—not that he had blood anymore, but the sensation was similar.

There, halfway down the list:

[Name: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Date of Death: 2024-03-15]
[Original Disposition: Reincarnation - Tier 2 (Comfortable)]
[Current Disposition: Deceased - Temp Contract (Probationary)]
[Discrepancy Flagged: Y]
[Investigation Status: Pending - Assigned to Supervisor Hu]
[Notes: Fat fingered. Mistyped 8475-B as 7845-B. Both souls sent to wrong destinations. Subject 8475-B currently reincarnated as a golden retriever in suburban Ohio. Subject 7845-B currently working as a junior clerk in Department of Mortal Affairs.]

Zhang stared at the screen.

A golden retriever. In Ohio.

Someone had typed his ID number wrong, and now some other poor soul was living their best life as a pampered pet while Zhang was stuck filing paperwork for eternity.

And Supervisor Hu knew. The note said "Investigation Status: Pending - Assigned to Supervisor Hu." The man had been sitting on this information, probably hoping Zhang would just accept his fate and fade into bureaucratic obscurity.

Zhang's fingers trembled as he closed the folder. He had proof now. Proof that his death was a mistake, proof that someone in the department had covered it up, proof that Supervisor Hu was either incompetent or complicit.

But proof meant nothing if he couldn't use it.

He pulled up the department directory and found what he was looking for: the Internal Complaint System. A way for celestial employees to report misconduct without fear of retaliation. In theory.

Zhang composed a message:

[TO: Internal Affairs Division - Complaint Intake]
[FROM: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[SUBJECT: Formal Complaint - Administrative Misconduct]
[BODY: I am formally lodging a complaint against Supervisor Hu of the Department of Mortal Affairs. Evidence of administrative error resulting in wrongful death and subsequent cover-up is attached. Request immediate investigation.]

He attached the folder and hit send.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Complaint Submitted]
[Estimated Processing Time: 6-8 Business Weeks]
[Due to high volume of complaints, please allow additional time for review.]
[Note: Anonymous complaints are processed with higher priority. Your name has been attached to this complaint, which may result in reduced processing speed.]

*Six to eight weeks.* He'd be lucky to last six to eight hours.

Zhang logged out of the Core Administrative Interface and found himself back at his desk, exactly where he'd been before. The jade token sat innocently in his hand, the same as always. But something had changed.

A woman was standing at his cubicle entrance.

She was tall, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to see right through him. Her robes were a deep crimson, embroidered with golden characters that Zhang couldn't read but somehow understood: "Internal Affairs Division - Senior Investigator."

"Zhang Wei?" she asked, her voice flat and professional.

"That's me." Zhang kept his face neutral, his hands still on the desk. "Can I help you?"

"I'm Investigator Chen." She held up a jade token similar to his own, but hers glowed with a steady, pulsing light. "I'm here about an alert that was triggered approximately seven minutes ago. Unauthorized access to restricted records. Do you know anything about that?"

Zhang met her eyes. "I accessed the Core Administrative Interface to check my status. I was curious about the probationary period."

"Curious." Investigator Chen's lips twitched. "Curiosity is not a valid reason for accessing restricted files."

"I'm new." Zhang shrugged. "I didn't know."

"The system requires confirmation before granting access. There are multiple warning screens. You had to actively bypass them."

"I thought that was standard procedure."

Investigator Chen's eyes narrowed. She stepped into his cubicle, her presence filling the small space. "Mr. Zhang, I've been doing this job for three thousand years. I've interviewed souls who tried to cheat the system, souls who tried to bribe the system, and souls who tried to fight the system. But I've never met a soul who tried to *hack* the system within their first week."

"I didn't hack anything. I just clicked some buttons."

"You accessed the Administrative Override function. That's not clicking buttons. That's knowing exactly where to look." She leaned closer. "Who told you about the Override?"

"No one."

"Liar."

"I'm not lying. I figured it out."

Investigator Chen stared at him for a long moment. Then, unexpectedly, she laughed. It was a sharp, barking sound that seemed out of place coming from someone so severe.

"You're either the bravest soul I've ever met or the stupidest." She straightened up. "I'm going to give you a choice, Mr. Zhang. Option one: I take you to Internal Affairs right now, where you'll be processed and reassigned to a position with significantly less access to sensitive information. I hear the Stygian Ferry Logistical Support Division is always hiring."

Zhang waited.

"Option two: You tell me what you found, and we work together."

"Work together?"

"I've been watching Supervisor Hu for six months." Investigator Chen's voice dropped to a whisper. "He's been covering up administrative errors for years. But every time I get close to proving it, the evidence disappears. Someone in the department is protecting him. Someone with high-level access."

"And you think I can help?"

"I think you're reckless enough to try things that experienced investigators wouldn't. And I think you have nothing to lose." She pulled out a small, black token. "This is a direct line to my desk. Use it if you find anything useful. Don't use it for anything else."

Zhang took the token. It was cold, almost frozen, and it hummed with a faint energy.

"There's one catch," Investigator Chen added. "If you're caught, I don't know you. You're on your own. And if you try to implicate me, I'll make sure your next life is as a dung beetle."

"Understood."

Investigator Chen turned and walked away, her crimson robes trailing behind her. Zhang watched her go, then looked down at the black token in his hand.

He had an ally. Sort of.

And he had seventy-two hours before Investigation Team 7 returned.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]
[Quest: The Investigator's Gambit]
[Objective: Gather evidence of Supervisor Hu's misconduct without alerting his protectors.]
[Reward: Full investigation into your wrongful death + Reassignment to Tier 2 Reincarnation Queue]
[Failure Condition: Soul Dissolution]
[Accept? Y/N]

Zhang selected Y.

[Quest Accepted]
[Time Limit: 71 Hours, 59 Minutes, 47 Seconds]

He tucked the black token into his robe and stood up. Time to go exploring.

The Department of Mortal Affairs was a labyrinth of cubicles, hallways, and endless filing rooms. Zhang had been here for less than a week, but he'd already memorized the layout of his floor. Now he needed to find Supervisor Hu's office.

He navigated through the maze, keeping his head down and his pace steady. Other clerks passed him without a glance—they were too focused on their own work, too buried in the endless flow of paperwork to notice one more face in the crowd.

Supervisor Hu's office was on the third floor, tucked away in a corner that seemed designed to be forgotten. The door was shut, but a faint light glowed through the crack at the bottom.

Zhang pressed his ear to the door.

"...I told you, it's handled." Hu's voice, muffled but audible. "The new clerk doesn't know anything. He's just another expendable soul."

A second voice, higher pitched and slightly distorted: "The system flagged an access attempt. Someone went into the Core Interface."

"Probably a glitch. The system's been acting up since the last update."

"The system doesn't glitch, Hu. It's divine code. It's perfect."

"Then explain the bug I found last week."

Silence.

"What bug?"

Hu laughed. "Exactly my point. You don't know about it because the system hides its own mistakes. We're not dealing with a perfect machine. We're dealing with a machine that's been programmed to *look* perfect."

Zhang's blood—metaphorical blood—ran cold. Supervisor Hu knew about the bugs. He knew the system wasn't infallible. And he was using that knowledge to cover his tracks.

The second voice spoke again: "You're playing a dangerous game, Hu."

"I'm playing

title: The Department of Unexpected Outcomes
content:
Zhang Wei pulled his hand back from the glowing sphere as if burned. The notification hung in the air, pulsing with an ominous amber light.

*Investigate and what?* he wondered. *Delete? Reprogram? Turn into a paperweight?*

He needed to move fast. If the system had already flagged his access, then Supervisor Hu would know soon. Maybe already did know. Zhang looked around the white void, searching for an exit. There had to be a way back to his desk. There was always a way back to a desk in the Celestial Bureaucracy.

"Exit," he said.

Nothing.

"Log out."

The sphere continued to pulse.

"Administrative override, please let me go back to my cubicle so I can pretend I didn't see anything."

A new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Vocal Commands Require Tier 4 Clearance or Higher]
[Current User Clearance: Tier 2]
[Alternative Action: Manual Navigation via Thought Projection]
[Instructions: Visualize your desired destination with emotional clarity]

Zhang closed his eyes. He pictured his cubicle—the flickering light, the stack of unresolved destiny reports, the half-empty cup of celestial tea that had gone cold three centuries ago. He imagined the weight of his chair, the scratch of his pen, the dull ache in his lower back from sitting for too long.

When he opened his eyes, he was back.

The cubicle looked exactly as he'd left it. The jade token sat on his desk, its surface now dark and inert. The stack of reports hadn't moved. Even the tea remained cold.

For a moment, Zhang wondered if the whole thing had been a hallucination. Maybe the stress of death had finally broken his mind. Maybe he was in a special hell where he got to imagine having power before it was cruelly taken away.

Then he looked at his hand.

On his palm, faint but visible, was a glowing pattern of interlocking gears. It pulsed with the same rhythm as the sphere.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Administrative Interface Bound to User]
[Warning: This connection is permanent and cannot be removed]
[Recommended: Do not draw attention to yourself]

Great. A permanent mark that labeled him as a system hacker. Exactly what he needed.

"Zhang Wei!"

The voice came from behind him. Zhang turned to find Supervisor Hu standing in the entrance of his cubicle, face red, mustache quivering with barely contained fury.

"I just received an interesting notification," Hu said, each word clipped and precise. "It seems someone in my department has been accessing restricted files. Files that are *far* above their clearance level."

Zhang forced his face into an expression of innocent confusion. "Restricted files? Supervisor, I've been here all day. I haven't even finished processing the morning's death queue."

"Don't play dumb with me." Hu stepped closer, his bulk filling the cubicle entrance. "The system flagged your soul ID. You accessed the Core Administrative Interface. You looked at the records of your own death."

So much for plausible deniability.

"I was curious," Zhang said, keeping his voice steady. "Wouldn't you be curious if you died and found out it was a clerical error?"

Hu's face went through a series of emotions—anger, surprise, and finally, a cold calculation that Zhang didn't like.

"Ah. So you know."

"Know that you're responsible? Yes. The log was very clear. Clerk ID 2319. That's you, isn't it?"

For a long moment, neither of them spoke. The ambient hum of the office seemed to grow louder, filling the space between them.

Then Hu laughed.

It was not a pleasant laugh. It was the laugh of someone who had been caught but didn't care because they held all the cards.

"Congratulations, Zhang Wei. You've discovered the truth. Your death was indeed a mistake. A filing error. A misplaced decimal point in the reincarnation queue." Hu leaned in close, his breath hot against Zhang's face. "But what are you going to do about it?"

Zhang's hand tightened into a fist. "I could report you."

"To whom? The Department of Internal Affairs?" Hu laughed again. "I am the Department of Internal Affairs. I've been the head of administrative oversight for three hundred years. Every complaint, every audit, every investigation—they all cross my desk before anyone else sees them."

That explained a lot. How Hu had gotten away with so much. How he'd managed to keep his position despite what must have been a long history of errors.

"So I'm trapped," Zhang said.

"Completely. Utterly. Irrevocably." Hu smiled, showing yellowed teeth. "But don't worry. I'm not going to delete your soul. That would raise questions. Instead, I'm going to give you a promotion."

"A promotion?"

"To the Department of Unexpected Outcomes." Hu pulled a scroll from his sleeve and unrolled it. "It's a special department. Handles all the anomalies, paradoxes, and reality glitches that the main system can't process. Very prestigious. Very dangerous. Very likely to kill you permanently."

Zhang read the scroll. It was a transfer order, already signed and stamped. His new title: Junior Anomaly Corrector. His new supervisor: not listed. His expected lifespan: three days, according to the fine print.

"I can't refuse?"

"You can. But then I'll report your unauthorized system access to the Celestial Security Bureau. They'll investigate. They'll find the bind mark on your palm. They'll determine that you're a security threat. And then they'll delete you from existence. Not just death, Zhang Wei. Complete erasure. No reincarnation. No afterlife. Just nothing."

The choice was clear. Accept the transfer and probably die in a few days, or refuse and definitely die immediately.

"Fine," Zhang said through gritted teeth. "I'll take the transfer."

"Excellent." Hu rolled up the scroll and tucked it back into his sleeve. "Report to Floor 666, Section C, Room 13. Your new desk is waiting for you."

Hu turned and walked away, leaving Zhang alone in his cubicle.

Zhang looked at his hand. The gear pattern was still there, still glowing faintly. He had power. He had access to the system. He just needed to stay alive long enough to use it.

He gathered his few belongings—the jade token, a pen that had belonged to his predecessor, a small plant that had somehow survived despite receiving no sunlight or water—and headed for the elevator.

Floor 666. Of course. Because the Celestial Bureaucracy couldn't do anything without being dramatic.

The elevator ride was long and silent. The numbers on the display counted down—500, 400, 300, 200. Below Floor 100, the elevator passed through layers of reality that Zhang could feel in his bones. The air grew thick and heavy. The lights flickered.

*This is fine,* Zhang told himself. *I've been to bad neighborhoods before.*

Floor 666 arrived with a ding.

The doors opened onto a hallway that looked nothing like the rest of the Celestial Bureaucracy. The walls were made of what appeared to be living wood, pulsing with veins of golden light. The floor was covered in grass that rustled as Zhang stepped onto it. The ceiling was open to a sky full of stars that moved in patterns Zhang couldn't understand.

*This is not fine.*

He followed the signs to Section C, Room 13. The door was made of solid bronze, covered in runes that shifted and rearranged themselves as he approached. Above the door, a plaque read:

[Department of Unexpected Outcomes]
[Established: Before Time]
[Purpose: To Manage That Which Cannot Be Managed]
[Motto: "It Was Probably Fine Until You Touched It"]

Zhang hesitated. Then he pushed open the door.

The room inside was enormous. Desks stretched in every direction, piled high with papers, scrolls, and objects that didn't seem to obey the laws of physics. Clerks moved between the desks, some walking on the ceiling, some floating, one that appeared to be made entirely of smoke.

In the center of the room stood a desk that was larger than the others. Behind it sat a woman who looked like she had been fighting the universe for a very long time and was winning. She had silver hair tied back in a severe bun, eyes that glowed with inner fire, and a scar that ran from her forehead to her chin.

"New transfer?" she asked, not looking up from the papers she was reading.

"Zhang Wei. From the Department of Mortal Affairs."

"Ah. Hu's latest sacrifice." She finally looked up, and her eyes narrowed. "You're the one who accessed the Core Interface. The one who's been flagged."

Word traveled fast in the afterlife.

"That's me."

"Interesting." She stood up and walked around her desk. She was taller than Zhang had expected, and she moved with the grace of someone who had survived things that would have killed lesser beings. "I'm Li Mei. Head of the Department of Unexpected Outcomes. Welcome to the place where reality goes to die."

"That's comforting."

"It's not supposed to be." Li Mei gestured to one of the floating desks. "That's yours. Your first assignment is on the desk. Complete it, and you might survive the day. Fail, and I'll have to scrape your essence off the walls."

Zhang walked to his new desk. On it sat a single folder, marked with a symbol that hurt to look at directly.

He opened it.

Inside was a single sheet of paper, covered in handwriting that seemed to move and shift as he read it.

[CASE FILE: UO-7845-B]
[Status: Active]
[Description: Anomalous soul detected in the reincarnation queue. Soul was marked for death but did not die. Soul continues to exist despite administrative error. Soul has accessed restricted system functions. Soul has been transferred to Department of Unexpected Outcomes for resolution.]
[Recommended Action: Determine cause of anomaly. Eliminate anomaly. File report.]

Zhang looked up at Li Mei. "This is my own file."

"I know."

"You want me to investigate myself."

"Seemed appropriate." Li Mei smiled, and it was not a kind smile. "You're an anomaly, Zhang Wei. You shouldn't exist. The system made a mistake, and then you made it worse by hacking into places you shouldn't have gone. Now you're a problem that needs to be solved."

"And if I solve myself?"

"Then we'll have a very interesting report to file." Li Mei turned and walked back to her desk. "You have until the end of the day. After that, I'll have to escalate the case to the Celestial Security Bureau."

Zhang looked at the file in his hands. The words continued to shift, forming new sentences, new questions, new threats.

He had one day to figure out how to save himself from a system that wanted him dead.

And somewhere, in the depths of the Celestial Bureaucracy, he could feel the gears turning. Something was watching him. Something that had been waiting for someone like him to come along.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]
[Quest: The Anomaly's Gambit]
[Objective: Survive the Department of Unexpected Outcomes]
[Reward: Unknown]
[Penalty for Failure: Permanent Erasure]

The room fell silent as every clerk in the department turned to look at him. Their eyes glowed with the same inner light as Li Mei's.

Zhang Wei had a feeling he was about to have a very long day.

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The System Administers a Test

Zhang stared at the alert, his heart hammering against his ribs like a trapped bird.

*Recommended Action: Investigate and—*

He didn't wait to read the rest. His fingers flew across the glowing interface, pulling up every security protocol he could find. The Celestial Management System was ancient, built layer upon layer over millennia. Each new administrator had added their own patches, their own backdoors, their own little secrets.

And Zhang had just found one.

There, buried in the code from the Third Heavenly Dynasty, was a subroutine designed to let clerks bypass monitoring during "emergency system maintenance." It required three things: a valid ID, a karmic credit of at least 100 points, and the balls to use it.

Zhang had all three.

He activated the subroutine. The alert window flickered, turned yellow, then faded to nothing. A new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Emergency Maintenance Mode Activated]
[Duration: 3 Minutes, 47 Seconds]
[All Monitoring Functions: Suspended]
[All Logs: Temporarily Disabled]
[User ID 7845-B: Flagged as System Administrator (Temporary)]

Zhang let out a breath he didn't realize he'd been holding. Three minutes and forty-seven seconds. That was all the time he had.

He went back to the sphere, pulling up Supervisor Hu's personnel file. The man had been in the Department of Mortal Affairs for four hundred years. His performance reviews were mediocre at best—"satisfactory" in every category, never excellent, never poor. The kind of employee who did just enough to avoid getting fired and nothing more.

But there was something odd. Hu's promotion to supervisor had come exactly three months after Batch 374's error. That was fast. Too fast. In the Heavenly Bureaucracy, promotions took decades, sometimes centuries. Three months was unheard of.

Zhang dug deeper. The promotion had been approved by someone in the Department of Internal Affairs—but the approval signature was smudged, the name illegible. Not erased, just... inconveniently blurred.

*Convenient*, Zhang thought. *Very convenient.*

He copied the file to his personal storage and began searching for more. Three minutes and twelve seconds left. He needed to find something concrete, something he could use as leverage.

The sphere pulsed, and Zhang felt a strange sensation—like someone was watching him from behind a curtain. He turned, but the white void was empty.

"Paranoid," he muttered. "You're just paranoid."

But the feeling didn't go away.

Two minutes and forty-five seconds.

Zhang pulled up the Department of Mortal Affairs' internal chat logs. Thousands of conversations, spanning centuries of bureaucratic bickering. He filtered by Hu's ID and started scanning.

Most of it was mundane—complaints about filing, arguments about lunch schedules, passive-aggressive notes about who left the celestial tea kettle empty. But then he found something interesting.

[Private Message - Clerk 2319 to Supervisor Hu]
[Date: 3 Days Before Batch 374 Error]
[Subject: Urgent]

"Supervisor Hu, there's a problem with the reincarnation queue. I think someone's been tampering with the soul assignments. I've flagged it for review, but I wanted to inform you directly before filing the report."

Hu's response came thirty minutes later:

"Acknowledged. I'll look into it personally. Do not file the report yet—I want to verify the data first."

The next message from Clerk 2319 was dated after Batch 374's error:

"Supervisor Hu, I checked the queue again and found the tampering has been corrected. Everything looks normal now. Should I still file the report?"

Hu's response:

"No need. The issue has been resolved. Good work catching it early."

Zhang's blood ran cold. Clerk 2319 had found the tampering. Hu had told her not to file the report. And then Zhang had died.

He checked Clerk 2319's status. The file showed she had been transferred to a different department six months ago. Reason: "Career Advancement."

Zhang pulled up her current location: Department of Records, Sub-basement 7, Data Entry Division.

Sub-basement 7. That was where they sent clerks who had "disappointed" their superiors. It was a dead-end assignment, a place where careers went to die.

Clerk 2319 had been punished for finding the truth.

Two minutes and eleven seconds.

Zhang's jaw tightened. He copied the entire conversation chain and saved it to his personal storage. Then he started searching for more—any record of what had actually happened to Batch 374.

The sphere pulsed again. The watching feeling intensified.

One minute and fifty-three seconds.

Zhang found it. A single line buried in the system logs:

[Batch 374 - Soul ID 7845-B: Reassignment Approved by Supervisor Hu]
[Reason: Administrative Error - Misrouting]
[Compensation: Standard Death Benefits Applied]
[Closure: Case Filed]

It was the official record. The one that said Zhang's death was a simple mistake, already resolved, nothing to see here. But the timestamp caught his eye.

The case had been filed fifteen minutes before Zhang actually died.

Fifteen minutes. Someone had known he was going to die before it happened.

Zhang's hands were shaking now. He pulled up the original reincarnation queue for Batch 374, the one that should have sent him to his new life. The soul assignments were clear—Zhang Wei was supposed to be reborn as the third son of a merchant family in the capital. A comfortable life, decent prospects, nothing special but nothing terrible.

But someone had changed it. Someone had rerouted his soul to a "death" assignment.

And Supervisor Hu had approved it.

One minute and twelve seconds.

Zhang pulled up the audit trail. Every change to the queue left a digital footprint, a record of who had modified what and when. He traced the tampering back to its source.

The user ID was redacted. But the access point was not.

The access point was located in the Department of Internal Affairs.

Someone in Internal Affairs had killed him. And Supervisor Hu had helped cover it up.

Fifty-three seconds.

Zhang saved everything—the logs, the messages, the audit trail. He had enough evidence to blow the whole thing wide open. But he needed to figure out who in Internal Affairs was involved, and why they had targeted him specifically.

He was a nobody. A junior clerk. He had never even met anyone from Internal Affairs.

So why would they want him dead?

Thirty seconds.

The white void began to flicker. The emergency maintenance mode was about to end.

Zhang pulled his hands back from the sphere, ready to return to his desk and pretend nothing had happened.

But then he saw it.

A single file, hidden so deep in the system that even the search algorithms couldn't find it. It was labeled with his name—not his clerk ID, not his soul number, but his name. Zhang Wei.

It had been created before his birth.

Twenty seconds.

Zhang reached for the file, his fingers brushing against the glowing interface. Information flooded into his mind—not data, not records, but something else. A message.

"Zhang Wei. You were not supposed to die. But you were also not supposed to find this. The Celestial Bureaucracy is broken, and those who try to fix it are removed. You have three choices: accept your death, join the corruption, or burn it all down. Choose wisely."

The message ended. The file vanished.

Ten seconds.

Zhang stared at the empty space where the file had been. Someone had known he would find this. Someone had left that message specifically for him.

The question was: who?

And more importantly: could he trust them?

The white void dissolved. Zhang found himself back at his desk, the jade token cold in his hand. The monitoring systems reactivated with a soft chime.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Emergency Maintenance Complete]
[All Systems: Normal]
[Monitoring: Active]
[User ID 7845-B: Standard Access Level Restored]

Zhang sat in silence, his mind racing. He had evidence. He had questions. And he had a target on his back.

His terminal pinged with a new message.

[INCOMING TRANSMISSION]
[Source: Department of Internal Affairs]
[Priority: High]
[Subject: Your Immediate Attention]

Zhang's blood went cold. They knew. Somehow, they already knew.

He opened the message.

"Junior Clerk Zhang Wei,

You have been selected for a routine performance review. Please report to the Department of Internal Affairs, Floor 9, Room 912, at the next celestial hour.

Bring all personal files and work records.

Failure to appear will result in automatic disciplinary action.

Signed,
Internal Affairs Division
Celestial Oversight Committee"

Zhang read the message three times. A performance review. Right after he had accessed restricted records and bypassed monitoring.

There was no way this was a coincidence.

He looked at the jade token, still warm from his unauthorized access. The message from the hidden file echoed in his mind.

*Choose wisely.*

Zhang stood up from his desk. The clerks around him were busy filing reports, processing destinies, maintaining the delicate balance of mortal existence. None of them looked at him.

None of them knew that their system was about to collapse.

Zhang walked toward the elevator. The doors opened automatically, revealing a polished brass interior that gleamed with reflected light.

"Floor 9," he said.

The elevator began to rise.

Zhang checked his personal storage. The evidence was still there—the logs, the messages, the audit trail. He had enough to destroy Supervisor Hu, and maybe even the person in Internal Affairs who had ordered his death.

But he also had the message. The warning. The choice.

Accept. Join. Burn.

The elevator stopped. The doors opened.

Room 912 was directly in front of him. The door was made of solid jade, carved with intricate patterns of dragons and phoenixes. It looked ancient, powerful, and utterly unyielding.

Zhang took a step forward.

And the door opened on its own.

Inside, a figure sat behind a massive desk. The face was obscured by shadows, but the voice that spoke was calm, measured, and terrifyingly familiar.

"Come in, Zhang Wei. We have much to discuss."

Zhang stepped through the doorway.

The door closed behind him with a sound like a tomb sealing shut.

title: The Promotion Nobody Wanted
content:
Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the white void around him shattered like glass.

He was standing in the Celestial Archives again, the jade token still warm in his hand. The massive sphere of gears and runes was gone, replaced by endless shelves of scrolls and data crystals. The transition was so abrupt that Zhang stumbled, catching himself on a nearby shelf.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Investigating Officer: Assigned]
[Estimated Time of Arrival: 3 Minutes]
[Recommendation: Return to workstation immediately]

Three minutes. That wasn't much time.

Zhang's mind raced. He'd been flagged. Someone was coming. If they caught him in the Archives with restricted access records still glowing on his token, he'd be lucky to end up in Hell of Unending Data Entry. More likely, they'd delete his soul and file it under "recycling."

He needed to get out. Now.

But as he turned to leave, something caught his eye. A scroll on the shelf next to him, partially unrolled, with his name written in elegant calligraphy.

*Zhang Wei - Soul Profile - Updated*

That wasn't there before.

He grabbed it, scanning the contents. His birth date, his family line, his mortal accomplishments (few), his sins (moderate), his karma balance (slightly negative). All standard stuff.

But at the bottom, written in a different hand, was a note:

*Discrepancy detected: Soul ID 7845-B's death timestamp does not match predestined date. Investigation recommended. Filed by: ???*

The investigating officer's name was smudged, as if someone had tried to erase it.

Zhang stared at the note. Someone else had noticed the error. Someone else had flagged it. And someone—probably Supervisor Hu—had tried to cover it up.

A chime echoed through the Archives. [1 Minute Remaining]

Zhang shoved the scroll into his robe and ran.

---

He made it back to his desk with seconds to spare.

Li Mei was already there, watching him with narrowed eyes. "Where were you?"

"Bathroom," Zhang said, settling into his seat.

"For thirty minutes?"

"Gastrointestinal issues. Very serious. You don't want details."

Li Mei's eye twitched. "The supervisor was looking for you."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "Hu?"

"No. Someone from Internal Affairs. Said he needed to speak with you about 'irregularities in your access logs.'"

Great. They'd already sent someone.

Zhang forced a calm expression. "Did he say when he'd be back?"

"He said he'd wait in Conference Room C. And that you should bring your jade token."

The jade token. The one with incriminating access records still stored in its memory.

Zhang's mind raced through options. Delete the records? The token would log that too. Hide the token? They'd know. Claim he found it on the ground? Unlikely to work.

He was trapped.

Li Mei leaned closer, lowering her voice. "Zhang. What did you do?"

"Nothing illegal."

"Zhang."

"Mostly legal."

"Zhang Wei."

"Fine. I accessed restricted records. And I found out my death was a mistake. And now someone's trying to cover it up."

Li Mei stared at him for a long moment. Then she sighed, rubbing her temples. "I knew it. I knew the moment you showed up that you were going to be trouble."

"I prefer 'disruptive innovation.'"

"You prefer 'soul deletion waiting to happen.'" She grabbed his arm. "Come on. We need to get rid of that token before Internal Affairs gets here."

"Too late," a voice said behind them.

They turned.

Standing at the entrance to the cubicle farm was a man in a pristine white robe, his face completely expressionless. He wore a badge that read: *Internal Affairs - Investigator Zhao*

His eyes were empty. Not cold, not angry. Empty. Like he was looking at them through a telescope from very far away.

"Clerk Zhang Wei," Investigator Zhao said. "You will come with me."

Li Mei stepped in front of Zhang. "He hasn't been charged with anything."

"This is not a charge. This is a request for information." Zhao's expression didn't change. "If you resist, it becomes a charge."

Zhang gently moved Li Mei aside. "It's fine. I'll go."

"Zhang—"

"I said it's fine."

He followed Investigator Zhao through the Archives, past rows of clerks who pretended not to stare, past floating scrolls and humming data crystals. They entered a small room with a single table and two chairs.

"Sit," Zhao said.

Zhang sat.

The investigator sat across from him, placing a data crystal on the table. It pulsed with a soft blue light. "Do you know why I'm here?"

"Because I accessed restricted records."

Zhao's eyebrow twitched. The first sign of emotion. "You admit it?"

"No point in lying. You clearly know already."

"Indeed." Zhao tapped the crystal. It projected an image into the air—a log of Zhang's access history. Every file he'd opened, every record he'd viewed, every query he'd made.

Including the one from the Core Administrative Interface.

Zhang's stomach dropped.

"You accessed the Core Interface," Zhao said. "A privilege reserved for Tier 3 officials and above. You are a Tier 1 clerk."

"I found a bug in the system."

"A bug."

"Yes. There's a backdoor. I used it to check my own records. That's all."

Zhao stared at him. "You found a backdoor in the Celestial Management System. And you used it."

"Yes."

"Do you understand how that sounds?"

"Like a security flaw that someone should probably fix before a demon finds it?"

For a long moment, Zhao was silent. Then he did something unexpected.

He laughed.

It wasn't a warm laugh. It was the laugh of someone who has just heard the funniest joke in the universe and is trying very hard not to find it amusing.

"You're either very stupid," Zhao said, "or very clever."

"I prefer 'clever.'"

"I'm not sure which is worse." Zhao leaned forward. "Because if you're stupid, you'll be reassigned to Hell of Unending Data Entry within the week. But if you're clever..." He paused. "Then you're exactly what we need."

Zhang blinked. "What?"

Zhao pulled out a scroll and unrolled it on the table. It was covered in official seals and signatures. "Internal Affairs has been watching you since your arrival. The way you handled the karmic audit. The way you exposed Supervisor Hu's filing errors. The way you found a backdoor in a system that has existed for ten thousand years."

Zhang said nothing.

"We have a problem," Zhao continued. "A corruption problem. Someone in the upper tiers has been manipulating destinies, altering karma balances, and covering their tracks with bureaucratic paperwork. We've been investigating for centuries, but we keep hitting walls."

"And you want me to help."

"We want you to be our inside man."

Zhang stared at him. "You want me to spy on my own department."

"We want you to find the source of the corruption. The backdoor you discovered—it's not the only one. There are others. Hidden by someone with deep access to the system. We need to know who."

"And if I refuse?"

Zhao's expression went flat again. "Then you'll be charged with unauthorized access, soul tampering, and administrative misconduct. The minimum sentence is 500 years of data entry in the Hell of Unending Paperwork."

Zhang considered his options.

Option one: refuse, get sentenced to 500 years of paperwork, probably die of boredom.

Option two: accept, become a spy for Internal Affairs, and almost certainly get killed when the corrupt officials found out.

Option three: accept, but use the opportunity to further his own agenda, because he was Zhang Wei and he didn't work for anyone but himself.

"I'll do it," Zhang said.

Zhao nodded, as if he'd expected this. "Good. Your first task: there's a clerk in the Department of Destiny Adjustment named Chen. He's been flagged for suspicious activity. We need proof of his involvement."

"Proof?"

"Records. Transactions. Anomalies. Find them." Zhao stood. "You have one week."

"And if I find something?"

"Then we move to the next phase." Zhao paused at the door. "One more thing. Supervisor Hu has been transferred."

"Transferred?"

"To the Department of Mortal Affairs in the Eastern Division. He'll be processing reincarnations for the next three centuries." Zhao's lips twitched. "Apparently, someone filed a complaint about his filing errors."

Zhang watched him leave, then sat alone in the room for a full minute.

Hu was gone. The man who had covered up his death was gone.

But someone else had written that note on his soul profile. Someone else had flagged the discrepancy.

And Investigator Zhao hadn't mentioned it at all.

Zhang pulled out the scroll he'd stolen from the Archives and unrolled it again. The note at the bottom was still smudged. But now, looking closer, he noticed something he'd missed before.

The ink was slightly different. And there was a faint symbol at the bottom—a small, intricate character that looked like a seal.

*Li.*

Li Mei.

Zhang's blood went cold.

She had written the note. She had flagged the discrepancy. She had been trying to expose the truth all along.

And she had been right next to him this entire time.

He stood up, the scroll clutched in his hand. He needed to talk to her. He needed to know why she'd helped him, why she'd hidden it, and whether she was friend or foe.

He stepped out of the room—and stopped.

Li Mei was standing in the hallway, her back to him. She was talking to someone on a communication crystal.

"—yes, he's in. He doesn't suspect a thing."

A pause.

"Don't worry. I'll keep an eye on him. And if he gets too close to the truth..."

Another pause. This time, when she spoke, her voice was cold.

"I'll handle it."

The crystal went dark. Li Mei turned around and saw Zhang standing there.

Their eyes met.

The hallway fell silent.

And Zhang realized, with dawning horror, that he had just walked into a trap far larger than he'd ever imagined.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Heavenly Overtime Shifts

The white void around Zhang flickered.

A new message appeared, but it wasn't from the system. It was a direct communication, bypassing the standard notification protocols entirely:

[INCOMING TRANSMISSION - ENCRYPTED]
[Source: Unknown]
[Content: They know. Get out. NOW.]

Zhang's blood ran cold. He didn't know who sent the message, but he didn't wait to find out. He slammed his hand against the sphere, initiating an emergency exit protocol he'd discovered in the system logs.

The world dissolved into a stream of data. Numbers and equations screamed past him, and then he was tumbling through a corridor of light, crashing into something solid.

Zhang groaned, pushing himself off the floor. He was back in the main office. The fluorescent lights hummed above him. His desk sat exactly where he'd left it. The stack of filed reports was still slightly crooked.

Everything was normal.

Too normal.

"Zhang Wei!"

Supervisor Hu's voice cut through the office like a blade. Zhang scrambled to his feet just as Hu stormed over, his jade robes billowing behind him. The man's face was the color of an overripe tomato.

"I just received a very interesting system alert," Hu said, his voice dangerously low. "It seems someone accessed restricted administrative records. From inside this department."

Zhang kept his face neutral. "That sounds serious, Supervisor."

"It is." Hu's eyes narrowed. "The system flagged the access as originating from a junior clerk. One who was recently assigned to my department. One who should not have clearance for anything above basic filing."

"I wouldn't know anything about that," Zhang said. "I've been filing. Like you told me."

"Filing." Hu's lip curled. "Yes. Let me see your hands."

Zhang hesitated. "My... hands?"

"Your hands, Clerk Zhang. Now."

Zhang held out his hands. Hu grabbed them, turning them over, examining the palms with the intensity of a jeweler inspecting a diamond. His brow furrowed.

"No data burns," Hu muttered. "Fresh clerks always have data burns after accessing restricted files. The system leaves marks."

"I told you, I was filing."

Hu released his hands with a grunt of frustration. "Fine. But I'm watching you, Zhang Wei. The Celestial Monitoring Division has flagged your soul for Tier 3 surveillance. Every action you take, every file you touch, every breath you draw in this office—it's being recorded."

He turned to leave, then paused, his voice dropping to a whisper only Zhang could hear.

"And if I find even a single piece of evidence linking you to that breach, I will personally escort your soul to the Hell of Ten Thousand Unfinished Spreadsheets. You won't just be tortured. You'll be *audited*."

Hu stomped away, leaving Zhang standing alone in the middle of the office. The other clerks pretended not to have seen anything, their heads bowed over their desks, their hands moving in mechanical rhythms.

Zhang returned to his workstation and sat down. His hands were shaking. He willed them to stop, but they wouldn't listen.

*They know. Get out. NOW.*

Who sent that message? And how did they know what he'd done before the system even flagged it?

He pulled up his terminal, pretending to work on the backlog of destiny reports. But while his fingers typed out standard filing templates, his mind was racing through the code he'd glimpsed in the administrative interface.

There was something else in those records. Something he'd seen but hadn't fully processed in his rush to flee.

The administrative error that killed him—it wasn't just one mistake.

It was a pattern.

Zhang pulled up the file for Clerk ID 2319, the one responsible for misrouting his soul. Standard personnel record. Name: Li Ming. Position: Junior Clerk. Status: Transferred to Department of Karmic Adjustments.

Transferred. Not fired. Not demoted. *Transferred*.

He checked the date. Li Ming had been moved exactly three days after Zhang's death was processed.

That was too fast. Even in heaven, HR moved at the speed of frozen molasses. A transfer that quick meant someone had pulled strings. Someone high up.

Zhang dug deeper. He found Li Ming's access logs, his completed tasks, his performance reviews. Everything looked normal. Boring, even. Just another clerk doing his job.

But then Zhang found it.

A single line buried in Li Ming's metadata, hidden so deep that only someone who knew exactly what to look for would find it:

[Associated Case Files: 7845-B, 7831-B, 7822-B, 7809-B, 7798-B]

Five soul IDs. All processed by Li Ming. All with errors.

Zhang pulled up the records for the other four. His stomach dropped.

Soul 7831-B: A 45-year-old accountant from Shanghai. Died in a car accident. Actually scheduled to die of old age at 87.

Soul 7822-B: A 22-year-old student from Beijing. Died in a building collapse. Actually scheduled to win the lottery and retire at 40.

Soul 7809-B: An 8-year-old girl from Guangzhou. Died of a rare disease. Actually scheduled to become a doctor and cure thousands.

Soul 7798-B: A 70-year-old grandmother from Chengdu. Died peacefully in her sleep. Actually scheduled to discover the secret to extended longevity and revolutionize medicine.

Five souls. Five administrative errors. Five lives cut short for no reason.

And all five errors had been "corrected" by transferring the responsible clerk and burying the records.

Zhang felt something cold settle in his chest. This wasn't incompetence. This was systematic.

Someone in the Celestial Bureaucracy was deliberately killing people. And they were using the system to cover it up.

A new message appeared on his terminal:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Daily Overtime Shift Assignment]
[Clerk: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Assignment: Night Audit Division - Floor 13]
[Time: 19:00 - 07:00]
[Note: Mandatory attendance. Failure to report will result in automatic soul review.]

Zhang stared at the assignment. Night audit. On Floor 13.

Everyone knew about Floor 13. It was where the Celestial Bureaucracy sent clerks who were about to be "retired." The work was impossible, the hours were endless, and the survival rate was exactly zero percent.

This was a death sentence.

But Zhang noticed something odd. The assignment had been issued by an automated system protocol, not by Hu. And there was a hidden clause buried in the fine print, invisible to standard readers but glowing like a beacon in Zhang's enhanced interface:

[Clause: Assignment may be declined if clerk can provide proof of systemic corruption within the department.]

A loophole. A deliberate, carefully hidden loophole.

Someone wanted him to find this. Someone wanted him to fight back.

Zhang looked at the clock. 18:47. He had thirteen minutes.

He pulled up the encrypted communications channel and typed a single message:

[To: Unknown Sender]
[Subject: Re: Earlier Message]
[Content: I found the pattern. Five souls. All processed by the same clerk. All errors. I need more information.]

He sent it and waited.

The seconds ticked by. 18:49. 18:52. 18:55.

No response.

Zhang clenched his jaw. Fine. If he couldn't get answers through the system, he'd get them the old-fashioned way.

He stood up and walked to the office exit. The other clerks didn't look up. They never did.

But as he reached the door, a hand grabbed his arm.

Zhang spun around, ready to fight, and found himself staring at the elderly clerk from his first day. The one who had counseled him about the dangers of the system. The one who had seemed so fragile, so harmless.

The old man's eyes were sharp. Alive. And they held a warning.

"Don't go to Floor 13," the old man whispered, his voice barely audible. "They'll delete you. Not just your soul—your existence. Every memory of you, every mark you've made on the world, every life you've touched. Gone."

"How do you know about the assignment?" Zhang asked.

"I know a lot of things, Clerk Zhang." The old man's grip tightened. "I was the one who sent you that message."

Zhang's eyes widened. "You?"

"I've been waiting for someone like you. Someone who could see through the code. Someone who wouldn't just accept their fate." The old man glanced around, ensuring no one was watching. "There's a resistance. Clerks who know the truth. We've been working for centuries to expose the corruption."

"Then help me," Zhang said. "Give me the evidence I need to bypass the audit."

The old man's face twisted with regret. "I can't. If I give you the files directly, they'll detect it. But I can tell you where to find them."

"Where?"

"The records aren't in the system. They're in the physical archives. Sub-basement level 7. Look for the section marked 'Accounts Receivable.'"

"Accounts Receivable?"

"It's a cover. The real files are behind a wall that doesn't exist." The old man pressed a small jade key into Zhang's hand. "This will open the door. But once you go in, you have fifteen minutes before the system detects the breach. After that—"

"I know. I get deleted."

The old man nodded. "Good luck, Clerk Zhang. You're going to need it."

He released Zhang's arm and shuffled back to his desk, becoming the frail, forgettable clerk once more.

Zhang looked at the key in his hand. It was warm, pulsing with a faint light that matched the rhythm of his heart.

The clock read 18:59.

He had a choice: go to Floor 13 and die, or go to the archives and fight.

Zhang Wei had spent his entire mortal life playing it safe. He'd followed the rules, kept his head down, and died anyway.

He wasn't going to make that mistake twice.

He turned away from the exit and walked toward the stairwell leading down.

As he descended into the darkness, the lights behind him flickered and died.

And somewhere in the depths of the Celestial Bureaucracy, a system alarm began to sound.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Security Breach Detected - Level 4]
[Location: Main Office Complex - Sub-basement Access]
[Intruder: Soul ID 7845-B - Zhang Wei]
[Status: Hostile]
[Directive: Apprehend and Terminate]

[RESPONSE TEAM DISPATCHED]

[Estimated Time of Arrival: 14 minutes, 37 seconds]

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Eternal Tea Break

Zhang Wei barely had time to process the alert before the void around him shattered like glass.

He tumbled through screaming colors—reds that tasted like copper, blues that smelled of ozone, greens that whispered ancient secrets in languages he shouldn't understand. The sphere of gears and runes receded into the distance, growing smaller and smaller until it was nothing but a pinprick of light.

Then silence.

Zhang landed hard on a marble floor, the impact jarring through his spectral bones. He was back in the Celestial Bureaucracy's main office complex, but something was wrong. The halls were empty. The usual hum of administrative activity—the clicking of celestial keyboards, the shuffling of fate manifests, the distant screaming of souls being processed—had vanished.

"Hello?" Zhang's voice echoed through the corridor.

No response.

He checked his jade token. The screen flickered erratically, displaying garbled text interspersed with system warnings.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Network Connectivity: INTERMITTENT]
[Alert: Administrative access has been temporarily suspended pending investigation]
[Your current location: Restricted Zone 7 - Celestial Break Room Beta]
[Recommendation: Await further instructions. Do not move.]

Restricted Zone 7. That didn't sound good. And "do not move" sounded even worse.

Zhang moved.

He crept through the empty corridors, his footsteps silent against the marble. The office cubicles were abandoned—half-empty tea cups still steaming, reports left mid-sentence on desks, a jade monitor playing a looping video of a particularly dramatic mortal divorce. It was as if every clerk in the building had vanished mid-task.

Except they hadn't vanished.

Zhang found the first body around the corner.

A jade-skinned clerk lay sprawled across a desk, snoring loudly. His mouth hung open, and a thin line of drool connected his chin to a stack of destiny forms. Zhang checked his pulse—still alive, just unconscious.

"Hey." Zhang shook the clerk's shoulder. "Wake up."

The clerk mumbled something about "eternal tea breaks" and "manager's approval" before falling back into his slumber.

Zhang moved on. He found more bodies: clerks slumped over their workstations, supervisors dozing in their offices, even a minor god of fortune snoring beneath a celestial vending machine. All unconscious. All dreaming.

He was halfway to the department head's office when a sound stopped him.

Footsteps.

Zhang pressed himself against the wall, holding his breath. The footsteps grew closer—measured, deliberate, accompanied by the soft clink of what sounded like chains.

A figure emerged from the shadows.

She was tall, impossibly tall, with skin that shifted between shades of silver and midnight. Her robes were not the standard celestial uniform but something older—woven from what looked like solidified darkness and starlight. A dozen jade tokens hung from her belt, each one glowing with a different color. Her eyes were empty voids, and when she smiled, Zhang saw too many teeth.

"Ah," she said, her voice carrying the weight of centuries. "The little bug. I was wondering when you'd surface."

Zhang's hand moved instinctively to his jade token. "Who are you?"

"My name?" The woman laughed—a sound like breaking glass. "I've had many names. The Celestial Inspection Bureau calls me Auditor Xiulan. The Department of Mortal Affairs calls me a nightmare. And the souls who've crossed my path? They don't call me anything. They're too busy being unmade."

She took a step closer, and Zhang felt the temperature drop.

"You accessed restricted records," Xiulan continued. "You bypassed security protocols. You touched the Core Administrative Interface without authorization." She tilted her head, studying him like a biologist examining a particularly interesting bacteria. "Do you know what happens to mortal souls who interfere with celestial administration?"

"I'm guessing it's not a promotion."

"Sharp." Xiulan's smile widened. "Your soul gets flagged for immediate reprocessing. You're assigned to Hell of Unending Data Entry—not the shortcut version your friend mentioned, but the actual one. You spend eternity correcting typos in ancient texts that no one will ever read. Your consciousness slowly dissolves until there's nothing left but a vague awareness of misplaced commas."

Zhang's mind raced. He couldn't fight her—she was clearly some kind of celestial enforcer, probably millennia old. He couldn't run—where would he go? He couldn't even access the system anymore.

But he could talk.

"That's interesting," Zhang said, keeping his voice calm. "Because I've been thinking about the administrative error that killed me. And I've been wondering—who authorized my death? Who signed off on the misrouting? And why would someone in the Celestial Bureaucracy want to kill a random mortal?"

Xiulan's smile faltered. Just slightly. But Zhang noticed.

"You're deflecting," she said.

"Maybe. But I'm also curious. Because I accessed the logs, Auditor Xiulan. I saw the chain of command. And the signature on my death warrant wasn't Supervisor Hu's." Zhang took a gamble. "It was yours."

The temperature dropped another ten degrees.

Xiulan's eyes darkened, the voids in her sockets deepening into absolute nothingness. "You're lying."

"Am I?" Zhang pulled up the file he'd memorized before the system went down. "Soul ID 7845-B. Authorized for termination by Auditor Xiulan, Celestial Inspection Bureau. Reason cited: 'Administrative convenience.' That's what you wrote. You killed me because it was convenient."

The silence stretched between them like a blade.

Then Xiulan laughed. Not the broken-glass laugh from before, but something genuine—surprised, almost impressed.

"You're clever," she said. "I'll give you that. Most souls don't even think to check the authorization logs. Most souls just accept their fate and move on to whatever hell awaits them." She stepped closer, close enough that Zhang could see the starlight swirling in her eyes. "But you're not most souls, are you, Zhang Wei? You're the one who found the bug. The one who hacked the system. The one who thinks he can game the Celestial Bureaucracy."

Zhang held his ground. "I'm just a clerk trying to survive."

"No." Xiulan reached out and touched his forehead with one cold finger. "You're a variable. And the Celestial Bureaucracy hates variables."

The world went white.

When Zhang's vision cleared, he was standing in a different place. The Celestial Break Room Beta was gone, replaced by an endless expanse of gray nothingness. A single desk sat in the middle of the void, covered in papers. Behind the desk sat a figure so ancient that Zhang couldn't even perceive its shape—just a vague impression of age and power and absolute authority.

"Sit," the figure said. Its voice resonated through Zhang's very soul.

Zhang sat.

"You have been brought before the Eternal Committee for Administrative Review," the figure continued. "You stand accused of unauthorized system access, destiny tampering, and consciousness hacking. How do you plead?"

Zhang looked around. There was no jury, no witnesses, no one to speak in his defense. Just him, this ancient being, and a mountain of paperwork.

"I plead that I'm still alive," Zhang said. "Or at least, I'm still conscious. And I'd like to keep it that way."

The figure was silent for a long moment. Then it laughed—a sound like mountains crumbling.

"Clever," it said. "Very clever. But cleverness won't save you here, mortal. In this place, we deal in facts. And the facts are clear: you have broken every rule in the Celestial Administrative Code. You have accessed restricted information. You have modified your own destiny. You have—"

"I found a bug in your system."

The figure stopped.

"Excuse me?"

"Your system," Zhang repeated. "It's broken. The Celestial Management System has errors. Miscalculations. Inconsistencies. You think you're running a perfect bureaucracy, but you're not. You're running a house of cards, and one good push would bring it all down."

The figure leaned forward. For the first time, Zhang could make out its features—a face that seemed to contain all faces, eyes that held the weight of eternity.

"You're threatening the Celestial Bureaucracy?"

"I'm offering a solution." Zhang pulled out his jade token. The screen flickered, but he could still access the data he'd copied. "I've found seventeen critical bugs in the system. Seventeen ways that destinies are being misrouted, karma being miscalculated, souls being lost in administrative limbo. Your clerks don't know how to fix them. Your supervisors don't even know they exist."

"And you do?"

"I do." Zhang smiled. "And I'm willing to teach you. In exchange for a few small concessions."

The figure's eyes narrowed. "Such as?"

"First: my death is reversed. I get a new mortal body, full reincarnation with no memory loss. Second: the clerk who tried to send me to data entry hell gets reassigned to something less... permanent. Third: I want a permanent position in the Celestial Bureaucracy. Not as a clerk. As a consultant."

The figure stared at him. Then it burst out laughing.

"You think you can negotiate with the Eternal Committee? You, a mortal soul barely three days dead?"

"I think I can." Zhang stood up. "Because I know something you don't."

"And what's that?"

Zhang held up his jade token. The screen displayed a single line of code:

[ALERT: Critical System Failure Imminent]
[Source: Unknown]
[Time to Collapse: 00:03:47]

"Your system is going to crash in three minutes," Zhang said. "And I'm the only one who can stop it."

The figure's laughter died.

For a long, horrible moment, the void was silent.

Then the ancient being spoke:

"Name your price, mortal."

Zhang's grin spread across his face like a wound.

"I'm glad you asked."

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Secret in the Server Room

Zhang's hand snapped back from the sphere as if burned. The notification pulsed in his vision, a red warning that made his newly-acquired celestial heart—or whatever passed for a heart in this place—skip a beat.

*Unknown Process Detected. Source: Internal. Alert Level: Medium.*

"Internal," Zhang muttered, already moving. "That means someone inside the department flagged me."

He dismissed the notification and pulled up the department directory. Clerk ID 2319. That was the person responsible for his death. Whoever they were, they had access to the system, had made a mistake, and then—if Supervisor Hu was involved—had covered it up.

But Hu was just a supervisor. He didn't have the technical access to bury a record this deep. Someone higher up had helped.

Zhang closed the Core Administrative Interface and found himself back in the white void. The sphere floated before him, its gears still turning, its runes still pulsing. He needed to move fast. If the system had flagged his access, someone would be coming to investigate.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]
[Quest: The Unseen Hand]
[Objective: Identify the person who ordered the cover-up of your death]
[Reward: Access to Department of Internal Investigations]
[Penalty for Failure: Soul reset to Tier 1 Clerk status]

Zhang blinked. A quest. This was actually a quest. He'd been so focused on the bureaucratic nightmare of his new existence that he'd almost forgotten this was, technically, a game-like system.

"Accept," he said.

[Quest Accepted]

The white void dissolved, and Zhang found himself standing back in the filing room. The piles of paperwork were still there, the dusty shelves still loomed overhead, and the faint hum of celestial machinery still filled the air.

But something was different.

The door to the main office was slightly ajar. Through the gap, Zhang could see Supervisor Hu's desk. It was empty. The clock on the wall showed it was still early—most clerks wouldn't arrive for another hour.

This was his chance.

Zhang slipped through the door and into the main office. Rows of desks stretched before him, each covered in stacks of paper, glowing jade tablets, and the occasional forgotten teacup. The ceiling was a vaulted dome of cloudy sky, and somewhere above, Zhang could hear the faint sound of thunder.

He made his way to Hu's desk. The surface was immaculate—a stark contrast to the chaos surrounding it. A single jade tablet sat in the center, dark and inactive. Zhang picked it up.

[ACCESS DENIED: Requires Supervisor Clearance]

Of course. Hu wasn't stupid enough to leave his terminal unlocked.

Zhang set the tablet down and looked around. There had to be something. A clue, a note, anything that would point him toward Clerk ID 2319.

His eyes fell on a small cabinet behind Hu's desk. It was locked, but the lock was old—a simple brass mechanism that looked like it hadn't been updated since the Tang Dynasty.

Zhang smiled. "Some things never change."

He pulled a paperclip from his pocket—one of the few mortal possessions that had somehow made it to the afterlife with him—and bent it into shape. A few seconds of work, and the lock clicked open.

Inside the cabinet were files. Hundreds of them, stacked in neat rows, each labeled with a name and a date. Zhang scanned them quickly, looking for anything that stood out.

And then he found it.

A file labeled "Batch 374 - Reincarnation Queue." The same batch that contained his death record. Zhang pulled it out and opened it.

The first page was a summary of the batch's contents: 500 souls processed, 498 successfully reincarnated, 1 error, 1 pending correction.

The error was listed as "Soul ID 7845-B - Misrouting."

Zhang's fingers traced the entry. Below it, someone had written a note in careful, elegant calligraphy: "Assigned to Clerk ID 2319 for correction. Status: In Progress."

But the note was dated three weeks ago. And the correction had never been made.

Zhang flipped to the next page. It was a communication log, showing messages between various clerks and supervisors about the error. Most of them were routine—status updates, confirmation requests, the usual bureaucratic drivel.

But one message caught his attention.

[From: Clerk ID 2319]
[To: Supervisor Hu]
[Subject: Batch 374 Error]
[Body: The soul in question has been flagged for manual review. Recommend immediate termination of reincarnation process and reassignment to administrative role. Further investigation may reveal systemic issues that could compromise departmental integrity.]

Systemic issues. Compartmental integrity. This wasn't just a cover-up—it was a warning. Clerk ID 2319 had known that Zhang's death was a mistake, and instead of fixing it, they'd recommended burying it completely.

Zhang closed the file and replaced it in the cabinet. He needed to find Clerk ID 2319. But first, he needed to know who they were.

He pulled up the department directory on his internal interface. Clerk ID 2319 was listed as "Wang Lei - Junior Clerk, Department of Mortal Affairs." No photo, no additional information. Just a name and a position.

Wang Lei.

The name was common enough that it could be anyone. But the fact that their identity was so thoroughly scrubbed from the system told Zhang everything he needed to know. Someone wanted Wang Lei to remain anonymous.

Zhang turned to leave—and froze.

Standing in the doorway was a woman he'd never seen before. She was tall, with sharp features and hair the color of polished obsidian. Her robes were a deep crimson, embroidered with golden characters that Zhang couldn't read. And her eyes—her eyes were like twin pools of molten gold, burning with an intensity that made Zhang's soul feel like it was being weighed.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Detected Entity - Investigator Zhao, Department of Internal Affairs]
[Warning: This entity has authority to delete souls without due process]
[Recommendation: Do not antagonize]

Zhang swallowed. "Good morning?"

Investigator Zhao's lips curled into a smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Zhang Wei. Junior Clerk, Department of Mortal Affairs. You've been very busy this morning."

"I don't know what you mean."

She stepped forward, and the air around her seemed to grow heavy. "You accessed restricted records at 6:47 AM. You bypassed standard security protocols. You entered a restricted area without authorization." She stopped in front of him, close enough that he could smell incense and ozone. "And now I find you going through your supervisor's private files."

Zhang's mind raced. He needed a lie, and he needed it fast. "I was looking for a file. Supervisor Hu told me to find it before the morning meeting. Something about a karmic discrepancy in Batch 374."

Zhao's expression didn't change, but Zhang saw a flicker of something in her eyes. Recognition. Interest.

"Batch 374," she repeated. "That's an old batch. Three weeks old, in fact. Why would Hu want you to look into it now?"

"He didn't say. He just said it was urgent."

Zhao studied him for a long moment. Then she reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small jade token, identical to the one Zhang had used to access the Core Interface.

"Interesting," she said. "Because I've been investigating Batch 374 for the past week. And I haven't found a single clerk who's willing to talk about it."

Zhang's heart—or whatever—pounded. "Maybe they don't know anything."

"Or maybe they're afraid." Zhao pocketed the token. "Tell me, Zhang Wei. Do you know what happens to clerks who get involved in things they shouldn't?"

"They get reassigned to data entry?"

"Worse." She leaned in, her voice dropping to a whisper. "They get reassigned to the Department of Forgotten Things. It's a special branch of the bureaucracy where clerks process paperwork for souls that no longer exist. The work is endless. The pay is nonexistent. And once you're assigned there, you never leave."

Zhang felt a chill run down his spine. "That sounds terrible."

"It is." Zhao straightened and turned toward the door. "Consider this your only warning, Zhang Wei. Stay out of Batch 374. Focus on your filing. And if you know what's good for you, forget you ever saw that file."

She walked out, leaving Zhang alone in the silent office.

He stood there for a long moment, his mind racing. Investigator Zhao was clearly involved in the cover-up. But was she the one pulling the strings, or was she just another pawn?

There was only one way to find out.

Zhang pulled up his internal interface and accessed the Department of Internal Affairs directory. Investigator Zhao was listed as a mid-level investigator with a clean record. But there was something odd about her file—a series of redacted entries that dated back several years.

[ACCESS DENIED: Requires Tier 4 Clearance]

Tier 4. That was supervisor level. The same level as Hu.

Zhang closed the interface and made his way back to the filing room. He had a lot of work to do. But first, he needed to find Wang Lei.

And he needed to do it before Investigator Zhao decided to make good on her threat.

As he reached the door to the filing room, a new notification appeared in his vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert]
[The Department of Internal Affairs has flagged your soul for priority monitoring]
[All actions will be logged and reviewed]
[Recommendation: Cease all unauthorized activities immediately]

Zhang stared at the message for a moment. Then he dismissed it and pushed open the door.

"Not today," he muttered. "And probably not tomorrow, either."

The filing room was empty, but Zhang could feel eyes on him. Somewhere in the celestial bureaucracy, someone was watching.

And they were about to learn that Zhang Wei wasn't the kind of clerk who followed the rules.

He sat down at his desk and pulled up a blank jade tablet. Then he began to write.

The first step was finding Wang Lei. The second step was exposing the cover-up. And the third step—

Zhang smiled.

The third step was making sure that whoever had ordered his death paid for it.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Sub-Quest Available]
[Sub-Quest: Find Wang Lei]
[Objective: Locate Clerk ID 2319 and obtain their testimony]
[Reward: Access to sealed records]
[Penalty for Failure: Immediate reassignment to Department of Forgotten Things]

Zhang accepted the quest without hesitation.

It was time to go hunting.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Audit That Wasn't

Zhang's hand jerked away from the sphere as if burned.

The notification pulsed in his vision—an alert sent directly to someone in the system. Someone who would now come looking for him.

"Damn it."

He'd been careless. Of course the system monitored access to restricted records. He'd expected that. What he hadn't expected was for the alert to trigger so quickly. The celestial bureaucracy was supposed to be slow, inefficient, buried under mountains of red tape.

But apparently, security breaches got priority processing.

Zhang looked around the white void. It was empty, featureless, infinite. No exits. No doors. No windows. Just the massive sphere of gears and runes floating in the center.

He needed to get out. Now.

He accessed the interface again, fingers flying through the menus. There had to be a return function. A logout button. Something.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Returning to physical form will require 5 seconds of uninterrupted connection time]

[Current Connection Status: UNSTABLE]

[Threat Detected: Incoming Administrative Lockdown]

[Estimated Time to Lockdown: 12 seconds]

Zhang's heart hammered against his ribs. Twelve seconds. He needed five just to disconnect. That gave him seven seconds to figure out what to do next.

But he wasn't going to just run.

He pulled up the file again—the one that contained the truth about his death. He couldn't download it, couldn't copy it, but he could memorize it. The information burned itself into his mind: Clerk ID 2319. Supervisor Hu. Batch 374. The misrouting error that killed him.

And then he saw something he hadn't noticed before—a footnote at the bottom of the file, written in characters so small they were almost invisible.

[Note: This error was not accidental. Further investigation recommended.]

Not accidental.

Someone had killed him on purpose.

The realization hit Zhang like a physical blow. He'd assumed incompetence. Bureaucratic negligence. A simple mistake in the cosmic machine that could be corrected with enough paperwork and persistence.

But no. Someone in the Celestial Bureaucracy had deliberately misrouted his soul. Someone had wanted him dead.

The question was: why?

He didn't have time to answer that. The lock-down warning had turned red, the numbers counting down in his vision.

[5... 4... 3...]

Zhang initiated the return sequence. The white void began to dissolve around him, the sphere of gears fading into static.

[2... 1...]

The last thing he saw before the system ejected him was a face. Not a real face—a symbol, a glyph that represented an entity in the celestial hierarchy. It was the mark of the Office of Heavenly Investigations.

Someone was watching him.

---

Zhang gasped as his consciousness slammed back into his physical form. He was sitting at his desk, the jade token cold against his palm, his fingers aching from gripping it too tightly.

The office was quiet. Too quiet.

He looked around. The other clerks were still at their stations, but they weren't working. They were staring at him.

All of them.

The fluorescent lights flickered. A piece of paper drifted to the floor. Nobody moved to pick it up.

"Zhang Wei."

The voice came from behind him. He turned slowly, his muscles screaming in protest.

Supervisor Hu stood in the doorway of his office, his face unreadable. But his eyes—his eyes were different. They glowed with a faint golden light that Zhang had never seen before.

"Come with me."

It wasn't a request.

Zhang stood, his legs shaking. He could feel the other clerks' gazes boring into his back as he walked toward Hu's office. They knew. Somehow, they knew what he had done.

The door closed behind him with a soft click that sounded louder than a thunderclap.

Hu's office was the same as always—cluttered, dusty, filled with stacks of paperwork that seemed to defy gravity. But the atmosphere had changed. The air was thick, heavy, pressing down on Zhang's shoulders like a physical weight.

"Sit."

Zhang sat. The chair was uncomfortable. It was designed to be uncomfortable. Everything in this office was designed to make you want to leave.

Hu circled around to his desk, but he didn't sit down. Instead, he stood behind it, his hands clasped behind his back, his glowing eyes fixed on Zhang.

"I received an interesting alert just now," Hu said, his voice calm and measured. "It seems that one of my clerks accessed a restricted file. A file that contains information about a certain... administrative error."

Zhang said nothing. His mind raced, trying to formulate a lie, an excuse, anything that might get him out of this.

"Do you know what happens to clerks who access restricted files?" Hu asked.

"I imagine it involves a lot of paperwork," Zhang said.

Hu's lips twitched. It might have been a smile. It might have been a snarl. It was impossible to tell.

"Clever," Hu said. "But not clever enough."

He reached into his desk and pulled out a file. It was thick, bulging with papers, its edges worn and frayed. He held it up so Zhang could see the label.

[Zhang Wei - Soul ID 7845-B]
[Status: DECEASED]
[Assigned Department: Department of Mortal Affairs]
[File Classification: RESTRICTED]

"You accessed your own death file," Hu said. "Do you know why that's a problem?"

"Because it proves you killed me?"

The words were out before Zhang could stop them. He felt a cold rush of adrenaline as Hu's eyes widened, the golden light flaring brighter.

"I see." Hu's voice was soft, dangerous. "You saw the footnote."

"I saw everything." Zhang leaned forward, his fear transmuting into something else—anger. "I saw that my death wasn't an accident. I saw that someone in this department deliberately misrouted my soul. And I saw that you were the one who signed off on it."

Hu was silent for a long moment. Then he laughed.

It was not a pleasant sound.

"You think you understand what's happening," Hu said, shaking his head. "You think you've uncovered some great conspiracy. But you don't know anything."

"Then enlighten me."

Hu's laughter died. He stared at Zhang, his expression unreadable, and then he did something unexpected.

He sat down.

"I'm going to tell you a story," Hu said. "And then I'm going to give you a choice."

Zhang tensed. He didn't like the sound of a choice. Choices in the Celestial Bureaucracy never ended well.

"Five hundred years ago," Hu began, "there was a clerk named Li Wei. He was much like you—clever, ambitious, unwilling to accept the system's injustices. He discovered a flaw in the Celestial Management System. A bug that could be exploited to modify destinies, to change the course of mortal lives."

"The same bug I found."

"Yes." Hu nodded. "But Li Wei didn't stop there. He used the bug to save his family. His mother was destined to die in a plague. He changed her fate. His sister was supposed to marry a cruel man. He altered her timeline. For years, he manipulated the system for personal gain."

"And then?"

"And then someone noticed." Hu's eyes darkened. "The Office of Heavenly Investigations. They found him, just as they found you. But Li Wei was given a choice—the same choice I'm giving you now."

He reached into his desk and pulled out a small box. It was made of black jade, covered in runes that seemed to writhe and move in the dim light.

"Option one: you accept responsibility for your actions. You undergo a full karmic audit. Your soul is examined, your memories are erased, and you are reassigned to a position where you can do no harm."

"And option two?"

Hu opened the box.

Inside, nestled on a cushion of red silk, was a small golden badge. It was shaped like a gear, with a single rune etched into its center.

"You join us."

Zhang stared at the badge. "Join you? Join what?"

"The Office of Heavenly Reform." Hu's voice dropped to a whisper. "We are a secret organization within the Celestial Bureaucracy. Our goal is to fix the system from within—to root out corruption, to correct injustices, to make the bureaucracy work the way it was supposed to."

"And you think killing me was a correction?"

Hu shook his head. "I didn't kill you. I only signed off on the error because I was told to. The person who actually killed you—the one who misrouted your soul—was someone else. Someone high up in the department. Someone who has been watching you since before you died."

Zhang's mind reeled. Someone had been watching him? Before he died?

"Why?"

"Because you were flagged," Hu said. "The system detected your potential to discover the bug. And there are people in the Celestial Bureaucracy who don't want the bug to be found. People who benefit from the system's flaws."

He pushed the box closer to Zhang.

"Join us, and you'll have access to the information you need to find the person who killed you. You'll have the resources to expose them, to bring them to justice. You'll have power."

"And if I refuse?"

Hu's expression hardened. "Then you'll be processed. Your memories will be wiped. You'll be sent to the Hell of Unending Data Entry, where you'll spend the next thousand years filing paperwork in an endless loop. Do you want that?"

Zhang looked at the badge. It gleamed in the dim light, promising power, justice, revenge.

But something didn't add up.

"If you're a secret reform organization dedicated to fixing the system," Zhang said slowly, "then why did you let the person who killed me go free? Why did you cover up the error?"

Hu's face flickered. For just a moment, Zhang saw something there—fear.

"Because we couldn't expose them," Hu said. "Not yet. They're too powerful. If we moved too soon, they'd destroy everything we've built."

"So you sacrificed me instead."

"We tried to save you." Hu's voice was almost pleading. "We monitored you after your death. We made sure you were assigned to this department, where we could keep an eye on you. We gave you access to the jade token, hoping you'd discover the bug on your own."
