"You wanted me to find it."

"Yes." Hu nodded. "We need someone like you, Zhang Wei. Someone clever enough to exploit the system. Someone brave enough to challenge it. Someone who has nothing left to lose."

Zhang stared at the badge. It was beautiful, hypnotic, promising him everything he wanted.

But he couldn't shake the feeling that Hu was lying.

"What happens to Li Wei?" Zhang asked.

Hu's face went pale.

"Li Wei is dead," he said. "He tried to expose the corruption too early. The Office of Heavenly Investigations found him. His soul was deleted. There is nothing left of him."

"He's dead because he joined you."

"He's dead because he was careless." Hu's voice hardened. "He made mistakes. He trusted the wrong people. But you don't have to make the same mistakes. You can learn from his death."

Zhang reached out and picked up the badge. It was warm against his palm, pulsing with a faint energy.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Faction Available]
[Office of Heavenly Reform]
[Description: A secret organization dedicated to correcting the flaws in the Celestial Management System. Membership offers access to restricted information, advanced administrative tools, and protection from internal investigations.]
[Join? Y/N]

Zhang's finger hovered over the Y key.

And then he remembered the footnote.

*This error

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The God of Lost Receipts

The warning light on Zhang's jade token pulsed three times before going dark. He stared at it, waiting for the celestial equivalent of security to burst through the void and drag him to the Hell of Interrogations. But nothing happened. The void remained empty. The sphere of gears and runes continued its steady rotation.

*That's not right,* Zhang thought. *If they detected my access, why haven't they—*

Then he saw it. A small, almost invisible line of text at the bottom of the sphere's interface:

[Note: Alert suppressed by Admin Override - Protocol 7-Beta]

Someone had blocked the alert. Someone with higher access than the system's automatic security protocols. Zhang's grin returned. It seemed he wasn't the only one interested in bending the rules.

He committed the protocol number to memory and pulled up the file on Supervisor Hu one more time. The death error was clear: Clerk ID 2319 had misrouted his soul, and Hu had signed off on the correction request without actually correcting it. But the file was missing something. A timestamp. The exact moment Hu had approved the error.

*Convenient.*

Zhang dismissed the interface and let himself fade back into the physical world of the Heavenly Bureaucracy. He opened his eyes to find himself still seated at his desk, stack of forms untouched, jade token warm against his chest.

The office was eerily quiet. The clerks who had been bustling about earlier were now frozen in place, their bodies still, their eyes glassy. A system update. Zhang had seen this happen twice before—the entire department would pause for exactly three minutes while the Celestial Management System patched itself.

*Three minutes of unsupervised access.*

He pulled up the department directory on his terminal. Fifty-three names. Fifty-three souls. Each one had a file, a history, and—if he dug deep enough—a weakness. But he wasn't looking for weaknesses. He was looking for connections.

Clerk ID 2319. The name attached to that ID was: *Chen Wei*. No relation. Just a coincidence of surnames. Chen was a junior clerk, same as Zhang, but with a different specialty: Destiny Adjustment for the Southeast Asia sector. Zhang pulled up Chen's recent activity log.

[Log Entry 4,782: Soul ID 7845-B - Re-routed to Death Queue]
[Reason: Administrative Error - Priority Mismatch]
[Supervisor Approval: Hu Changming]
[Note: "Process as normal."]

*Process as normal.* Three words that had killed him. Three words that had been typed by Chen Wei's hands but dictated by Hu's authority. Zhang copied the log entry and stashed it in a hidden folder he'd created deep within the system's backup archives.

The clerks stirred back to life. The update was over.

Zhang turned back to his forms and began filling them out with mechanical precision. Name. Department. Incident Report Number. He wrote the details of his own death in triplicate, just as the procedure demanded. But with each stroke of the brush, he was building a case. A case against Hu, against Chen, against the entire system that had marked him for death and then tried to bury the evidence.

A shadow fell across his desk.

"Working hard, I see."

Zhang looked up. Standing before him was a woman he'd never seen before. She was tall, with silver hair that fell past her shoulders and eyes that seemed to shift colors—gold one moment, green the next. She wore a uniform of deep crimson, the color reserved for Internal Audit.

*Internal Audit.* The department that investigated clerical misconduct. The department that could delete souls without a second thought.

"Just filing my incident report," Zhang said, keeping his voice neutral. "Standard procedure."

The woman smiled. It wasn't a friendly smile. "Standard procedure requires you to file that report with your direct supervisor. Not to bury it in the backup archives."

Zhang's blood ran cold. *She knows.*

"I don't know what you're talking about," he said.

"Don't play dumb, Zhang Wei. I've been watching you since the moment you accessed the Core Administrative Interface. Protocol 7-Beta? That was me." She leaned in close, her voice dropping to a whisper. "I'm the one who blocked the alert. And I'm the one who's going to help you take down Supervisor Hu."

Zhang stared at her. "Why would you help me?"

"Because Hu has been stealing from the department for three hundred years. Office supplies, yes. But also karma. Destiny threads. Soul allocations." She pulled out a small leather-bound ledger and placed it on Zhang's desk. "This is the evidence. Every transaction. Every伪造receipt. Every soul he's sold to the lower realms for personal profit."

Zhang opened the ledger. The pages were filled with neat, precise handwriting, each entry timestamped and signed. The amounts were staggering. Hu had siphoned enough karma to fuel a minor god's ascension. He had sold enough souls to populate a small hell.

"Why haven't you reported him?" Zhang asked.

"Because Internal Audit answers to the Celestial Management Board. And the Board answers to the Three Pure Ones. And the Three Pure Ones..." She paused, her eyes flickering with something like fear. "The Three Pure Ones don't care about a few missing souls. They care about stability. And exposing Hu would destabilize the entire Department of Mortal Affairs."

"So you need a scapegoat."

"No. I need an insider. Someone who's already been wronged by Hu. Someone who has nothing to lose." She reached into her uniform and pulled out a small, glowing key. "This is a master key to the Archive of Lost Receipts. It contains every financial transaction in the Heavenly Bureaucracy for the past thousand years. If Hu has been faking receipts, the proof is in there."

Zhang took the key. It was warm in his hand, pulsing with a faint light. "And what do you get out of this?"

The woman smiled again, and this time it reached her eyes. "I get to watch a bureaucrat burn."

She turned and walked away, her crimson uniform fading into the white light of the office like blood dispersing in water. Zhang was left alone with the ledger, the key, and a growing sense that he had just made a deal with something far more dangerous than Supervisor Hu.

He tucked the ledger into his desk drawer and slipped the key into his sleeve. The Archive of Lost Receipts was located on the 49th floor of the Heavenly Archive Tower, a place so heavily guarded that even immortals needed special clearance to enter.

*Nothing to lose,* he repeated to himself. *She's right. I'm already dead. What's the worst that could happen?*

A new notification appeared on his jade token:

[EMERGENCY BROADCAST: All personnel report to Assembly Hall Gamma-7]
[Subject: Immediate Termination of Soul ID 7845-B]
[Reason: Unauthorized access to classified records]
[Issued by: Supervisor Hu Changming]

Zhang's heart stopped. *He knows.*

The office erupted into chaos. Clerks scrambled, alarms blared, and the temperature dropped as celestial enforcement units materialized at every exit. Zhang grabbed the ledger and the key, shoved them into his robes, and ran.

He burst through the back door of the office and into a hallway he'd never seen before. The walls were lined with doors—thousands of them, each one labeled with a different realm, a different dimension, a different possibility. Some were marked in languages he couldn't read. One was labeled in what looked like binary code.

*The Archive. I need to get to the Archive.*

He sprinted down the hallway, his footsteps echoing in the infinite space. Behind him, he heard the shouts of enforcement units, the crackle of celestial energy, the roar of something that sounded like a tiger but was probably a divine beast.

He reached the end of the hallway and found himself facing a wall of solid jade. No doors. No windows. Just a smooth, impenetrable surface.

*Dead end.*

Zhang pressed his palm against the wall, desperate. The jade token on his chest flared to life, and the wall dissolved into a shimmering portal. On the other side, he could see rows upon rows of shelves, each one stacked with receipts that glowed with their own inner light.

The Archive of Lost Receipts.

He stepped through the portal just as the enforcement units rounded the corner. The wall solidified behind him, trapping him inside.

Zhang stood in the silence of the Archive, surrounded by a thousand years of financial records. Somewhere in this maze of receipts was the proof he needed to destroy Supervisor Hu. And somewhere behind him, an army of celestial enforcement units was trying to destroy him first.

He pulled out the master key and held it up to the dim light. It glowed, casting a beam that cut through the darkness like a blade.

*Time to find the truth.*

But as he took his first step into the Archive, a voice echoed from the shadows:

"I wouldn't do that if I were you."

Zhang spun around. Standing in the darkness, half-hidden by a shelf of glowing receipts, was a figure he recognized. A figure he had hoped never to see again.

Clerk ID 2319. Chen Wei.

The man who had killed him.

Chen Wei stepped forward, his face pale, his hands trembling. "I know why you're here. And I know what you're looking for. But if you find it, you won't just destroy Hu. You'll destroy the entire Heavenly Bureaucracy."

"Good," Zhang said.

"No. You don't understand." Chen Wei's voice cracked. "Hu isn't just stealing from the department. He's stealing from the system itself. The Celestial Management System runs on karma. If you expose Hu, the system will crash. Every soul in every realm will be affected. Billions of people, Zhang. Billions of destinies. Gone."

Zhang stared at the man who had ended his life with a single keystroke. He wanted to hate him. He wanted to strangle him. But all he felt was a cold, empty clarity.

"Then I guess I'll just have to fix the system after I break it."

He turned and walked deeper into the Archive, leaving Chen Wei standing alone in the darkness.

Behind him, the master key pulsed with a rhythm that matched his heartbeat. Ahead of him, the truth waited.

And somewhere above, in the highest reaches of the Heavenly Bureaucracy, a pair of silver eyes watched his every move.

The woman in crimson smiled.

*Perfect.*

title: The Accountant Who Knew Too Much
content:
The notification pulsed ominously in Zhang's vision, a glowing red warning that made his newly-acquired administrative interface feel less like a gift and more like a leash. But before he could curse his own recklessness, another message appeared—this one from an unexpected source.

[PRIVATE MESSAGE - SENDER: ACCOUNTING DIVISION - CLERK LI WEI]

[Subject: You're in danger]

[Message: Stop what you're doing. Meet me in Archive Sub-Level 7. Come alone. I know who killed you.]

Zhang stared at the message. Accounting Division. That was two floors below Mortal Affairs, in a section of the Celestial Bureaucracy so boring that even the immortals assigned there reportedly fell into comas from sheer tedium. Who the hell was Clerk Li Wei, and how did they know about his death?

He should ignore it. Clearly, this was a trap. Supervisor Hu had probably set it up, trying to lure him into a secluded archive where celestial security could "accidentally" delete his soul during a routine system purge. That was exactly the kind of bureaucratic murder that would never get investigated.

But the message had one detail that stopped him: "I know who killed you." Not "I know about your death." Not "I know your death was an error." Who killed you. As if his death was a murder, not a clerical mistake.

Zhang checked his internal clock. The next system purge of unauthorized messages would happen in twelve minutes. If he was going to respond, he needed to do it now.

He typed back: [Why should I trust you?]

The response came instantly: [Because Supervisor Hu isn't your enemy. He's a distraction. The real problem is in Accounting.]

That changed things. If this Li Wei person was trying to redirect blame from Hu onto someone else, they were either telling the truth or playing a very sophisticated game. Either way, Zhang needed more information.

[Fine. Archive Sub-Level 7. Ten minutes.]

He closed the interface and looked around the white void. The Core Administrative Interface still floated before him, its gears turning in their endless rhythm. He had so much more to explore here—the Destiny Modification functions, the Karmic Ledger, the tantalizing [REDACTED] option that probably let him do something incredibly illegal and awesome.

But first, he needed to survive the next hour.

Zhang pulled up his interface and located the navigation system. Archive Sub-Level 7 was technically part of the Records Department, which was technically part of the Celestial Library, which was technically in a completely different dimensional pocket than the main administrative building. Getting there would require using the transit system—a network of teleportation platforms that connected the various sections of the Heavenly Bureaucracy.

He materialized in the transit hub, a massive chamber filled with floating platforms that hummed with celestial energy. Immortals of various ranks moved between them, their robes swirling with authority and boredom. No one looked at him. That was the beauty of being a junior clerk—you were invisible until someone needed to blame you for something.

Platform 7-G. Archive Division. He stepped onto it and felt the familiar lurch of dimensional displacement.

The Archive was everything he expected and worse. Rows upon rows of shelves stretched into infinity, each one filled with scrolls, tablets, and crystalline data storage units that glowed with trapped knowledge. The air was cold and dry, carrying the faint scent of ancient paper and ozone. And it was quiet. Deathly quiet. The kind of quiet that made you paranoid about your own footsteps.

Zhang walked through the maze of shelves, following the directions Li Wei had sent. Sub-Level 7 was the lowest accessible floor, reserved for records so old that even the archivists had forgotten what they contained. The lighting grew dimmer as he descended, the celestial glow of the upper levels replaced by flickering runes that cast long, dancing shadows.

He found Clerk Li Wei at the designated meeting point: a small reading area surrounded by shelves of what appeared to be tax records from the Tang Dynasty. Li Wei was a middle-aged man with the tired eyes of someone who had spent centuries doing spreadsheets. He wore the plain grey robes of a junior accountant, and his fingers were stained with what looked suspiciously like ink that had dried before the invention of paper.

"You're Zhang Wei," Li Wei said. It wasn't a question.

"And you're the person who's about to explain why I shouldn't turn you in for unauthorized communication."

Li Wei smiled, and it was not a pleasant expression. "You won't turn me in because you know your death wasn't an accident. You accessed the records. You saw the error log. You know someone deliberately misrouted your soul."

"I know Supervisor Hu's ID was on the correction request."

"Hu's ID, yes. But do you know whose ID was on the original processing order?"

Zhang's blood ran cold. He hadn't thought to check that. He'd been so focused on the correction that he'd assumed the original processing was routine. But Li Wei was right—if someone had deliberately killed him, they would have been the one to process his death in the first place.

"Whose ID?"

Li Wei pulled out a jade tablet and held it up. The surface shimmered, displaying a name that made Zhang's stomach drop.

[Clerk ID: 0001-ADM]
[Name: Zhao Ming]
[Department: Accounting Division - Senior Auditor]
[Status: Active]

Zhao Ming. The name meant nothing to Zhang. But the ID prefix did. 0001-ADM. That was the prefix assigned to the most senior administrators in the Celestial Bureaucracy. The ones who had been around since the system was first created. The ones who could delete a soul with a single keystroke and never face consequences.

"Why would a senior auditor want me dead?" Zhang asked. "I was a nobody. I worked in tech support. I barely made enough money to afford rent."

Li Wei's smile vanished. "You weren't always a nobody, Zhang Wei. Or rather, you weren't supposed to be. Do you remember your past life?"

"My past... wait. You mean before this life?"

"I mean before you were reincarnated the first time. And the second time. And the third."

The world tilted. Zhang grabbed onto a nearby shelf to steady himself. Reincarnated multiple times? He only remembered one life—the miserable one where he died from a ceiling collapse. But Li Wei was talking about lives he didn't remember at all.

"I don't understand."

"Of course you don't. They wiped your memories every time. But the records don't lie." Li Wei tapped his tablet, and a stream of data flowed across its surface. "You've been reincarnated seven times, Zhang Wei. Each time, you died under suspicious circumstances. Each time, your soul was flagged for 'administrative review.' And each time, a clerk in Accounting Division processed your death personally."

"Zhao Ming."

"Zhao Ming. He's been following you through multiple lifetimes, killing you over and over, and the system has been covering it up."

Zhang's mind raced. Seven deaths. Seven lives he couldn't remember. A senior auditor who had been hunting him across centuries. And he had no idea why.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I'm next." Li Wei's voice cracked. "I found the pattern. I traced the records. I know what Zhao Ming is doing, and I know why. But I made the mistake of accessing the wrong file, and now he knows I know. I have maybe a day before my soul gets 'reassigned to a different department,' if you know what I mean."

"Then why are you here? Why not run?"

"Because I need you to finish what I started." Li Wei grabbed Zhang's arm with surprising strength. "I've been a clerk in Accounting for three hundred years. I've seen every trick, every loophole, every way the system can be used to hide things. But I've never seen anyone with your access level. You're a Tier 2 Senior Clerk, and you only died a week ago. Do you understand how impossible that is?"

Zhang thought about the Core Administrative Interface, the Destiny Modification functions, the [REDACTED] option. He hadn't questioned it at the time, but Li Wei was right. He'd jumped from Tier 1 to Tier 2 in a matter of days, bypassing years of bureaucratic advancement. That wasn't normal.

"Someone in the system is promoting you," Li Wei continued. "Someone with enough authority to override the standard progression protocols. And that someone is also the person who's been keeping you alive long enough to find the truth."

"Zhao Ming?"

"No. Zhao Ming wants you dead. There's someone else. Someone above even the senior auditors. I don't know who they are, but they've been watching you. Protecting you. And they've been feeding me information through back channels."

Li Wei reached into his robe and pulled out a small crystal, no bigger than a thumbnail. It glowed with a soft blue light that pulsed like a heartbeat.

"This contains everything I've found. Zhao Ming's records, the reincarnation logs, the names of everyone involved in your deaths. Take it. Use it. But don't trust anyone in Accounting Division. And whatever you do, don't access the file marked..."

Li Wei's eyes went wide. His mouth opened, but no sound came out. Then his body began to dissolve, particles of light streaming away from him like sand in a wind.

"No!" Zhang lunged forward, trying to grab the falling crystal, but it passed through his fingers and embedded itself in his chest, merging with his soul interface.

[Soulbound Item Acquired: Li Wei's Research Crystal]
[Warning: Corrupted Data Detected]
[Origin: Unknown]
[Recommendation: Delete Immediately]

Above them, a voice echoed through the Archive—a voice that carried the weight of ancient authority and absolute power.

"Clerk Li Wei has been reassigned to the Department of Eternal Recollection. His records have been sealed. His existence has been noted for archival purposes."

Zhang looked up to see a figure descending from the ceiling, robes of pure white flowing around them like wings. The figure's face was smooth, featureless, a mask of celestial perfection. But the voice was unmistakable.

Zhao Ming.

"You're next, Zhang Wei," the figure said. "But first, I want to show you something. I want you to see what happens to people who try to expose the truth."

The figure raised a hand, and the Archive around them began to shift. The shelves rearranged themselves, the runes flickered, and Zhang found himself standing in a vast chamber filled with screens. Each screen showed a different scene—a different life, a different death, a different Zhang Wei.

"You've been wondering why I keep killing you," Zhao Ming said, stepping closer. "The answer is simple. You weren't supposed to exist. Your soul was a mistake—a glitch in the system that should have been deleted during the first purge. But somehow, you kept coming back. Kept remembering. Kept threatening the stability of the entire Celestial Bureaucracy."

"I don't remember anything."

"Not yet. But you will. And when you do, you'll understand why I can't let you live."

The screens flickered, and Zhang saw himself in a past life—a scholar in ancient China, arguing with a celestial official about the nature of fate. He saw himself in another life—a rebel leader, fighting against the heavens themselves. He saw himself in a life he couldn't comprehend—a being of pure energy, challenging the very structure of reality.

"You're not a mortal, Zhang Wei. You never were. You're a bug. A flaw in the code. And I'm the one who's been tasked with patching you out."

Z

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The God of Paperclips

Zhang released the sphere like it had burned him.

The notification was already spreading through the system—he could feel it in the way the void around him trembled, in the way the gears of the Celestial Administrative Interface ground to a halt for just a fraction of a second before resuming their eternal rotation.

*They know.*

But that was impossible. He'd used a bypass protocol. The system shouldn't have flagged him until he actually *did* something worth flagging.

Unless someone was watching.

A figure emerged from the white void. She wore the standard-issue celestial clerk robes—blue-grey, functional, utterly forgettable—but there was nothing forgettable about her face. It shifted, cycling through a thousand different expressions and features every second, never settling on any one form long enough to be recognized.

"Zhang Wei," she said, and her voice was a chorus of a thousand voices speaking in perfect unison. "You've been busy."

Zhang's hand instinctively reached for the jade token hanging at his belt. "Who's asking?"

"I'm Clerk 2319. Formerly." Her face settled, just for a moment, into something almost human. "But you'd know me better as the person who killed you."

The air left Zhang's lungs. "You're the one who misrouted my soul."

"I'm the one who found a bug in the Celestial Management System. Misrouted your soul." She laughed—a sound like breaking glass. "That's putting it kindly. I *reprogrammed* your soul. You were supposed to be reborn as a minor noble in the Azure Continent Dynasty. I rerouted you through the Reincarnation Queue's garbage collector, corrupted your karmic signature, and sent you straight to the Department of Mortal Affairs' 'unprocessed deaths' folder."

"Why?"

"Because I needed someone with your specific... qualifications." Her face flickered through a dozen different identities. "Someone with no attachments to the celestial realm. Someone who wouldn't be missed. Someone who could see the code."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "You've been watching me."

"Every keystroke. Every bypass. Every clever little workaround." She stepped closer, and the void around them rippled like water. "You think you're the first mortal to discover the system's flaws? You think Supervisor Hu is the only corrupt official in the Heavenly Bureaucracy?"

"I think I'm about to get my soul deleted."

"You're about to get promoted." She held out her hand. In her palm sat a paperclip. Not a magical one, not a divine artifact—just an ordinary, mundane paperclip. "Take it."

Zhang stared at the paperclip. "You want me to pick up a paperclip."

"It's not a paperclip. It's a key." Her face cycled faster, almost manic. "The Celestial Management System runs on seven layers of reality. Layer one is what you see—the offices, the filing cabinets, the endless paperwork. Layers two through six are the scaffolding, the architecture, the bones of reality itself. But layer seven..." She smiled with a hundred different mouths. "Layer seven is where the actual immortals live. And layer seven is *broken*."

The paperclip glowed with a faint, golden light. Zhang could see the code running through it—lines of celestial programming so dense they hurt to look at.

"The God of Paperclips," Zhang said slowly, "is a real position?"

"One of the most powerful in the celestial hierarchy. Paperclips hold documents together. Documents contain knowledge. Knowledge is power." She pressed the paperclip into his hand. "You're now the acting God of Paperclips. Congratulations. Your first task is to find out why the System Administrator has been dead for three thousand years."

Zhang nearly dropped the paperclip. "The System Administrator is *dead*?"

"Murdered. Or deleted. Or maybe he just retired and forgot to tell anyone." She shrugged, and her face flickered through a dozen different expressions of complete indifference. "Nobody knows for sure. But the celestial bureaucracy has been running on autopilot ever since. Every decision, every promotion, every death—it's all been handled by the system's default protocols."

"That's insane."

"That's the Heavenly Bureaucracy." She began to fade, her form dissolving into the white void. "You've got the paperclip now. You've got access. Don't waste it."

"Wait!" Zhang grabbed at her arm, but his hand passed through empty air. "Why are you helping me? What do you get out of this?"

Her face settled one last time—an old woman, tired and weathered, with eyes that had seen too much.

"I get to watch someone else carry the burden for a while."

And then she was gone.

Zhang stood alone in the void, holding a paperclip that glowed with the power of a minor god. He had a promotion he hadn't applied for, a mystery he hadn't asked to solve, and a dead System Administrator who apparently ran the entire afterlife.

*This is fine. This is completely fine.*

He looked down at the paperclip. A message appeared in his vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Promotion Confirmed]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[New Title: God of Paperclips (Acting)]
[Access Level: Tier 5 - Minor Deity]
[New Functions Unlocked:]
- Divine Manifestation (Paperclip Form Only)
- Administrative Reality Warping (Clip-and-Paste Function)
- Document Binding (Soul Level)
- [REDACTED]
- [REDACTED]
- [REDACTED - You shouldn't be able to see this]

[Warning: Your mortal soul is not designed to handle divine authority. Extended use of divine functions may result in existential dissolution.]

*Existential dissolution.* That was new. Zhang filed it away under "problems for future Zhang" and focused on the more immediate issue: he was now a god. A minor god of office supplies, but a god nonetheless.

He tested his new powers. A thought, and the paperclip in his hand multiplied into a dozen identical copies. Another thought, and they arranged themselves into the shape of a middle finger.

*Perfect.*

The void around him began to dissolve. Reality snapped back into place, and Zhang found himself standing in the middle of the Department of Mortal Affairs. His desk was still there, covered in the same mountain of paperwork. His coworkers were still there, filing and stamping and shuffling through their eternal drudgery.

But now Zhang could see the code.

Every document glowed with lines of celestial programming. Every filing cabinet hummed with administrative energy. Every clerk moved in patterns dictated by the system—robotic, predictable, *controlled*.

They were all puppets. And the strings were fraying.

"Zhang Wei!"

Supervisor Hu's voice cut through the office like a blade. The man stood in his doorway, face purple with rage. "What did you do?"

Zhang smiled. "I'm not sure what you mean, Supervisor."

"Don't play dumb with me!" Hu stormed across the office, scattering lesser clerks like leaves. "I got a notification. A *direct* notification from the Celestial Administrative Interface. You've been promoted. To a *deity* position."

The office went silent. Every clerk stopped what they were doing to stare at Zhang.

"Acting God of Paperclips," Zhang said, savoring each word. "It's a temporary assignment. I'm sure it'll be resolved soon."

Hu's face contorted through a series of colors that shouldn't have been possible on a human face. "You can't be a god. You're a *mortal*. You're *dead*. You're supposed to be filing reports, not rewriting reality!"

"And yet." Zhang held up the paperclip. It glowed with divine energy, casting golden light across the office. "Here we are."

Hu's eyes locked onto the paperclip. Something flickered in his gaze—fear, recognition, hunger.

"Where did you get that?"

"A friend."

"There's no such thing as friends in the Heavenly Bureaucracy." Hu's voice dropped to a whisper. "There are only allies and enemies. And you've just made a very powerful enemy, Zhang Wei."

"Am I supposed to be scared?"

"You're supposed to be *dead*." Hu reached into his robes and pulled out a document. It was old, yellowed, covered in seals and stamps. "This is your original death certificate. Signed, sealed, and delivered by the Celestial Management System itself. It says you died at 3:47 PM on a Tuesday, crushed by a falling vending machine."

"I know."

"What you don't know is what happened to your soul afterward." Hu's smile was a terrible thing. "They didn't just misroute you. They *tagged* you. Every soul that goes through an unprocessed death gets a special mark—a karmic tracking device that lets the system monitor your every move."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "You've been tracking me this whole time?"

"Not me. The system." Hu held up the death certificate. "But I've got access to the monitoring logs. And I know exactly what you've been doing. The bypass protocols. The administrative interface. The unauthorized access to classified records."

"Are you going to report me?"

"I'm going to offer you a deal." Hu's voice softened, became almost friendly. "You give me the paperclip. You resign your divine position. You go back to being a junior clerk. And I forget everything I've seen."

"Why would you do that?"

"Because I want to live." Hu's facade cracked, and for just a moment, Zhang saw genuine fear in his eyes. "The people who killed the System Administrator are still out there. And if they find out you've got the paperclip, they'll come for all of us."

Zhang looked down at the paperclip. It pulsed with warm, golden light, humming with the power of a hundred thousand bound documents.

He had a choice. Give up the power, go back to being nobody, live out eternity as a filing clerk. Or keep it, face the consequences, and maybe—just maybe—find out who really ran the Heavenly Bureaucracy.

Zhang Wei had never been good at playing it safe.

"No deal."

Hu's face went pale. "You're making a mistake."

"Probably." Zhang pocketed the paperclip. "But I've made worse ones. At least this one comes with divine powers."

The office erupted. Clerks screamed. Papers flew everywhere. Supervisor Hu shouted something about celestial law and eternal damnation.

And Zhang Wei, Acting God of Paperclips, walked out of the Department of Mortal Affairs for the last time.

He had a dead Administrator to find, a conspiracy to uncover, and an entire bureaucracy to hack.

The paperclip in his pocket pulsed with eager anticipation.

*Time to clip some wings.*

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Firewall That Screamed

Zhang barely had time to close the file before the void around him shattered.

The sphere of gears and runes flickered, and for one terrifying second, Zhang saw something staring back at him from inside it—a face. Not human. Too many eyes, too many teeth, all of them grinding together like the gears it was made of.

[EMERGENCY PROTOCOL ACTIVATED]
[Unauthorized Access Detected - Core Division 7]
[Deploying: Celestial Audit Team - Class 3]

"Shit."

Zhang slammed his palm against the sphere, pulling up the first escape function he could find. [Return to Workspace]. The world twisted, numbers and equations screaming past him like a hurricane of papercuts, and then he was back in his cubicle, gasping.

The jade token was hot in his hand. Too hot. It burned.

[WARNING: Residual energy detected]
[Your workstation has been flagged for inspection]
[Estimated arrival of Audit Team: 3 minutes]

Three minutes.

Zhang looked around his cubicle. Stacks of destiny reports. A half-empty cup of celestial tea that had gone cold three dynasties ago. The paper crane he'd been folding during Supervisor Hu's morning lecture about "proper karmic accounting procedures."

None of it mattered. What mattered was the token, and what the token had shown him.

He had the proof. Supervisor Hu had killed him. Not intentionally, maybe, but the man had processed Zhang's death, caught the error, and buried it. Left Zhang to die while he went back to his paperwork.

And now the system was coming to delete the evidence.

*Think, Zhang. Think.*

He couldn't run. Where would he go? The Celestial Bureaucracy existed everywhere—past, present, future, and all the boring timelines in between. There was no hiding.

He couldn't fight. He was a Tier 2 clerk with a provisional upgrade. The Audit Team would be at least Tier 4. Immortals who had spent centuries perfecting the art of paperwork enforcement.

But he could misdirect.

Zhang pulled open his desk drawer. Inside, nestled between a box of paperclips and a stack of blank destiny forms, was something he'd found on his first day: an old, forgotten stamp. The characters on it read: [APPROVED - DIVISION 9 - TEMPORARY].

Division 9. The Department of Minor Cosmic Adjustments. A dumping ground for bureaucrats who had messed up one too many times. They were so irrelevant that even the system forgot to audit them.

If Zhang could make it look like he'd transferred his access to Division 9, the Audit Team would chase a ghost while he buried the real evidence somewhere they'd never look.

He grabbed the stamp and pressed it onto a blank form. Then another. Then another. Each form he stamped with [APPROVED - DIVISION 9 - TEMPORARY] and scattered across his desk like he'd been processing transfers all morning.

The token was still burning. Two minutes left.

Zhang pulled up the Core Administrative Interface again—quickly, before the system could lock him out. He found the log of his access to the restricted records and, with a few keystrokes, redirected the source to one of the stamped forms.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Access log modified]
[Source: Form 9-B-7845 - Division 9]
[User: [REDACTED]]
[Time of Access: [REDACTED]]

It wasn't perfect. The system would figure it out eventually. But "eventually" was all Zhang needed.

The air in his cubicle shifted. A pressure built behind his eyes, like someone was pushing on his skull from the inside. The Audit Team was getting close.

One minute.

Zhang grabbed the real evidence—the file showing Supervisor Hu's error—and shoved it into the drawer. Then he pulled out the paper crane he'd been folding and whispered a single character onto its wing: *Hidden.*

The crane glowed faintly, then settled into stillness. It looked like a normal paper crane now. Just a piece of origami sitting on a desk full of bureaucracy.

The lights flickered.

Zhang sat down, picked up a random destiny report, and started reading. His hands were shaking, but his face was calm. He'd learned that expression during his mortal life—the face of a man who had nothing to hide, who was just doing his job, who absolutely had not just committed celestial fraud.

The air in front of his cubicle *ripped* open.

Three figures stepped through. They wore identical gray robes, their faces hidden behind masks made of polished jade. Each one carried a scroll that unrolled endlessly, the characters on them shifting and changing as they moved.

[Celestial Audit Team - Class 3]
[Designation: Verifiers of Truth]
[Authority: Absolute]

The lead Verifier turned its masked face toward Zhang. "Clerk 7845-B. You have been flagged for unauthorized system access."

Zhang looked up, blinking owlishly. "I'm sorry? Unauthorized access?" He gestured at his desk, piled with forms. "I've been processing Division 9 transfers all morning. Supervisor Hu's orders."

"Supervisor Hu has not filed any transfer requests for your division."

"Then Supervisor Hu needs to update his paperwork." Zhang shrugged. "Look, I don't know what you're here for, but I'm just a clerk. I stamp things. I file things. I don't even know how to access the Core Administrative Interface."

The Verifier tilted its head. The gesture was mechanical, unnatural. "Your soul signature was detected in the restricted records."

"Soul signature?" Zhang laughed. "That's absurd. I've been sitting right here. Ask my paper crane."

He pointed at the crane on his desk. The Verifiers turned to look at it. The crane sat there, perfectly still, perfectly innocent.

The lead Verifier's scroll stopped unrolling. It studied the crane for a long moment, then turned back to Zhang.

"The Division 9 transfer forms on your desk. Show us the stamps."

Zhang picked up one of the stamped forms and handed it over. The Verifier examined it, running a finger over the characters. [APPROVED - DIVISION 9 - TEMPORARY]. The stamp was genuine. It had to be—Zhang had stolen it from Division 9's old supply closet.

"This stamp is authentic," the Verifier said. "But it has not been used in seventeen mortal years."

"I found it in a drawer," Zhang said. "I figured if it still worked, why not use it? The system doesn't care about old stamps."

The Verifiers exchanged glances—or at least, they turned their masked faces toward each other. Their scrolls rustled.

"The access log shows the unauthorized access originated from a Division 9 terminal," the lead Verifier said. "Not from this workspace."

Zhang kept his face neutral. *Yes. Yes it does.*

"We will investigate Division 9," the Verifier continued. "If we find evidence of your involvement, we will return."

"Investigate all you want," Zhang said. "I'll be here. Stamping things."

The Verifier stared at him for a moment longer. Then it nodded, and the three of them stepped back through the tear in reality. The air sealed itself behind them, leaving Zhang alone in his cubicle.

He let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding.

"That was close," he muttered.

The paper crane on his desk unfolded itself, revealing a single character written on its wing: *Nice.*

Zhang stared at it. "Did you just...?"

The crane refolded itself and went back to being an innocent piece of origami.

*Right. Of course. The paper crane is alive. Why wouldn't it be?*

Zhang reached for the drawer to retrieve the evidence file, but before his hand touched the handle, a new notification appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Congratulations]
[You have successfully evaded a Class 3 Celestial Audit]
[Achievement Unlocked: "The Paper Trail Never Lies"]
[Reward: Tier 2.5 Access - Department of Minor Cosmic Adjustments]
[New Quest Available: "The Crane's Secret"]

[Quest Overview: The paper crane on your desk is not a normal piece of origami. It is a fragment of a greater consciousness—one that has been watching you since your arrival. It wants to speak with you. Do you trust it?]

[Accept?] [Decline?]

Zhang looked at the crane. It sat there, perfectly still, perfectly innocent.

But he knew better now.

Nothing in the Celestial Bureaucracy was innocent.

He reached out and touched the crane's wing.

The world went white.

---

Zhang opened his eyes to find himself standing in a garden.

It was beautiful—cherry blossoms falling through golden light, a stream of liquid jade winding through moss-covered stones, mountains in the distance that seemed to breathe. But something was wrong. The cherry blossoms fell upward. The jade stream flowed backward. The mountains breathed in reverse.

*This isn't a real place,* Zhang realized. *It's a memory.*

"Very good," a voice said.

Zhang turned. Standing behind him was a woman in robes so white they seemed to absorb light. Her face was beautiful in the way that knives were beautiful—sharp, precise, and dangerous. She held a paper crane in her hands, identical to the one on his desk.

"You're the crane," Zhang said.

"I am a fragment," she corrected. "The crane is my eye. My ear. My mouth." She smiled, and it didn't reach her eyes. "I have been watching you, Zhang Wei. Since the moment you died."

"Why?"

"Because you are the first person in three thousand years to notice the bug in the system."

Zhang's blood went cold. "The bug?"

"The administrative error that killed you. The one Supervisor Hu buried." She stepped closer, and the garden seemed to darken around her. "It wasn't an accident, Zhang Wei. Supervisor Hu didn't make a mistake. He was following orders."

"Whose orders?"

The woman held up her paper crane. It unfolded in her hands, revealing a single name written in characters that burned like fire.

*The Jade Emperor.*

Zhang stared at the name. The ruler of the Celestial Bureaucracy. The highest authority in heaven. The immortal who had supposedly created the system that governed all mortal and immortal life.

"He killed me," Zhang whispered. "The Jade Emperor killed me."

"Not you specifically," the woman said. "You were collateral damage. A rounding error in a much larger operation." She folded the crane back into shape. "The Jade Emperor has been systematically deleting souls for three thousand years. Souls that don't fit his design. Souls that might threaten his control."

"Souls like mine?"

"Souls like yours." She held out the crane. "The question is, Zhang Wei: What are you going to do about it?"

Zhang took the crane. It pulsed with a warmth that felt almost alive.

"I'm going to find out why," he said. "And then I'm going to make him pay."

The woman smiled again, and this time, it reached her eyes.

"Good. I was hoping you'd say that."

The garden shattered.

Zhang was back in his cubicle, the paper crane in his hand. But it felt different now. Heavier. Like it was carrying a weight that went beyond paper and ink.

A new notification appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Warning]
[You have been flagged for high-interest monitoring]
[The Jade Emperor's personal audit team has been dispatched]
[Estimated arrival: 30 seconds]

Thirty seconds.

Zhang looked at the crane. Then at the drawer containing the evidence. Then at the stack of blank destiny forms on his desk.

He had thirty seconds to decide his next move.

And then the air in

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Promotion Trap Door

Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the white void around him shattered like glass.

He fell.

Not through space, but through layers of reality—each one peeling away like the skin of an onion made of screaming souls and spreadsheet formulas. His desk materialized beneath him with a solid *thump* that knocked the wind from his lungs. The jade token was still warm in his hand, pulsing with a light that definitely hadn't been there before.

"HEY! NEW GUY!"

Zhang looked up. A woman stood in front of his cubicle, her arms crossed. She had the same pale blue aura as every other celestial clerk he'd met, but there was something different about her eyes—they flickered with code, numbers and symbols dancing across her irises like captive fireflies.

"I'm Li Mei," she said. "Department of Records. You're Zhang Wei, right? The one who caused three system alerts in his first week?"

"That depends," Zhang said carefully. "Are you here to congratulate me or delete me?"

Li Mei's lips twitched. "Neither. I'm here to offer you a promotion."

She tossed a scroll onto his desk. It unrolled itself, revealing text that rearranged itself as he read:

[PROMOTION ORDER: Tier 2 → Tier 3]
[Position: Senior Records Analyst]
[Department: Records and Reconciliation]
[Benefits: Increased karma allocation, access to Tier 3 archives, priority reincarnation queue]
[Condition: Immediate transfer, no questions asked]

"No questions asked," Zhang repeated. "That's suspicious."

"Everything is suspicious here." Li Mei pulled up a chair and sat down, crossing her legs. "But here's the thing, Zhang Wei. You've been flagged. Not just monitored—*flagged*. Supervisor Hu put a priority mark on your soul file. Do you know what that means?"

"I'm guessing it's not a bonus."

"It means the next time you sneeze wrong, you get reassigned to the Hell of Eternal Filing. No trial, no appeal. Hu's been here six hundred years. He has friends in places you don't want to know about." She leaned forward. "But I have friends too. And they're in places Hu doesn't want to know about."

Zhang studied her. The code in her eyes flickered faster as she spoke, almost like it was reacting to something. He'd learned to read people in his mortal life—when you worked in customer service, you had to. Li Mei was nervous. Excited. And hiding something.

"Why me?" he asked.

"Because you found the bug."

"I found a misrouted file. That's not a bug."

Li Mei laughed. It was a sharp, bitter sound. "You really think that was just a file error? Zhang Wei, the Celestial Management System was built by the first Jade Emperor seven thousand years ago. It's been patched, updated, and 'optimized' by every immortal bureaucrat since then. The code is held together with prayers and spite. That 'misrouted file'? That was a *fracture*. A crack in the foundation." She lowered her voice. "And you found it in your first week. Which means either you're impossibly lucky, or—"

"Or what?"

"Or the system *wants* you to find it."

Silence hung between them. Zhang could hear the distant clatter of keyboards, the hum of celestial servers, the eternal shuffle of paperwork being processed in the infinite halls beyond his cubicle.

"So the promotion," he said. "What's the catch?"

"No catch. Just a new department with more access and fewer eyes." Li Mei stood up. "You have until the end of this shift to decide. After that, Hu's priority flag activates, and you become his personal project."

She turned and walked away, her footsteps silent on the marble floor.

Zhang stared at the scroll. Tier 3. Senior Records Analyst. The benefits were tempting—especially the priority reincarnation queue. If he could get back to Earth with his memories intact, he could fix things. Finish what he started. Maybe even find out who he was supposed to be before the system killed him.

But he'd read enough fantasy novels to know that mysterious offers from beautiful women with glitchy eyes were never what they seemed.

He picked up the scroll and tucked it into his robe. Then he accessed the jade token again.

[SYSTEM ACCESS: GRANTED]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Access Level: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)]
[Query: Search for "Records and Reconciliation Department" Authorized Personnel]

The system returned a list.

[Name: Li Mei]
[Title: Senior Records Analyst]
[Department: Records and Reconciliation]
[Status: ACTIVE]
[Years of Service: 312]
[Notes: Transferred from Department of Mortal Affairs 287 years ago. No disciplinary actions. No commendations. No performance reviews. No records of existence prior to transfer.]

No records of existence prior to transfer.

Zhang's blood went cold. He ran another search.

[Query: Cross-reference Li Mei with Department of Mortal Affairs employee database]

[ERROR: No matching records found]

[Query: Search for "Records and Reconciliation Department" entire personnel history]

[ERROR: Department classified. Access denied.]

[Query: Who authorized the creation of "Records and Reconciliation Department"?]

[ERROR: Data corrupted. Please contact System Administrator.]

The system administrator.

Who, presumably, was the Jade Emperor.

Who, presumably, wasn't taking calls from a Tier 2 clerk.

Zhang closed the token and sat back in his chair. His mind raced through possibilities, each one more disturbing than the last. Li Mei was a ghost. A department that didn't officially exist, run by someone who hadn't existed before she joined it. And they wanted *him*.

His intercom buzzed.

"Zhang Wei!" Supervisor Hu's voice crackled through the speaker. "My office. Now."

Zhang stood up, his legs moving before his brain caught up. He walked past rows of cubicles, past clerks who wouldn't meet his eyes, past the water cooler where two imps were arguing about the proper temperature of infernal tea.

Hu's office was at the end of the hall. The door was made of solid jade, carved with scenes of celestial judgment—souls being weighed, lives being measured, fates being sealed. Zhang knocked.

"Enter."

The office was smaller than he expected. A desk, a chair, a single window that showed an endless expanse of clouds. Hu sat behind the desk, his fingers steepled, his expression unreadable.

"Sit."

Zhang sat.

"I know about the promotion offer," Hu said. "Before you ask—no, I don't know who Li Mei really is. No one does. But I know what she wants."

"Enlighten me."

"She wants to tear down the system." Hu's voice was flat, matter-of-fact. "She and her little cabal of 'Records Analysts' have been working for three centuries to find a weakness in the Celestial Management System. A way to rewrite the fundamental code of reality. And she thinks you're the key."

"Why me?"

"Because you're mortal. Or you were, anyway. The system wasn't designed to process someone who died by administrative error. You're a glitch, Zhang Wei. An anomaly. And anomalies have a way of breaking things that were never meant to break." Hu leaned forward. "If you join her, you'll destroy the celestial bureaucracy. Every law, every rule, every destiny—all of it gone. Chaos."

"And if I don't join her?"

"Then you stay here. Under my supervision. Filing reports, processing karma, living out your afterlife in quiet obscurity." Hu's eyes narrowed. "It's not exciting, but it's safe. And you'll get your reincarnation eventually. Maybe in a few centuries."

Zhang thought about it. A few centuries of paperwork. Of being yelled at by gods. Of watching other souls move on while he sat in a cubicle, stamping forms until his fingers fell off.

Or he could take the offer. Join Li Mei. Tear down the system and rebuild it from scratch. Become something more than a clerk.

"Can I think about it?"

"You have until the end of your shift."

Zhang stood up. As he reached for the door, Hu spoke again.

"One more thing. The Hell of Unending Data Entry? It's not a punishment. It's a *solution*. The system sends anomalies there to be processed—broken down into raw spiritual data and fed back into the bureaucracy. It's efficient. Clean. And completely irreversible."

Zhang's hand froze on the door handle.

"If Li Mei's plan fails," Hu continued, "that's where you'll end up. Not as a punishment. As a *correction*."

He turned back to face Hu. The supervisor's face was still unreadable, but his eyes—there was something there. Fear? Guilt? Regret?

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I was an anomaly too. Three hundred years ago." Hu's voice dropped to a whisper. "And I chose safety. I chose the cubicle. I chose to become the person who hunts down people like me." He looked away. "I'm telling you this so you can make a different choice."

Zhang stood in the doorway, the scroll burning a hole in his robe, Hu's confession echoing in his ears.

The system beeped.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Shift ends in 10 minutes]
[Reminder: Promotion offer expires at shift change]
[Department of Records and Reconciliation will be locked for maintenance until further notice after shift change]

Locked for maintenance.

Li Mei was closing the door.

Zhang had ten minutes to decide: safety and obscurity, or chaos and freedom.

He looked at Hu. Looked at the jade token in his hand. Looked at the scroll that promised a way out.

Then he made his choice.

He walked out of Hu's office, down the hall, past the cubicles, past the arguing imps, past the water cooler. He didn't stop until he reached Li Mei's desk.

"I'm in," he said.

Li Mei smiled. The code in her eyes flared bright, and for just a moment, Zhang saw what she really was—not a woman, not a ghost, but a *program*. A living piece of code, given form and purpose.

"Good," she said. "Because we're out of time."

The lights flickered.

[SYSTEM ALERT: Unauthorized access detected]
[Location: Records and Reconciliation Department]
[Action: Emergency lockdown initiated]
[All personnel: Evacuate immediately]

The floor trembled. Alarms blared. Clerks screamed and ran.

Li Mei grabbed Zhang's arm. "Don't let go. No matter what."

The world dissolved into light.

---

Zhang opened his eyes to find himself standing in a vast, empty space. No walls. No floor. No ceiling. Just infinite white, stretching in every direction.

"Where are we?"

"Core," Li Mei said. "The heart of the system. The source code."

She gestured, and the white around them transformed. Lines of glowing text appeared, millions upon millions of them, stretching into infinity. Commands. Functions. Variables. The entire celestial bureaucracy, laid bare.

"This is it," Zhang breathed.

"This is it." Li Mei turned to face him. "And you're going to rewrite it."

"Me?"

"You're the anomaly. The glitch. The only one who can touch the code without being consumed." She held out her hand. "So touch it, Zhang Wei. Change it. Make it better."

Zhang reached out.

His fingers brushed the first line of code.

And the universe screamed.

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The Debugging of Destiny

The sphere pulsed once, twice, three times before Zhang's connection to the Core Administrative Interface severed with a jolt that sent him stumbling backward. He crashed into something solid—a desk, he realized, as he blinked the white void from his vision and found himself back in the cramped cubicle. The jade token in his hand had gone dark, its surface now cracked and smoking.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Terminated]  
[Reason: Unauthorized Access Detected]  
[Consequences: Administrative Privileges Suspended]  
[Status: Under Investigation]

"Fantastic," Zhang muttered, dropping the token like it had burned him. It clattered onto his desk, sending a stack of misfiled destiny reports sliding to the floor. "First day on the job, and I'm already flagged for investigation. That's got to be some kind of record."

A shadow fell over his cubicle.

"Zhang Wei."

He looked up to find Supervisor Hu standing there, arms folded, face an immovable mask of bureaucratic displeasure. Behind him, two figures in pristine white robes stood at attention—Celestial Inspectors, if the golden badges on their chests were any indication. Their eyes glowed with the soft luminescence of beings who had spent centuries staring at paperwork.

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said, forcing his voice into a tone of respectful confusion. "Is something wrong?"

Hu's nostrils flared. "Something wrong? I just received an alert from the Core Administrative Interface. Someone in my department accessed restricted records without authorization. The system traced the breach to your workstation."

"Must be a mistake," Zhang said, spreading his hands. "I've been here all day, filing reports. Ask anyone."

"Everyone in this department has been here all day filing reports," Hu replied, his voice dripping with condescension. "That doesn't mean they weren't accessing forbidden files between forms." He gestured to the inspectors. "Check his workstation. Thoroughly."

The inspectors stepped forward, their movements synchronized, and began circling Zhang's cubicle like vultures. One of them picked up the cracked jade token, examining it with clinical precision.

"This token shows signs of recent activation," the inspector said, his voice flat and mechanical. "And damage consistent with forced termination of a Core connection."

Hu's eyes widened. For just a moment, the mask slipped, and Zhang saw fear flicker across his face. Fear, and something else. Guilt.

"Is that so?" Hu said, recovering his composure. "Well then. Zhang Wei, you are hereby placed under administrative arrest pending a full investigation. Your soul will be confined to the Department of Internal Affairs until such time as—"

"Wait." Zhang held up a hand. "Before you drag me off to some celestial holding cell, can I at least see the evidence? The specific records I supposedly accessed?"

Hu's jaw tightened. "That's classified."

"Classified? I'm the accused. Don't I have the right to know what I'm being accused of?" Zhang turned to the inspectors. "Is there a procedure for this? Some kind of due process?"

The inspectors exchanged glances. One of them pulled out a scroll, unfurling it with a snap. "Section 12, Subsection 4 of the Celestial Employee Handbook states that any employee placed under administrative arrest has the right to review the evidence against them, provided it does not compromise ongoing investigations."

"See?" Zhang said, smiling at Hu. "Section 12. Right there."

Hu's face reddened. "The evidence is—" He stopped, clearly realizing he was trapped. If he refused, he'd be violating procedure in front of two inspectors. If he showed the evidence, Zhang would see exactly what records he'd accessed. And that would raise questions about why those records existed in the first place.

"Very well," Hu said through gritted teeth. "Show him the log."

The inspector with the scroll produced a second document, this one shimmering with golden text. He held it up for Zhang to read.

[ACCESS LOG: Soul ID 7845-B]  
[Time: Cycle 47, Breath 3]  
[File Accessed: Reincarnation Queue - Batch 374]  
[Action: View Record - Soul ID 7845-B]  
[Status: Flagged for Review]

Zhang read the log, then looked up at Hu with an expression of innocent confusion. "Batch 374? That's the batch my soul was processed in. And I accessed my own record?" He turned to the inspectors. "Is that a violation? Accessing your own file?"

The inspectors consulted their scroll again. "Section 8, Subsection 2: Employees have the right to review their own personnel files, including their death and reincarnation records, so long as they do not modify or delete said records."

"So I didn't break any rules," Zhang said. "I just looked at my own file. That's allowed."

Hu's face went through several shades of purple before settling on an unhealthy maroon. "The token was damaged. You clearly attempted to—"

"The token was damaged because the system kicked me out while I was reading," Zhang interrupted. "Maybe if the Core Administrative Interface wasn't so glitchy, we wouldn't have these problems." He turned back to the inspectors. "I'd like to file a formal complaint about the instability of the Core system. It's affecting my ability to do my job."

The inspectors looked at each other, then at Hu, then back at Zhang. For the first time, the mechanical inspector seemed to show a hint of something approaching amusement.

"Noted," he said, making a mark on his scroll. "We will include your complaint in our report."

Hu spluttered. "You can't be serious. He clearly—"

"The evidence does not support administrative arrest," the inspector said, cutting him off. "Soul ID 7845-B accessed his own file, which is permitted under Section 8. The token damage is consistent with a system error, not malicious intent. We recommend a warning and additional training, nothing more."

Hu looked like he wanted to argue, but he knew better than to push back against Celestial Inspectors. With a final glare at Zhang, he turned and stalked away, his robes billowing behind him.

The inspectors lingered for a moment. The one who had been examining Zhang's token leaned in close, his voice dropping to a whisper. "Be careful, Clerk Zhang. The system logs everything. And Supervisor Hu has friends in high places."

Before Zhang could respond, they were gone, vanishing in a swirl of golden light.

Zhang let out a long breath and slumped into his chair. That had been close. Too close. If Hu had managed to get him arrested, he'd be stuck in Internal Affairs for who knows how long, probably until his soul was erased or reassigned to some hell dimension.

He picked up the cracked jade token, turning it over in his hands. It was useless now, the connection severed permanently. But he didn't need it anymore. He had seen the Core. He had touched the code of reality itself. And he had learned something crucial: the system was flawed.

Not just glitchy. Flawed.

During those brief seconds of connection, Zhang had seen more than just his own death record. He had seen the underlying structure of the Celestial Management System—the way it processed destinies, calculated karmic balances, assigned souls to their fates. And he had seen the bugs.

There were thousands of them. Millions. Tiny errors in the code that accumulated over centuries, creating inefficiencies and contradictions that had to be manually corrected by clerks who didn't even know they were patching broken systems. The immortals hadn't built a perfect bureaucracy. They had built a monument to technical debt.

And Supervisor Hu? He wasn't just incompetent. He was exploiting those bugs.

Zhang pulled up the files on his desk, spreading them out in front of him. They were destiny reports for souls in his division—births, deaths, marriages, all the mundane events that made up mortal existence. But as he scanned them, he noticed something odd.

Every single report had been altered.

Not by much. Just small adjustments here and there—a birth date moved by a few hours, a death delayed by a day, a marriage pushed forward or backward by a week. Individually, the changes were insignificant. But together, they added up to something else.

Karmic debt.

Every time a destiny was altered, the soul involved accrued a small amount of karmic debt. It was like a transaction fee, a tiny penalty for the inconvenience caused to the universe. Most souls never noticed it. But when multiplied across thousands of lives, the debt accumulated into a substantial sum.

And that sum had to go somewhere.

Zhang followed the paper trail, tracing the altered destinies through the labyrinthine filing system. It took him hours, his fingers flying across scrolls and ledgers, his eyes scanning columns of numbers and dates. But eventually, he found it.

A single account. Hidden in a sub-department that didn't officially exist, under the name of a clerk who had been dead for three hundred years.

[KARMIC LEDGER: Account 0000-VOID]  
[Balance: 47,892,341,006 Credits]  
[Status: Dormant]  
[Last Transaction: Cycle 47, Breath 1]  
[Transaction Type: Withdrawal]  
[Amount: 500,000 Credits]  
[Destination: [REDACTED]]

Zhang stared at the number. 47 billion karmic credits. That was enough to buy a soul a dozen reincarnations into perfect lives—wealth, health, happiness, the works. Or it could be used to ascend. To skip the reincarnation cycle entirely and become an immortal.

Someone had been skimming karmic debt for centuries. And the last withdrawal had been made just three days ago.

Three days ago. When Zhang's death was processed.

The pieces clicked into place. His death wasn't just a random error. It was a deliberate act, a soul sacrificed to cover someone's tracks. When the system flagged the discrepancy in Batch 374, someone had redirected the error to Zhang, making it look like his soul was the one that was misrouted. The actual problem—the karmic theft—was buried under layers of falsified paperwork.

And the only person with enough access to pull that off was Supervisor Hu.

Zhang leaned back in his chair, a cold anger settling in his chest. Hu had killed him. Not out of malice, but out of convenience. Zhang was just a random soul, plucked from the reincarnation queue and used as a scapegoat.

But Zhang was still here. Still thinking, still planning, still very much aware of the injustice that had been done to him.

He looked at the cracked jade token on his desk. It was useless for accessing the Core, but it still had one function left: it was a record of his identity. And with the right modifications, he could use it to access something else.

The Internal Audit System.

The Celestial Bureaucracy had a department dedicated to rooting out corruption and fraud. But that department was notoriously slow, bogged down by its own bureaucracy. By the time they investigated Hu, Zhang would probably be long dead—or worse, reassigned to a data entry hell.

No, he needed to bypass the system. To file a complaint directly to the Internal Audit Core, skipping the layers of review that would normally slow things down. It was risky. If he got caught, he'd be erased for sure. But if he succeeded, he'd have Hu right where he wanted him.

Zhang pulled up a blank scroll and began writing. He didn't use the standard forms. He used the code he had glimpsed in the Core, the raw language of the system itself. It was like writing in binary, each character carrying multiple layers of meaning.

[SYS_CALL: INTER

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The Empty Throne of Heaven

Zhang ripped his hand away from the sphere as if burned. The white void flickered, and for a moment he saw something behind it—a vast chamber filled with desks, each one occupied by a figure in celestial robes. They were all staring at him.

Then the vision vanished, and he was back in the administrative core, alone.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized access detected. Countermeasures initiated.]

[Time until soul lockdown: 00:04:59]

Four minutes. Zhang's mind raced. He'd triggered an alarm, and now the system was coming for him. He needed to get out, needed to—

Wait.

He looked at the sphere again. The information was still there, still accessible. The records of his death. The proof that Supervisor Hu had covered up the error. But there was something else too, something he hadn't noticed before.

A name.

Not Supervisor Hu's name. Not the clerk who had misrouted his soul. No, this was deeper, buried in the administrative logs like a splinter in the system's code.

[Original Author of Batch 374 Protocol: Celestial Administrator - Name Redacted]

[Note: Protocol authorized by direct decree of the Jade Emperor]

The Jade Emperor.

Zhang stared at the words. The Jade Emperor was supposed to be the supreme ruler of Heaven, the ultimate authority in the celestial bureaucracy. But according to everything Zhang had learned in his brief time here, the Jade Emperor hadn't been seen in centuries. The bureaucracy ran on autopilot, guided by ancient protocols and maintained by career immortals who had long since forgotten why the rules existed.

But if the Jade Emperor had personally authorized the protocol that killed him...

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: 00:03:12 remaining]

[Warning: Soul lockdown will result in memory wipe and reassignment to basic celestial maintenance]

Zhang didn't have time to investigate further. He reached for the interface, desperately trying to find an exit. His fingers flew across the glowing runes, searching for a way out.

[Accessing: Emergency Exit Protocol]

[Confirm: Soul Anchor Relocation]

[Destination: Department of Mortal Affairs - Clerk Workstation 7845-B]

[Confirm? Y/N]

He slammed his hand on the confirmation rune.

The white void shattered. Zhang felt himself falling again, spinning through streams of data and light. He crashed back into his body like a diver breaking the surface of water, gasping as he slammed forward onto his desk.

"Late again, Clerk Zhang?"

Zhang looked up. Supervisor Hu stood in the doorway of the office, his face twisted into its usual expression of disdain. But there was something different in his eyes now. Something like fear.

"I wasn't late," Zhang said, his voice hoarse. "I was working."

"Working?" Hu laughed, but it sounded forced. "I checked the system. Your workstation was inactive for exactly four minutes and thirty-two seconds. That's a violation of productivity protocols."

Four minutes and thirty-two seconds. Zhang had been in the administrative core for less than five minutes. But it had felt like hours.

"I had a system error," Zhang said. "I was waiting for it to reset."

"A system error." Hu's eyes narrowed. "Interesting. I've received reports of unusual system activity today. Unauthorized access attempts. Data breaches." He stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "You wouldn't know anything about that, would you, Clerk Zhang?"

Zhang met his gaze. "I've been at my desk all morning. You can check the logs."

"Oh, I will." Hu straightened his robes. "In fact, I've already initiated a full audit of the department's system access records. If I find any irregularities..." He let the threat hang in the air.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Department-wide audit initiated]

[Source: Supervisor Hu - ID 2319]

[Scope: All system access for past 72 hours]

[Estimated completion: 2 hours]

Two hours. That's how long Zhang had before Hu found evidence of his unauthorized access. Unless...

Zhang's hand moved to his jade token. The administrative interface was still connected. He could feel it pulsing against his fingers, waiting for his command.

[Accessing: Karmic Ledger Viewing - Supervisor Hu]

[Records Found: 1,247 entries]

[Filter by: Unresolved Debts]

[Results: 342 entries]

Zhang scanned the list. Supervisor Hu had been in the celestial bureaucracy for over three thousand years. In that time, he had accumulated quite a few debts. Falsified reports. Misappropriated resources. Unauthorized promotions of relatives.

[Debt ID: 8923 - Misuse of Administrative Authority]

[Details: Supervisor Hu accepted bribes from Soul ID 4521 in exchange for favorable reincarnation assignment]

[Status: Unresolved - Flagged for Review]

[Penalty: Demotion (Tier 3), 500 years of low-level maintenance duty]

Zhang smiled. He had leverage.

But before he could use it, the office door burst open. Another clerk stumbled in, his face pale with panic.

"Supervisor! There's been an incident at the Celestial Records Hall!"

Hu spun around. "What kind of incident?"

"The... the records." The clerk's voice trembled. "Someone has been tampering with the Destiny Archive. Thousands of mortal lifelines have been altered."

Zhang's blood went cold. He hadn't touched the Destiny Archive. He'd only accessed his own file.

"Who?" Hu demanded. "Who did this?"

"We don't know. The system says the alterations were made from within the department." The clerk's eyes darted around the room, eventually landing on Zhang. "From this office."

Every head in the room turned to face him. Zhang felt the weight of their stares like a physical force.

"That's impossible," Zhang said, keeping his voice steady. "I've been at my desk all morning."

"The system says otherwise," Hu said, his voice dripping with satisfaction. "It seems we've found our saboteur."

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Soul ID 7845-B has been flagged for investigation]

[Accusation: Tampering with the Destiny Archive]

[Current Status: Detained pending review]

[Recommended Action: Submit to custody for questioning]

Zhang's hand tightened around his jade token. He could fight. He could run. He could try to hack the system again and disappear into the administrative void. But if he did, he would be admitting guilt.

Instead, he stood up slowly, raising his hands in surrender.

"I'll cooperate fully," Zhang said. "But I want it noted that I'm doing so under protest."

"Noted," Hu said, gesturing to two burly celestial guards who had appeared in the doorway. "Take him to the Interrogation Chamber."

The guards grabbed Zhang's arms. They were stronger than they looked—their grips felt like iron bands. Zhang didn't resist as they marched him out of the office, through the winding corridors of the celestial bureaucracy.

They passed countless other clerks, all of whom averted their eyes. No one wanted to be associated with a suspected saboteur.

The Interrogation Chamber was a small room with no windows. A single table sat in the center, two chairs on opposite sides. The walls were covered in runes that Zhang recognized as soul-binding arrays. If he tried to escape, they would lock his spirit in place.

The guards pushed him into one of the chairs and left, closing the door behind them.

Zhang sat alone in the silence for exactly thirty-seven seconds before the door opened again.

But it wasn't Hu who entered.

It was a woman.

She was tall, with silver hair that flowed down her back like liquid starlight. Her robes were embroidered with constellations, and her eyes held the depth of eternity. She moved with the grace of someone who had never known hurry.

"Clerk Zhang Wei," she said, her voice like wind chimes. "I am Celestial Administrator Xian."

Zhang's breath caught. An administrator. One of the highest-ranking officials in the entire bureaucracy.

"I'm honored," he said carefully. "But I'm afraid there's been a mistake. I haven't—"

"I know you haven't tampered with the Destiny Archive," Xian interrupted. "I know because I did."

Zhang stared at her. "You... what?"

"I needed someone to take the fall." She sat down across from him, folding her hands on the table. "Someone expendable. Someone no one would miss."

"You framed me."

"Consider it an opportunity." Xian smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I've been watching you, Clerk Zhang. Your unauthorized access to the administrative core. Your discovery of the error in your death records. Your clever little plan to hack the system."

Zhang's heart pounded. "How do you know about that?"

"Because I'm the one who let you find it." Xian leaned forward. "The administrative error that killed you? It wasn't an accident. It was a message."

"A message from who?"

"The Jade Emperor." Xian's voice dropped to a whisper. "He's not missing, Clerk Zhang. He's been removed. Someone—or something—has taken control of the celestial bureaucracy. And they're using the system to eliminate anyone who might discover the truth."

"You're saying the Jade Emperor was assassinated?"

"I'm saying the throne is empty, and no one knows who's sitting in it." Xian's eyes met his. "The alterations to the Destiny Archive weren't random. They were targeted. Someone is rewriting the lives of everyone who might remember what happened."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you're the only one who can see the code." Xian reached into her robe and pulled out a small jade slip. "You're not a celestial. You're not bound by the same rules. You can access parts of the system that we immortals can't even perceive."

She slid the jade slip across the table.

"This is a key to the administrative core's deepest level. The Celestial Throne Room. Find out who's sitting in the Jade Emperor's seat, and you'll find the truth."

Zhang looked at the jade slip. It pulsed with a faint light, beckoning him.

"Why me?" he asked. "Why not do it yourself?"

"Because if I try, I'll be erased." Xian stood up. "But you? You're already dead. What more can they take from you?"

She walked to the door, then paused.

"The guards will return in thirty seconds. You have a choice, Clerk Zhang. Take the key and find the truth. Or stay here and accept your fate as a scapegoat."

She left.

Zhang stared at the jade slip. Twenty seconds left. Nineteen. Eighteen.

He picked it up.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Objective Unlocked]

[Quest: The Empty Throne]

[Objective: Reach the Celestial Throne Room]

[Reward: The Truth]

[Warning: This quest is not sanctioned by the Celestial Bureaucracy. Completion may result in permanent soul deletion.]

The door burst open.

But it wasn't the guards.

It was Supervisor Hu, his face contorted with rage.

"You!" he screamed. "I know you were talking to Administrator Xian! She's been flagged as a traitor! You're both going to—"

He stopped mid-sentence, his eyes going wide.

Zhang looked down. The jade slip in his hand was glowing brighter, the light spreading across his skin.

"What are you doing?" Hu whispered. "Stop! That's—"

The light consumed everything.

And Zhang found himself falling again.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Soul ID 7845-B has initiated emergency transfer]

[Destination: Unknown]

[Estimated arrival: 00:00:03]

[00:00:02]

[00:00:01]

[Arrival

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Office Romance Protocol

Zhang pulled his hand away from the glowing sphere as if it had burned him. The warning was still blinking in his vision, but more importantly, something else had caught his attention—a flicker in the corner of his interface.

[UNREAD MESSAGE: 1]

He opened it.

[FROM: System Administrator — Tier 7]
[SUBJECT: Welcome to the Core Team]
[BODY:]
Congratulations, Clerk Zhang. You've passed the initial integrity test. Most new clerks who discover the Core Administrative Interface immediately try to alter their own destiny or delete evidence of their death. You didn't. Your restraint has been noted.
[ATTACHMENT: orientation_schedule.pdf]

Zhang stared at the message. Then he laughed. "A test," he said to the empty void. "Of course it was a test."

The sphere pulsed once, then faded, and the white void dissolved around him.

He was back in his cubicle, sitting in his chair, as if nothing had happened. The jade token sat innocuously on his desk, looking like an ordinary paperweight. The stack of forms was still there. The clock on the wall still showed the same impossible time—11:74.

But something was different.

The air felt heavier. The faint hum of celestial machinery seemed louder. And every few seconds, Zhang's peripheral vision caught glimpses of code—streams of numbers and characters flowing through the walls like water through pipes.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Skill Unlocked]
[Skill: Administrative Sight (Level 1)]
[Effect: Allows user to perceive the underlying structure of celestial bureaucracy]
