[Note: Prolonged use may cause headaches, nosebleeds, or spontaneous enlightenment.]

"Great," Zhang muttered. "Now I can literally see the paperwork."

He picked up his jade token and examined it. The notification from the System Administrator had opened a new connection—a direct line to someone who could actually help him. Or someone who could delete him for knowing too much.

There was only one way to find out.

Zhang opened the reply and typed: *What's the catch?*

The response came immediately.

[FROM: System Administrator — Tier 7]
[SUBJECT: Re: Welcome to the Core Team]
[CATCH:]
You're now part of an experimental program designed to identify clerks with administrative potential. Your advancement will be accelerated. Your failures will be magnified. And if you reveal the existence of this program to anyone, your soul will be reassigned to the Department of Eternal Recalculation.
[END]

Eternal Recalculation. Zhang had heard whispers about that department. It was where they sent clerks who had to manually compute pi for all eternity. No breaks. No sleep. Just infinite decimal places.

"Charming," he said.

A new icon appeared on his interface—a small gear with a question mark in the center. [Access Experimental Features: Y/N]

Zhang selected [Y].

His token vibrated, and a holographic screen materialized above his desk. It displayed a list of options:

[ADMINISTRATIVE TOOLS - EXPERIMENTAL]
- Destiny Thread Manipulation (Requires Level 3 Administrative Sight)
- Karmic Shortcut Creation (Requires Supervisor Approval)
- Temporal Buffer Zone Access (Risky — High Probability of Paradox)
- [REDACTED - Unlock at Tier 4]
- Office Romance Protocol (Beta)

Zhang blinked. "Office Romance Protocol?"

He clicked on it.

[OFFICE ROMANCE PROTOCOL — BETA]
[Purpose: Facilitate interaction between celestial employees to improve workplace morale]
[Warning: This protocol has been known to cause emotional complications, reality distortions, and in extreme cases, the creation of new minor deities]
[Proceed?]

"No," Zhang said firmly. "Absolutely not."

But his finger hovered over the [Proceed] button.

He was curious. That was his problem. He'd always been curious—curious about the locked door in his apartment building, curious about why his boss always left work exactly at 4:47 PM, curious about the strange symbols that appeared in his dreams after he died.

Curiosity had killed him.

But it had also brought him here.

Zhang clicked [Proceed].

The screen flickered, and a new window opened. It showed a list of celestial employees in his department, each with a compatibility rating. The names scrolled by—Supervisor Hu, Senior Clerk Mei Lin, Junior Clerk Wang, Assistant Director Zhao—until it stopped on one name.

[Compatibility Match Found]
[Name: Li Mei — Tier 3 Clerk]
[Department: Mortal Affairs — Record Correction Division]
[Compatibility Rating: 97.3%]
[Note: This employee has accessed your file 47 times in the past three days]

Zhang leaned forward. Someone had been looking at his file. Repeatedly.

He pulled up Li Mei's profile.

[Employee: Li Mei]
[Status: Active]
[Years of Service: 347]
[Specialty: Memory Adjustment and Reality Synchronization]
[Current Assignment: Pending — Awaiting Review Board Decision]
[Last Action: Accessed File #7845-B (Yours) — 3 minutes ago]

Three minutes ago. While he was in the Core Administrative Interface.

Zhang's instincts screamed at him. This wasn't a coincidence. Nothing in the celestial bureaucracy was a coincidence.

He minimized the Office Romance Protocol and opened a direct message to Li Mei.

[TO: Li Mei — Clerk Tier 3]
[SUBJECT: Your interest in my file]
[BODY:]
I noticed you've been checking my records. Want to tell me why, or should I report this as suspicious activity?

He sent the message and waited.

One second. Two seconds. Three.

A reply appeared.

[FROM: Li Mei]
[SUBJECT: Re: Your interest in my file]
[BODY:]
Meet me in the Archives. Section 7-B. Come alone. Don't tell anyone.

Zhang frowned. The Archives. He'd heard about that place from Senior Clerk Mei Lin—it was where they stored records that had been "deleted" from reality. Things that never happened. People who never existed.

If Li Mei was meeting him there, she either had information that could get them both killed, or she was setting a trap.

There was only one way to find out.

He stood up from his desk and looked around the office. The other clerks were still working, their heads down, their fingers moving across their tokens. Supervisor Hu's office door was closed. The glowing figures from earlier were gone.

Zhang walked toward the elevator at the end of the hall. The doors opened as he approached—a convenience he appreciated—and he stepped inside.

"Floor?" a disembodied voice asked.

"Archives. Section 7-B."

The elevator hummed and began to descend. The floor numbers ticked downward past levels Zhang hadn't even known existed. Basement 12. Basement 23. Basement 47.

The elevator stopped.

The doors opened onto a vast, dark space. Shelves stretched in every direction, disappearing into shadows that seemed to have no end. The air was cold and dry, and it smelled faintly of ozone and old paper.

"Over here."

Zhang turned. A woman stood between two shelves, her face half-lit by the glow of her jade token. She was shorter than him, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to hold too much knowledge for someone who looked so young.

"You're Li Mei," Zhang said.

"And you're Zhang Wei. The dead man who's not supposed to be here."

"Word travels fast."

Li Mei stepped closer. "Word doesn't travel at all in this place. Information moves exactly as fast as the system allows it to move. And the system is allowing me to know about you."

"Why?"

"Because I'm supposed to help you." She held up her token, and a holographic display appeared between them. It showed a complex diagram—a network of lines and nodes that looked like a map of the celestial bureaucracy.

"This is the true structure of Heaven," Li Mei said. "Not the one they show new clerks. This is the one that actually runs things."

Zhang studied the diagram. At the center was a massive node labeled [CELESTIAL MANAGEMENT SYSTEM — CORE]. Surrounding it were smaller nodes—departments, divisions, sub-sections—all connected by lines of different colors.

"There's a problem," Li Mei continued. "Look at this."

She zoomed in on one section of the diagram. A cluster of nodes near the edge was flickering—pulsing with a red light that made Zhang's Administrative Sight itch.

[ANOMALY DETECTED]
[Source: Department of Mortal Affairs — Reincarnation Queue]
[Error Type: Reality Fracture]
[Affected Souls: 1,247]
[Status: Spreading]

"That's your department," Li Mei said. "The same one that processed your death. The same one where Supervisor Hu works."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "What's happening?"

"The system is breaking. Small cracks at first—a misfiled destiny here, a misrouted soul there. But the cracks are spreading. And someone is making sure they don't get fixed."

"Hu."

"That's my theory." Li Mei's eyes met his. "But I need proof. And you're the only one who can get it."

"Why me?"

"Because you're the only one who's died because of this. The only one who has a personal stake. And because..." She hesitated. "Because you have access to the Core Administrative Interface. I've been trying to reach it for three hundred years, and I've never succeeded."

Zhang's hand drifted to his token. "How do you know about that?"

"The System Administrator told me. He's been watching you since you arrived. He thinks you might be the key to fixing this."

"Or the key to making it worse."

"That too." Li Mei smiled—a thin, humorless expression. "Welcome to the celestial bureaucracy, Zhang Wei. Where every promotion is a potential death sentence, and every ally might be a trap."

Her token beeped.

She looked down, and her face went pale. "We have to go. Someone's coming."

The shelves around them began to glow with an angry red light. From the darkness, Zhang heard footsteps—heavy, rhythmic, like a heartbeat made of stone.

[ALERT: Unauthorized Archive Access Detected]
[Security Response: Incoming]
[Estimated Time of Arrival: 15 seconds]

Li Mei grabbed Zhang's arm. "Follow me. Don't look back."

They ran.

The Archives twisted around them, shelves rearranging themselves as if the building itself was trying to trap them. Li Mei led them through a maze of corridors and hidden passages, her token casting flickering shadows on the walls.

Behind them, the footsteps grew louder.

"What is that?" Zhang shouted.

"Archive Guardians. They're not supposed to exist anymore—they were decommissioned three centuries ago. But someone reactivated them."

"For us?"

"For you." Li Mei pulled him around a corner and through a door that materialized just as they reached it. "The System Administrator said you'd be flagged. He didn't say they'd send the Guardians."

They emerged into a small room filled with filing cabinets. Li Mei slammed the door shut and pressed her hand against it. A seal of light formed around the frame.

"That won't hold them long," she said. "But it'll give us time."

"For what?"

She turned to face him. In the dim light, her expression was unreadable. "Time for me to tell you the truth. The real reason I've been watching your file."

Zhang's heart pounded. "I'm listening."

"Your death wasn't a mistake." Li Mei's voice was barely a whisper. "It was deliberate. Someone in the Department of Mortal Affairs killed you—not through incompetence, but through design. You were targeted."

"Why?"

"Because of what you are." She

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Audit of Infinite Regret

Zhang pulled his hand back from the celestial sphere as if burned. The notification blinked ominously in his vision, pulsing with increasing urgency.

[ALERT LEVEL: MEDIUM]
[INVESTIGATION INITIATED]
[SOURCE: Department of Internal Oversight]

"Internal Oversight?" Zhang muttered. "That sounds official and terrifying."

He didn't wait to find out what that meant. With a thought, he closed the interface and the white void dissolved around him. He materialized back in his cramped office, exactly where he'd been standing before the jade token had transported him to the core system.

The office looked different now. The walls seemed thinner, the filing cabinets more like prison bars. Every shadow held potential watchers. Every creak of the building was a footstep approaching.

Zhang sat down at his desk and pretended to work. His hands moved automatically, sorting through destiny reports and assigning karma values, but his mind was racing.

He'd accessed restricted records. That was bad. But what was worse was that the system had detected him immediately. He'd expected at least a few hours before anyone noticed. Instead, he'd gotten minutes.

*They're watching closer than I thought.*

A knock at his door made him jump. He composed himself before calling out, "Enter!"

The door slid open to reveal a woman he hadn't seen before. She was tall, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to see right through him. She wore a pristine white robe with golden trim—the uniform of a department head, but Zhang didn't recognize her division's symbol.

"Zhang Wei?" she asked, her voice flat and professional.

"That's me."

"I am Inspector Li Mei, Department of Internal Oversight." She stepped into the room without waiting for an invitation. "I'm here to conduct a routine audit of your department's operations."

"Routine?" Zhang raised an eyebrow. "On my first day?"

"Especially on your first day." Li Mei's eyes scanned his office, taking in every detail. "New employees often bring... irregularities into the system. We like to catch them early."

Zhang forced a smile. "Happy to cooperate. What do you need?"

She pulled a scroll from her sleeve and unrolled it. The parchment glowed with celestial script that shifted and rearranged itself as she read.

"I need access to your terminal, your jade token, and a complete log of all actions you've taken since your arrival."

*That's everything. Including the restricted records.*

"I don't have a terminal yet," Zhang said, thinking fast. "Supervisor Hu said I'd get one after my probation period. And my jade token is..."

He patted his pockets, making a show of looking for it.

"I seem to have misplaced it. Must have left it in the break room."

Li Mei's eyes narrowed. "You lost your jade token?"

"Misplaced. There's a difference."

"The difference is irrelevant. A lost token is a security breach." She made a note on her scroll. "I'll need to report this to the Celestial Security Division."

*Great. Now I'm on two watchlists.*

"Before you do," Zhang said, standing up, "maybe I can help you with your audit another way. I've been going through the records all morning, and I've noticed some... inconsistencies."

Li Mei's expression didn't change, but Zhang caught the slightest flicker of interest in her eyes. "Inconsistencies?"

"Yes. For example, did you know that the Department of Mortal Affairs has been processing death certificates with a 0.07% error rate for the past three centuries?"

"That's within acceptable parameters."

"It is. But what if I told you that 0.07% represents approximately 4,723 souls per century? And that every single one of those errors has been corrected by reassigning the soul to a lower-tier reincarnation, rather than proper restoration?"

Li Mei's scroll stopped glowing. She stared at Zhang for a long moment.

"How do you know that?"

"I'm observant." Zhang leaned against his desk, projecting confidence he didn't feel. "And I've been wondering: where do all those misrouted souls go? Because according to the official records, they're 'reassigned to appropriate karma cycles.' But when I checked the actual destination logs..."

He let the sentence hang. Li Mei's face remained impassive, but her fingers tightened on the scroll.

"You're bluffing."

"Am I?" Zhang pulled a piece of paper from his pocket—a scrap he'd grabbed from the filing room when no one was looking. It was a printout of a single record, one he'd memorized during his brief access to the core system.

[SOUL ID: 7845-B]
[ERROR LOG: Administrative Misrouting]
[ORIGINAL DESTINY: 82 additional years, wealth, family]
[CORRECTED DESTINY: Death at age 27]
[REASSIGNMENT: Soul Harvest - Department of Karmic Reclamation]
[NOTES: Subject flagged for disposal approval. Authorized by: Supervisor Hu]

Li Mei read the paper. Her eyes widened, just slightly.

"This is..."

"My death certificate. Official, signed, and completely fraudulent." Zhang's voice was calm, but inside his heart was pounding. "I was supposed to live to 109. Instead, I died at 27 because someone in this department made a typo. And then, instead of fixing it, they decided to just... get rid of me."

"That's not how the system works."

"That's exactly how the system works. I've seen the code." Zhang stepped closer to her. "The Celestial Management System has a built-in 'cleanup' protocol. Any soul that gets misrouted can be marked for disposal if the error would be too embarrassing to correct. It's hidden in the subroutines, buried under seventeen layers of administrative privilege, but it's there."

Li Mei's face had gone pale. She looked at the paper, then at Zhang, then at the paper again.

"This is a very serious accusation."

"I know."

"If it's true, it implicates not just Supervisor Hu, but everyone above him who signed off on the protocol."

"I know."

"Do you have proof? Real proof that would stand up in a Celestial Tribunal?"

Zhang hesitated. He had the code, buried in his memories from his brief access. But presenting it would expose his unauthorized system intrusion. He'd be punished before Hu was.

*Unless I can find a way to make the evidence speak for itself.*

"I can get you the proof," Zhang said. "But I need time. And I need access."

"Access to what?"

"The Department of Internal Oversight's own records. I need to see who else has been flagged for disposal. Because I have a feeling I'm not the only one."

Li Mei was silent for a long time. Finally, she rolled up her scroll and tucked it away.

"I can't give you access to our records. That would be a breach of protocol."

"Then what can you give me?"

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small, black jade token. It was different from the white one Zhang had been issued—darker, heavier, pulsing with a faint inner light.

"This is a Shadow Token. It grants temporary access to the Celestial Archives, with no logging." She held it out to him. "Take it. But understand: if you're caught using it, I will deny ever giving it to you. You'll be erased from existence, and I'll testify that you stole it."

Zhang took the token. It felt cold in his hand, almost alive.

"Why are you helping me?"

"Because if what you're saying is true, then the Celestial Bureaucracy has a cancer." Li Mei's voice was barely a whisper. "And I've suspected it for a long time. But I've never had anyone on the inside willing to dig for the truth."

She turned to leave, then paused at the door.

"One more thing. The Hell of Unending Data Entry? It's real. And it's not just for paperwork errors. It's where they send anyone who discovers too much."

She left, closing the door behind her.

Zhang stared at the black token in his palm. The shadows in the room seemed to swirl around it, drawn to its darkness like moths to a flame.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Item Acquired]
[Item: Shadow Token (Temporary)]
[Effect: Grants access to Celestial Archives (All Tiers) without logging]
[Duration: 72 hours (Real Time)]
[Warning: Use of this token is a Class 1 Celestial Offense]
[Penalty for Unauthorized Access: Soul Deletion (Permanent)]

*Three days. I have three days to find evidence that will bring down a system that's been running for millennia.*

Zhang pocketed the token and sat back down at his desk. Outside his window, the eternal twilight of the Celestial Bureaucracy stretched on, unchanging, unending.

But something had changed. Zhang could feel it in the air, in the subtle shifts of energy that moved through the halls.

The system was watching him. But now, he had eyes in the dark too.

His terminal flickered to life, displaying a new message:

[FROM: Department of Internal Oversight]
[TO: All Personnel]
[SUBJECT: Mandatory Systems Audit]
[NOTICE: Due to recent security concerns, all celestial employees will be subject to a full karmic review. Please report to your designated audit station within the next hour. Failure to comply will result in immediate disciplinary action.]

*They're moving faster than I expected.*

Zhang stood, adjusted his robe, and walked to the door. As he reached for the handle, his black token pulsed once, sending a shiver through his hand.

A voice whispered in his mind, ancient and cold:

*The Archives remember everything. But some things are buried so deep, even the archivists have forgotten.*

*Are you sure you want to dig them up?*

Zhang opened the door and stepped into the hallway, where a line of nervous clerks was already forming.

He didn't answer the voice. But his hand tightened around the token, and he smiled.

*I'm just getting started.*

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Clockwork Revelation

Zhang yanked his hand back from the sphere as if burned. The notification pulsed in his peripheral vision—[Alert Level: Medium]—and he knew exactly what that meant. Someone was coming.

He scanned the white void frantically. There was nowhere to hide, no cover, no shadows. Just the infinite nothing and the giant, glowing sphere of celestial bureaucracy.

Think, Zhang. Think.

His eyes caught something—a flicker in the sphere's surface, like a glitch in a video game. He reached out again, but this time not to access information. He touched the sphere with his palm and *pushed*.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: You have encountered a system integrity breach]
[Would you like to report this anomaly?]
[Yes] [No]

Zhang selected [No].

The sphere's surface rippled, and a small crack appeared. From it emerged a stream of golden light that wrapped around Zhang's wrist like a bracelet. He felt something click into place—a connection, a backdoor, a key.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Backdoor Access Granted]
[This connection is unauthorized]
[All actions performed through this connection will be untracked]
[Warning: If discovered, your soul will be immediately deleted]

A figure materialized in the void. It was Supervisor Hu, but not the same Hu from the office. This version was made of light and bronze armor, his eyes burning with celestial fire. In his hand, he held a spear that crackled with the same energy.

"Zhang Wei," Hu said, his voice echoing like thunder. "You've been a very busy clerk."

Zhang forced his face into a mask of confusion. "Supervisor? What's happening? I was just—I don't know how I got here."

Hu's eyes narrowed. "Don't play dumb. The system flagged you the moment you accessed those records. Do you have any idea what you've done?"

"I accessed records? I don't remember—"

"Liar." Hu raised his spear. "The Celestial Management System doesn't make mistakes. And neither do I."

Zhang's heart hammered against his ribs. He could feel the backdoor connection pulsing on his wrist, hidden beneath his sleeve. If Hu saw it, he was dead.

"I think there's been a misunderstanding," Zhang said, backing away slowly. "I was just trying to understand my paperwork. The forms are—"

"The forms are irrelevant." Hu took a step forward. "What matters is that you've seen something you shouldn't have. And now I have to clean up the mess."

He lunged.

Zhang threw himself to the side, barely avoiding the spear's tip. The weapon passed so close that he felt the heat of it singe his robes. He rolled and scrambled to his feet, running through the white void with no sense of direction.

"I'm not trying to escape," he shouted over his shoulder. "I just want to understand why I died!"

"Your death was processed. That's all that matters."

"It was an ERROR!"

Hu stopped. The spear lowered slightly. For a moment, Zhang saw something flicker in the supervisor's eyes—not anger, but... recognition.

"You know," Hu said quietly.

Zhang's blood ran cold. "Know what?"

"That it was an error. That you shouldn't be here." Hu's voice dropped to a whisper. "I've been waiting for someone like you."

The spear dissolved into light. Hu's bronze armor faded, replaced by his standard celestial robes. He looked suddenly tired, old, worn down by centuries of paperwork.

"How many others?" Zhang asked.

"Others?"

"How many souls have been processed in error? How many people died because someone in the Celestial Bureaucracy made a mistake and covered it up?"

Hu's jaw tightened. "More than you can imagine. And fewer than you'd hope."

The white void shimmered, and suddenly they were standing in an office—not the one on Floor 37, but something older, more decrepit. Papers were stacked to the ceiling, covered in dust. A single desk sat in the center, and on it rested a jade token identical to the one Zhang had received, but cracked and dark.

"This was my predecessor's office," Hu said. "She discovered the truth. They deleted her soul. I was promoted to replace her."

Zhang stared at the dark token. "And you just... accepted that?"

"What choice did I have?" Hu's voice cracked. "They can erase you, Zhang. Not just kill you—erase you. No reincarnation. No afterlife. Nothing. You become a statistical anomaly in the cosmic ledger, and no one remembers you ever existed."

"But you flagged my access. You came to stop me."

Hu nodded slowly. "Because I had to see. Had to know if you were like her. If you had the same fire."

"And do I?"

For a long moment, Hu said nothing. Then he reached into his robe and pulled out a small, bronze key. It glowed faintly, covered in runes that shifted and writhed like living things.

"This key opens the Central Archive," Hu said. "Not the public one, not the one Senior Clerks can access. The *real* one. The place where the original contracts between Heaven and Earth are stored."

Zhang's hand moved toward the key, but Hu pulled it back.

"There's a cost," Hu warned. "Once you see the truth, you can't unsee it. The system will know. They will hunt you. And if they catch you, they won't just delete your soul—they'll delete every trace of your existence from all timelines."

"I'm already dead," Zhang said. "What more can they take?"

Hu's eyes met his. "Everything."

Zhang reached out and took the key.

The moment his fingers touched the bronze, a shockwave blasted through the office. Papers flew everywhere. The walls cracked. Hu cried out and stumbled backward, his form flickering between bronze armor and celestial robes.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: CRITICAL ALERT]
[Unauthorized artifact detected: Archive Key of the First Scribe]
[Initiating emergency protocols]
[All Celestial Guards have been alerted]

"I have to go," Zhang said, clutching the key.

Hu grabbed his arm. "There's a door. Behind my desk. It leads to the maintenance tunnels. Follow them until you reach a blue door—that's the Archive entrance."

"What about you?"

"I'll hold them off. Buy you time." Hu smiled grimly. "I've been waiting for this moment for three thousand years. Don't waste it."

Zhang didn't argue. He ran.

The maintenance tunnels were exactly what he expected—dark, cramped, filled with pipes that hummed with celestial energy. He followed them for what felt like hours, the key glowing in his hand, lighting the way.

Behind him, he heard alarms. Shouts. The clang of weapons.

And then silence.

He reached the blue door. It was massive, covered in the same shifting runes as the key. He inserted the bronze artifact into a slot in the center, and the door groaned open.

Inside was a library. But not like any library Zhang had ever seen. Books floated in the air, rearranging themselves in patterns that defied physics. Scrolls unrolled and re-rolled themselves. And in the center of the room, on a pedestal of pure light, sat a single document.

[The Original Contract Between Heaven and Earth]

Zhang approached it slowly. The document was written in a language he'd never seen, but as he looked at it, the words rearranged themselves into characters he could understand.

*"We, the Celestial Bureaucracy, do hereby agree to manage the destinies of all mortal souls in accordance with the principles of fairness, justice, and compassion."*

Below that, in smaller text:

*"Any soul processed in error shall be entitled to immediate reincarnation with full memory retention and appropriate compensation."*

Zhang's hands trembled. There it was. The law. The rule. The justification he needed.

But as he read further, his blood froze.

*"Addendum 7, Section B: In the event that an administrative error threatens the stability of the Celestial Management System, the Department of Mortal Affairs reserves the right to deny reincarnation and proceed with soul deletion."*

*"Ratified by: The Jade Emperor"*

*"Counter-signed by: Supervisor Hu (then Junior Clerk)"*

Zhang stared at the name.

Hu had signed the addendum.

Hu had known about the loophole—had helped create it.

And now Hu had given him the key to the Archive.

The door behind him slammed shut. A voice echoed through the library—familiar, triumphant.

"You found it," Supervisor Hu said, stepping out of the shadows. His robes were torn, his face bloody, but he was smiling. "Thank you, Zhang Wei. You've just found the evidence I needed to destroy the entire Celestial Bureaucracy."

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Immortal Who Knew Too Much

The white void shimmered, and Zhang Wei found himself back in his cubicle, the jade token still warm in his hand. His heart hammered against his ribs. He'd done it. He'd actually accessed the Core Administrative Interface. And now Supervisor Hu's corruption sat in his memory like a loaded weapon.

But the system knew.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert Level Raised to Medium]

[Monitoring Frequency: Increased]

[Soul ID 7845-B - All Actions Now Logged in Real-Time]

Zhang's grin faded. Real-time logging. That meant every keystroke, every file access, every suspicious glance at a celestial document would be recorded and analyzed. He was trapped in a panopticon made of divine bureaucracy.

"Back from your extended rest break, Clerk Zhang?"

Zhang looked up. Supervisor Hu stood at the entrance of his cubicle, arms crossed, a thin smile playing on his lips. The man's aura flickered—not with anger, but with something colder. Calculation.

"Just getting reacquainted with my workspace, Supervisor." Zhang kept his voice level. "Lots to catch up on."

"Indeed." Hu stepped closer, his jade robes rustling. "I noticed you accessed some... unusual files during your orientation. The system flagged it."

Of course it did. Zhang's mind raced. Deny? Deflect? Confront?

"I was checking my death records," Zhang said, choosing honesty as his shield. "Wanted to see how I ended up here."

Hu's smile didn't waver, but his eyes narrowed. "And what did you find?"

"A clerical error." Zhang met his gaze. "Someone misrouted my soul. Wrong queue, wrong destiny. I was supposed to live another forty years."

The silence stretched between them. A bead of sweat trickled down Zhang's spine.

"Errors happen," Hu said finally. "The system processes billions of souls daily. Perfection is... aspirational, not achievable."

"Of course." Zhang nodded. "But I was wondering—who files the correction report? Or does it just... get lost in the backlog?"

Hu's composure cracked. Just a fraction. His eye twitched.

"I handle all correction reports personally," he said. "The matter is being addressed."

"Great. Then I should expect reincarnation soon?"

"No." The word came out sharp. Hu recovered, smoothing his robes. "The correction process takes time. Your current assignment is stable. Focus on your work, Clerk Zhang. Fulfill your duties, and the system will reward you."

He turned and walked away, but Zhang caught the set of his shoulders, the tension in his gait. Supervisor Hu was nervous. And nervous people made mistakes.

---

The first mistake came three hours later.

Zhang had spent the afternoon processing reincarnation requests—souls waiting to be reborn, their destinies already written by the celestial scriptwriters. It was tedious work, but he'd noticed something: every request that crossed his desk had been pre-approved by Supervisor Hu.

Every. Single. One.

That wasn't unusual on its own. But the timestamps were. Hu had approved all of them between 3 AM and 5 AM celestial standard time. The middle of the night shift. When no one else was watching.

Zhang pulled up Hu's profile in the internal system. [Supervisor Hu - Access Level: Tier 5] [Years of Service: 847] [Performance Reviews: Exemplary] [Disciplinary Actions: None]

None. Eight hundred and forty-seven years, and not a single mark on his record.

That was suspicious in itself.

Zhang opened a secondary window and began cross-referencing Hu's approval logs with the actual reincarnation outcomes. The system made it tedious—every query required three confirmation prompts and a signature in triplicate—but Zhang had spent his mortal life working in corporate HR. He knew how to find the cracks in a system.

Three hours later, he had his answer.

Of the 12,847 souls Hu had personally approved for reincarnation in the last century, 847 had been misrouted. Not to the wrong lives, but to the same life. A specific life. A single mortal existence that had been occupied by 847 different souls in the past hundred years.

Zhang pulled up the records for that life.

[Mortal ID: 9847-ALPHA]

[Name: Various (Current: Li Wei)]

[Location: Hangzhou, China]

[Status: Alive (Age: 34)]

[Occupied By: Soul ID 7845-B - ORIGINAL DESTINY]

Zhang's blood ran cold. That was his soul ID. His original destiny. The life he was supposed to have lived.

He checked the current occupant. Soul ID 9847-ALPHA-77. The seventy-seventh soul to be stuffed into his body.

Someone had been using his life as a dumping ground for misplaced souls. And Supervisor Hu was the one approving it.

---

"I need to speak with someone in Internal Affairs."

The celestial clerk at the information desk didn't look up from her screen. "Internal Affairs is on the Ninth Heaven. You need a Tier 4 clearance to access that floor."

"I have Tier 2."

"Then you can't access that floor."

"What about filing a formal complaint?"

"You can do that through the system." She finally glanced at him, her eyes glazed with centuries of boredom. "Form 87-B. Submit it to your supervisor for approval."

"My supervisor is the one I'm complaining about."

That got her attention. She sat up straighter, her fingers hovering over her keyboard. "That's... a serious allegation."

"I know. That's why I need to talk to someone who isn't him."

The clerk chewed her lip. "There's a deputy director in charge of supervisory oversight. Director Feng. She's on the Fifth Heaven, but she doesn't take unscheduled appointments."

"How do I get a scheduled appointment?"

"You don't. She picks her cases based on severity." The clerk leaned forward, lowering her voice. "But if you want my advice? Get proof. Hard proof. The kind that can't be deleted or redirected. Then send it through the anonymous reporting system. Use a dead drop code."

"A dead drop code?"

"Every clerk gets one when they're hired. It's in your orientation packet." She looked at him pointedly. "The one you probably didn't read."

Zhang thanked her and rushed back to his cubicle. The orientation packet. He'd dismissed it as celestial busywork, but now it might be his lifeline.

He found it buried in his desk drawer, still sealed in its jade envelope. Inside were forms, protocols, and a single piece of paper with a string of characters: [Dead Drop Access Code: 7X9-ALPHA-OMEGA-441]

He entered it into the system. A new interface opened, completely blank except for a single prompt:

[Anonymous Submission Portal]

[Upload File: ________]

[Note: All submissions are encrypted and untraceable. Director Feng reviews all flagged materials personally.]

Zhang's fingers hovered over the keyboard. He had the evidence—the reincarnation logs, the misrouted souls, the timing of Hu's approvals. But if he submitted it, there was no going back. Hu would be investigated. The corruption would be exposed. And Zhang would be the one who set it in motion.

Or the system would bury it, and he'd be sent to the Hell of Unending Data Entry.

He thought of Li Wei—the man living his life, breathing his air, loving his family. Seventy-seven souls had been stuffed into that body. Each one a mistake. Each one a cover-up.

Zhang uploaded the file.

[Submission Received]

[Tracking Number: 441-X7-ALPHA-99]

[Estimated Response Time: 3-5 Business Days (Celestial Standard)]

[Thank you for your contribution to administrative integrity.]

Three to five business days. That was an eternity in celestial time. Anything could happen in three to five days.

---

He didn't have to wait that long.

Two hours later, Zhang's terminal chimed with an urgent message.

[SYSTEM ALERT: Emergency Meeting]

[Location: Director's Office, Fifth Heaven]

[Attendees: Zhang Wei (ID 7845-B)]

[Subject: Administrative Review]

[Priority: Critical]

Zhang's stomach dropped. That was fast. Too fast. Either Director Feng had already reviewed his submission, or someone had intercepted it.

He made his way to the Fifth Heaven, passing through corridors of polished jade and floating screens. The Director's office was at the end of a long hall, guarded by two celestial lions with glowing eyes.

"Clerk Zhang Wei." The lion on the left spoke, its voice like grinding stones. "You're expected."

The doors swung open.

Inside, the office was surprisingly modest—a desk, some shelves, and a single window overlooking the clouds. Behind the desk sat a woman who looked about forty, with silver-streaked hair and eyes that held the weight of centuries.

Director Feng.

"Close the door, Clerk Zhang." Her voice was calm. Too calm. "We need to discuss your submission."

Zhang stepped inside. The doors closed behind him with a soft click.

"I reviewed your evidence," Feng continued. "Very thorough. Impressive for a junior clerk who's only been here three days."

"I had a good teacher," Zhang said. "Corporate bureaucracy. Same system, different window dressing."

Feng smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "You've uncovered something significant. Supervisor Hu has been running a reincarnation scheme for centuries. Using your original destiny as a disposal site for misplaced souls. It's a serious violation of celestial law."

"So you'll investigate?"

"I already have." Feng pulled up a screen. "I've placed Supervisor Hu on administrative leave pending a full audit. His access has been revoked. His files are being seized as we speak."

Zhang blinked. That was... easy. Too easy.

"And me?"

"You're being promoted." Feng's smile widened. "Effective immediately, you're the new Supervisor of the Department of Mortal Affairs. Tier 4 clearance. Full administrative authority."

"I..." Zhang's mind raced. "Just like that?"

"Just like that." Feng stood, extending her hand. "Congratulations, Supervisor Zhang. You've earned it."

Zhang shook her hand. Her grip was cold. Iron-cold.

"There's just one thing," Feng said, still holding his hand. "The system flagged something unusual during your orientation. An access to the Core Administrative Interface."

Zhang's heart stopped.

"I'm sure it was a mistake," Feng continued. "A junior clerk wouldn't have the ability to access such restricted systems. Would they?"

"It was a glitch," Zhang said. "The interface opened by accident. I closed it immediately."

"Of course you did." Feng released his hand. "Because if you had accessed it deliberately—if you had seen things you weren't supposed to see—that would be a violation of celestial law. And I would be forced to take action."

She leaned closer, her voice dropping to a whisper.

"I would hate to take action against my new supervisor. We have so much work to do together."

Zhang forced a smile. "Understood, Director Feng. The Core Interface was a glitch. Nothing more."

"Excellent." Feng stepped back, her smile returning. "Welcome to management, Supervisor Zhang. I look forward to our working relationship."

She turned to her desk, dismissing him.

Zhang walked out of the office, his hands shaking. He'd been promoted. He'd gotten what he wanted.

But as the doors closed behind him, he realized the truth: he hadn't exposed Hu. He'd just stepped into his place.

And Director Feng wasn't an ally.

She was the one running the whole operation.

title: The Demon King’s Resignation Letter
content:
Zhang Wei stared at the alert, his hand still pressed against the glowing sphere.

*Investigate and what?* The notification cut off, which meant either the system was glitching, or someone had interrupted the process. Given his luck, probably the latter.

He pulled his hand back, and the sphere dimmed, the flood of information receding to a manageable trickle. The white void around him flickered, and for a moment, he saw the ghostly outlines of cubicles and filing cabinets superimposed over the infinite space. The Core Administrative Interface was bleeding into reality, or reality was bleeding into it. Hard to tell which.

Then the flickering stopped, and the void stabilized. Zhang checked his new permissions.

[Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)]

*Provisional.* Of course. Nothing in heaven was permanent unless it was an inconvenience.

He navigated the interface with gestures, swiping through menus that appeared in the air before him. Destiny modification. Karmic ledger viewing. Timeline adjustment. Each function was grayed out except for the blueprints of their limitations. He could see what he *could* do if he climbed higher, but for now, he was stuck with read-only access to most of it.

But read-only was still useful. Information was power, especially in bureaucracy.

Zhang pulled up the karmic ledger for his department. Names and numbers scrolled past—every soul processed, every destiny assigned, every point of karma tallied. He searched for Supervisor Hu.

[Hu Zhong - Karmic Balance: -12,847]

Negative twelve thousand. That was *bad*. A mortal with negative karma would be reborn as a cockroach. An immortal with negative karma was supposed to be demoted or stripped of rank. Yet Hu sat comfortably in his office, yelling at clerks about filing errors.

Zhang dug deeper.

[Karmic Offenses: 47 (Suppressed)]
[Suppression Method: Bribery of Celestial Auditor - Batch 4]
[Suppression Method: Falsified Merit Reports - Batch 7]
[Suppression Method: [REDACTED BY ORDER OF DEPARTMENT OF INTERNAL AFFAIRS]]

Someone was covering for Hu. Someone with access to redaction protocols.

Before Zhang could investigate further, a new notification flashed.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Emergency Override]
[Source: Department of Celestial Security]
[Priority: Absolute]
[Action: All personnel report to their designated stations. Core Administrative Interface access suspended for non-authorized users.]

The sphere before him vanished, and the white void shattered like glass. Zhang stumbled backward, his vision spinning, and found himself back in his cubicle. The cheap fluorescent lights of the office hummed overhead. His hands trembled as he gripped the edge of his desk.

"Zhang Wei!"

He looked up. Hu stood in the doorway of his glass office, face pale, phone pressed to his ear. "Get in here. Now."

Zhang's instincts screamed at him to run. But there was nowhere to run in heaven. He stood, smoothed his robes, and walked to Hu's office.

Inside, Hu was pacing, his phone conversation rapid and frantic. "No, I understand, but the alert came from *internal* systems, not external. Yes, I know what that means. No, I didn't authorize any—" He paused, listened, then his face went white. "Understood. I'll handle it."

He hung up and stared at Zhang.

"Someone accessed the Core Administrative Interface from your terminal."

Zhang kept his face neutral. "Impossible. I'm a Tier 1 clerk. I don't have those permissions."

"Exactly." Hu's eyes narrowed. "Which means either you're lying, or someone else used your credentials. And since I know for a fact that you're the only one who's been at that desk all morning..."

"I didn't access anything restricted."

"You *did* something." Hu's voice dropped to a growl. "The system flagged a security breach. Celestial Security is launching a full investigation. Do you understand what that means?"

Zhang understood perfectly. It meant his cover was blown. It meant he had maybe minutes before a squad of celestial enforcers materialized in the office and dragged him to whatever hell awaited rogue clerks.

But Hu wasn't done.

"They'll audit *everything*. Every file you've touched. Every soul you've processed. Every karmic adjustment." Hu's face twisted. "They'll find *my* name in your files. They'll see you accessed my records."

Ah. So that was Hu's real concern. Not the security breach itself, but what the investigation would uncover about *him*.

"I can fix this," Zhang said.

Hu laughed bitterly. "Fix it? How? You're a dead mortal who doesn't even have a proper reincarnation slot. What could you possibly do?"

"Delete the access logs."

Hu stopped laughing. He stared at Zhang with a mixture of disbelief and suspicion. "You can't delete celestial system logs. They're immutable. That's the whole point."

"Everything is mutable if you know where to look." Zhang had seen the code. He'd touched the core. He understood now that the Celestial Management System wasn't a perfect divine construct—it was a piece of software, built by immortals who were just as fallible as mortals. And software had bugs.

Hu was silent for a long moment. Then he walked to his desk, opened a drawer, and pulled out a jade token identical to the one Zhang had stolen.

"You have ten minutes," Hu said, tossing the token to him. "Delete the logs, and I'll make sure the investigation doesn't touch you. Fail, and I'll personally ensure your soul is scattered across the void."

Zhang caught the token. It hummed with power in his hand. "I need time."

"You have nine minutes now."

Zhang turned and walked out of the office, his mind racing. He didn't have access to the Core Administrative Interface anymore—it had been suspended. But the token in his hand was a higher-level key than the one he'd stolen. Senior Clerk's token, probably Tier 3 or 4.

He returned to his desk, sat down, and placed the token against his terminal. The screen flickered, and a new interface appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Administrative Override Accepted]
[User: Hu Zhong - ID 2319]
[Access Level: Tier 4 - Supervisor]

Tier 4. Hu was higher-ranked than Zhang had assumed. Which meant Hu had even more to hide.

Zhang navigated to the system logs. The security breach had been timestamped and flagged. Multiple copies existed across redundant servers. He couldn't delete them all—that would require physical access to the celestial server farms, which were probably guarded by divine beasts and automated defense arrays.

But he could *alter* them.

He pulled up the log entry for his unauthorized access and began editing. Instead of showing that Zhang Wei accessed the Core Interface, he changed the source to an external IP address. Then he added a secondary flag: [SOURCE: Unknown - Possible Demon Realm Incursion].

Let Celestial Security chase that rabbit hole for a while.

He finished with seconds to spare. The logs now showed that an external entity had breached the system, accessed the Core Interface briefly, and then retreated. Zhang's name didn't appear anywhere in the altered records.

He sat back, breathing hard. His hands were shaking again.

A notification appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Investigation Suspended]
[Reason: Higher priority alert detected - Demon Realm activity]
[Note: Celestial Security resources redirected. Investigation of internal breach postponed indefinitely.]

Zhang almost laughed. It worked. He had just framed the Demon Realm for a cyberattack on heaven's bureaucracy.

But his triumph was short-lived.

A new message appeared, this one from an unfamiliar source.

[PRIVATE MESSAGE - ENCRYPTED]
[From: ????]
[To: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Subject: Nice work]

Zhang's blood ran cold.

[I saw what you did with the logs. Clever. But you made a mistake.]

[The Demon Realm doesn't have the technology to breach celestial systems. Everyone knows that. So when Celestial Security realizes their "higher priority alert" was fabricated, they'll look for who created the false flag.]

[Lucky for you, I've already deleted the evidence that leads back to your terminal.]

[Unlucky for you, I've kept a copy for myself.]

[You have a choice, Clerk Zhang. Work for me, or I expose you.]

[Reply to this message if you're interested. Don't bother trying to trace it—I'm behind seven layers of proxies and a divine concealment array.]

Zhang stared at the message. Someone else was hacking the system. Someone with access to encrypted communication channels and the ability to delete celestial evidence.

Someone who had been watching him.

He looked around the office. Clerks shuffled papers. Supervisors yelled at subordinates. The fluorescent lights hummed. Everything seemed normal.

But somewhere in this vast celestial bureaucracy, someone was playing a deeper game. And they had just recruited Zhang Wei.

Or threatened to destroy him.

Same thing, really.

He typed a reply.

[Who are you?]

The response came instantly.

[Call me The Archivist. I keep records that heaven wants forgotten.]

[And I've been watching you since the moment you accessed the Core Interface. You're not the first mortal to discover the system's flaws. But you might be the smartest.]

[Meet me at the Repository of Forgotten Souls, Sector 7-G, in one hour. Come alone. Don't tell Hu.]

[If you don't show up, I'll release the evidence to Celestial Security. And I'll make sure they know you're the one who hacked the system.]

[See you soon, Clerk Zhang.]

The message vanished, leaving no trace.

Zhang sat in his cubicle, the jade token cold in his hand. He had just survived one crisis and walked straight into another.

But this time, he had something he didn't have before: a contact. An ally, maybe. Someone who knew the system's secrets.

Or someone who wanted to use him as a pawn.

Only one way to find out.

He stood, grabbed his robes, and headed for the exit.

Hu's voice rang out from his office. "Zhang! Where are you going?"

"Bathroom," Zhang called back. "The investigation's suspended, remember?"

Hu grumbled something but didn't stop him.

Zhang stepped into the hallway and walked toward Sector 7-G, his heart pounding.

The Repository of Forgotten Souls. The place where heaven stored the souls that fell through the cracks. The ones that didn't get reincarnated, didn't get judged, didn't get processed.

The lost ones.

And somewhere among them, an immortal who called himself The Archivist was waiting.

Zhang quickened his pace.

He had a feeling this meeting would change everything.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Threads of Paradise Protocol

Zhang Wei snatched his hand back from the sphere as if burned. The notification pulsed in his vision, red and urgent.

*Investigate and... what?*

He didn't wait to find out.

[SYSTEM COMMAND: Close Core Interface]
[Confirm? Y/N]

He smashed Y.

The white void collapsed around him like a deflating balloon, and Zhang found himself back in his cramped office cubicle. His hands were shaking. The jade token was warm against his chest, almost hot.

*That was stupid. That was really, really stupid.*

He'd been a clerk for less than a week. He hadn't even processed his first full batch of destinies without Supervisor Hu breathing down his neck. And he'd just accessed the *core administrative interface* of the entire celestial bureaucracy.

"Having fun?"

Zhang nearly jumped through the ceiling.

Gao Ling stood in the doorway of his cubicle, arms crossed, one eyebrow raised. She was holding a stack of papers that looked suspiciously like his incomplete destiny reports.

"I was just—"

"Lying. You were just lying." She walked in and sat on the edge of his desk, crushing a half-finished form. "I saw the alert."

"What alert?"

"The one that went to every senior clerk in this department. 'Unauthorized core access detected. User ID 7845-B.'" She tilted her head. "That's you, genius."

Zhang's stomach dropped. "How many people saw it?"

"Just the seniors. And Hu." She paused. "Hu didn't see it."

"What?"

"I intercepted it." Gao Ling pulled a small, crystalline device from her sleeve—something that looked like a phone but glowed with internal light. "Modified karma router. Redirects certain notifications to a quarantine queue. Hu's too busy screaming at the new batch of souls to check his messages."

Zhang stared at her. "Why?"

"Because I want to know what you found."

The air between them grew heavy. Zhang studied her face—the sharp eyes, the slight downturn of her mouth, the way her fingers drummed against his desk. She was watching him the way a predator watches prey, but there was something else there too. Curiosity. Maybe even hope.

"Why should I tell you?" he asked.

"Because I just saved your soul from deletion." She held up the device. "And because I've been trying to crack that interface for three hundred years."

Three hundred years.

Zhang felt the weight of those words. He'd been dead for less than a month. She'd been stuck in this bureaucratic nightmare for centuries.

"Fine." He leaned forward, lowering his voice. "I found the error. The one that killed me."

Gao Ling's eyes widened. "You found the *specific* error?"

"Batch 374. Reincarnation Queue. My soul was misrouted by Clerk ID 2319. The correction was assigned to Supervisor Hu."

"Hu." Her voice went flat. "Of course."

"You knew?"

"I suspected." She stood up and began pacing in the tiny cubicle, her robes swishing against the walls. "Hu's been processing corrections for decades. But the error rate in his department is suspiciously low. Too low. Everyone else makes mistakes. Hu never does."

"Because he buries them?"

"Because he *sells* them." Gao Ling stopped pacing and faced him. "You're not the first soul to die from a 'clerical error.' But none of the others ever figured it out. They just accepted their punishment and moved on to reincarnation or dissolution."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "How many?"

"At least fifty that I've traced. Probably more." She held up the karma router again. "I've been building a case against him for two centuries. But every time I get close, the evidence disappears. Files get corrupted. Witnesses get reassigned to distant departments. One clerk who agreed to testify was sent to the Hell of Endless Mondays."

"I've heard of that one."

"Everyone has. Nobody comes back."

Zhang sat back in his chair, his mind racing. Supervisor Hu was corrupt. That much was clear. But there was something else nagging at him, something he'd seen in the core interface.

"Gao Ling," he said slowly. "When I accessed the records, I saw more than just my file. I saw the whole system—the current status of every destiny, the pending karma balances, the upcoming audits."

"Impossible. That level of access requires a Tier 5 clearance at minimum."

"I had it. For a few seconds, I had it." He pulled out his jade token and held it up. "This thing. It's not just a standard administrative token. It's... modified."

Gao Ling's face went pale. "Let me see that."

She snatched the token from his hand and held it up to the light. Her eyes moved rapidly, scanning something Zhang couldn't see. Then she swore—a word so profane that the air around them crackled.

"This isn't a standard token. This is a *master key*."

"A what?"

"A skeleton key for the celestial bureaucracy. It can access any system, any file, any department." She looked at him with something like fear. "Where did you get this?"

"Lao Wang. The old man at the Reassignment Desk."

"Old man with a white beard? Missing two fingers on his left hand?"

"Yeah, that's him."

Gao Ling handed the token back like it was radioactive. "That's not Lao Wang. That's *Feng Bo*, the former Minister of Records. He was exiled three thousand years ago for trying to reform the bureaucracy."

Zhang looked down at the token in his hand. The jade seemed to pulse with a light of its own. "Why would he give this to me?"

"Because he's still alive, and he's still fighting." Gao Ling's voice was barely a whisper. "He's been hiding in the lower departments, waiting for someone who could use that key. Someone who isn't afraid to break the rules."

"I'm terrified of breaking the rules."

"Good. That means you're smart." She grabbed his arm. "But we don't have time to be smart. If Hu finds out about that token, he'll have you deleted and the token destroyed. We need to move fast."

"Move where?"

"To the Archive of Lost Destinies."

Zhang blinked. "That's a real place? I thought it was a myth."

"It's real. It's where the bureaucracy stores all the souls that were supposed to have great destinies but died too early. Heroes, inventors, revolutionaries—all cut down before their time." She paused. "And it's where I've been hiding my evidence."

The door to the cubicle slammed open.

Supervisor Hu stood in the doorway, his face purple with rage. Behind him, two celestial enforcers in golden armor loomed, their eyes burning with white fire.

"Zhang Wei." Hu's voice was a low growl. "You're coming with me."

Gao Ling stepped in front of Zhang. "Supervisor Hu, I was just reviewing Zhang's reports. He's been making excellent progress—"

"Save your lies, Clerk Gao." Hu pushed past her and grabbed Zhang by the collar. "I know what you did. I know you accessed the core. And I know you found the file."

"File?" Zhang tried to look confused. "What file?"

"Don't play dumb." Hu's breath smelled like burnt incense and stale tea. "You found the error. You found Clerk ID 2319. And you found my name."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Liar." Hu's grip tightened. "But it doesn't matter. The enforcers are going to take you to processing. By the time they're done, you won't remember your own name, let alone any files."

The enforcers stepped forward, their golden armor gleaming.

Zhang's hand closed around the jade token.

He had one shot.

[CORE ACCESS: Emergency Override]
[Loading...]

The world slowed down.

Hu's face contorted in slow motion. Gao Ling reached for something in her sleeve. The enforcers raised their hands, golden light gathering around their palms.

Zhang saw them all as lines of code.

[Threat Assessment: Level 7]
[Recommended Action: Temporal Stasis]

He selected it.

The world stopped.

Hu froze mid-gesture. The enforcers' light hung in the air like frozen fire. Gao Ling was caught with her hand halfway out of her sleeve.

Zhang could move.

He stepped out of Hu's grip and walked around the frozen tableau. The token was burning hot now, pulsing with power he could barely comprehend.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Temporal Stasis Active]
[Duration: 30 Seconds]
[Warning: Extended use may cause Paradox Degradation]

Thirty seconds. That was all he had.

He looked at Gao Ling's frozen form, then at Hu's hate-filled face. The enforcers stood like golden statues, their eyes still burning.

He could run. He could hide. He could find Feng Bo and figure out what the hell was going on.

Or he could do something much, much stupider.

He turned to Hu's desk and began going through the drawers. Files, reports, requisition forms—and there, in the bottom drawer, hidden under a false bottom: a ledger.

The Karmic Black Market Ledger.

Names. Dates. Transactions. Souls bought and sold like commodities.

And at the top of the list: a name Zhang recognized.

*Client: Celestial Merchant's Guild*
*Purchase: 47 Souls (Premium Grade)*
*Delivery: Sector 7 - Temporal Variance Zone*
*Payment: 10,000 Years of Refined Karma*

The Celestial Merchant's Guild. The same organization that had "processed" his death.

His employer.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Temporal Stasis Ending]
[5 Seconds Remaining]

Zhang shoved the ledger into his robes and turned to run.

Then he stopped.

He looked at Hu's frozen form. At the enforcers. At Gao Ling, caught in a moment of desperate action.

If he ran now, he'd be a fugitive. Hunted by the entire celestial bureaucracy. No allies, no resources, no plan.

But if he stayed, he'd be deleted. His soul erased from existence.

He made his choice.

[3 Seconds Remaining]

He grabbed Gao Ling's hand.

[2 Seconds Remaining]

He activated the token's emergency transport function.

[1 Second Remaining]

The world shattered into light.

[0 Seconds Remaining]

Hu's scream echoed through the void as Zhang and Gao Ling vanished into the unknown.

---

They landed hard on a surface that felt like broken glass.

Zhang gasped, his lungs burning. The air here was thin and cold, carrying a strange metallic taste. Above them, the sky was the color of bruised flesh, filled with swirling clouds that seemed to move in reverse.

Gao Ling groaned beside him. "Where... where are we?"

Zhang looked around.

They were in a vast, open space filled with floating islands of data. Screens hung in the air, showing scenes from mortal lives. Books floated past, their pages flipping on their own. And in the distance, a massive structure loomed—a library made of pure light.

"I don't know," Zhang admitted. "I just hit the first transport option I saw."

Gao Ling sat up and looked around. Her face went pale.

"This is the Archive of Lost Destinies," she whispered. "But it's not the one I know."

"What do you mean?"

She pointed at the floating screens. "Look. Those aren't mortal lives. Those are *immortal* lives. Gods, celestial officials, even some of the archons."

Zhang followed her gaze. One screen showed a young woman with golden hair, her hand raised to strike down

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The God of Lost Packages

The white void flickered.

Zhang pulled his hand back from the sphere as the system alert pulsed in his vision. [Recommended Action: Investigate and—] The message cut off mid-sentence. Then it changed.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Process Detected]
[Source: External]
[Action: Unauthorized Access Attempt - Administrative Core]
[Status: BLOCKED]
[Alert Level: Critical]
[Recommended Action: EVACUATE]

Zhang's blood ran cold. The sphere of gears and runes began to spin faster, its rhythm quickening from a heartbeat to a drumroll. The white void rippled like disturbed water.

"What the—"

The ground beneath him shattered.

Zhang fell through layers of reality—offices, filing rooms, celestial gardens, a man eating noodles who looked up and waved—before slamming onto a marble floor. He gasped, lungs burning, and found himself staring at a familiar ceiling.

Department of Mortal Affairs. His desk. His stack of pending destines.

But something was wrong.

The office was silent. No typing. No gossiping clerks. No Supervisor Hu yelling about filing errors.

Zhang pushed himself up and looked around. Every desk was empty. Every jade terminal was dark. The only light came from the emergency lanterns that lined the walls, casting long shadows that seemed to move when he wasn't looking directly at them.

"What the hell happened?" he muttered.

A door creaked open behind him.

Zhang spun around. The break room. The door was slightly ajar, and a single finger—pale, thin, with an impossibly long nail—curled around the edge.

"Hello?" Zhang's voice echoed in the empty office. "Anyone there?"

The finger withdrew. The door swung open a little wider.

Zhang's hand went to his waist, searching for a weapon. He found nothing—clerks weren't issued weapons. They were issued papercuts and existential dread.

He crept toward the break room, every instinct screaming at him to run. But there was nowhere to run. He was dead. He was in the afterlife. And apparently, the afterlife had horror movies now.

The break room looked normal. A kettle. A stack of cups. A plate of pastries that had been there since Zhang's first day and somehow never spoiled or ran out.

And in the corner, hunched over the counter, was a figure in a tattered robe.

"So you're the one who set off the alarm."

The voice was low, raspy, like someone who hadn't spoken in centuries and was trying to remember how.

Zhang took a step back. "Who are you?"

The figure turned. It was an old man—ancient, withered, with eyes that glowed faintly gold. His robe was covered in stamps and seals, the marks of celestial authority faded and worn. In his hand, he held a scroll that unrolled endlessly, spilling across the floor like a river of paper.

"I'm the one they forgot to promote," the old man said. "The one they filed away in the wrong drawer. The one who's been waiting for three thousand years for someone to notice."

He unrolled another section of his scroll. Zhang caught a glimpse of names, dates, locations—all crossed out in red ink.

"You're a clerk," Zhang said.

"I was a clerk." The old man's eyes narrowed. "Now I'm a discrepancy. An irregularity. An error in the system that no one bothered to correct." He pointed a crooked finger at Zhang. "Just like you."

A new notification appeared in Zhang's vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Entity Detected]
[Designation: Clerk 0001 - First Administrative Appointee]
[Status: ERRONEOUS]
[Warning: This being should not exist. System integrity compromised.]
[Recommended Action: Report Immediately]

Zhang stared at the message. First Administrative Appointee. That meant this man had been here since the beginning. Since before the current system. Since...

"You're the original," Zhang breathed. "You helped build this place."

"I AM this place!" The old man slammed his fist on the counter. The pastries jumped. "I wrote the first lines of code! I filed the first destinies! I processed the first souls!" His voice cracked. "And then they replaced me. With a promotion system. With performance reviews. With—" He spat the word like poison. "—office supplies."

Zhang's mind raced. "The office supplies. The audits. You're the one who's been messing with them."

The old man smiled. It was not a pleasant smile. "Every time they order new jade tablets, I redirect them to the Void of Forgetting. Every time someone requests a celestial quill, I stamp it 'Return to Sender' and send it to a minor god who doesn't even have hands." He giggled. "You should see the look on his face when packages arrive."

"So that's why Supervisor Hu was so angry," Zhang said. "He thought I was stealing supplies."

"Oh, you're stealing much more than supplies." The old man unrolled another section of his scroll. "You accessed the Administrative Core. You viewed restricted records. You learned the truth about your death." He tapped the scroll. "Do you know what happens to clerks who learn too much?"

"They get promoted to middle management?"

The old man laughed—a dry, rattling sound. "No. They get reassigned. To Hell of Unending Data Entry. I've been there. It's worse than you think."

Zhang's jaw tightened. "You've been to the Hell of Unending Data Entry? How did you get out?"

"I found a loophole." The old man leaned in close. His breath smelled of old paper and ink. "The same loophole that brought you to the Administrative Core. The one that lets me exist despite being an error in the system."

He reached into his robe and pulled out a small object: a jade token, identical to the one Zhang had used to access the core.

"You have one too," the old man said. "I can sense it. They gave it to you when you asked too many questions. When you refused to accept your fate."

Zhang's hand went to his pocket. The token was still there, warm against his thigh.

"What do you want?" Zhang asked.

"Want?" The old man's eyes gleamed. "I want to be recognized. I want my work to be acknowledged. I want to sit in a proper office with a proper title and a proper salary of—" He paused. "Actually, I don't need a salary. I'm dead. I want a window. With a view of something other than the Filing Abyss."

"That's... surprisingly reasonable."

"I've had three thousand years to think about it." The old man shrugged. "But first, I need to fix the problem."

"What problem?"

The old man unrolled his scroll completely. It stretched across the break room, through the wall, into the office beyond. Names and dates covered every inch—thousands, millions, billions of them. And in the center, circled in red ink, was a single name:

Zhang Wei.

"You weren't supposed to die," the old man said. "I know that. You know that. But do you know WHY?"

Zhang shook his head.

"Because Supervisor Hu is incompetent. But that's not the whole story." The old man's voice dropped to a whisper. "Hu was following orders. Someone above him—someone in the Celestial Administration—altered your destiny. Deliberately."

"Altered my destiny? Why?"

"To test the system." The old man pointed at the scroll. "See here? Your life was a perfectly balanced equation. You would have lived to eighty-seven, died peacefully in your sleep, and reincarnated as a moderately successful businessman. Nothing special. Nothing notable."

"Gee, thanks."

"But someone wanted to see if they could change a destiny without triggering the alarms. So they chose you. A nobody. Someone who wouldn't be missed." The old man's eyes met Zhang's. "They wanted to prove they could kill anyone, at any time, and the system would accept it."

Zhang's blood boiled. "They killed me for a TEST?"

"Not just a test. A demonstration." The old man rolled up his scroll. "And they're about to do it again. To someone else. Unless we stop them."

A new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Emergency Broadcast - All Personnel]
[Alert: Administrative Core Breach Detected]
[Source: Department of Mortal Affairs - Break Room]
[Offending Entity: Clerk 0001 (Error State)]
[Secondary Offending Entity: Clerk Zhang Wei (Soul ID 7845-B)]
[Directive: Immediate Termination Authorized]
[Reinforcements: ETA 4 minutes]

"Four minutes," Zhang read aloud. "We have four minutes."

"Three minutes, fifty-seven seconds now." The old man grabbed Zhang's arm. "Come with me. I know a way out."

"Where?"

"The one place they never look. The Filing Abyss. The basement of the Celestial Bureaucracy. The place where lost documents go to be forgotten." The old man's grip tightened. "It's dangerous. The Abyss is full of errors and glitches and things that shouldn't exist. But it's also full of secrets."

Zhang hesitated. He could stay. Let the reinforcements find him. Try to explain.

But what would he explain? That an ancient ghost-clerk had recruited him to fight the system? That his death was a corporate experiment? That the entire Celestial Bureaucracy was built on a foundation of paperwork and lies?

"I have a question," Zhang said.

"One minute fifty-three seconds."

"Why did you show yourself to me? Why now?"

The old man's expression softened. "Because you're the first clerk in three thousand years who looked at the system and asked 'why.' The first one who refused to accept the answer. The first one who—"

A crash echoed from the office. The doors burst open. Figures in golden armor flooded in—celestial enforcers, their faces hidden behind featureless masks, weapons drawn.

"There they are!" one of them shouted.

"Thirty seconds," the old man said. "Decision time, Clerk Zhang. Stay and die again. Or come with me and become something worse."

Zhang looked at the enforcers. Then at the old man. Then at the jade token in his pocket.

He thought of his life. His death. The job offer. The endless filing. The audits. The lies.

He thought about all the people who would suffer if the system remained broken.

"I have one condition," Zhang said.

"Name it."

"We fix this. Not just for me. For everyone. We expose whoever ordered my death. We tear down the corruption. And we make sure no one else gets killed for a test."

The old man grinned. It was the first genuine smile Zhang had seen on his face.

"I was hoping you'd say that."

The enforcers were ten feet away. Their weapons glowed with killing light.

The old man grabbed Zhang's shoulder and shoved him toward the break room's back wall.

"There's a door here. Only I can see it. Only I can open it." He pressed his hand against the wall. It shimmered, revealing a dark passage. "Go. I'll hold them off."

"You'll be terminated!"

"I'm already an error in the system." The old man's eyes gleamed gold. "What's one more termination? Now GO!"

Zhang dove through the passage as the enforcers reached the break room. He heard the clash of battle, the old man's laughter, and then—

Silence.

The passage closed behind him, leaving Zhang in absolute darkness.

He stood there, heart pounding, alone in the unknown.

Then a voice echoed from the depths:

"Welcome to the Filing

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Ghost in the Machine

Zhang stared at the alert notification, his heart racing. *Investigate.* That meant someone was coming. He needed to move fast.

But before he could act, the white void around him flickered. The massive sphere of gears and runes began to spin, faster and faster, until the individual components blurred into a single, solid light. Zhang shielded his eyes, feeling the pull of something—*someone*—trying to drag him out of the interface.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Forced Disconnect Initiated]
[Source: Unknown Administrator]
[Override Code: ████████]
[Warning: User integrity compromised. Saving current state...]

"Wait," Zhang said, but the void had already dissolved around him.

He snapped back to reality, slumped over his desk, his cheek pressed against a stack of forms. The office hummed with its usual ambient noise: the clatter of keyboards, the murmur of clerks, and the distant echo of Supervisor Hu's voice berating someone in his office.

Li Mei was staring at him from across the aisle, her eyebrows raised. "You okay? You just... zoned out for a second."

"Fine," Zhang said, sitting up straight. His head was pounding. "Just tired."

"You've been saying that all week." She leaned closer, lowering her voice. "And you've been acting weird. What's going on?"

Zhang considered telling her. Li Mei was the closest thing he had to an ally in this place. But trust was a luxury he couldn't afford. If she was compromised—if she reported him to Hu—he'd be in the Hell of Unending Data Entry before lunch.

"Nothing," he said. "Just trying to figure out how to survive this place."

Her eyes narrowed, but she didn't push. "Fine. But if you're planning something stupid, at least let me know so I can distance myself from the explosion."

"Appreciate the support."

Before she could respond, a shadow fell over Zhang's desk. He looked up to find Bai, the pale-faced clerk from Division Three, standing there with a thin smile that didn't reach his eyes.

"Zhang Wei," Bai said, his voice soft and oily. "Supervisor Hu wants to see you. Immediately."

Zhang's stomach dropped. *The alert.* Had Hu already been notified? Was this the end of his brief, ill-fated rebellion?

"Any idea what it's about?" Zhang asked, keeping his voice neutral.

Bai's smile widened. "I'm just the messenger. But if I had to guess?" He leaned in, whispering. "Someone's been poking around where they don't belong."

*Definitely the alert.*

Zhang stood, smoothing down his robes. "Lead the way."

As he followed Bai through the maze of cubicles, Zhang's mind raced. He had two options: play dumb and hope the system's alert was vague enough to deflect, or go on the offensive and accuse Hu of incompetence. The first was safer. The second was more satisfying.

He chose the third option: bluff.

Hu's office was a cramped room at the back of the department, cluttered with scrolls and paper talismans. The supervisor sat behind a desk that looked like it had been through a war—scratched, dented, covered in sticky notes that had long since faded to illegibility.

Hu didn't look up when Zhang entered. He just kept scribbling on a document, his pen scratching against the paper in a rhythm that set Zhang's teeth on edge.

"Sit," Hu said, gesturing to a chair that was conspicously lower than his own.

Zhang sat. The chair creaked.

For a long moment, the only sound was Hu's pen. Zhang waited, forcing himself to remain calm. He'd learned from the best—his old boss at the insurance company had been a master of the silent treatment, using it to make underlings squirm. It only worked if you let it.

Finally, Hu set down his pen and looked up. His eyes were small and hard, like two black pebbles.

"I've received a report," Hu said, "that someone in my department has been accessing restricted files. Administrative records that are above their clearance level."

Zhang kept his face blank. "That's concerning, Supervisor. Who was it?"

Hu's lips twitched. "The system flagged your token ID."

"Interesting." Zhang leaned back, crossing his arms. "I haven't accessed any restricted files today. Unless..." He paused, letting the silence stretch. "Unless someone else is using my credentials."

Hu's eyes narrowed. "Are you accusing a fellow clerk of identity theft?"

"I'm not accusing anyone of anything, Supervisor. I'm just pointing out that my token has been with me all day, and I've been at my desk filing forms. You can check the system logs—my terminal hasn't accessed anything above Tier 1 clearance."

It was a lie, of course. But Zhang was counting on Hu not knowing how to actually check the logs. The man barely understood the filing system he was supposed to be in charge of.

Hu stared at him for a long moment. Then he pulled a scroll from his desk and unrolled it, revealing a dense matrix of numbers and symbols.

"The system doesn't lie," Hu said, tapping the scroll. "It says your token was used to access the Core Administrative Interface. That's Tier 2 access. Three levels above your current position."

*Damn.* Zhang had hoped the Core Interface would be harder to trace. He needed to pivot.

"With respect, Supervisor, the system has been glitchy lately. You know that better than anyone." Zhang met Hu's gaze. "Remember the filing error that caused my—" he almost said *death* "—my reassignment? That was a system error too."

Hu's face darkened. "That was not a system error. That was a clerical mistake."

"Exactly my point." Zhang pressed on. "If the system can make mistakes with mortal souls, why can't it make mistakes tracking logins? Maybe someone accessed the Core Interface through a backdoor and the system misattributed it to my token."

Hu's hand hovered over the scroll, his fingers twitching. Zhang could see the gears turning in the man's mind. Hu was paranoid—Zhang had noticed that within his first hour in the department. The supervisor was constantly looking over his shoulder, constantly checking and rechecking his work, constantly afraid of being exposed as incompetent.

Paranoid people were easy to manipulate.

"It's possible," Hu said slowly, "that the system has been... compromised."

"Exactly." Zhang nodded. "And you'd be the one to fix it, Supervisor. You have the highest clearance in this department. If anyone can track down the real culprit, it's you."

Flattery. The oldest trick in the book. But Hu's chest swelled visibly, his shoulders straightening.

"Of course," Hu said. "I'll launch a full investigation. In the meantime, your token will be flagged for monitoring. If you try to access anything above your clearance again, I'll have you sent to the Hell of Unending Data Entry myself."

"Understood, Supervisor."

Zhang stood, bowing slightly. As he turned to leave, Hu spoke again.

"One more thing, Zhang Wei."

Zhang paused.

"The error that killed you," Hu said. "I remember it now. Soul ID 7845-B. It was a tragedy. A true tragedy."

Zhang didn't turn around. He could feel Hu's eyes boring into the back of his head.

"But mistakes happen," Hu continued. "And sometimes, it's better to just move on. Dwelling on the past doesn't change anything."

Zhang's hands curled into fists. *He knows I know.* Hu was threatening him, warning him to drop the investigation.

But Zhang had never been good at taking warnings.

"Of course, Supervisor," Zhang said, his voice perfectly neutral. "I'll keep that in mind."

He walked out of the office, his heart pounding. As he passed Bai, the pale clerk gave him a knowing look.

"Everything okay?" Bai asked.

"Fine," Zhang said. "Just a misunderstanding."

"Good." Bai's smile was razor-thin. "Because misunderstandings can get you killed. Or worse, reassigned."

Zhang didn't respond. He walked back to his desk, sat down, and stared at his terminal.

He needed a new plan. Hu was onto him. The system was monitoring him. And somewhere in the depths of the Celestial Bureaucracy, someone had triggered a forced disconnect from the Core Interface—someone with higher access than even the department supervisor.

But that also meant someone had noticed him. Someone had seen his potential.

Zhang pulled up his system messages, scrolling through the logs until he found what he was looking for:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Forced Disconnect Initiated]
[Source: Unknown Administrator]
[Override Code: ████████]

The override code was redacted. But the source wasn't.

Zhang squinted at the text. The source field was supposed to display the administrator's department and rank. Instead, it showed something else:

[Source: System_Nexus.exe]

That wasn't a department. That wasn't a person.

That was a file name.
