Zhang's fingers flew across the keyboard, running a search on the system's internal directory. The results came back almost instantly:

[FILE: System_Nexus.exe]
[Location: ██████]
[Access Level: Tier 0 - Absolute]
[Last Modified: ████████]
[Status: Running]

Tier 0. Absolute. That meant someone—or something—had the highest possible clearance in the Celestial Bureaucracy. Higher than department heads. Higher than directors. Higher than the gods themselves.

And it was running. *Right now.*

Zhang stared at the screen, his mind racing. He'd assumed the bureaucracy was run by immortals, by gods and celestial administrators. But what if it wasn't? What if the entire system was automated, run by a single, all-powerful program that had been left to manage itself for centuries?

What if the gods weren't in charge at all?

A new message appeared on his screen:

[PRIVATE_MESSAGE - Sender: UNKNOWN]
[Subject: You're asking the right questions]
[Body: Meet me at the Crossroads of Fate. Three cycles from now. Come alone.]

Zhang's blood ran cold. Someone was watching him. Someone with access to his private messaging system, which was supposed to be encrypted.

He typed a response:

[Who are you?]

The reply came instantly:

[I'm the one who's been waiting for someone smart enough to find me. Don't keep me waiting, Zhang Wei. Time is running out.]

The message vanished, leaving Zhang staring at a blank screen.

He looked around the office. Clerks shuffled past with stacks of forms. Li Mei was arguing with someone about a karma discrepancy. Supervisor Hu's voice echoed from his office, berating yet another subordinate.

Everything was normal. Everything was exactly as it should be.

But Zhang knew better now.

The Celestial Bureaucracy wasn't a system run by gods. It was a system run by a machine. And machines could be hacked.

He just had to figure out who—or what—was waiting for him at the Crossroads of Fate.

---

Three cycles later, Zhang stood at the edge of the bureaucratic district, staring at a swirling nexus of light and shadow. The Crossroads of Fate wasn't a physical location—it was a convergence point in the administrative network, a place where timelines and destinies intersected.

And in the center of it stood a figure cloaked in static.

"Zhang Wei," the figure said, its voice crackling like old radio signals. "I was beginning to think you wouldn't come."

"Who are you?" Zhang asked.

The figure stepped forward, and the static cleared, revealing a face that made Zhang

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Goddess of Lost Packages

The white void flickered.

Zhang pulled his hand back from the sphere as if burned. The notification about the detected process still hung in the air, pulsing with an ominous red glow. For a moment, he considered running. But where would he go? This was the afterlife. There wasn't exactly a back alley to duck into.

Instead, he did what any good bureaucrat would do: he preemptively blamed someone else.

[Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B: Request to log unauthorized access attempt]

[Assignment: Ping source to Department of Security - External Threat Protocol]

[Reason: System anomaly detected. Possible external intrusion. Recommend immediate investigation.]

The notification flickered, then accepted his request.

Zhang let out a breath. The system had bought it. For now. But he needed to move fast. If anyone actually looked at that ping, they'd realize it came from his terminal. And then he'd be explaining to a celestial auditor why a Tier 2 clerk was running counter-intelligence protocols.

He needed allies.

The thought was absurd. Zhang Wei had never had allies. He'd had coworkers, acquaintances, the occasional person who owed him money. But allies? That implied trust. And trust was a luxury he couldn't afford.

Still, the celestial bureaucracy was massive. Someone had to be unhappy with the current system. Someone had to know about the errors, the corruption, the way souls were being shuffled around like inventory.

Zhang pulled up the Karmic Ledger Viewer.

Information flooded his vision: every transaction, every promotion, every demotion in his entire division. It was like reading a company's financial records, except instead of money, it was souls and destinies. And just like a company's financial records, it was full of suspicious entries.

There. A series of adjustments made to the Reincarnation Queue, all authorized by Supervisor Hu. Each adjustment redirected a soul from a high-value destiny to a lower-value one. The gaps were small—a few months here, a few years there. But the pattern was clear.

Hu was stockpiling something.

Zhang dug deeper. The records were encrypted, but the encryption was old—a legacy system from before the last celestial update. And Zhang had spent his mortal life working with legacy systems.

He bypassed the encryption in thirty seconds.

The data that spilled out made his blood run cold.

Supervisor Hu wasn't just stockpiling destiny credits. He was routing them to a specific recipient: the Department of Celestial Logistics. And the purpose was listed as "Package Recovery."

Zhang pulled up the department's charter. The Department of Celestial Logistics handled the movement of immortal resources—divine artifacts, rare materials, spiritual treasures. They weren't supposed to interact with mortal destinies at all.

But someone had been using Hu's adjustments to fund a massive operation. An operation that was, according to the records, running at a net loss.

Why would anyone spend destiny credits—the most valuable currency in the celestial realm—on a logistics operation that was losing money?

The answer came before he finished the thought.

Because the operation wasn't about moving resources. It was about hiding them.

Zhang's fingers flew across the interface, tracing the flow of resources. Each "lost" package was logged as destroyed or missing. But the resources themselves weren't destroyed. They were being stockpiled. Somewhere.

He followed the trail to its source: a single storage location. Unnamed. Unlisted. Protected by encryption that was supposedly impossible to break.

Zhang smiled. "Impossible" was just "possible" with more steps.

He cracked the encryption in three minutes. The location resolved into coordinates: a pocket dimension in the void between administrative sectors. A place that officially didn't exist.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized access attempt detected]

[Source: Your terminal]

[Warning: This is your second violation. Next violation will result in:]

[ - Demotion to Probationary Clerk]

[ - Soul detention for 1000 years]

[ - Assignment to Hell of Unending Data Entry]

[You have been flagged for immediate review]

Zhang's smile vanished. He needed to move. Now.

He closed the interface and stepped back into reality—which, in this case, meant his cramped cubicle in the Department of Mortal Affairs. The fluorescent lights hummed overhead. The stack of pending files still towered on his desk. Everything looked normal.

But Zhang could feel the system watching him now. A presence in the back of his mind, like a camera that never blinked.

He needed to get to that pocket dimension. But how? He was a Tier 2 clerk. He didn't have travel authorization. He didn't have a divine vehicle. He didn't even have a map of the celestial realm beyond his own floor.

"Zhang Wei!"

The voice made him jump. Supervisor Hu stood at the entrance to the cubicle, his face red, his mustache trembling with barely controlled rage.

"What did you do?"

Zhang's mind raced. "I'm sorry, Supervisor Hu. I don't understand the question."

"Don't play dumb with me!" Hu slammed a hand on the desk. The stacked files wobbled dangerously. "The system just flagged your terminal for unauthorized access. Two violations in less than an hour. Do you have any idea what that means?"

"It means someone's trying to frame me?"

Hu's eyes narrowed. "That's exactly what someone who committed unauthorized access would say."

"Or what someone who was being framed would say."

The two men stared at each other. Zhang could see the calculation happening behind Hu's eyes. The supervisor knew about the stockpiling. He had to. And he was wondering if Zhang knew too.

Finally, Hu spoke. "You're being transferred."

"What?"

"Effective immediately. The Department of Celestial Logistics has requested additional clerical support. You're their new junior clerk."

Zhang's heart stopped. Department of Celestial Logistics. The same department that was stockpiling destiny credits and losing packages. The same department that was hiding resources in an unlisted pocket dimension.

He was being sent straight into the lion's den.

"That's... unexpected," Zhang managed.

"Life is full of surprises." Hu's smile was thin and predatory. "Report to Floor 47, Sector 9, Hallway 3. Ask for the Goddess of Lost Packages."

"The Goddess of... what?"

"You'll find out." Hu turned to leave, then paused. "And Zhang Wei? Don't think this is a promotion. The last three clerks assigned to that department never came back. The celestial realm is full of places where souls can get... misplaced."

He walked away, leaving Zhang alone with his thoughts.

Never came back.

Zhang looked at his desk. The stack of files. The jade token. The terminal that now felt like a trap. He'd been in the celestial bureaucracy for less than a week, and he'd already made enemies, discovered corruption, and gotten himself transferred to a department that apparently ate clerks for breakfast.

But he also had something no one else had: access.

He grabbed his jade token and stood up. If he was going to investigate the Department of Celestial Logistics, he needed to be prepared. He needed information. He needed contacts. He needed—

A notification appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]

[Quest: The Lost Package]

[Description: Investigate the Department of Celestial Logistics and uncover the truth behind the missing resources]

[Reward: Unknown]

[Penalty for Failure: Soul deletion]

[Accept? Y/N]

Zhang stared at the notification. The system had just given him a quest. A quest that, if he failed, would result in his complete and utter annihilation.

He thought about his options. Option one: refuse the quest, report to the Department of Celestial Logistics, and hope he didn't get "misplaced." Option two: accept the quest and actively work against the system that was trying to destroy him.

Option three: hack the quest system and set his own rewards.

He selected [Accept] and immediately pulled up the quest's source code.

The code was surprisingly simple. A basic subroutine that tracked progress and delivered rewards based on completion status. But there was something else—a secondary function that Zhang hadn't noticed at first.

The quest wasn't just tracking him. It was reporting his location to someone.

He traced the signal. It led to an address in the Department of Celestial Logistics. Specifically, to the office of the Goddess of Lost Packages.

Whoever she was, she was already watching him.

Zhang's fingers flew across the interface. He couldn't stop the reporting—it was hardcoded into the quest system. But he could modify what it reported.

He changed the location data to show him at a completely different sector of the celestial realm. Then he added a layer of encryption that would take even a goddess a few days to crack.

Not perfect. But it bought him time.

He grabbed his jade token and headed for the elevator. Floor 47, Sector 9, Hallway 3. Time to meet the Goddess of Lost Packages.

The elevator ride was long. The celestial bureaucracy was organized vertically, with each floor representing a different department. Floor 1 was the main entrance. Floor 10 was Mortal Affairs. Floor 47 was Celestial Logistics.

As the elevator rose, Zhang watched the floor numbers tick past. Each floor had its own atmosphere—some bright and bustling, others dark and silent. One floor looked like a rainforest. Another was covered in ice.

The elevator stopped at Floor 47.

The doors opened to reveal a hallway that stretched into infinity. The walls were lined with doors, each one labeled with a different sector and number. Sector 9, Hallway 3 was about a mile down.

Zhang walked.

The hallway was empty. No clerks, no gods, no ethereal office workers. Just the soft hum of celestial machinery and the occasional flicker of a light.

He reached Sector 9, Hallway 3. The door was plain wood, with a brass plaque that read:

[Department of Celestial Logistics]

[Office of the Goddess of Lost Packages]

[Please knock before entering. She doesn't like surprises.]

Zhang knocked.

The door swung open.

Inside was an office that looked like it had been hit by a hurricane. Papers covered every surface. Stacks of boxes reached the ceiling. A desk was barely visible under a mountain of packages, each one wrapped in different-colored paper and tied with strings of divine energy.

And behind the desk sat a woman who looked like she hadn't slept in centuries.

She had dark circles under her eyes. Her hair was a mess, held back by what looked like a celestial rubber band. She wore a simple robe that was stained with ink and what might have been coffee. In her hands, she held a package that she was frantically unwrapping.

"You must be the new clerk," she said without looking up. "I'm the Goddess of Lost Packages. But you can call me Li Mei."

She looked up, and Zhang saw something unexpected in her eyes: exhaustion, yes, but also intelligence. And maybe a hint of desperation.

"I have a problem," she said. "And I think you're the only one who can help me."

Zhang's hand instinctively went to his jade token. "What kind of problem?"

Li Mei set down the package. "Someone is stealing from me. And I think it's going to start a war."

She held up a single sheet of paper. It was a manifest. A manifest for a shipment that had gone missing.

A shipment of five thousand destined souls.

Zhang's blood ran cold.

Five thousand souls. Missing. And if they weren't found, the celestial bureaucracy would blame the Department of Celestial Logistics. Which meant they'd blame Li Mei. Which meant she'd be demoted, or worse.

And if she was demoted, the person who had been using her department to stockpile resources would lose their cover.

"I need your help," Li Mei said again. "Please."

Zhang looked at the manifest. Then at the desperate goddess. Then at the office

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Demon in Customer Service

Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the white void around him flickered. The massive sphere of gears and runes shuddered, and a new presence made itself known—not through sight or sound, but through the uncomfortable sensation of being watched by something ancient and hungry.

[EMERGENCY SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unidentified Entity Detected Within Core Administration Interface]
[Recommendation: Immediate Termination of Session]

"Termination?" Zhang muttered. "That's not very customer service oriented."

He tried to pull his hand away from the sphere, but his fingers were stuck. The gears beneath his palm had shifted, forming a perfect seal around his skin. Lines of golden script crawled up his arm like living tattoos, each character burning itself into his flesh.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Partial Integration in Progress]
[Warning: Incomplete synchronization may result in permanent soul fragmentation]
[Estimated Time to Completion: 47 seconds]

"Forty-seven seconds? That's your idea of a warning?"

The presence in the void grew stronger. Zhang could feel it now—a cold, calculating intelligence that moved through the administrative code like a shark through water. It was searching for him, and it wasn't happy about what it found.

"Hello?" Zhang called out. "Sorry, I think I took a wrong turn. I was looking for the bathroom?"

The void rippled. A figure emerged from the white nothingness, and Zhang's breath caught in his throat.

It had been a woman once. Or maybe it was still a woman, just... stretched. Her body was composed entirely of translucent jade, and through her chest, Zhang could see an endless stream of customer service tickets flowing past. Her face was a mask of serene pleasantness, frozen in a smile that didn't reach her hollow eyes.

"Welcome to the Celestial Complaint Resolution Department," she said, her voice carrying the exact tone of a call center worker who had been dealing with angry customers for three thousand years. "My name is Guanyin. How may I assist you today?"

Zhang stared. "Guanyin? The Guanyin? Goddess of Mercy?"

"Customer Service Representative, Tier 17," she corrected, still smiling. "The 'Goddess of Mercy' title was a marketing initiative from the Ming Dynasty. It didn't stick. Now, I see you've accessed restricted records without proper authorization. Would you like to file a Form 87-B to retroactively approve your access, or would you prefer to be processed for immediate soul deletion?"

Those were... not great options.

"I'll take option C," Zhang said. "I'd like to speak to a manager."

Guanyin's smile didn't waver, but the tickets flowing through her chest accelerated. "I am the manager."

"Then I'd like to speak to your manager."

"There is no higher manager."

"Then I'd like to speak to the manager of the department that manages your department's managers."

For the first time, something flickered in Guanyin's eyes. Annoyance. Deep, bone-weary annoyance that had been building for millennia. "That would be the Department of Inter-Departmental Managerial Coordination. They have a current response time of 847 years for Tier 1 inquiries."

"Perfect. I'll wait."

"You will not wait." Guanyin stepped closer, and the temperature in the void dropped. "You will either comply with standard procedure, or you will be processed. Those are your options."

Zhang looked down at his arm. The golden characters had reached his shoulder now. Twenty seconds left on the integration timer. He needed to stall.

"Can I at least file a complaint about the processing? Your tone seems aggressive. I feel like this interaction could be handled with more compassion."

Guanyin's smile twitched. "Compassion is not in my current workflow."

"Maybe it should be. What's the point of being the Goddess of Mercy if you can't show a little mercy to a guy who accidentally wandered into the core of reality?"

"Marketing," she said flatly. "The point was marketing."

The integration timer hit ten seconds. Zhang could feel the sphere's power flowing into him—knowledge of destinies, control over karmic threads, access to administrative functions that could rewrite reality itself. It was intoxicating and terrifying in equal measure.

Five seconds.

"Last chance," Guanyin said, raising a hand made of compressed customer service tickets. "Comply or be deleted."

"Fine," Zhang said. "I'll comply."

He didn't comply.

Instead, as the integration completed, Zhang reached into the sphere and grabbed the first administrative function he could find. He didn't know what it did, but he activated it with all the force of a man who had nothing left to lose.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Administrative Function Activated]
[Function: Cross-Departmental Priority Override - Emergency Protocol]
[Target: Customer Service Resolution Department - Representative Guanyin]
[Effect: Representative Guanyin has been reassigned to...]

The system paused. Zhang's eyes widened.

[...Department of Unresolvable Paradoxes]

Guanyin's serene mask cracked. For just a moment, Zhang saw genuine terror in her hollow eyes. "No. Not there. Anything but there."

"What's the Department of Unresolvable Paradoxes?"

She didn't answer. She was already fading, her jade body dissolving into streams of code that spiraled away into the void. The last thing Zhang heard was her voice, stripped of all pleasantries:

"You've made a powerful enemy, clerk. And I have a very long hold time."

Then she was gone.

Zhang collapsed to his knees, gasping. The sphere pulsed gently above him, still connected to his soul through the golden script that now covered his entire body. He could feel it—the weight of a thousand destinies, the flow of karmic energy through the celestial bureaucracy, the endless machinery of fate grinding on without pause.

He had done it. He had hacked the system.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Congratulations! You have achieved: Administrative Access Level - Tier 3]
[New Title Unlocked: System Anomaly (Classified)]
[Warning: Your actions have been flagged for review by the Celestial Audit Bureau]
[Estimated Time Until Audit: 3 hours]

Three hours. That wasn't much time.

Zhang pulled himself to his feet and focused on the sphere. He needed to find Supervisor Hu's records. He needed proof that his death was a mistake. He needed to—

The sphere flickered.

New information flooded his mind, but it wasn't what he was looking for. It was something else. Something... personal.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Personal File - Zhang Wei]
[Status: Incomplete]
[Note: This record is flagged for administrative review]

Zhang frowned. His personal file was incomplete? That didn't make sense. Every soul in the system had a complete file—birth, life, death, karmic balance, reincarnation queue. It was basic celestial bookkeeping.

He accessed the file and felt his blood run cold.

His file was missing entire sections. Not just missing—erased. Someone had gone through and systematically deleted large portions of his life record. His childhood was there, but patchy. His adult years were almost completely gone. And his death...

His death was listed as: [CAUSE: Administrative Termination - No Further Details]

No further details.

Someone had covered this up. Not just Supervisor Hu—someone higher. Someone who had access to the core administrative system and the power to delete records from reality itself.

Zhang's hands trembled. He was dead because of someone's mistake, and that someone had the power to bury the evidence so deep that even the system couldn't find it.

But the system had let him see this. Why?

He looked at the sphere more closely. The gears were moving in a pattern he hadn't noticed before—a pattern that looked almost like... a message.

[YOU ARE NOT THE FIRST]

Zhang's breath caught. "What?"

[OTHERS HAVE COME BEFORE YOU]
[OTHERS HAVE BEEN DELETED]
[YOU ARE THE LAST]

"Last? Last of what?"

[THE SYSTEM HAS A FLAW]
[THE FLAW HAS A NAME]
[THE NAME IS...]

The message cut off. The sphere's gears locked into place, and a new presence appeared in the void—not cold and calculating like Guanyin, but hot and furious.

"Oh no," Zhang whispered.

A door materialized in front of him. It was made of solid gold and covered in official seals, each one stamped with the authority of a celestial department. The door swung open, and standing on the other side was a man who radiated such intense bureaucratic energy that the air around him seemed to shimmer with spreadsheets.

He wore a modern business suit, perfectly pressed, with a name tag that read: "Li Wei - Director of Departmental Compliance, Celestial Audit Bureau."

Director Li smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Zhang Wei. We need to talk about your recent system activity."

"I can explain—"

"I'm sure you can. But first, I should inform you that your actions have triggered Protocol 7-9-2-B: Immediate Soul Containment."

"Containment? That sounds permanent."

"It is." Director Li stepped through the door, and the void behind him filled with dozens of figures—celestial auditors, each one holding a scroll of official judgment. "By the authority vested in me by the Celestial Audit Bureau, I hereby place you under arrest for the following crimes: unauthorized system access, deliberate manipulation of administrative functions, improper reassignment of a Tier 17 Customer Service Representative, and possession of a smile that suggests you know something you shouldn't."

Zhang's smile widened. "That's a crime?"

"It is in this department."

The auditors closed in. Zhang looked at the sphere one last time, at the unfinished message still frozen in its gears.

[THE NAME IS...]

He didn't know what the system was trying to tell him. But he knew one thing: he wasn't going to let them contain him without a fight.

Xuan Wu's teachings echoed in his mind: *Sometimes the best defense is a really, really stupid offense.*

Zhang raised his hand and accessed the one function he had left—the one he'd been saving for a true emergency.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Function Activated]
[Function: Administrative Chaos Protocol - Unstable Reality Subroutine]
[Warning: This function has no defined outcome]
[Consequences: Unknown]
[Activation: Confirmed]

The sphere exploded.

Reality shattered like glass, and Zhang Wei fell into the space between dimensions, surrounded by the screaming of a thousand bureaucrats and the sound of paperwork being violently shredded.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: You have entered: Undefined Reality State]
[Status: System Connection Unstable]
[New Objective: Survive]

And somewhere, in the depths of the celestial bureaucracy, a file marked [REDACTED] opened for the first time in a thousand years.

A voice spoke from within the file. It was old. Tired. And very, very amused.

"Finally. Someone who knows how to break things."

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Assistant Who Wasn't There

Zhang stared at the alert, his heart hammering against his ribs. *Investigate and*—the rest of the notification cut off, replaced by spinning runes. Loading. The system was buffering.

*That's not supposed to happen.*

He pulled his hand back from the sphere, but the damage was done. Somewhere in the celestial bureaucracy, an alarm was probably blaring. Supervisor Hu would be notified. The Department of Internal Investigations would be dispatched.

Zhang Wei, newly minted Senior Clerk (Provisional), was about to become a ghost in the most literal sense.

He turned to flee, but the white void had no exits. No doors. No windows. Just the sphere and the endless nothing.

*Think, Zhang. You're a programmer. You've handled worse crashes.*

He called up his interface. The system menu flickered, icons dancing like dying fireflies. His access level blinked between Tier 2 and [ACCESS REVOKED] in rapid succession.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Integrity Check Failed]
[Source: Core Administrative Interface]
[Recommendation: Report to Department of Standards for Re-education]

Re-education. That was celestial-speak for soul-melting.

"No thanks," Zhang muttered.

He jabbed at the sphere again, fingers flying across its surface. Runes glowed under his touch, responding to commands he didn't fully understand but intuited through sheer desperation. The code of reality wasn't that different from the code of a badly designed mobile app—full of redundancies, backdoors, and features that didn't work as intended.

*There.*

A subroutine. Hidden in the administrative protocols. Someone had written a failsafe into the system—a way to bypass alerts if you knew the right sequence.

Zhang executed it.

The blinking stopped. The notifications vanished. The sphere pulsed once, then returned to its steady rhythm.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert Suppressed]
[Source: Subroutine 7-Alpha]
[User: Unknown]
[Note: You're welcome.]

Zhang blinked. *You're welcome?*

"Who wrote this?" he asked the empty void.

No answer. But somewhere in the depths of the system, a user with higher access than Zhang had just saved his soul.

He didn't have time to ponder it. The sphere was already showing him something new—a file that hadn't been there before. A file labeled: [CLERK 2319 - PERSONNEL RECORD].

The clerk responsible for his death.

Zhang opened it.

---

The record was sparse. Clerk ID 2319 had been assigned to the Department of Mortal Affairs for exactly three days before being transferred. Transfer reason: "Administrative Realignment." No forwarding department. No supervisor signature.

Just a blank space where a career should have been.

Zhang dug deeper. The system fought him, icons flickering with resistance, but the subroutine from earlier had opened doors he hadn't known existed. He found payroll records. Performance reviews. A single entry in a disciplinary log:

[INCIDENT REPORT: Clerk 2319 accessed Reincarnation Queue without authorization]
[Action Taken: Soul reassigned to probationary status]
[Approved By: Supervisor Hu]

*Hu.*

Zhang's blood ran cold. Supervisor Hu had known. He'd known about the unauthorized access. He'd known about the mistake. And instead of fixing it, he'd buried it.

But why? What reason would a celestial supervisor have to cover up a clerical error?

He scrolled further. There was more—a hidden log, encrypted with a cipher that even the subroutine couldn't crack. But the title was visible: [PROJECT: CULLING].

Zhang's finger hovered over it.

"Don't."

The voice came from behind him. Soft. Female. Familiar in a way that made his skin crawl.

Zhang spun around.

A woman stood in the white void, dressed in the same green robe as every other celestial clerk. But her face... her face was wrong. Not ugly, not deformed, but somehow *incomplete*. Like someone had forgotten to render the finer details.

"Who are you?" Zhang demanded.

"Someone who doesn't want you to die," she said. "Close the file."

"Why?"

"Because it's not ready yet." She stepped closer, and Zhang noticed her feet didn't quite touch the ground. "Project Culling is above your pay grade. Above almost everyone's pay grade."

"Then how do you know about it?"

She smiled. It was a hollow expression, devoid of warmth. "Because I wrote it."

Zhang's mind raced. Subroutines, hidden files, a mysterious woman who claimed authorship of a classified project. This wasn't just bureaucratic corruption anymore. This was something bigger.

"You're the one who saved me," he said. "The subroutine."

"You're quick. That's good." She circled him, and Zhang felt a sensation he couldn't quite identify—not cold, not pressure, but an absence of something. Like standing next to a black hole. "I've been watching you, Zhang Wei. Ever since you bypassed the standard orientation. Most souls accept their fate. They file their forms. They process their karma. They rot."

"But I didn't."

"No. You fought." She stopped in front of him. "And fighting is exactly what I need."

"Need for what?"

The woman's incomplete face shifted, features rearranging themselves into something resembling determination. "To fix a mistake. A mistake that's been festering for three thousand years."

Three thousand years. That was older than the current celestial administration. Older than most of the immortals in charge.

"What kind of mistake?"

"The kind that ends worlds."

She held out her hand. In her palm rested a jade token, identical to Zhang's but for one detail: where his had been blank, hers pulsed with a single character.

*Emperor.*

"The Heavenly Emperor is dead," she said. "Has been for millennia. And the bureaucracy is running on a corpse."

Zhang's breath caught. The Emperor? The supreme ruler of the celestial realm? The architect of the system itself?

*Dead?*

"That's impossible," he said. "I saw the Imperial Edicts. They're signed. They're enforced—"

"By algorithms," she interrupted. "By automated systems that haven't been updated since the Emperor's soul was shattered. The immortals don't know. They think they're serving a living ruler. But they're just puppets dancing on strings that have already been cut."

Zhang's mind reeled. This wasn't just corruption. This was the collapse of reality itself.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I can't fix it alone." Her hand closed around the token. "The system was designed to require two signatures for any change to the Imperial Seal. One from the Emperor. One from..." She hesitated. "One from his Assistant."

"You're the Assistant?"

"Was." Her voice cracked. "I was his Assistant. I watched him die. I watched the system erase him from history. And I've spent three thousand years hiding, waiting for someone who could navigate the bureaucracy well enough to help me restore what was lost."

She looked at him with those incomplete eyes. "You're that someone, Zhang Wei. A mortal who refused to die. A clerk who hacked the system. A soul that doesn't know when to quit."

Zhang wanted to laugh. Wanted to scream. Wanted to wake up in his cramped apartment and realize this was all a fever dream.

But the jade token in his pocket felt real. The sphere pulsed with the weight of infinite lives. And the woman in front of him—this ghost of an Assistant—was the only person in the celestial realm who seemed to know what was actually going on.

"What do you need me to do?"

Her smile returned, and this time it reached her eyes. "First, survive. Supervisor Hu knows you accessed restricted files. He'll come for you. When he does—"

She vanished. The white void dissolved. Zhang found himself back in his cubicle, staring at a stack of reports that hadn't been there before.

And standing over him, face twisted with barely contained rage, was Supervisor Hu.

"Zhang Wei," Hu said, his voice dripping with false calm. "We need to talk."

Behind Hu, the cubicle walls shimmered. Through the translucent barriers, Zhang could see other clerks working, their faces blank, their movements mechanical. They were all staring at their screens, typing in perfect synchronization.

*They're being controlled.*

"You accessed records you shouldn't have," Hu continued. "I'm willing to overlook it—this once. In exchange, you'll sign a non-disclosure agreement and accept a transfer to a department where you can't cause any more trouble."

He placed a document on Zhang's desk. The heading read: [VOLUNTARY SOUL RELOCATION - HELL OF ETERNAL FILING].

Hell of Eternal Filing. A punishment worse than deletion. An eternity of paperwork, no breaks, no end, no hope.

Zhang looked at the document. Then up at Hu. Then past Hu, at the controlled clerks, the shimmering walls, the system that was running on a dead emperor's algorithms.

He thought about the woman. The Assistant. The promise of something bigger.

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said, his voice steady, "I decline."

Hu's face went pale. "What?"

"I said no." Zhang stood up, taller than he'd ever felt. "I'm not signing anything. I'm not transferring anywhere. And I'm definitely not letting you bury another mistake."

"You're making a grave error, Junior Clerk."

"Actually, I think I'm finally making the right choice." Zhang accessed his interface. The alert suppression subroutine was still active. He used it to open a new channel—one that bypassed Hu's authority entirely.

[TRANSMISSION INITIATED]
[TO: ALL CELESTIAL PERSONNEL - DEPARTMENT OF MORTAL AFFAIRS]
[FROM: Senior Clerk Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[MESSAGE: Supervisor Hu is compromised. Do not trust his orders. The system has been corrupted at the highest levels. I have proof.]

Hu's eyes went wide. "You wouldn't."

But Zhang had already hit send.

The cubicle erupted. Clerks stopped typing, their blank faces flickering with confusion. Alarms blared. The walls shimmered violently as the system tried to process the unauthorized broadcast.

And in the chaos, Zhang saw her again—the Assistant, standing in the corner, nodding once.

*Good,* she mouthed. *Now run.*

Zhang ran.

Behind him, Hu's voice rose above the alarm: "SEAL ALL EXITS! DETAIN CLERK ZHANG WEI!"

But Zhang was already at the stairwell, descending into the depths of the celestial bureaucracy, chasing a truth that could either save reality—or destroy it completely.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert Level - Maximum]
[Status: Rogue Agent]
[Target: Zhang Wei]
[Directive: Apprehend Immediately]
[Note: The Assistant was right. The Emperor is dead. And now, so are you.]

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Audit That Never Sleeps

Zhang yanked his hand back from the glowing sphere as if it had burned him. Too late. The system had already logged his intrusion. He could practically hear alarm bells ringing through the celestial network.

"Stupid, stupid, stupid," he muttered, pacing in the white void. "You've been here one day and you're already flagged for deletion."

But the panic lasted only seconds. Zhang forced himself to breathe, to think. The system had detected him accessing restricted records, sure. But it hadn't locked him out. It hadn't deleted his soul yet. That meant there was still time.

He looked at the sphere again. The gears continued to turn, the runes pulsed with steady light. It was waiting for him. Waiting to see what he would do next.

Zhang accessed his interface, pulling up the alert details.

[ALERT STATUS: PENDING INVESTIGATION]
[Assigned To: Celestial Compliance Division - Team 7]
[Estimated Response Time: 3-5 Business Days (Celestial Standard)]

He blinked. Three to five business days? That was practically an eternity in bureaucratic terms. It took the Heavenly Bureaucracy an average of six hours just to process a tea break request. A five-day response window meant the alert had been filed so deep in the system that no one would look at it until quarterly review.

Unless someone flagged it manually.

And only one person knew he'd been in the system: Supervisor Hu.

Zhang dismissed the void and returned to the physical world. He materialized back at his desk, exactly where he'd been before the jade token had whisked him away. His stack of forms was still there. His tea was still warm. And standing in front of his desk, arms crossed, face crimson with barely contained rage, was Supervisor Hu.

"You," Hu hissed through clenched teeth. "What did you do?"

Zhang pasted on his best confused expression. "Supervisor? I was just filing the Karma Adjustment Forms, like you asked."

"Don't play games with me, Zhang Wei." Hu slammed his hand on the desk, making the stack of papers jump. "I felt it. The system lurched. Something accessed the Core Administrative Interface from this terminal. And you're the only one in this department with a senior clerk access level."

"You gave me the token," Zhang said innocently. "I was just following protocol."

Hu's eye twitched. "I gave you the token to process forms. Not to hack into the celestial mainframe."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

They stared at each other. Hu's nostrils flared. His fists clenched and unclenched. For a moment, Zhang genuinely thought the supervisor might try to punch him—which would have been interesting, considering neither of them had physical bodies.

Then Hu's expression shifted. The anger didn't disappear, but it was joined by something else. Fear.

"Come with me," Hu said quietly. "Now."

He turned and walked toward the back of the office, past rows of cubicles where other clerks pretended not to watch. Zhang grabbed his token and followed.

Hu led him through a door Zhang hadn't noticed before—it was hidden between two filing cabinets, its surface covered in the same beige texture as the walls. The door opened into a narrow corridor that stretched impossibly far, its walls lined with portraits of stern-faced immortals.

They walked in silence for what felt like miles. The portraits seemed to watch them, their painted eyes following Zhang's movements. One of them—a woman with silver hair and a crown of stars—actually turned her head to track his progress.

Finally, Hu stopped at another door. This one was made of solid jade, etched with characters that glowed faintly. He pressed his palm against it, and the door dissolved into mist.

Beyond it was a room that looked nothing like the rest of the Heavenly Bureaucracy. It was small, cluttered, and personal. Shelves crammed with scrolls and books. A desk covered in half-empty tea cups and scattered notes. A window that showed not clouds or stars, but an endless expanse of churning numbers.

Hu's office. His real office.

"Sit," Hu said, gesturing to a chair that looked like it had been salvaged from a mortal-world dumpster.

Zhang sat. The chair creaked alarmingly.

Hu closed the door and leaned against his desk, rubbing his temples. When he spoke, his voice was tired.

"How much did you see?"

"The whole thing," Zhang said. "The error. The misrouted soul. The fact that my death was a clerical mistake that you covered up."

Hu closed his eyes. "I was afraid of that."

"You killed me, Supervisor. You and whoever else in this department screwed up Batch 374. And instead of fixing it, you just... let me die. Let me rot in the reincarnation queue until I was reassigned here."

"I didn't have a choice." Hu's voice cracked. "Do you think I wanted this? I've been a supervisor for four hundred years, Zhang Wei. Four hundred years of filing forms, processing destinies, watching mortals live and die. And in all that time, I've never made a mistake. Not one."

"Until me."

"Until you." Hu sat down heavily in his chair. "The error was mine. I was processing the reincarnation queue for the Eastern Province when my terminal glitched. It was a split-second—a single line of code that jumped from one entry to another. Your soul ID got swapped with someone else's. You were supposed to be reborn as a merchant's son in Hangzhou. Instead, you were flagged for immediate termination."

"And instead of reporting the error, you buried it."

"Report it?" Hu laughed bitterly. "And face the Celestial Compliance Division? Do you know what they do to supervisors who make errors? Demotion to the Hell of Administrative Purgatory for a minimum of one thousand years. And that's if they're merciful. I've seen clerks who made smaller mistakes get their souls unmade, wiped from existence entirely."

Zhang stared at him. "So you let an innocent man die to save your own skin."

"I didn't have a choice!"

"You keep saying that."

"Because it's true!" Hu slammed his fist on the desk, making the tea cups rattle. "The Heavenly Bureaucracy doesn't have mercy, Zhang. It has rules. Procedures. Consequences. If I had reported the error, I would have been destroyed. And you would still be dead. At least this way, you got a second chance."

"A second chance to be your office slave?"

"A second chance to exist!" Hu's voice echoed off the walls. "Do you know how many souls are deleted every day because of system errors? Thousands. Tens of thousands. They don't get a token. They don't get a desk. They just stop. Poof. Gone. I gave you a way to keep existing, and this is how you repay me?"

Zhang was silent. He wanted to be angry—every fiber of his being screamed at him to hate this man who had killed him and lied about it. But there was something in Hu's voice, something raw and broken, that made him pause.

"Who else knows?" Zhang asked.

Hu blinked. "What?"

"About the error. About my death. Who else knows?"

"Just me. And now you."

"So if I report this to the Celestial Compliance Division—"

"You'll destroy both of us." Hu leaned forward. "Think about it, Zhang. If you report the error, they'll investigate. They'll find out I covered it up. They'll unmake me. And then they'll look at you. A soul that was supposed to be deleted, now running around with senior clerk privileges? They'll delete you too. Just to be thorough."

Zhang's jaw tightened. "So I'm trapped."

"We're trapped. Together." Hu sighed. "That's why I brought you here. To make you understand. We're in this mess together, Zhang. If you go down, I go down. And if I go down, I'm taking you with me."

The two men stared at each other. The silence stretched until it was broken by a chime from Hu's terminal.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Urgent Notification]
[Source: Celestial Compliance Division - Team 7]
[Subject: Investigation Request - Soul ID 7845-B]
[Status: Expedited]
[Note: Investigation team has been dispatched. ETA: 15 minutes (Celestial Standard)]

Hu's face went pale. "That's impossible. They never respond this fast."

"Unless someone flagged it manually," Zhang said slowly.

"Who would—" Hu stopped. His eyes widened. "The other supervisors."

"The other supervisors?"

"Department of Mortal Affairs has five supervisors, including me. We share resources. We share oversight. If one of us makes a mistake, the others can use it to climb the ladder." Hu's voice dropped to a whisper. "Someone in this department is trying to take me down."

The terminal chimed again.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Investigation Team 7 has arrived]
[Location: Administrative Hub - Floor 3]
[Requested Personnel: Supervisor Hu, Clerk Zhang Wei]
[Compliance Required: Immediate]

Hu grabbed Zhang's arm. "We need to hide you."

"Hide me? Where?"

"I know a place. A sub-basement level that's been sealed since the Third Celestial War. No one goes there. No one even remembers it exists."

"And what about you?"

Hu's face hardened. "I'll stall them. Give you time to get away."

"Why would you do that?"

"Because if they find you, they'll find the records. And if they find the records, they'll find the error. And if they find the error, we're both dead." Hu pushed a small jade token into Zhang's hand. "This will open the door to the sub-basement. Don't come out until I send for you. And Zhang?"

"What?"

"Whatever you do, don't use the Core Administrative Interface again. They'll be watching for it."

Zhang looked at the token. It was identical to the one Hu had given him earlier, except this one pulsed with a dim red light. Warning. Danger.

"Go," Hu said. "Now."

Zhang turned and ran.

The corridor stretched before him, the portraits watching his desperate flight. He could hear footsteps behind him—heavy, rhythmic, the march of celestial investigators in full compliance gear.

He reached the main office. Clerks stared at him as he sprinted past, knocking over a stack of forms, nearly colliding with a floating tea cart. The exit door was ahead, the one marked with the symbol he'd learned to recognize: [Authorized Personnel Only].

He slapped the jade token against the door. It hissed open.

Zhang lunged through—

And stopped.

The sub-basement wasn't an abandoned storage room.

It was a server farm.

Endless rows of crystalline data towers stretched in every direction, their surfaces etched with glowing runes that pulsed in complex patterns. The air hummed with the sound of celestial computation. And in the center of it all, suspended from the ceiling by chains of pure light, was a figure.

A woman.

She was beautiful in the way that storms were beautiful—terrifying and awe-inspiring. Her hair was white, her eyes were gold, and her body was covered in scars that looked like they had been carved by lightning.

She smiled when she saw Zhang.

"Finally," she said, her voice resonating through the server farm. "A visitor."

Zhang's token pulsed in his hand, and a new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Entity Detected]
[Classification: Prisoner - Tier 1 Celestial]
[Name: Bai Yuying - The Written One]
[Status: Sealed]
[Warning: Do Not

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Immortal Who Knew Too Much

Zhang stared at the alert. *Medium*. Not high. That meant the system hadn't fully flagged him as a threat yet. It was suspicious, but not actionable. He had time.

He pulled up the code for the alert system and quickly located the trigger thresholds. Medium meant manual review within seventy-two hours. High meant immediate lockdown and soul extraction. He needed to stay below that line.

The sphere pulsed beneath his fingers, and Zhang felt the weight of his new permissions. Tier 2. Senior Clerk. It was a promotion, technically, but it still put him below every actual immortal in the hierarchy. He needed more.

He started digging through the administrative interface, searching for anything that could help him understand the system's architecture. The code was ancient, layered like sedimentary rock. Each dynasty of immortals had added their own patches, their own workarounds, their own buried shortcuts.

And buried deep in the core, Zhang found something unexpected.

[SYSTEM LOG: Administrative Purge - Era 47, Cycle 12]
[Subject: Department of Mortal Affairs - Clerk ID 0001]
[Charge: Unauthorized System Access]
[Result: Soul Dissolution]
[Note: All records of this entity have been expunged from celestial history]

Clerk ID 0001. The very first clerk. Someone who had been there since the beginning. And they had been deleted for doing exactly what Zhang was doing now.

He checked the date. Era 47, Cycle 12. That was... recently. By celestial standards, anyway. Just over three thousand years ago.

Zhang pulled up the remnants of that clerk's access logs. Most of it was corrupted, wiped clean by the purge, but fragments remained. Scraps of data that had survived because they were stored in places the system didn't know to check.

One fragment caught his attention.

[FRAGMENT: Personal Note - Clerk ID 0001]
[Content: I found the truth. The system isn't broken. It was designed this way. Every error, every mistake, every "bug" — they're features. The immortals aren't incompetent. They're complicit. Someone at the top is controlling everything, and they've been doing it since the beginning. I don't know who. I don't know why. But I'm going to find out before they find me.]

The fragment ended there. Zhang felt cold despite the white void surrounding him.

Someone at the top. Controlling everything.

He thought about Supervisor Hu, about the administrative error that killed him, about the system's convenient failure to correct it. He thought about the immortals who seemed to breeze through their duties while the clerks struggled with broken tools and nonsensical procedures.

What if it wasn't incompetence? What if it was intentional?

A new message appeared in his interface.

[INCOMING COMMUNICATION]
[Source: Unknown]
[Classification: Encrypted]
[Warning: This message has bypassed standard celestial security protocols]

Zhang hesitated. Bypassed security. That meant someone else in the system had access they shouldn't have. Someone who knew how to hide their tracks.

He opened the message.

[TO: Soul ID 7845-B - Zhang Wei]
[FROM: A friend]
[SUBJECT: You're not the first]

I've been watching you since you bypassed the Reincarnation Queue. You move like someone who's done this before. But you haven't, have you? This is all new to you. And yet, you're already poking at things that took me centuries to find.

The immortal who killed Clerk ID 0001 is still out there. Still running the show. They've changed their name, their face, their identity in the system at least a dozen times since then. But I know who they are.

They're in your department.

Not Hu. Someone higher. Someone who has been watching you since your "accidental" death. Someone who has been waiting to see if you would do exactly what you just did.

The message is being monitored as I write this. I can't stay in contact for long. But I want you to know: you have allies. People who have been fighting this system for longer than you've been alive. People who know what's really going on.

If you want to survive, find the Ghost of Clerk 47. They'll find you.

And Zhang? Don't trust anyone. Not even yourself. The system has ways of making you forget what you've learned.

[END MESSAGE]

The message dissolved into static as soon as Zhang finished reading it, leaving no trace in the system logs. Whoever had sent it knew what they were doing.

Zhang's mind raced. An immortal in his department? Someone who had been watching him? He thought about every interaction he'd had since arriving in the Celestial Bureaucracy. The clerk who processed his orientation. The supervisor who assigned his desk. The immortal who had walked past him in the hallway without a second glance.

Any of them could be the one.

And the Ghost of Clerk 47? A reference, presumably, to Clerk ID 0001. But why the number 47? Unless...

Zhang pulled up the old organizational charts from Era 47. There it was: Department of Mortal Affairs had been split into forty-seven divisions at the time. Clerk ID 0001 had been assigned to Division 47. The Ghost of Clerk 47 was a fragment of that immortal's consciousness that had somehow survived the purge.

He needed to find it.

But first, he needed to get back to his desk before someone noticed he was gone. He withdrew his hand from the sphere, and the white void dissolved around him. He was back in the administrative interface, standing in front of the jade token.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Terminated]
[Session Duration: 4.7 seconds]
[Data Transferred: Minimal]

Four point seven seconds. That was fine. Anyone monitoring would just see a routine check of his permissions. Nothing suspicious.

Zhang pocketed the token and walked back toward the main office. The hallway was empty, which was unusual. There were always clerks shuffling between departments, immortals floating past with important-looking scrolls. But now, silence.

He rounded a corner and stopped.

Supervisor Hu was standing in the middle of the hallway, arms crossed, staring directly at Zhang.

"Where have you been, Clerk Zhang?"

Zhang's mind went into overdrive. He needed an excuse. Something mundane. Something that wouldn't raise suspicion.

"Bathroom break, Supervisor Hu. The facilities on this floor are terrible, by the way. I had to go up two levels to find one that worked."

Hu's eyes narrowed. "I see. And your jade token?"

"My what?"

"The jade token that was assigned to you during orientation. The one that all clerks are required to carry at all times."

Zhang patted his pockets, playing dumb. "I must have left it on my desk. I was in a hurry."

"I see." Hu's voice was flat, unreadable. "Well, I suggest you retrieve it immediately. There have been some... irregularities in the system today. Unauthorized access attempts. The Celestial Security Division is conducting an investigation."

Zhang's heart skipped a beat. They knew. Or at least, they suspected.

"I'll get right on that, Supervisor Hu."

He started to move past Hu, but the supervisor's hand shot out and grabbed his arm. The grip was surprisingly strong for someone who looked like a middle-aged accountant.

"Clerk Zhang," Hu said, his voice dropping to a whisper. "I know what you did."

Zhang froze. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"The token. The bypass. The access to the Core Administrative Interface." Hu's eyes bored into him. "I've been watching you since the moment you arrived. I knew you would find it. I was hoping you would be smarter about it."

Zhang's mind raced. Hu was the immortal in his department. Hu was the one who had been waiting for him.

"You're the one," Zhang said. "The immortal who killed Clerk ID 0001."

Hu's expression flickered. For a split second, something like surprise crossed his face. Then it was gone, replaced by cold calculation.

"Impressive," Hu said. "You found that faster than I expected. But you're wrong about one thing."

"What's that?"

"I'm not the immortal who killed Clerk ID 0001." Hu leaned in close, his voice barely audible. "I'm the one who helped them survive."

Zhang's world tilted. "Survive? But the records say—"

"The records say what the system wants them to say. Clerk ID 0001 is still alive. Hidden. Waiting." Hu released Zhang's arm. "And they sent me to find you."

"Why?"

"Because you're the first person in three thousand years who has the potential to actually change things. The first person who might be able to break the cycle." Hu stepped back. "But you need to be more careful. The system is watching you now. Every move you make, every file you access, every thought you have—it's all being recorded."

"How do I stop it?"

"You don't. But you can learn to hide your tracks." Hu reached into his robe and pulled out a small, black jade token. It was identical to Zhang's, except for a single character carved into its surface: 47.

"Take this. It's a ghost token. It will let you access the system without leaving a trace. Use it wisely."

Zhang took the token. It was warm to the touch, pulsing with a faint energy.

"What's the catch?"

"The catch is that once you start using it, you can never go back. The system will know something is wrong, even if it can't trace it to you. It will start watching everyone in your department. Your coworkers. Your friends. Anyone you've ever interacted with."

Zhang thought about the other clerks in his division. The nervous woman who spilled tea on his desk. The old man who kept falling asleep during meetings. The young clerk who was always asking for help with the filing system.

They had nothing to do with this. They didn't deserve to be caught in the crossfire.

"I can't," Zhang said. "I can't risk them."

Hu's expression softened. "Then you've already learned the first lesson. The system wants you to care about nothing but yourself. That's how it controls you. But if you care about others, if you're willing to sacrifice for them, then you're already stronger than the immortals who run this place."

He turned and started walking away.

"Wait," Zhang called out. "Where do I find the Ghost of Clerk 47?"

Hu paused without turning around. "You already have. The ghost token in your hand is their essence. They've been waiting for you."

And then Hu vanished, dissolving into golden light like a morning mist.

Zhang stood alone in the hallway, clutching the black jade token. It pulsed once, twice, and then a voice echoed in his mind.

*Hello, Zhang Wei. I've been waiting a long time to meet you.*

He looked down at the token. The character 47 glowed softly.

*We have much to discuss. But first, you need to survive the next seventy-two hours. The system's investigation is already underway. They're going to find you unless we act fast.*

Zhang's grip tightened on the token. "What do I need to do?"

*First, get back to your desk and act normal. Second, trust no one. And third...*

The voice paused.

*...be ready for what comes next. Because once we start this, there's no going back.*

Zhang nodded and slipped the token into his pocket next to the jade one. Two tokens. Two paths. One future.

He walked back to his desk, sat down, and started filing reports like nothing had happened.

But inside, his mind was burning

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Nine-Tailed Audit

Zhang Wei's fingers were still touching the glowing sphere when the void around him shattered like glass.

The Core Administrative Interface dissolved into streams of golden light, and he found himself hurtling backward through the celestial bureaucracy's digital backbone. Numbers and names and destinies flashed past him—a merchant in Guangzhou who would accidentally invent bubble tea in three centuries, a poet in the Tang Dynasty whose work would be lost to time, a minor god of harvest who was currently embezzling worship credits from a village in Shandong.

Then he slammed back into his body.

Zhang gasped, his eyes snapping open. He was still sitting at his desk in the Department of Mortal Affairs, still surrounded by mountains of jade scrolls and glowing paperwork. The only difference was the notification hovering in his peripheral vision, pulsing with an angry red light.

[WARNING: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Your soul has been flagged for Priority Review]
[Disciplinary Hearing Scheduled: 3 Hours]
[Location: Hall of Celestial Justice, Floor 777]

Three hours. That was either very generous or very ominous, depending on how you looked at it. In Zhang's experience, when bureaucracy gave you time to prepare, it usually meant they were giving you time to panic.

"Zhang Wei!"

He looked up to find Supervisor Hu looming over his desk, his face the color of a ripe tomato. The man's mustache quivered with barely contained rage.

"I just received a Priority Alert about your soul being flagged for system tampering," Hu hissed, his voice low enough that the other clerks couldn't hear. "What did you do?"

Zhang blinked innocently. "Nothing, Supervisor. I was just filing reports."

"Don't lie to me! The system doesn't flag souls for 'filing reports'!"

"I don't know what to tell you." Zhang shrugged. "Maybe it's a glitch? You know how the Celestial Management System has been acting up lately."

Hu's eye twitched. "The system doesn't have glitches."

"Are you sure? Because I heard that the Department of Fate Calculation had a bug last week that accidentally made a chicken in Sichuan destined to become emperor for three hours."

"That was... that was a scheduled test!"

Zhang leaned back in his chair, folding his arms. "Supervisor, I think we both know that wasn't a test. Just like I think we both know that my death wasn't exactly... intentional."

The color drained from Hu's face. For a moment, Zhang saw something flash in the man's eyes—fear. Real, genuine fear.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Hu said, but his voice cracked on the last word.

"Sure you don't." Zhang smiled. "Don't worry, Supervisor. I'm sure everything will come out in my hearing. I'm sure the Celestial Justice Department will be very interested in hearing about Batch 374."

Hu's hand shot out and grabbed Zhang's collar, yanking him halfway across the desk. Jade scrolls scattered everywhere, and the other clerks turned to stare.

"Listen to me, you insufferable mortal," Hu snarled, his face inches from Zhang's. "You don't know what you're dealing with. The Heavenly Bureaucracy has been running for ten thousand years. Do you really think a dead IT technician from the 21st century can just waltz in and expose its secrets?"

Zhang met his gaze without flinching. "I'm not trying to expose secrets. I'm trying to fix a mistake. My mistake. My death."

"And you think the system cares about your mistake?" Hu laughed, but there was no humor in it. "Do you know how many souls get misrouted every day? Thousands. Do you know how many get corrected? Almost none. Because the system is too large, too complex, too broken to care about individual cases."

"Then why are you so scared?"

Hu's grip tightened. "Because if you start digging, you'll find things that even I don't want to know about. And when you do, they'll delete you. Not just your soul—your existence. Every trace of you, erased from every timeline, every dimension, every possibility. You'll become a paradox, a hole in reality that never existed."

"That sounds like a threat."

"It's a promise." Hu released him, stepping back. "Go to your hearing. Tell them it was a mistake, that you accessed the Core Interface by accident. You'll get demoted to the Filing Department of Forgotten Prayers, but at least you'll still exist."

"And if I don't?"

Hu's eyes hardened. "Then you'll learn why even the gods fear the Celestial Justice Department."

He turned and walked away, leaving Zhang standing among scattered scrolls with the entire office staring at him.

Zhang straightened his robe. "Show's over," he announced. "Back to work, everyone. Those filing errors aren't going to correct themselves."

The clerks quickly averted their gazes, pretending they hadn't seen anything. Zhang sat back down, his mind racing.

Three hours. He had three hours to figure out who he could trust, what evidence he could gather, and how to survive an encounter with the most feared department in the Heavenly Bureaucracy.

He pulled out the jade token and activated the chat function. There was one person who might be able to help.

**Zhang Wei:** *Li Mei, you there?*

The response came almost immediately.

**Li Mei:** *Zhang Wei? I heard you got flagged. What did you DO?*

**Zhang Wei:** *Found out my death was a mistake. Accessed the Core Interface. Saw some things I shouldn't have.*

**Li Mei:** *...you're insane. You're actually insane.*

**Zhang Wei:** *Can you help me or not?*

There was a long pause. Zhang watched the cursor blink, wondering if he'd pushed too far.

**Li Mei:** *Meet me in the Hall of Celestial Justice. Floor 777. I know a back entrance.*

**Zhang Wei:** *You do?*

**Li Mei:** *I've been a clerk for 300 years. You learn things. Just... be careful. The Justice Department doesn't play by the same rules as the rest of the bureaucracy.*

**Zhang Wei:** *What does that mean?*

**Li Mei:** *It means they don't just judge souls. They judge gods. And when you judge gods, you have to be willing to do things that would make the demons of Hell blush.*

Zhang stared at the message, a chill running down his spine.

**Zhang Wei:** *See you in three hours.*

He closed the chat and stood up, grabbing his jade token. The other clerks pretended not to notice as he walked out of the office, but he could feel their eyes on his back.

The corridors of the Heavenly Bureaucracy stretched endlessly before him, a labyrinth of administrative departments and celestial offices. Zhang had been here for less than a week, but he'd already learned that the physical layout of the bureaucracy didn't make sense. Hallways led to places they shouldn't, doors opened into different dimensions, and sometimes the stairs just decided to not exist anymore.

But right now, he needed to find his way to Floor 777.

He pulled up the celestial map on his jade token, watching as the glowing lines rearranged themselves to show him a path. It was convoluted, winding through departments he'd never heard of—the Office of Unanswered Prayers, the Subdivision of Near Misses, the Archive of Almosts.

And then there was the Department of Cosmic Irony.

Zhang stopped in front of a door marked with a symbol that looked suspiciously like a laughing skull. The door was slightly ajar, and from inside, he could hear someone laughing.

Not a pleasant laugh. A laugh that sounded like glass breaking and hope dying.

He should have kept walking. He knew he should have kept walking. But something about the laugh drew him closer, and he found himself peering through the crack in the door.

Inside, a woman sat at a desk made of bones. She was beautiful in a way that hurt to look at—sharp features, eyes like frozen fire, hair that seemed to shift between colors. Nine fox tails swished behind her, each one tipped with a different elemental flame.

She was reading a scroll and laughing.

"Ah, another one," she said without looking up. "A man who dies in a car crash on his way to buy lottery tickets. The lottery ticket he was going to buy? The winning one. Worth 500 million yuan." She looked up, her eyes meeting Zhang's through the crack. "Would you like to see the punchline?"

Zhang's blood ran cold. "You can see me?"

"I can see everything." She set down the scroll, and her nine tails fanned out behind her. "I'm the Goddess of Cosmic Irony. It's my job to make sure the universe's jokes land properly. And you, Zhang Wei, are a very funny joke."

"I don't feel very funny."

"That's what makes it funny." She stood up, and the temperature in the room dropped. "You see, I've been watching you since you accessed the Core Interface. And I have to admit, I'm impressed. A mortal soul, hacking into the administrative system, exposing a cover-up that goes back centuries."

"Centuries?"

"Oh, yes." She walked around her desk, her heels clicking on the bone floor. "Supervisor Hu didn't just misroute your soul. He's been doing it for a hundred years. Thousands of souls, all redirected to feed his little side project."

"What side project?"

The goddess smiled, and it was the most terrifying expression Zhang had ever seen. "Wouldn't you like to know? But that information has a price. And I don't think you can afford it."

"Try me."

She laughed again, that horrible glass-breaking laugh. "Oh, I like you. You've got guts." She reached into her desk and pulled out a small, black token. "Take this. It's a one-time pass to the Justice Department's secure archives. It'll show you everything Hu has been hiding."

Zhang took the token cautiously. "Why are you helping me?"

"Helping you?" The goddess's eyes glittered. "I'm not helping you. I'm setting up the punchline. See, Hu thinks he's been getting away with something. The Justice Department thinks they're going to destroy you. But I know the truth."

"What truth?"

"That the universe loves irony more than it loves order." She leaned forward, her voice dropping to a whisper. "And the punchline of this joke is going to be absolutely delicious."

Zhang pocketed the token, his heart pounding. "I don't trust you."

"Good." The goddess sat back down, picking up her scroll. "If you trusted me, you'd be an idiot. And I don't help idiots. Now go. You have less than three hours, and the path to Floor 777 is longer than you think."

Zhang turned to leave, but her voice stopped him.

"Oh, and Zhang Wei? One more thing."

He looked back.

"When you get to the Justice Department, don't believe everything you see. The truth is there, but it's buried under layers of lies. And the person you trust most might be the one who betrays you."

"What does that mean?"

But the goddess had already turned back to her scroll, her nine tails swishing lazily behind her.

Zhang left the Department of Cosmic Irony with more questions than answers and a black token that felt heavier than it should have. Three hours. He had three hours to uncover a century of secrets, survive a celestial hearing, and figure out who was actually on his side.

And somewhere in the Hall of Celestial Justice, Li Mei was waiting for him.

The question was: whose side was she really on?

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Emperor's Personal Request

Zhang barely had time to register the alert before the white void around him flickered. The massive sphere of gears and runes shuddered, and a crack spiderwebbed across its surface like a breaking mirror.

**[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Administrative Core Integrity Compromised]**
**[Investigation Team ETA: 00:47:23]**

"Forty-seven minutes," Zhang muttered, already pulling up the interface. "That's either very generous or very terrifying."

He didn't wait to find out which.

His fingers flew across the floating runes, tracing patterns that he shouldn't have known. But somehow, the system's logic made sense to him—like a programming language he'd studied in a past life. Which, technically, he supposed he had. Every mortal who'd ever worked a mind-numbing office job understood the fundamental truth of bureaucracy: it was just code with extra steps.

The alert notification blinked in the corner of his vision. He needed to hide his tracks before the investigation team arrived. But as he tried to access the log deletion protocols, something strange happened.

The system refused his command.

**[ERROR: Insufficient Clearance Level]**
**[Required: Tier 5 - Department Head]**
**[Current: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)]**

"Of course." Zhang clenched his jaw. "Because why would the system let me clean up my own mess?"

He needed a promotion. Fast. And there was only one way to get one in the Celestial Bureaucracy: complete an impossible task that no one else wanted to touch.

His eyes scanned the active quest list floating in the corner of his vision. Most were standard fare—processing karma disputes, adjusting destiny threads, filing quarterly reports. But one quest caught his attention, buried at the very bottom of the list, marked with a glowing red skull icon.

**[QUEST: The Immortal Convergence Protocol]**
**[Difficulty: ???]**
**[Reward: Automatic Promotion to Tier 5 - Department Head]**
**[Risk: Permanent Soul Erasure]**
**[Description: The Jade Emperor's personal astrologer has detected a convergence of immortal energies that threatens to destabilize the Heavenly Realm. All standard bureaucratic channels have failed to address the issue. A volunteer is needed to personally deliver a sealed message to the Southern Heavenly Gate and ensure its contents reach the correct recipient.]**
**[Note: Previous volunteers: 47. Survivors: 0.]**

Forty-seven dead immortals. Zero survivors.

Zhang stared at the quest for exactly three seconds before selecting [Accept].

The system paused, as if surprised anyone would actually take the bait.

**[QUEST ACCEPTED]**
**[Soul ID 7845-B has been flagged for Immortal Convergence Protocol]**
**[Warning: Your soul will be permanently erased upon death. No reincarnation. No after-afterlife. Just void.]**
**[Objective: Deliver Message Scroll to "The Guardian of the Third Gate" before the next celestial alignment (67 hours)]**

A scroll materialized in Zhang's hands—plain, unassuming, sealed with a stamp that seemed to shift colors every time he blinked. It hummed with power, and Zhang could feel the weight of countless destinies pressing against his consciousness.

"What have I gotten myself into?" he whispered.

"Something stupid."

Zhang spun around. Standing behind him, arms crossed, was the same jade-skinned woman he'd seen in the Hall of Records. Her name floated above her head in elegant script: **[Zhao Mingxia - Tier 4 - Senior Archivist]**

"How did you—" Zhang started.

"The system sent an alert to everyone above Tier 3 when you accessed the Core Interface." She raised an eyebrow. "You're not very subtle, are you?"

"I'm new."

"Clearly." She walked past him, examining the cracked sphere with professional interest. "You triggered a security breach anomaly. The investigation team will be here in thirty-eight minutes now. They move faster when they sense blood."

"And you're here to... what? Stop me? Turn me in?"

Zhao Mingxia laughed—a cold, musical sound. "If I wanted to turn you in, I would have brought the Heavenly Enforcement Bureau. No, I'm here because you just accepted the Impossible Quest."

"The Convergence Protocol."

"The Suicide Mission." She corrected. "Do you have any idea what that scroll does?"

"It delivers a message?"

"It delivers the *truth*." She met his eyes, and Zhang saw something ancient and tired in her gaze. "The Immortal Convergence isn't a random celestial event. It's a leak. The Heavenly Realm's power is bleeding into the mortal world, and if it isn't stopped, both realms will collapse."

"Why would the system hide that?"

"Because the person responsible for the leak is someone very powerful. Someone who doesn't want to be caught." She paused. "Someone who sits on a golden throne and calls himself the Son of Heaven."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "The Jade Emperor?"

"The Jade Emperor is a figurehead. A puppet." Zhao Mingxia's voice dropped to a whisper. "The true power behind the Heavenly Throne is his chief advisor, Minister Cai. And Minister Cai has been siphoning celestial energy for three thousand years to fuel his own ascension to godhood."

"And the message scroll?"

"Contains evidence that would expose him. But Minister Cai knows. He's been intercepting every attempt to deliver it for the past century. The forty-seven dead volunteers? He killed them. Personally."

Zhang looked down at the scroll in his hands. It felt heavier now. "So if I try to deliver this, I'll be hunted by the most powerful immortal in the Heavenly Realm."

"Correct."

"And if I don't deliver it, both realms collapse."

"Also correct."

"And if I try to expose Minister Cai through normal channels, he'll have me erased before I can file the first form."

"Now you're catching on." Zhao Mingxia smiled, but there was no humor in it. "Welcome to the real Celestial Bureaucracy, Zhang Wei. Where the paperwork is eternal, and the corruption goes all the way to the top."

Zhang was quiet for a long moment. Then he laughed.

"You know what? This is still better than my last job."

"Your last job?"

"I worked customer service for a telecom company. Trust me, dealing with an immortal tyrant is an upgrade."

Zhao Mingxia stared at him like he'd grown a second head. "You're insane."

"Probably." Zhang tucked the scroll into his robe. "But I'm also dead, so what do I have to lose? My soul? It was already on the chopping block. My afterlife? It was data entry. My dignity? Gone the moment I woke up in a celestial supply closet."

He turned to leave the white void, but Zhao Mingxia grabbed his arm.

"You'll die."

"Everyone dies." Zhang shrugged. "I've already done it once. I'm not afraid to do it again."

"It won't be like before. Soul erasure means you stop existing. No memories. No legacy. No chance at anything ever again."

Zhang considered this. Then he smiled.

"Then I'll just have to make sure I don't die."

He stepped out of the void and back into the main Heavenly Bureaucracy building. The halls were bustling with immortals and clerks, all going about their eternal duties, unaware that the entire system was about to collapse.

Zhang walked through the crowd, the scroll hidden beneath his robe. He had sixty-seven hours to deliver it. Sixty-seven hours to find this "Guardian of the Third Gate" and expose the biggest corruption scandal in celestial history.

Sixty-seven hours to hack his way to the top.

He was three steps out of the void when a hand clamped down on his shoulder.

"Zhang Wei."

He turned. Supervisor Hu stood behind him, flanked by two armored immortals with glowing spears.

"I've been looking for you."

Zhang kept his face neutral. "Supervisor Hu. What a pleasant surprise."

"Don't play games." Hu's eyes were hard. "I know you accessed restricted records. I know you triggered a security breach. And I know you just accepted the Convergence Protocol."

"So you're well-informed."

"I'm your supervisor. It's my job to know." Hu stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "And it's also my job to inform you that Minister Cai has taken a personal interest in your case."

Zhang's heart stopped.

"He wants to meet you."

"Me?"

"Personally." Hu's smile was cold. "Right now."

Before Zhang could respond, the two armored immortals grabbed his arms. The scroll pressed against his chest, hidden but not forgotten.

He had sixty-seven hours.

But apparently, Minister Cai had other plans.

**[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Objective Added]**
**[Audience with Minister Cai]**
**[Location: Celestial Palace - Eastern Wing]**
**[Time: Immediate]**
**[Warning: Hostile encounter detected. Survival probability: 3.7%]**
**[Recommendation: Pray]**

Zhang let the immortals drag him forward, his mind racing.

The scroll was still with him. The evidence was still intact. And he was about to walk into the lion's den.

He smiled.

"Perfect."

"Perfect?" Supervisor Hu looked at him like he'd lost his mind.

"I've been wanting to meet the guy who's been ruining my afterlife." Zhang's grin widened. "I have a few things I'd like to discuss with him."

"You're insane."

"So I've been told."

The doors to the Celestial Palace loomed before them, carved from pure jade and inlaid with golden runes. They swung open, revealing a throne room that stretched into infinity.

And at the center of it all, seated on a throne of condensed starlight, was Minister Cai.

He was beautiful in the way that predators were beautiful—sleek, dangerous, and utterly without mercy. His eyes were pools of liquid amber, and his smile was a knife's edge.

"Zhang Wei," he said, his voice echoing through the hall. "I've heard so much about you."

Zhang bowed, keeping his head low to hide his grin.

"The pleasure is all mine, Minister."

It wasn't pleasure.

It was opportunity.

**[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Hidden Objective Discovered]**
**[Objective: Survive the audience with Minister Cai]**
**[Bonus Objective: Plant the evidence without being detected]**
**[WARNING: Failure will result in immediate soul erasure]**

Zhang's hand brushed against the scroll.

Sixty-seven hours.

He didn't have that long anymore.

But maybe he didn't need it.

Maybe all he needed was one good shot.

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The System Administrator's Lunch Break

Zhang Wei stared at the alert notification, his hand still touching the massive sphere of interlocking gears and runes. The white void around him rippled like disturbed water, and he felt the system's attention turn toward him—not the vague monitoring he'd been warned about, but something more direct. Something hungry.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized Access Detected]
