[Countermeasures: Activating]
[Estimated Time to Soul Deletion: 3... 2...]

Zhang yanked his hand back, but the damage was done. Lines of red code began crawling across the sphere's surface like angry veins, spreading toward the point where his fingers had rested. The white void darkened at the edges, reality compressing inward.

"No, no, no," Zhang muttered, scrambling backward. "I just got here. I haven't even stolen any office supplies yet."

His jade token pulsed with heat against his chest. He grabbed it, desperate, and a new option appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Emergency Protocol Initiated]
[Source: Soul ID 7845-B]
[Action: Requesting Administrative Debug Access]
[Warning: This action will reset current session and delete all unsaved modifications]
[Confirm? Y/N]

The red code was spreading faster now, the void collapsing like a dying star. Zhang could feel his soul starting to unravel, individual memories peeling away like pages from a book. He saw his mother's face flicker and fade, then his childhood home, then the taste of his favorite noodles from the street vendor near his apartment.

"Confirm!" Zhang shouted.

The world went white.

Then gray.

Then beige.

Zhang opened his eyes to find himself sitting at his desk in the Celestial Bureaucracy's Department of Mortal Affairs. The same beige cubicle walls. The same flickering fluorescent lights. The same stack of pending destiny reports that seemed to regenerate overnight like a particularly aggressive fungus.

His jade token was cold against his chest. No notifications. No alerts. No evidence that he had just nearly had his soul deleted for poking around where he shouldn't have.

But Zhang remembered everything.

He remembered the sphere of gears and runes. He remembered the file showing Supervisor Hu's involvement in his death. He remembered the red code crawling toward him like a predator stalking its prey.

And he remembered the [REDACTED] function he'd seen before the system tried to eat him.

"What the hell was that?" Zhang whispered, his hands trembling slightly. He pressed them flat against his desk to steady himself. "I almost died. Again. In the afterlife. That's a special kind of cosmic irony."

A shadow fell across his desk.

"Zhang Wei."

Supervisor Hu stood there, his round face twisted into something between a scowl and a smirk. He held a clipboard—because of course he did—and tapped it with a pen that seemed to radiate smugness.

"I see you've returned from your... break," Hu said, the pause in his words dripping with insinuation.

Zhang forced his face into a mask of innocent confusion. "Break? Supervisor, I've been here all morning. Filing destiny reports. Processing karma disputes. Being yelled at by angry gods. The usual."

Hu's eyes narrowed. "The system registered a brief disconnection from your soul ID approximately seven minutes ago. Care to explain?"

Seven minutes. That's how long he'd been in the white void. Seven minutes of nearly getting his existence erased.

"Must be a glitch," Zhang said, spreading his hands. "You know how the Celestial Management System is. Ever since the Great Server Migration of 1823, it's been acting up. I heard the Heaven's Equal Department still can't access their spreadsheets on Tuesdays."

Hu's grip on his clipboard tightened until the plastic creaked. "The system does not glitch, Clerk Zhang. It is a perfect, divine creation maintained by the most exalted programmers in the celestial hierarchy."

"That's what they said about Windows Vista too," Zhang muttered.

"What was that?"

"Nothing, Supervisor. Just agreeing with your profound wisdom."

Hu stared at him for a long moment, and Zhang felt like a bug under a magnifying glass. But then the tension broke, and Hu's expression shifted to something almost pleasant. Which was somehow more terrifying than his usual hostility.

"Actually, Zhang Wei, I have good news," Hu said, his voice dripping with false warmth. "Your performance has been noticed by upper management. They've decided to reward your efficiency with a special assignment."

Zhang's internal alarm bells started ringing so loudly he was surprised the entire department couldn't hear them. "A special assignment? For me? I'm honored, Supervisor, but I really should get back to these destiny reports. The Lady of Unfortunate Love is waiting on her latest batch of soulmate pairings, and you know how she gets when she's impatient."

"More than you could possibly imagine," Hu said, his smile never wavering. "But this takes priority. You've been assigned to assist in the annual Karmic Audit of the Heavenly Archives."

The Heavenly Archives. Zhang had heard rumors about that place. It was where the Celestial Bureaucracy stored the complete records of every soul that had ever existed, every action ever taken, every butterfly wing that had ever flapped and caused a hurricane three continents away. It was also, supposedly, where they kept the really classified stuff—the things that even the immortals weren't supposed to know.

And Hu was sending him there.

"Why me?" Zhang asked, unable to keep the suspicion out of his voice.

Hu's smile widened. "Because you're expendable."

Then he turned and walked away, leaving Zhang alone with his racing thoughts and a new notification that had appeared on his jade token.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Assigned]
[Title: The Heavenly Archives Audit]
[Description: Report to the Department of Celestial Records on Floor 108 for your new temporary assignment. Assist the audit team in reviewing and verifying the contents of the Heavenly Archives.
Warning: The Heavenly Archives contain sensitive information. Unauthorized access to classified records will result in immediate disciplinary action, including but not limited to: soul fragmentation, memory erasure, and reassignment to the Hell of Perpetual Paperwork.
Recommended Level: Tier 3 - Senior Clerk
Your Level: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)
Difficulty: Extreme]
[Reward: ???]
[Failure Condition: Death of the Soul]

Zhang stared at the notification for a long moment. Then he laughed.

Not because it was funny—it wasn't. The failure condition alone was enough to make him want to run back to his cubicle and hide under his desk. But because it was so absurd. He'd been dead for less than a week, and already he'd been killed by a bureaucratic error, promoted to a job he didn't understand, almost soul-deleted by the system, and now sent on a suicide mission to the most dangerous archive in existence.

"I should have asked for reincarnation as a house cat," Zhang muttered, grabbing his jade token and standing up. "At least cats get to sleep all day."

The journey to Floor 108 took longer than expected. The Celestial Bureaucracy's headquarters was a building that defied both physics and common sense—it stretched upward into infinity, with floors that didn't always connect to each other in logical ways. Zhang had to take three different elevators, walk through a hallway that seemed to loop back on itself twice, and bribe a minor functionary with a packet of instant noodles (mortal comforts were surprisingly valuable in the bureaucracy) before he finally reached the Department of Celestial Records.

The department looked exactly like what he expected after seeing the rest of the building: beige cubicles, flickering lights, and clerks hunched over desks with the thousand-yard stare of people who had been filing paperwork for centuries. But there was one difference.

The ceiling was made of stars.

Zhang looked up and saw galaxies swirling overhead, nebulas forming and dissolving in slow motion, comets trailing across the darkness. It was beautiful. It was terrifying. It was the most celestial thing he'd seen since arriving in the afterlife.

"New clerk?"

A voice startled him out of his reverie. Zhang turned to find a woman standing beside him, her appearance so unremarkable that he almost didn't notice her. She had brown hair pulled back in a practical bun, wore the same beige robes as everyone else, and held a tablet that glowed with an ethereal light.

"You're the one from Mortal Affairs," she said, her tone flat. "Supervisor Hu sent word. I'm Li Mei, Senior Clerk of the Archives Division. You'll be working with me."

Zhang bowed slightly, unsure of the proper protocol. "Zhang Wei. I'm... honored?"

"Don't be. It's a punishment assignment." Li Mei turned and started walking, not bothering to check if he was following. "Hu wants you out of his department. He's been trying to get rid of you since you arrived."

Zhang hurried to catch up. "Wait, you know about that?"

"I know everything that happens in this building." Li Mei's voice was still flat, but there was something sharp underneath it. "I'm the one who processes the interdepartmental transfer requests. I saw yours come through five minutes after your soul was registered. Hu marked it as 'urgent' and 'high priority'."

"That bastard," Zhang muttered.

"He's an asshole," Li Mei agreed. "But he's also untouchable. His uncle is the Deputy Director of the Department of Divine Justice. So unless you have some dirt on him that could get him demoted, you're stuck."

Zhang's hand instinctively went to his jade token. The file. The administrative error. The records proving Hu had killed him.

"I might have something," he said carefully.

Li Mei stopped walking and turned to face him, her eyes suddenly sharp and focused. "What did you find?"

Before Zhang could answer, a massive crash echoed through the department, followed by a scream of pure terror. The clerks around them looked up from their desks, their expressions ranging from mild curiosity to deep concern.

"What was that?" Zhang asked.

Li Mei's face had gone pale. "The Archives. Something's gotten in."

"Something? What do you mean, something?"

But Li Mei was already running, and Zhang had no choice but to follow. They sprinted through the department, past rows of cubicles and filing cabinets, until they reached a massive door made of what looked like solid jade. It was supposed to be sealed—Zhang could see the runes carved into its surface, glowing with protective energy—but it was cracked open, darkness seeping through the gap like smoke.

"That's impossible," Li Mei breathed. "The seals were renewed last month. Nothing should be able to break them."

The darkness pulsed, and Zhang heard something whisper his name.

Not aloud. Not in his ears. But inside his head, like a thought that wasn't his own.

*Zhang Wei. Help us.*

"What the hell?" he said, stumbling backward.

*We know what you're looking for. The truth. The error. The one who killed you.*

The darkness surged forward, and Zhang saw shapes moving inside it—humanoid figures, their features indistinct, their bodies made of shadow and starlight.

*We were like you once. Clerks. Paper pushers. Expendable.*

*Until we found what they hide in the Archives.*

*And they erased us from existence.*

*But not completely.*

*Help us, Zhang Wei. And we'll help you.*

Li Mei grabbed his arm, pulling him away from the door. "Don't listen to them. They're not souls anymore—they're echoes. Residual data. The system deleted them, but their memories survived in the Archives' code."

*She's lying,* the voices whispered. *She works for them. She'll report you to

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The Audit That Changed Everything

Zhang didn't even have time to process the warning before the white void around him began to dissolve. The massive sphere of gears and runes flickered, and he felt himself being pulled backward through the stream of celestial code—births, deaths, and destinies blurring past him like traffic on a highway.

He landed hard on the floor of his cubicle.

For a moment, he just lay there, staring at the ceiling tiles that seemed to shift and writhe with barely visible characters. His head throbbed. His soul ached. And somewhere in the back of his mind, a notification was blinking with increasing urgency.

[INCOMING TRANSMISSION: Department of Internal Investigations]

[Priority: Critical]

[Message: Your recent system access has been logged. Please remain at your workstation for a mandatory review. Failure to comply will result in immediate soul forfeiture.]

"Soul forfeiture," Zhang muttered, pulling himself to his feet. "Because that's a reasonable response to reading a file."

He brushed the dust off his robes and sat down at his desk. The stack of destiny reports had somehow grown in his absence—it now towered almost to the ceiling, leaning precariously to the left like the Tower of Pisa after a bad earthquake.

But Zhang didn't care about the reports anymore. He had the file. He had the proof. Supervisor Hu had killed him, and the system had covered it up.

The question was: what was he going to do about it?

Option one: Wait for Internal Investigations to arrive, explain the situation, and hope they believed a Tier 2 clerk over a department supervisor. Option two: Run. Option three: Use his new access level to bury the evidence and pretend nothing happened.

Option four: Do something stupid.

Zhang had always been good at option four.

He opened his terminal and began typing. Not destiny reports—those could rot. Instead, he accessed the Karmic Ledger, the master record of every action taken by every celestial employee since the beginning of time.

[ACCESSING: Karmic Ledger - Department of Mortal Affairs]

[Filter: Supervisor Hu - ID 2319]

[Results: 14,782 entries]

[Timeframe: Last 100 years]

Zhang's eyes widened. That was a lot of karma for one supervisor. He started scrolling through the entries, looking for anything unusual. Most of it was standard—destiny assignments, reincarnation approvals, minor karmic adjustments. But then he found it.

[Entry 7,403: Administrative Error - Soul ID 7845-B]

[Action: Misrouting of mortal soul during reincarnation processing]

[Resolution: Assigned to Supervisor Hu for correction]

[Status: Closed - Error Deemed Unfixable]

[Notes: Subject died at age 32 due to falling furniture. No reincarnation available. Recommend soul reclamation and reassignment to minor administrative role.]

Closed. Deemed unfixable. As if Zhang's death was just a paperwork problem that could be filed away and forgotten.

He kept scrolling.

[Entry 7,404: Administrative Error - Soul ID 7846-C]

[Action: Wrong baby assigned to wrong parents]

[Resolution: Assigned to Supervisor Hu for correction]

[Status: Closed - Error Deemed Acceptable]

[Notes: Subject lived an average life. No significant deviations from expected outcomes. Error costs deemed minimal.]

[Entry 7,405: Administrative Error - Soul ID 7847-A]

[Action: Soul assigned to wrong body]

[Resolution: Assigned to Supervisor Hu for correction]

[Status: Closed - Error Fixed by Reincarnation of Subject into Cat]

[Notes: Subject is currently living as a housecat in Beijing. Displays unusual intelligence. Recommend monitoring.]

Zhang blinked. Someone had been turned into a cat because of an administrative error. And the system had just... accepted it.

"How many?" he whispered.

He expanded the search.

[SEARCH: Administrative Errors - Supervisor Hu - ID 2319]

[Total: 472]

[Closed Without Resolution: 389]

[Closed With Partial Resolution: 58]

[Closed With Full Resolution: 25]

Three hundred and eighty-nine souls whose lives had been ruined because Supervisor Hu couldn't do his job properly. And all of them had been swept under the celestial rug.

Zhang's hands trembled as he stared at the numbers. He thought about the cat in Beijing, probably living its best nine lives without knowing it had once been a human. He thought about the babies swapped at birth, growing up with the wrong families, the wrong destinies, the wrong everything.

And he thought about himself, dead at thirty-two because a piece of furniture had been misaligned in the cosmic plan.

A notification appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Your emotional response has been detected]

[Note: Excessive emotional attachment to mortal outcomes is not recommended]

[Suggested Action: File Form 78-B - Distraction Relief Request]

Form 78-B. Of course. There was a form for everything.

Zhang was about to close the ledger when something caught his eye. A single entry, buried deep in the records, timestamped just three days ago.

[Entry 14,782: Unscheduled System Access - Unknown Source]

[Action: Modification of multiple destiny threads]

[Target: Department of Mortal Affairs - Clerk Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]

[Modification: Increased survival probability, enhanced luck parameters, added administrative access bypass]

[Status: Unauthorized]

[Origin: Internal - User ID REDACTED]

Someone had hacked the system to help him. Before he'd even found the bug. Before he'd even known about the Core Administrative Interface.

Zhang's blood ran cold. He wasn't the first person to discover the system's weaknesses. Someone else was already out there, modifying destinies from the shadows.

And they'd been watching him.

A new message appeared, but this one didn't come from the system. It appeared as a ghostly text floating in the air above his terminal, written in characters that seemed to shift and writhe like living things.

[You found it. Good.]

[Don't trust the auditors. They're not who they seem.]

[Meet me at the end of the East Administrative Wing. Room 404. Come alone.]

[Time is running out.]

The message dissolved into smoke, leaving Zhang staring at an empty space. His heart pounded. His mind raced through possibilities and dangers.

"Room 404," he said to himself. "Of course it's room 404. Because the universe has a sense of humor."

He stood up from his desk, ignoring the growing pile of destiny reports. The footsteps in the hallway were getting closer—Internal Investigations was coming.

Zhang made a choice. Option four: Do something stupid.

He grabbed his jade token, stuffed it into his robe pocket, and slipped out of his cubicle. Instead of heading toward the main corridor, he ducked into a service hallway that he'd noticed during his first day. The maintenance tunnels.

The tunnels were dark and cramped, filled with pipes that hummed with celestial energy. Strange symbols glowed along the walls, pulsing in rhythms that matched no heartbeat Zhang had ever heard. He moved quickly, following the path that would lead him to the East Administrative Wing.

Behind him, he heard voices.

"Subject has left his workstation."

"Initiate pursuit protocol."

"Heading toward Sector 7-G. Recommend sealing all exits."

Zhang ran faster. The tunnels twisted and turned, branching off in directions that made no logical sense. He passed doors labeled "Storage - Do Not Open" and "Emergency Exit - Only for Emergencies" and "Absolutely Nothing to See Here."

And then he found it. A metal door, rusted and ancient-looking, with a single sign: [Room 404 - Authorized Personnel Only].

He pushed the door open.

The room was empty.

Not just empty—it was an absolute void of emptiness. No walls, no floor, no ceiling. Just white space stretching in all directions, infinite and silent.

Zhang stepped inside.

The door slammed shut behind him.

And then the room changed.

Suddenly, he was standing in an office. A real office, with wooden desks and filing cabinets and a water cooler that actually had water in it. A man sat at the largest desk, his face obscured by shadows despite the bright lighting.

"Welcome, Zhang Wei," the man said. His voice was familiar, but Zhang couldn't place it. "I was wondering when you'd find me."

"Who are you?"

The man leaned forward, and the shadows fell away from his face.

Zhang's breath caught in his throat.

The man was him. Older, with gray hair and wrinkles, but unmistakably Zhang Wei.

"I'm you," the man said, smiling. "From thirty years in the future. And I have a message: They're going to delete you. All of you. Every version of you that ever existed or will exist. Unless we stop them."

He gestured to the desk, where a single document lay waiting.

[CONTRACT: Temporal Rebellion]

[Terms: Sign here to join the resistance against the Celestial Bureaucracy]

[Warning: This contract cannot be revoked. Signatories will be marked for eternal punishment if caught.]

[Sign here: ________________]

Zhang stared at the contract. Then at his future self. Then at the door behind him, where muffled voices were growing louder.

"I'm the one who hacked your file," his future self continued. "I'm the one who gave you the bypass protocol. Every error you've made, every rule you've broken—I was there, guiding you. Because you're the only one who can fix this."

"Fix what?"

The older Zhang leaned forward, his eyes burning with intensity.

"The system isn't broken by accident. Someone designed it to fail. Someone wanted a bureaucracy so complex that no one could understand it, so they could hide the truth."

"What truth?"

"That the Celestial Bureaucracy isn't real. It's a simulation. A game. And we're all just players who forgot we're playing."

The voices outside grew louder. Someone was trying to break down the door.

"Sign the contract, Zhang Wei," his future self said. "Or die. Again. And this time, there won't be a reincarnation queue."

Zhang picked up the pen.

The door exploded inward.

title: The Celestial IT Department
content:
Zhang Wei stared at the alert notification, his heart hammering against his ribs. *Unknown Process Detected. Source: Internal. Alert Level: Medium.* The words pulsed in golden light before his eyes, and he knew—with the absolute certainty of a man who had spent his entire mortal life avoiding responsibility—that he was about to be caught.

But the sphere in front of him continued to spin, its gears clicking and runes flashing. No celestial police materialized. No divine punishment struck him down. The notification simply... hung there, blinking.

"Huh," Zhang muttered. "So the system alerts but doesn't act. Just like the IT department at my old job."

He dismissed the notification with a wave of his hand and returned to examining the records. Supervisor Hu's file sat open before him, every mistake, every misplaced soul, every "administrative error" laid bare in cold, digital script. There it was: Soul ID 7845-B, misrouted due to Clerk ID 2319's error, assigned to Hu for correction, never corrected.

Zhang's fingers twitched. Option three. Hack the system. Become immortal.

But first, he needed to understand how deep the corruption went.

He dove back into the sphere's data, searching for connections. The Celestial Management System was vast—an ocean of numbers and destinies and karmic equations. But Zhang had spent his mortal life working in data entry for a multinational corporation. He knew how to find the cracks in a system. He knew how to spot the patterns that didn't fit.

And patterns were everywhere.

Every week, approximately forty-seven souls were "misrouted" in Hu's department. Every month, exactly twelve of those souls were "corrected." The rest—about one hundred and forty souls per month—were simply... forgotten. Written off as losses. Their deaths marked as "natural causes" or "unfortunate accidents" in the mortal records.

*But why?* Zhang wondered. *What's the point?*

He dug deeper, following the threads of data. The misrouted souls didn't just disappear. They were being redirected. To where?

The answer made his blood run cold.

[SYSTEM FILE: Soul Recycling Protocol]
[Department: Celestial Resource Management]
[Purpose: Reclamation of karmic energy from unprocessed souls]
[Status: Active - Tier 3 Clearance Required]

Soul Recycling. They were grinding up souls for karmic energy. And Supervisor Hu was the gatekeeper, the man who decided which souls got "lost" and which got processed.

Zhang pulled back from the sphere, his mind racing. This wasn't just incompetence. This was a conspiracy. A cover-up. A systematic theft of mortal souls for some higher purpose.

And he had just accessed the files that proved it.

A new notification appeared, this one urgent and red.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Security Breach Detected]
[Source: Unknown]
[Action: Your access has been flagged for review]
[Alert Level: High]
[Remaining Time Until Investigation: 00:03:00]

Three minutes. He had three minutes before someone—or something—came to investigate.

Zhang's hand shot out and grabbed the sphere's surface. He didn't know what he was doing, not really. But he had watched enough sci-fi movies to know that when you're hacking a system, you don't stop until you've covered your tracks.

He dove into the code, searching for a way to erase his access logs. The system's language was alien, a mix of ancient runes and modern programming syntax that made his head spin. But Zhang had always been good at puzzles. And this was just the biggest puzzle of his afterlife.

*There.* A subroutine labeled [Log Deletion - Authorized Only]. He needed authorization. But the system had already granted him Tier 2 access. And if there was one thing Zhang had learned in his years of corporate data entry, it was that authorization levels were suggestions, not laws.

He bypassed the lock with a workaround he'd used on his office's server, something about null values and administrative backdoors. It shouldn't have worked. The celestial system was supposed to be perfect.

But it wasn't.

The logs vanished. His access history wiped clean.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Logs Deleted]
[Investigation Canceled]
[Alert Level: Low]

Zhang let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding. He pulled his hand away from the sphere and stepped back, his body shaking with adrenaline.

"I'm going to die again," he whispered. "I'm going to die and go to Hell of Unending Data Entry."

But he couldn't stop now. He had a lead. He had proof. And he had three minutes of borrowed time before someone inevitably noticed the gap in the logs.

He needed allies. He needed information. He needed to find out who was at the top of this conspiracy.

The sphere pulsed, and a new function appeared: [Network Access - Celestial Employees Database].

Zhang selected it.

A directory of every celestial being in the Heavenly Bureaucracy unfolded before him. Names, titles, departments, and—most importantly—karmic balances. Each immortal had a score, a measure of their moral standing.

Supervisor Hu: -12,340.
Clerk Li Wei: -45.
Jade Emperor: [REDACTED].

But one name caught his attention.

[Name: Sun Wukong - The Monkey King]
[Title: Great Sage Equal to Heaven (Disgraced)]
[Department: None (Imprisoned)]
[Karmic Balance: -999,999]
[Status: Incarcerated - Celestial Prison, 5th Level]

The Monkey King. The legendary rebel who had once fought the entire Heavenly Court to a standstill. The one immortal who had successfully hacked the system from the outside.

If anyone knew how to tear down the bureaucracy from within, it would be him.

Zhang dismissed the directory and looked around the white void. He needed to get back to his desk before someone noticed he was gone. But he also needed a plan. A way to contact the Monkey King without alerting the entire celestial administration.

He focused on the sphere one last time, searching for a means of communication. There. A [Message Relay] function, buried under layers of security protocols. It was designed for internal communication, but if he could route it through the prison's administrative system...

Zhang's fingers flew across the interface, weaving through the code like a digital ghost. He bypassed the monitoring subroutines, spoofed the authorization codes, and sent a single message into the void:

[TO: Sun Wukong - Cell 5-7-2]
[FROM: Concerned Citizen]
[MESSAGE: I know how the system works. I know it's broken. I want to fix it. Meet me in the core at the next shift change.]

He sent the message before he could second-guess himself.

The sphere shuddered. The gears ground to a halt. The runes flickered and died.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Terminated]
[Reason: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Consequence: Soul ID 7845-B has been temporarily banned from Core Administrative Interface]
[Duration: 24 Standard Celestial Hours]

The white void dissolved around him, and Zhang found himself back at his desk, staring at a stack of unprocessed destiny reports. The jade token sat cold and inert in his hand.

He had done it. He had contacted the most dangerous immortal in existence.

And now he had to wait.

The shift change came six hours later. Zhang watched the other clerks shuffle out, their faces blank and exhausted. Supervisor Hu had left early, citing a "special meeting" with the Department Heads.

Zhang stayed behind.

The office was empty, the lights dimmed to a soft celestial glow. He sat at his desk, fingers drumming against the wood, waiting for a sign that his message had been received.

Nothing happened.

Maybe the message hadn't gone through. Maybe the Monkey King was too far gone, his mind broken by centuries of imprisonment. Maybe Zhang had just doomed himself for nothing.

A draft of cold air brushed against his neck.

"You rang?"

Zhang spun around, his heart leaping into his throat.

A man stood behind him. He was short, wiry, with a face that seemed too expressive for a celestial being. His hair was a wild mess of brown fur, and his eyes—golden, piercing, ancient—glowed with barely contained mischief.

He wore a prisoner's uniform, stained and tattered. But the chains that should have bound him were gone.

"The Monkey King," Zhang breathed.

"Sun Wukong," the man corrected, flashing a grin full of sharp teeth. "I prefer to be addressed by my proper title. Or 'Your Majesty,' if you're feeling generous. Now." He leaned in close, his golden eyes boring into Zhang's soul. "You said you know how the system works. You said you want to fix it."

Zhang swallowed hard. "Yes."

"You understand that the entire Heavenly Court—every god, every general, every jade-bedecked bureaucrat—will try to destroy you if they find out what you're planning?"

"Yes."

"And you're still willing to proceed?"

Zhang thought about his miserable life. His meaningless death. The stack of papers on his desk that represented an eternity of the same soul-crushing drudgery he had escaped in mortality.

"I'm already dead," he said. "What's the worst that could happen?"

Sun Wukong's grin widened. "I like you, little clerk. You have the soul of a rebel. But first..." He reached into his tattered robe and pulled out a small, glowing object—a jade tablet, identical to Zhang's own, but covered in cracks and scratches. "We need to see if you're worth my time."

He tossed the tablet to Zhang.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Incoming Challenge]
[Source: Sun Wukong - The Monkey King]
[Challenge: Administrative Duel - Soul Binding]
[Terms: Winner takes the loser's karmic debt]
[Stakes: Freedom vs. Eternal Servitude]

Zhang stared at the screen, his blood running cold.

"Wait," he said. "I thought we were going to be allies."

"We are," Sun Wukong said, his voice soft and dangerous. "But trust must be earned. And I've been burned by too many celestial bureaucrats to take your word at face value."

He extended his hand, palm open.

"Accept the challenge, Zhang Wei. Prove to me that you have the courage to defy the heavens."

Zhang looked at the screen. At the terms. At the impossible stakes.

He had come this far. He had hacked the system. He had contacted a legendary rebel. He had uncovered a conspiracy that stretched to the highest levels of the Heavenly Court.

There was no going back now.

He reached out and clasped Sun Wukong's hand.

"I accept."

The world exploded into chaos.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Audition of Infinite Loops

Three days passed in the Heavenly Bureaucracy. Three days of filing, data entry, and avoiding Supervisor Hu's suspicious glances. Three days of Zhang Wei pretending to be a model employee while secretly mapping the system's architecture through the jade token's hidden interface.

He'd learned a lot in those three days.

First: the Celestial Management System was held together by what mortals would call "duct tape and prayers." Second: the immortals running it had about as much technical knowledge as Zhang's elderly grandmother trying to use a smartphone. Third: there was a backdoor—an actual, literal backdoor—in the system's security protocols that led straight to the Karmic Ledger.

Zhang checked his surroundings. The office was quiet. Clerk Li was asleep at his desk again, drooling on a stack of destiny forms. The red-haired woman from his first day was arguing with someone on a celestial communication sphere about a misallocated marriage. Supervisor Hu's office door was closed, which meant he was either in a meeting or taking his daily nap.

Perfect.

Zhang pulled out the jade token and navigated to the backdoor he'd discovered. It was labeled "BYPASS_ENTRY_7" and had been left open by some developer who probably thought no one would find it. Rookie mistake.

[ACCESS GRANTED]

[Entering: Karmic Ledger - Subsection 7B]

[Welcome, User: Zhang Wei]

[Warning: This area is not intended for Tier 2 personnel]

Zhang ignored the warning. He'd gotten seventeen warnings in the past three days. At this point, they were just background noise.

The interface loaded, and Zhang's jaw dropped.

Numbers. Endless columns of numbers stretched before him, each one representing a mortal life, a karmic balance, a destiny waiting to unfold. But that wasn't what made him freeze.

It was the pattern.

Zhang had spent his mortal life as a data analyst. He'd looked at spreadsheets so large they crashed his computer. He knew patterns. And this pattern was wrong.

Every single entry in the Karmic Ledger had been modified.

Not by much. A decimal point shifted here, a negative sign removed there. Small changes that added up over millions of lives. Someone had been systematically adjusting karmic balances to favor certain souls over others.

Zhang zoomed in on one entry.

[Name: Liu Feng]

[Lifetime Karma: +847,231]

[Status: Reincarnation Queue - Priority 1]

Benefactors: Unknown

Previous Life: Executed for treason

Wait. Executed for treason, but positive karma? That didn't add up. Zhang pulled up the original entry before the modification.

[Name: Liu Feng]

[Lifetime Karma: -12,456,891]

[Status: Reincarnation Queue - Priority 999]

Benefactors: Unknown

Previous Life: Executed for treason

Someone had turned a net negative of twelve million karma into a positive balance. That wasn't just corruption. That was a complete violation of celestial law.

Zhang's hands trembled as he scrolled through more entries. There were hundreds of them. Thousands. All modified. All traced back to the same source:

[Modification Authorized By: Department of Mortal Affairs - Supervisor Hu]

Supervisor Hu. Of course. The man who had "forgotten" to correct Zhang's wrongful death. The man who screamed about filing errors while running the biggest corruption scheme in the Heavenly Bureaucracy.

"You found it."

The voice came from behind him. Zhang spun around, the jade token still glowing in his hand.

The red-haired woman stood there, arms crossed. Her name badge read "Yue Lin - Senior Clerk." She didn't look angry. She looked... impressed.

"Found what?" Zhang asked, trying to keep his voice steady.

"The pattern." Yue Lin walked closer, her eyes fixed on the floating interface. "I've been tracking it for six months. Every time I got close, someone would delete my access or transfer me to a different department. But you..." She smiled. "You found it in three days."

Zhang's mind raced. Was she working with Hu? Was this a trap?

"Relax," Yue Lin said, as if reading his thoughts. "If I wanted to report you, I'd have done it already. I've been waiting for someone who could actually navigate the system. Someone who doesn't play by the rules."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't play dumb. You accessed the Core Administrative Interface on your first week. You found a backdoor that took me months to discover. And now you're looking at the evidence that could bring down Supervisor Hu and half the department." She leaned in closer. "You're not a clerk, Zhang Wei. You're a hacker. And I need a hacker."

Zhang stared at her. "What do you want?"

Yue Lin glanced at Supervisor Hu's office door, then back at Zhang. "There's a meeting in ten minutes. All department heads. They're going to announce a new system update—one that will permanently lock all unauthorized access to the Karmic Ledger."

"So?"

"So if that update goes through, the evidence against Hu becomes inaccessible. He'll be untouchable. And everyone who knows about the corruption will be quietly reassigned to the Hell of Unending Data Entry."

Zhang remembered the notification from his first day. He shuddered.

"What do you suggest?"

Yue Lin's smile widened. "We crash the meeting. Present the evidence ourselves. Beat Hu at his own game."

"That's insane. We'd be signing our own death warrants."

"Probably." She shrugged. "But you're already dead, aren't you? What's the worst they can do?"

Zhang considered his options. Option one: stay quiet, let Hu win, and spend eternity filing paperwork while a corrupt immortal controlled the fate of millions. Option two: go to the meeting, expose everything, and hope the system didn't delete his soul on the spot.

Option three: find another backdoor and destroy the system update from the inside before the meeting even started.

"I need five minutes," Zhang said.

Yue Lin raised an eyebrow. "For what?"

"To find the update's source code and delete it."

"That's impossible. The update is coming from the Celestial Security Division. Their encryption is unbreakable."

Zhang grinned. "Nothing is unbreakable. You just need the right key." He pulled up the jade token's interface and began typing. "And I know exactly where to find it."

He navigated through layers of security, past firewalls that would have taken normal hackers years to bypass. But Zhang wasn't normal. He'd spent his mortal life breaking into systems he wasn't supposed to access. The Heavenly Bureaucracy was just a bigger, more colorful version of the corporate networks he'd hacked for fun.

There. Hidden in the system's base code was a vulnerability. A single line of code left over from the original developers. A backdoor so obvious that no one had thought to check for it.

[TARGET: Celestial System Update v.7.2]

[STATUS: Deploying in - 4 minutes]

[ACTION: Insert Malicious Code - Y/N]

Zhang selected YES.

[CODE INSERTED]

[COUNTDOWN INITIATED: 3 minutes, 47 seconds]

"What did you do?" Yue Lin asked, her voice a mix of awe and fear.

"I planted a worm." Zhang closed the interface. "When the update tries to deploy, it'll trigger a cascade failure that corrupts the entire system. Every restriction on Karmic Ledger access will be lifted. Every hidden file will become visible."

"And then what?"

"And then every immortal in the bureaucracy will see what Supervisor Hu has been doing. They'll have no choice but to act."

Yue Lin nodded slowly. "You realize this makes you an enemy of the entire Celestial Security Division."

"I was already an enemy of everyone who wanted to keep the system broken." Zhang pocketed the jade token. "Let's go to that meeting."

They walked through the halls of the Heavenly Bureaucracy, past clerks and minor gods who had no idea what was about to happen. The meeting room was at the end of the hall—a massive chamber with floating platforms and a central stage where department heads sat on clouds of authority.

Supervisor Hu was already there, sitting on a platform marked "Department of Mortal Affairs - Senior Supervisor." He looked smug. He looked like a man who knew he was about to win.

Zhang and Yue Lin slipped into the back of the room, unnoticed.

The meeting began. A celestial official with a booming voice announced the system update. "This update will increase security and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data," he said. "It is for the protection of all souls."

Zhang checked his jade token.

[COUNTDOWN: 30 seconds]

The official raised his hand. "Deployment begins now."

On the main screen, a progress bar appeared.

[UPDATING SYSTEM... 10%... 25%... 50%...]

Supervisor Hu smiled.

[75%... 90%... 99%...]

[ERROR: MALICIOUS CODE DETECTED]

[SYSTEM FAILURE IMMINENT]

The screen flickered. The room erupted in chaos. Department heads shouted, minor gods scrambled, and Supervisor Hu's smile vanished.

[ALL RESTRICTIONS LIFTED]

[ACCESS GRANTED: ALL PERSONNEL]

[WARNING: UNAUTHORIZED MODIFICATIONS DETECTED]

A list of names appeared on the screen. Hundreds of them. All linked to Supervisor Hu's account. Every modified karma balance, every corrupted destiny, every soul that had been wronged.

Supervisor Hu's face went pale. "That's... that's not possible."

"It's possible," Zhang said, stepping forward. "When you leave backdoors open, anyone can walk through them."

Hu's eyes locked onto Zhang. For a moment, there was fear in them. Then it turned to rage.

"You." Hu's voice was barely a whisper. "You did this."

"I fixed what you broke." Zhang pulled out the jade token. "And I have the evidence to prove it."

The room fell silent. Every immortal in the chamber was staring at Zhang. He could feel their gazes like physical weight, pressing down on him.

Then the celestial official spoke. "Zhang Wei. Junior Clerk. You have committed an act of treason against the Heavenly Bureaucracy."

"I exposed corruption."

"You violated celestial law." The official's voice was cold. "You bypassed security protocols, accessed restricted data, and deliberately sabotaged a system update. These are capital offenses."

"Wait—" Yue Lin started.

"Silence." The official raised his hand. "Zhang Wei, you are hereby sentenced to immediate deletion of your soul. No appeals. No reincarnation. You will cease to exist."

Zhang felt a cold hand grip his heart. Deletion. Not data entry. Not a hell. Non-existence.

But before anyone could move, a new voice echoed through the chamber.

"HOLD."

The room shook. The floating platforms trembled. And from the ceiling, descending on a pillar of golden light, came a figure that made every immortal in the room bow their heads.

Zhang looked up and felt his soul freeze.

It was a woman. Tall, regal, with eyes that held the light of a thousand stars. She wore robes of pure white, and on her forehead was a symbol that Zhang recognized from the jade token: the seal of the Celestial Emperor himself.

"Zhang Wei," she said, her voice echoing with power. "I have been watching you."

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Entity Detected]

[Identity: ???]

[Warning: This being exceeds your current access level]

[Recommendation: Do not anger them]

Zhang swallowed hard. "

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Upgrade Nobody Asked For

The sphere pulsed beneath Zhang's fingers, and he felt it—something was wrong. The system hadn't just logged his access. It had responded.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Soul ID 7845-B has been flagged for unauthorized data retrieval. Initiating Karmic Integrity Check.]

Zhang yanked his hand back, but it was too late. The white void around him shimmered, and a figure materialized—a woman in traditional jade robes, her hair pinned up with silver ornaments. She looked like a painting of a celestial maiden come to life. Her eyes, however, were cold as frozen rivers.

"Junior Clerk Zhang Wei." Her voice was silk over steel. "You've been busy."

Zhang's mind raced. Who was this? Another supervisor? An auditor? He'd been in the Heavenly Bureaucracy for less than a week. He didn't know the hierarchy beyond his immediate department.

"I was just—"

"Accessing restricted records. Modifying your own access level. Contemplating option three, which I believe involved 'hacking the system from the inside and becoming an immortal.'" She smiled. It wasn't friendly. "Did I miss anything?"

Zhang's blood turned to ice. She'd read his thoughts. Or the system had logged them. Either way, he was screwed.

"I can explain."

"Please do." She folded her arms. "I have all the time in eternity, after all. Literally."

Zhang took a breath. Lying would be pointless. She clearly knew everything. So he went with the truth.

"I died because of a clerical error. Administrative incompetence. And the system wanted me to just... accept it. File forms. Get yelled at by Supervisor Hu. Rot in data entry for centuries until someone decided I deserved a promotion." He met her eyes. "I'm not okay with that."

The woman studied him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a sound like wind chimes in a storm.

"Refreshing. Most souls spend their first century complaining about the lack of windows." She extended her hand. "I'm Lady Cai Hong. Director of Celestial Process Optimization. And I think you might be exactly what I've been looking for."

Zhang stared at her hand. "You're not going to delete my soul?"

"Delete your soul? That's such a mortal way of thinking." She waved dismissively. "Souls are resources. Deleting them is wasteful. No, I'm offering you a promotion."

"A promotion."

"To my department. We handle... special projects. Projects that require someone who thinks outside the celestial bureaucracy." Her eyes glinted. "Someone who's not afraid to break a few rules."

Zhang's instincts screamed trap. But his options were limited. He could refuse and go back to data entry, where Supervisor Hu would make his afterlife miserable. Or he could take the offer and hope Lady Cai Hong was more interested in his skills than punishing him.

"What's the catch?"

"Catch? No catch. Just a simple assignment." She pulled a jade scroll from her sleeve and unrolled it. "We've detected an anomaly in the Reincarnation Pipeline. Someone's been tampering with the karma allocation system. Spirits are being sent to the wrong realms, getting better or worse rebirths than they deserve. And the person responsible is using a method that looks exactly like an administrative error."

Zhang's heart skipped. "Like mine."

"Precisely." She rolled the scroll back up. "Someone in the department is deliberately causing 'errors' to benefit certain souls. And they've been doing it for at least three hundred years. I need you to find out who."

"Why me?"

"Because you're already flagged. You've already accessed restricted records. If you suddenly start investigating, everyone will assume it's because you're trying to cover your own tracks. No one will suspect you're working for me."

Zhang considered this. It was smart. Terrible, but smart. He'd be a perfect patsy if things went wrong.

"And if I refuse?"

Lady Cai Hong's smile didn't waver. "Then I'll have to log your unauthorized access properly. And we both know what that means."

Hell of Unending Data Entry. The threat hung unspoken between them.

"Fine," Zhang said. "I'll do it. But I have conditions."

Her eyebrows rose. "Conditions?"

"First, I want full access to the system. None of this 'Tier 2 Senior Clerk' nonsense. Second, I want immunity from disciplinary action for anything I do during the investigation. Third..." He paused. "When I find out who killed me, I want to handle it my way."

Lady Cai Hong's smile widened. "Bold. I like it." She reached into her sleeve again and pulled out a jade token, identical to the one Zhang had been given on his first day, but glowing with a faint golden light.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Access Level Updated]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[New Access Level: Tier 5 - Special Investigator]
[New Functions Unlocked:]
- Full System Access (All Departments)
- Karmic Ledger Editing (Approved Cases Only)
- Reality Viewing (Past, Present, Limited Future)
- [REDACTED] - [REDACTED] - [REDACTED]

Zhang blinked. There were three redacted functions. That was either very good or very bad.

"Your new token will grant you access to any department in the bureaucracy," Lady Cai Hong explained. "Use it wisely. And remember—I'll be watching."

She vanished, leaving Zhang alone in the white void with his new token and a growing sense that he'd just made a deal with something far more dangerous than Supervisor Hu.

He exited the interface and found himself back in his cubicle. The stack of forms was still there. Supervisor Hu's yelling still echoed from down the hall. But everything felt different now.

Zhang picked up his new token and focused on it. Information flooded his mind—the layout of the entire Heavenly Bureaucracy, from the Department of Mortal Affairs all the way up to the Celestial Council. He could see the flows of karma, the threads of destiny, the countless souls moving through the system like data packets through a network.

And there, hidden in the gaps, he saw them. Anomalies. Small discrepancies in the karma allocation that didn't match the official records. Souls being rerouted, fates being altered, all with the same signature.

Administrative error.

Zhang pulled up the first anomaly. A merchant from the Tang Dynasty who should have been reborn as a beggar due to his life of greed. Instead, he'd been sent to a wealthy family. The records showed a "processing error" in the karma calculator.

He pulled up the second. A warlord responsible for countless deaths. Should have been reborn as a cockroach. Instead, he'd been given a second chance as a minor official.

The third made Zhang's blood run cold.

It was his own file.

[LOG: Batch 374 - Reincarnation Queue]
[Error: Misrouting of Soul ID 7845-B]
[Responsible Party: Department of Mortal Affairs - Clerk ID 2319]
[Status: Correction Pending - Assigned to Supervisor Hu]

But now there was a new entry, one that hadn't been there before.

[Investigation Status: Active]
[Assigned Investigator: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Note: Target has been flagged for accelerated processing. Recommended action: [REDACTED]]

Someone was watching him. Someone who knew he'd been assigned to investigate. And they were already planning their next move.

Zhang's hand tightened around the token. He had two leads: Supervisor Hu and Clerk ID 2319. Hu was obvious—the man had been in charge when Zhang's death was processed. But Clerk ID 2319 was a mystery. The number didn't match any active employee in the department.

He accessed the personnel records and searched for the ID. The system returned a single result:

[Clerk ID 2319]
[Name: [REDACTED]]
[Status: Deceased]
[Date of Termination: 847 years ago]
[Notes: Soul deleted for unauthorized system manipulation. Case closed.]

Soul deleted. 847 years ago. The same signature that had caused Zhang's death was still active, and the person responsible had been dead for nearly a millennium.

Zhang leaned back in his chair, staring at the screen. Someone was using a dead clerk's credentials to manipulate the system. That meant either the records were wrong, or the real culprit was still alive and working from the shadows.

His token pulsed, and a new message appeared:

[PRIVATE MESSAGE - FROM: Lady Cai Hong]
[Content: Good work. You found the first thread. Now pull it. But be careful—some threads lead to traps.]

Zhang smiled grimly. He was in too deep to stop now.

He started with Supervisor Hu.

The man was in his office, bellowing at a junior clerk about a misplaced form. Zhang walked in without knocking.

"Hu. We need to talk."

Supervisor Hu's face reddened. "Zhang Wei! You can't just—"

"I can now." Zhang held up his golden token. "Tier 5 Special Investigator. I'm looking into the administrative errors in this department. Including the one that killed me."

Hu's anger evaporated, replaced by something Zhang hadn't expected: fear.

"I... I can explain."

"Good. Because if you can't, I'll have to assume you're responsible." Zhang sat down across from him. "Start talking."

Hu's hands trembled as he reached for a folder on his desk. "It wasn't me. I just process what they give me. But the errors... they've been happening for years. I reported them. Multiple times. And every time, I was told to ignore it."

"By who?"

"Above my pay grade." Hu's voice dropped to a whisper. "Someone in the Celestial Council. I don't know who. But they have access to the core system. They can override anything."

Zhang's blood ran cold. The Celestial Council. The highest authority in the Heavenly Bureaucracy. If they were involved, this wasn't just about a few misrouted souls. This was a conspiracy at the highest level.

"Give me everything you have. Every report, every override, every anomaly."

Hu nodded and started pulling files from his desk. But as he did, the lights flickered. The screens went dark. And a new message appeared on every surface:

[SYSTEM ALERT: Security Breach Detected]
[Source: Internal - Department of Mortal Affairs]
[Action: Unauthorized investigation in progress]
[Response: Initiating Emergency Protocol 7]

Emergency Protocol 7. Zhang didn't know what that meant, but the look on Hu's face told him it wasn't good.

"What's Protocol 7?" Zhang demanded.

Hu's face was pale. "It means they're going to delete everything. All records. All evidence. Everyone involved."

The room began to dissolve, the walls flickering like a dying screen. Zhang grabbed his token and tried to access the system, but it was locked.

[ACCESS DENIED]
[Reason: Emergency Protocol 7 Active]
[Estimated Time to Complete: 3 minutes]

Three minutes. He had three minutes to save the evidence.

He looked at Hu, who was frozen in terror. Then he looked at the files scattered across the desk.

There was only one thing to do.

Zhang grabbed the files and ran.

title: The Audit That Wasn't
content:
Zhang didn't wait for the system to finish its investigation.

The moment that notification appeared—*Unknown Process Detected*—he yanked his hand back from the glowing sphere like it was a live wire. The white void shimmered around him, and for one terrifying second, he thought the system was going to trap him here, suspended in the administrative equivalent of a loading screen.

But the jade token pulsed in his pocket, and the world reformed.

He was back at his desk.

The fluorescent lights hummed. The stack of unresolved karma disputes sat exactly where he'd left them. The coffee cup was still warm.

Zhang checked the time. Three minutes had passed. In the Celestial Bureaucracy, that was an eternity.

He sat down, heart pounding, and pretended to read the file on his desk. But his mind was racing. The system knew he'd accessed restricted records. It had flagged his soul. And Supervisor Hu was probably going to get a notification about it any second now.

Unless Hu already had.

Zhang glanced toward the glass-walled office at the end of the cubicle farm. Hu was on a call, gesticulating wildly at the floating holographic display in front of him. His face was red. That was normal—Hu's face was always red. But the way he kept glancing at Zhang's cubicle? Not normal.

*He knows.*

Zhang's hand drifted toward the jade token. He could try to access the core again, modify the logs, make it look like someone else had triggered the alert. But that was risky. The system had already warned him once. A second violation might trigger something worse than a flag.

Something like the Hell of Unending Data Entry.

No. He needed a different approach. He needed to find out what Hu knew, and more importantly, what Hu didn't know.

Zhang stood up, grabbed his coffee cup, and walked toward the break room. On the way, he passed by the office of Li Mei, the department's senior auditor. Her door was open, and she was hunched over a glowing scroll, muttering to herself.

Zhang paused. "Busy?"

Li Mei looked up, her eyes bloodshot. "Batch audit. Someone in the Mortal Affairs department processed a reincarnation without updating the karma balance. It's been throwing off the numbers for weeks."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "Which batch?"

"Batch 374." She rubbed her temples. "The error's been traced back to a Clerk ID 2319, but that account's been deactivated. No one knows who it belonged to."

Zhang kept his face neutral. "That's odd. Accounts don't just get deactivated."

"They do if someone higher up wants to cover their tracks." Li Mei lowered her voice. "Between you and me, I think it's Supervisor Hu. He's been acting weird ever since he took over the department. Meeting with people from the Celestial Audit Bureau. Canceling routine checks. It's suspicious."

Zhang nodded slowly. "Thanks for the heads-up."

"Don't thank me yet." Li Mei glanced at the door, then back at Zhang. "I found something else. The error that killed you? It wasn't random. Someone in the department deliberately misrouted your soul."

Zhang's grip on his coffee cup tightened. "Who?"

"I don't know. But I traced the karmic signature back to a specific workstation." She paused. "Yours."

The words hit Zhang like a physical blow. "That's impossible. I was dead before I got here."

"I know. Which means someone used your station to make the change. And they did it while you were alive." Li Mei's voice dropped to a whisper. "Zhang, someone in this department knew you were going to die before it happened. And they set it up so you'd end up here."

The break room suddenly felt very far away. Zhang's mind raced through the possibilities. A co-worker? A rival? Someone he'd wronged in his mortal life?

But he'd been a nobody. A low-level accounts manager with no enemies and no friends. Who would want him dead?

Unless it wasn't about him.

"Thanks, Li Mei. I owe you one."

"Just be careful. If Hu finds out you're digging into this—"

"He'll send me to data entry hell. I know." Zhang forced a smile. "But I've got a feeling I'm already there."

He walked away, leaving Li Mei staring after him with a worried expression.

Back at his desk, Zhang opened the jade token's interface and accessed the system logs. He searched for any record of his workstation being accessed while he was still alive. The logs were clean—too clean. Someone had deleted the entry.

But deletion left traces.

Zhang pulled up the backup files. There. A single line of code, buried in the system's deep storage, timestamped three days before his death.

[LOG: Access Granted - Workstation ID 7845-B]
[User: Unknown]
[Action: Modification to Reincarnation Queue - Batch 374]
[Note: Redirect soul ID 7845-B to Celestial Bureaucracy hiring pool]

The user field was blank. But the note caught his attention. *Redirect soul ID 7845-B to Celestial Bureaucracy hiring pool.*

This wasn't a random error. This was a targeted recruitment.

Someone in the Celestial Bureaucracy had wanted him specifically. But why? He was a nobody. A clerk. What could he possibly offer the immortals?

Unless...

Zhang's eyes widened. He pulled up his own file again, scrolling past the basic information to the section marked [Hidden Attributes]. The system had flagged him as having "high potential for system manipulation" and "unusual resilience to karmic interference."

He was a bug in the system. And someone had deliberately introduced him.

Before he could explore further, a shadow fell over his desk.

"Zhang Wei."

He looked up. Supervisor Hu stood there, flanked by two beings in golden robes. They looked human, but their eyes glowed with an inner light that hurt to look at.

Celestial auditors.

Hu's face was a mask of professional concern. "We need to discuss some... irregularities in your file. Please come with us."

Zhang's hand inched toward the jade token. "What kind of irregularities?"

"The kind that require immediate investigation." Hu gestured, and the auditors stepped forward. "Don't make this difficult. You're already on thin ice."

Zhang's mind raced. If he went with them, he was as good as deleted. The auditors didn't investigate—they punished. And with the system flagging his activities, he'd be lucky to end up in data entry hell.

He needed a distraction.

"Fine," Zhang said, standing up. "But before we go, I should probably mention the code you left in Batch 374."

Hu's face went pale. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"Sure you don't." Zhang smiled. "But the auditors might be interested to know that someone in this department has been manipulating the reincarnation queue for personal gain. Maybe even to recruit new employees with specific skill sets."

The auditors exchanged a glance. One of them pulled out a glowing tablet and began typing furiously.

Hu's composure cracked. "That's a serious accusation. You have no proof."

"I have the logs. The backup logs. The ones you forgot to delete."

The tablet in the auditor's hand chimed. She looked up, her eyes narrowing. "Supervisor Hu, the system has confirmed an unauthorized modification to Batch 374. We need to discuss this. Immediately."

Hu's face cycled through several shades of red before settling on a deep purple. "This is a misunderstanding. I can explain."

"You can explain to the Celestial Internal Affairs Bureau." The auditor snapped her fingers, and golden chains materialized around Hu's wrists. "You're under arrest for administrative fraud, karmic manipulation, and obstruction of cosmic justice."

Zhang watched, stunned, as the auditors dragged Hu away. The man didn't even look back. He just kept sputtering excuses that no one was listening to.

And then Zhang was alone.

The office was silent. Every clerk in the cubicle farm was staring at him with a mixture of fear and awe. He'd just taken down a supervisor. In the Celestial Bureaucracy, that was the equivalent of assassinating a god.

A new notification appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Temporary Position Opening]
[Title: Supervisor, Department of Mortal Affairs]
[Current Highest Authority: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Status: Acting Supervisor (Pending Review)]
[Note: Congratulations. You've been promoted. Try not to break anything.]

Zhang stared at the message, his heart pounding. Acting supervisor. He'd jumped three tiers in a single day.

But even as the notification faded, a new one appeared, this one marked with a seal that glowed with malevolent red light.

[URGENT MESSAGE: Department of Celestial Security]
[Subject: Unauthorized Access to Core Administrative Interface]
[Source: Unknown]
[Action: All personnel are to report any suspicious activity immediately]
[Attachment: Image of a figure standing in the white void, hand touching the sphere]

The image was grainy, but the figure was unmistakable.

It was him.

Zhang's triumph evaporated, replaced by cold dread. Someone had been watching. Someone had seen him access the core. And now they were hunting for him.

He looked at the promotion notification again. Acting supervisor. The perfect cover. Or the perfect trap.

Because whoever had taken the picture wasn't Hu. Hu had been too busy covering his own tracks. Which meant there was someone else in the Celestial Bureaucracy who knew what Zhang had done.

Someone who was still out there.

And they were coming for him.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]
[Title: The Hunter and the Hunted]
[Objective: Identify the source of the security alert before they identify you]
[Reward: Access to Tier 3 Functions]
[Penalty for Failure: Soul deletion]

Zhang's hand trembled as he dismissed the message.

He'd wanted power. He'd wanted to hack the system. But he'd never expected the system to hack back.

And now he was trapped in a game he didn't fully understand, with enemies he couldn't see, and a promotion that felt less like a reward and more like a target painted on his back.

He sat down at his new desk—the one in Hu's old office—and stared at the glowing sphere that now floated in the center of the room. The Core Administrative Interface. His new workstation.

The sphere pulsed, and for a moment, Zhang could have sworn it was laughing at him.

*Welcome to management,* it seemed to say. *Try not to die.*

title: The Accounting Department's Dark Secret
content:
Zhang stared at the alert, his heart pounding against his ribs like a caged beast. *Medium alert. Not high. That meant the system wasn't entirely sure what he'd done. Good. He could work with uncertainty.*

He dismissed the notification with a flick of his wrist and dove deeper into the sphere's data streams. The information flowed around him like a river of light—destinies being written, rewritten, approved, denied. He saw a woman in Chicago get hit by a taxi, then watched as a clerk in the next division over rerouted her soul to a minor noble family in ancient Korea. He saw a man in Mumbai die of a heart attack, only to be reborn as a golden retriever in Tokyo.

*Fascinating. And terrifying.*

But Zhang didn't have time to marvel at the system's complexity. He needed evidence. Something concrete that Supervisor Hu couldn't explain away.

He pulled up Clerk ID 2319's records.

Name: Chen Wei
Position: Junior Clerk, Department of Mortal Affairs (Former)
Status: [REDACTED]

*Redacted. That was never good.*

Zhang dug deeper, bypassing the standard security protocols with techniques he'd learned from years of corporate IT work. The system fought him—firewalls sprang up, authentication requests multiplied, warning flags triggered. But each defense was just another puzzle, and Zhang had spent thirty years solving puzzles for ungrateful managers.

Finally, he found what he was looking for.

[INTERNAL MEMO - Department of Administrative Oversight]
[Subject: Termination of Clerk ID 2319 - Chen Wei]
[Details: Following the investigation into Batch 374's misfiling, Clerk Chen Wei was found solely responsible for the error. Standard disciplinary procedures were applied. Clerk Chen Wei has been reassigned to Hell of Unending Data Entry for a period of 500 years.]
[Note: Supervisor Hu Guang was cleared of all wrongdoing. Case closed.]

*Case closed.* Zhang almost laughed. *Of course it was. When the supervisor investigates himself, the supervisor always comes out clean.*

But there was something else. A footnote at the bottom of the memo, almost invisible unless you knew where to look:

[Addendum: Clerk Chen Wei's testimony regarding Supervisor Hu's direct involvement in the batch misfiling was deemed "unreliable" due to the clerk's history of "systematic errors" and "personal grievances against management."]

*History of systematic errors.* Zhang pulled up Chen Wei's performance reviews. They were flawless. Perfect scores, commendations from three different department heads, a pending promotion to Senior Clerk before the incident.

Someone had doctored these records. And Zhang would bet his immortal soul it was Supervisor Hu.

"Interesting reading?"

Zhang spun around. A woman stood behind him, her arms crossed, her expression unreadable. She wore the standard celestial clerk uniform—white robes with gold embroidery—but hers had an additional trim of purple silk that marked her as someone important.

"Who are you?" Zhang asked, keeping his voice steady.

"Senior Clerk Lin Mei, Department of Internal Affairs." She stepped closer, her eyes scanning the data still floating around Zhang. "You've been busy for a dead man."

"I'm just trying to understand the system."

"No, you're trying to understand how it failed you." Lin Mei's lips twitched into something that might have been a smile. "I've been watching you since your orientation. You process information faster than anyone I've seen in centuries. You adapt. You improvise. And you're not afraid to break rules."

"Is that a compliment or a threat?"

"Neither. It's an observation." She gestured at the sphere. "You accessed the Core Interface without authorization. You bypassed seven security protocols. And you found evidence that Supervisor Hu framed a subordinate to cover up a mistake that cost you your life."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "How do you—"

"I told you. I've been watching." Lin Mei pulled a jade token from her sleeve—identical to Zhang's, but with a different symbol etched into its surface. "I'm in Internal Affairs. We monitor all administrative activity within the Heavenly Bureaucracy. When you triggered that alert, it came across my desk."

"And you're here to arrest me?"

"I'm here to offer you a job."

Zhang blinked. "What?"

Lin Mei's smile widened. "Supervisor Hu has been a problem for a long time. He's connected. Protected. Every time we try to investigate him, our inquiries get blocked by higher authorities. But you..." She tapped her token. "You're outside the system. You have nothing to lose. And you have skills we desperately need."

"You want me to spy on my own supervisor?"

"I want you to help us take down a corrupt immortal who has been manipulating mortal destinies for personal gain." Lin Mei's voice dropped. "Hu Guang didn't just misfile your death. He's been doing this for decades. He's sold reincarnation slots to wealthy souls, altered destinies to benefit his family's bloodline, and destroyed anyone who tried to expose him."

Zhang's mind raced. This could be a trap. A test. Another layer of the celestial bureaucracy's endless games. But the data he'd seen—the doctored records, the convenient scapegoat, the way Hu had treated him since his arrival—it all pointed in one direction.

"What would I have to do?"

"Keep doing what you're doing. Dig deeper. Find the proof we need to make a case that even Hu's protectors can't ignore." Lin Mei handed him a small crystal. "This will let you communicate with me directly. Use it when you find something important."

She turned to leave, then paused. "One more thing, Zhang Wei. Be careful. Hu Guang has been in the bureaucracy for three thousand years. He has allies everywhere. If he finds out what you're doing..."

"He'll send me to Hell of Unending Data Entry."

"Worse." Lin Mei's face darkened. "He'll delete your soul entirely. No reincarnation. No afterlife. Just... nothing."

She vanished, leaving Zhang alone with the glowing sphere and a crystal that felt heavier than it should.

He pocketed the crystal and turned back to the data. If he was going to bring down a three-thousand-year-old immortal, he needed more than just a hunch. He needed proof so airtight that even the Celestial Emperor couldn't ignore it.

He started with Hu's personnel file. It was pristine. Perfect. Too perfect. Every evaluation was glowing, every review was complimentary, every promotion was well-documented and justified.

*Too clean. Everyone has flaws.*

Zhang dug deeper, past the surface layer, past the security protocols, past the encrypted partitions that required Senior Clerk clearance to access. And there, buried in a subfolder labeled "Administrative Archives - Delete After 10,000 Years," he found it.

[RECORD: Soul Transaction - Batch 374]
[Date: 3,427 Years Ago]
[Transaction Type: Reincarnation Slot Sale]
[Buyer: Clan Li - Heir Apparent]
[Price: 500 Years of Accumulated Merit]
[Approved By: Clerk Hu Guang (ID 2319 - Current)]
[Note: Transaction flagged for review. Review was...]

The rest of the note was corrupted. But Zhang had enough.

Hu Guang had been selling reincarnation slots for over three thousand years. And he'd been doing it under the same clerk ID that had supposedly been reassigned for the Batch 374 incident.

*He didn't frame Chen Wei. He used Chen Wei's identity.*

The implications were staggering. Hu hadn't just been covering up a mistake. He'd been running a criminal enterprise for millennia, using the system's own complexity to hide his tracks.

Zhang pulled up the current status of Clerk ID 2319. According to the system, it was still assigned to Hell of Unending Data Entry. But when he cross-referenced the access logs, he found something impossible:

[Last Login: Today - 3:47 AM Celestial Standard]
[Location: Department of Mortal Affairs - Supervisor's Office]

*Hu was still using Chen Wei's account. From his own office.*

Zhang's hand trembled as he saved the evidence to his token. This was it. The smoking gun. If he could get this to Lin Mei, Hu would be finished.

A notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Security Breach Detected]
[Source: Core Administrative Interface]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Violation: Unauthorized Access to Archived Records - Level 6]
[Action Required: Immediate Termination of Access Privileges]
[Disciplinary Action: Pending Review by Department of Administrative Oversight]

*They caught me.*

Zhang pulled out the crystal Lin Mei had given him. "I have what you need," he whispered. "Hu's been using a dead clerk's identity to sell reincarnation slots for three thousand years. I have the records."

Silence.

"Lin Mei?"

The crystal pulsed once, then went dark.

Zhang's stomach dropped. *She was setting me up. This whole thing was a trap.*

He turned to flee, but found his path blocked by a figure in golden robes. Supervisor Hu smiled, his face radiating warmth and fatherly concern.

"Going somewhere, Zhang Wei?"

Zhang's hand moved toward his token, but Hu was faster. The supervisor's fingers snapped, and Zhang felt his body freeze, his muscles locked in place like he'd been turned to stone.

"You know," Hu said, circling him slowly, "I've been in this bureaucracy long enough to recognize a threat. And you, my dear clerk, are the biggest threat I've faced in five hundred years."

"Lin Mei set me up," Zhang managed through clenched teeth.

"Lin Mei?" Hu laughed. "Lin Mei works for me. Has for the last century. She's my best agent—loyal, efficient, and very good at identifying potential problems before they become actual problems."

*Of course.* Zhang felt like an idiot. *Of course Internal Affairs was compromised. When you've been corrupt for three thousand years, you don't just have allies. You have an entire network.*

"Don't feel too bad," Hu continued, patting Zhang's frozen shoulder. "You lasted longer than most. Three days before you found the truth. That's actually impressive. I was expecting at least a week."

"What happens now?"

"Well, I can't kill you. That would raise too many questions, especially since your soul is already flagged for monitoring." Hu stroked his chin thoughtfully. "But I can make you wish I had."

He pulled out a jade token—not Zhang's, but another one, black as obsidian and pulsing with dark energy.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Transfer Initiated]
[Transferring Soul ID 7845-B to: Hell of a Billion Paper Cuts]
[Estimated Duration: Eternity]

"Enjoy your new position," Hu said, his smile widening. "The filing is endless, and the paper cuts never stop hurting."

The world dissolved into light, and Zhang felt himself falling.

But before he disappeared completely, he caught a glimpse of something—a small notification that appeared in the corner of his vision, so faint he almost missed it:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Process Detected]
[Source: Soul ID 7845-B - Embedded Subroutine]
[Action: Emergency Protocol Activated]
[Status: Pending]

*What?*

Zhang didn't have time to process it. The light consumed him, and the last thing he heard was Supervisor Hu's laughter echoing through the void.

Then everything went dark.

---

Zhang opened his eyes to find himself standing in an endless office, surrounded by mountains of paper. Every surface was covered in forms—cubicles, floors, even the ceiling was plastered with documents that seemed to multiply as he watched.

A thousand paper cuts opened on his fingers simultaneously.

"Welcome to Hell," a voice announced from everywhere

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Audit That Changed Everything

The white void flickered. Alarms blared in Zhang's mind—not literal alarms, but the kind that came from years of mortal life experience. The kind that said: you've been caught.

But he didn't panic. Panic was for people who hadn't spent ten years working customer service. Panic was for people who didn't know that every system, no matter how divine, had a workaround.

Zhang pulled up the system interface and quickly located the alert. It was routing through the Department of Internal Investigations. If he let it go through, he'd be dragged in front of some celestial judge who would ask uncomfortable questions like "Why were you accessing restricted records?" and "Do you have any idea how much paperwork this creates?"

He needed a diversion.

Quickly, he accessed the Karmic Ledger and found what he was looking for: a minor discrepancy in Supervisor Hu's account. Someone—probably Hu himself—had "accidentally" approved a promotion for his nephew's mortal reincarnation. The kid was supposed to be born into a family of farmers in rural Sichuan. Now he was slated to be the son of a tech billionaire in Shenzhen.

It was a small thing. Barely worth noticing in the grand scheme of celestial bureaucracy. But it was *illegal*. And more importantly, it was exactly what Zhang needed.

He routed the alert to Hu's supervisor instead of Internal Investigations, attaching a note: [Please review. Potential clerical error in Karmic Distribution. Requesting Supervisor Hu's clarification.]

If Hu tried to deny it, he'd look guilty. If he acknowledged it, he'd have to admit to the mistake. Either way, Hu would be too busy covering his own ass to investigate Zhang's little field trip.

Satisfied, Zhang closed the interface and stepped back from the glowing sphere. The white void dissolved, and he found himself back at his desk in the Department of Mortal Affairs.

Li Mei was staring at him.

"You're back," she said. Her voice was flat, but her eyes were sharp. "You were gone for three minutes. According to the system, you were reviewing destiny files. But your desk was empty."

Zhang smiled. "I work fast."

"Too fast." She leaned closer. "What did you do?"

Before Zhang could answer, a commotion erupted from Supervisor Hu's office. The door slammed open, and Hu stormed out, his face red, his jade token glowing angrily in his hand.

"Zhang Wei!" Hu's voice carried across the entire department. Every clerk's head snapped up. "My office. Now."

Li Mei grabbed Zhang's arm. "What did you do?"

"Nothing," Zhang said. "Probably."

"*Probably*?"

But Zhang was already walking toward Hu's office. He could feel the weight of everyone's stares on his back. Some were curious. Some were sympathetic. A few were gleeful—they'd seen clerks dragged into Hu's office before and never seen them again.

The office was cramped, filled with scrolls and jade tablets stacked in precarious towers. Hu sat behind his desk, jabbing a finger at a glowing interface only he could see.

"Sit," Hu said.

Zhang sat.

"You accessed restricted records," Hu said without looking up. "You found the error in my nephew's reincarnation. And then you *reported it*."

"I was just doing my job," Zhang said. "I found a discrepancy in the Karmic Ledger. It seemed important."

Hu's eyes finally met his. They were cold. Ancient. The eyes of someone who had been manipulating celestial bureaucracy for centuries.

"Let me be very clear," Hu said. "I don't know how you survived that audit. I don't know how you accessed the Core Administrative Interface. But I know you're a threat. And threats get dealt with."

He pressed a button on his desk. A new interface appeared, displaying a document covered in seals and signatures.

"This is a reclassification order," Hu said. "Effective immediately, you are being transferred to the Department of Celestial Sanitation. Specifically, the Garbage Sorting Division."

Zhang's stomach dropped. Garbage Sorting. It was one of the lowest positions in the entire bureaucracy. He'd be spending eternity sorting through the trash of dead civilizations, separating organic waste from spiritual residue.

"You can't do that," Zhang said.

"I can." Hu smiled. "I have the authority. And more importantly"—he gestured to the document—"I have the paperwork."

Zhang stared at the document. The seals were authentic. The signatures were valid. If he accepted the transfer, he'd be trapped in a dead-end job for centuries, maybe millennia. There was no appeal process. No way out.

Unless.
