Zhang reached into his pocket and pulled out the jade token. It was still glowing faintly from his earlier breach. He held it up.

"Before I accept this transfer," Zhang said, "I'd like to file a formal complaint."

Hu laughed. "A complaint? Against who?"

"Against you." Zhang's voice was steady. "For abuse of authority, obstruction of justice, and—" He paused, checking the token. "—unauthorized use of celestial resources for personal gain."

Hu's smile faltered. "That's not possible. The complaint system requires a two-week waiting period for processing."

"I know." Zhang's grin returned. "That's why I filed it three days ago. Before I even knew about the error in your nephew's reincarnation."

It was a lie. But the token didn't know that. It simply displayed the status of the complaint: [Pending Review - Received 3 Days Ago].

Hu's face went pale. "That's... that's not possible. The system would have flagged it."

"It did," Zhang said. "But I routed it to your supervisor. You know, the one who's about to investigate you for nepotism."

For a long moment, Hu just stared at him. Then, slowly, he leaned back in his chair. When he spoke again, his voice was quiet. Dangerous.

"You think you're clever, don't you? You think you've outsmarted me."

"I think I've bought myself some time," Zhang said.

"Time for what?"

Zhang stood up. "Time to figure out how to hack this system so thoroughly that even a celestial being like you won't be able to stop me."

He turned and walked out of the office. As he passed through the main room, the other clerks stared at him with a mixture of awe and fear. Even Li Mei looked impressed.

But Zhang didn't stop. He walked straight to the elevators at the end of the hall and pressed the button for the lowest level. The doors slid open, revealing a small, cramped car that smelled like old paper and ozone.

"Floor?" a disembodied voice asked.

"Sub-level 7," Zhang said. "The Archive of Forgotten Things."

The elevator began to descend. Around him, the walls flickered with ancient runes and half-erased sigils. The Archive of Forgotten Things was a place of legend in the Heavenly Bureaucracy—a repository of every soul, every destiny, every karmic record that had been deemed "irrelevant" by the celestial administrators.

It was also, according to rumor, the only place in the entire bureaucracy where the original source code of the Celestial Management System was stored.

If Zhang could find that code, he could rewrite the rules. He could undo his death. He could—.

The elevator stopped. The doors opened.

Zhang stepped out into darkness.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: You have entered an unregistered zone]
[Warning: Karmic sensors are inoperative]
[Recommendation: Turn back immediately]

Zhang ignored the message. He pulled out his jade token and used its light to illuminate the space around him.

The Archive stretched in all directions—endless shelves of scrolls and tablets, each one containing the forgotten history of a soul that no one remembered. The air was thick with dust and the faint whisper of dying memories.

And in the center of it all, sitting on a throne made of discarded destinies, was a figure.

"Welcome," the figure said. Its voice was old. Older than the bureaucracy itself. "I've been waiting for you, Zhang Wei."

Zhang's hand tightened around the token. "Who are you?"

The figure leaned forward, and its face caught the light. It was a woman, or at least it had been once. Now its features were blurred, shifting between ages and faces like a reflection in disturbed water.

"I am what happens when the system forgets you," it said. "I am the first clerk. The one who wrote the original code. And I've been trapped here for ten thousand years."

Zhang's mind raced. The first clerk. The architect of the Celestial Management System. If the legends were true, this being had designed the entire bureaucracy from scratch.

"I need your help," Zhang said.

"I know." The figure smiled. "I've been watching you. You're clever. Resourceful. And you've already figured out the one truth that the immortals don't want you to know."

"And what's that?"

The figure leaned closer. Its breath was cold, carrying the scent of ancient paper and forgotten promises.

"The Celestial Management System is broken," it whispered. "It was never designed to handle the weight of all mortal souls. It's been failing for millennia. And soon—very soon—it's going to crash."

Zhang felt a chill run down his spine. "Crash? What happens then?"

The figure's smile widened. "Every soul. Every destiny. Every karmic balance. All of it, erased in an instant. Billions of lives. Trillions of moments. Gone."

Zhang stared at the figure, his mind spinning. The system was going to crash. Everything he'd seen—the errors, the corruption, the incompetence—it wasn't just bad management. It was the death rattle of a dying machine.

And he was trapped inside it.

"I can help you," the figure said. "I can teach you how to fix the system. But there's a price."

"Name it."

The figure reached out and touched Zhang's forehead. Instantly, a vision flooded his mind—the Core Administrative Interface, rewritten from scratch. The Karmic Ledger, perfectly balanced. Every soul, every destiny, flowing through the system like a river instead of a clogged pipe.

It was beautiful. Perfect. And it belonged to someone else.

"What do you want?" Zhang asked again.

The figure's eyes glowed. "I want my throne back. I want to be remembered. And I want"—it paused, its voice dropping to a whisper—"revenge against the ones who locked me away."

Zhang didn't ask who that was. He already knew.

The immortals. The ones who ran the Heavenly Bureaucracy. The ones who had created the system, then abandoned it when it started failing.

"If I help you," Zhang said slowly, "what happens to everyone else? The souls in the queue? The ones waiting for reincarnation?"

The figure shrugged. "They'll be fine. Probably. Most of them."

"Most of them?"

"Some might get lost. A few might get stuck in limbo. But that's already happening." The figure's smile turned cruel. "The question is: do you want to save the people you can, or do you want to watch everything burn?"

Zhang closed his eyes. The vision of the perfect system still lingered in his mind. He thought about Li Mei, trapped in a dead-end job for centuries. He thought about the souls in the reincarnation queue, waiting endlessly for a destiny that might never come. He thought about himself, murdered by administrative error.

And then he thought about Supervisor Hu. About the other immortals who had let the system rot while they enjoyed their power.

"Teach me," Zhang said. "Teach me everything you know."

The figure

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Audit That Changed Everything

Zhang pulled his hand back from the sphere as if burned. The notification blinked insistently in his vision.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Process Detected]
[Source: Internal]
[Action: Soul ID 7845-B has accessed restricted records]
[Alert Level: Medium]
[Recommended Action: Investigate and]

The message cut off, replaced by a spinning hourglass icon. Zhang's heart hammered against his ribs. Someone had noticed. Someone was watching.

He needed to move. Now.

Zhang dismissed the notification and grabbed the file he'd accessed—the log of his own death. He copied it to his jade token, the data transferring in a stream of golden light. Then he closed the Core Administrative Interface and let the white void dissolve around him.

He reappeared in his cubicle, gasping. The fluorescent lights of the Celestial Office hummed overhead. His desk was exactly as he'd left it—stack of pending destiny reports, half-empty cup of immortal tea, the paperclip that kept escaping its container.

But something was different.

The air felt wrong. Static prickled across his skin, raising the hairs on his arms. Every immortal in the cubicle farm had stopped working. They sat frozen, staring at their screens, their faces blank and expressionless.

"Hey," Zhang whispered to the clerk in the next cubicle. "You okay?"

No response. The clerk didn't even blink.

Zhang stood up slowly. The entire floor had gone silent. No clicking of keyboards. No shuffling of papers. No muttered complaints about karma balances. Just silence, broken only by the hum of the office's celestial machinery.

Then the announcement came.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Mandatory System Update in Progress]
[All Personnel: Remain at your workstations]
[Estimated Duration: 17 Standard Minutes]
[Warning: Unauthorized movement will be flagged]

Seventeen minutes. Zhang had seventeen minutes before the system finished its update and whatever security protocols had been triggered came looking for him.

He looked down at his jade token. The file was there, nestled among his other records. But it wasn't enough. He needed more—evidence of Supervisor Hu's involvement, proof that the Department of Mortal Affairs had covered up the error, names of everyone who had been in on the conspiracy.

The file he'd copied only showed the error log. It didn't show who had authorized it.

Zhang made a decision. He stepped out of his cubicle.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Warning - ID 7845-B has left designated workspace]
[Flagging behavior as non-compliant]
[Recommended Action: Return immediately]

The message appeared in his vision, bordered in angry red. Zhang ignored it. He walked past the frozen immortals, their vacant stares following him like mannequins in a department store. The office stretched on, row after row of identical cubicles, identical desks, identical souls trapped in identical afterlives.

At the end of the row, he found what he was looking for: the office of Supervisor Hu.

The door was locked. Of course it was. But Zhang had spent thirty years dealing with locked doors in his mortal life—apartment doors, office doors, the door to his ex-girlfriend's heart. He knew a few tricks.

He pulled out his jade token and pressed it against the lock. The system recognized his access level and rejected him.

[ACCESS DENIED]
[Required Clearance: Tier 4 - Department Supervisor]
[Your Clearance: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)]

Zhang gritted his teeth. He needed to bypass the lock, but he didn't have time to hack the system properly. The update would finish in fifteen minutes. Maybe less.

Then he remembered.

The Core Administrative Interface had given him a new function: Timeline Adjustment. Single thread only, but still—

He pulled up the interface on his jade token. The code of reality appeared before him, a shimmering web of cause and effect. He found the thread that represented Supervisor Hu's office door. It was a thin, golden strand, connected to thousands of other threads—the key that opened it, the hands that turned the key, the decisions that led to Hu locking it in the first place.

Zhang selected the thread and adjusted it. Just slightly. Just enough.

In the original timeline, Hu had locked the door this morning before leaving for his meeting with Director Feng. But what if Hu had forgotten? What if he'd been distracted by a phone call, a sudden thought, a momentary lapse in immortal attention?

Zhang made the change.

The lock clicked open.

He pushed the door open and slipped inside. Hu's office was exactly what he'd expected—neat, organized, with a desk that looked like it had never seen a single piece of clutter. The walls were lined with shelves of jade scrolls, each labeled with meticulous calligraphy. A single window looked out onto the endless white expanse of the celestial realm.

Zhang went straight for the desk. He pulled open drawers, searching for anything useful. Reports. Memos. A drawer full of immortal snacks that Hu had probably confiscated from clerks. But nothing incriminating.

He was about to give up when he noticed something: a small crack in the wall behind the desk, hidden by a scroll about karmic accounting procedures. Zhang pushed the scroll aside and found a compartment built into the wall.

Inside was a single jade scroll, wrapped in black silk.

Zhang unrolled it. The text was written in a script he didn't recognize—ancient characters that predated the current celestial bureaucracy. But his jade token translated them automatically.

[PRIVATE MEMORANDUM]
[From: Unknown]
[To: Supervisor Hu]
[Subject: Disposal Protocol 7845-B]

The document was brief. It stated that Soul ID 7845-B (Zhang's soul) had been flagged for "administrative termination" due to "system irregularities." It instructed Hu to process the death as a routine error and to ensure that no investigation was conducted.

At the bottom was a seal. Zhang didn't recognize it, but the token identified it for him.

[SEAL: Department of Divine Oversight - Internal Affairs Division]

Zhang's blood ran cold. Internal Affairs. The celestial equivalent of police internal investigations. The department that was supposed to catch corrupt immortals and ensure the system ran smoothly.

They were the ones who had ordered his death.

The door to Hu's office burst open.

Zhang spun around, the scroll clutched in his hands. Standing in the doorway was a figure he'd never seen before—a woman in a severe black robe, her hair pulled back in a tight bun, her eyes glowing with the cold fire of celestial authority. Behind her stood two armored enforcers, their faces hidden behind golden masks.

"Zhang Wei," the woman said. Her voice was calm, measured, and utterly terrifying. "You are in possession of classified materials. Surrender them immediately."

Zhang looked at the scroll in his hands. Then back at the woman. He had two options: comply and hope for mercy, or run and hope for freedom.

He ran.

He dove past the woman, ducking under the enforcers' reaching arms, and sprinted down the hallway. The frozen immortals watched him pass, their blank faces somehow accusatory. Behind him, he heard the woman shout orders.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert Level Elevated to Critical]
[ID 7845-B has been designated as Fugitive]
[All Personnel: Report any sightings immediately]
[Security Protocols: Maximum]

The office transformed. The fluorescent lights flickered, then turned red. Alarms blared. The frozen immortals suddenly snapped to life, their eyes locking onto Zhang as he ran past. They didn't chase him—they just watched, their faces twisted into expressions of fear and betrayal.

Zhang reached the main lobby. The massive doors that led to the Department of Mortal Affairs loomed ahead. Beyond them lay the rest of the celestial bureaucracy—halls of power, offices of influence, and, somewhere, the answers he needed.

But the doors were sealed. Red barriers shimmered across their surface, blocking his path.

He turned around. The woman in black was walking toward him, her enforcers flanking her. She held up a jade token that glowed with the unmistakable light of administrative authority.

"Zhang Wei," she said. "By the authority of the Celestial Bureaucracy, I am placing you under arrest. You are charged with unauthorized system access, theft of classified materials, and interference with divine administrative processes. The penalty for these crimes is immediate soul deletion."

Zhang's hand tightened around the scroll. "You ordered my death."

The woman tilted her head. "I ordered the correction of a system error. Your continued existence is an anomaly that must be resolved."

"I'm not an anomaly," Zhang said. "I'm a person. I had a life. I had a job. I had a cat that I'm pretty sure is still waiting for me to come home."

"The cat has been rehomed," the woman said flatly.

"That's not the point!"

"The point," she said, "is that you have seen things you were never meant to see. The celestial bureaucracy runs on order, Mr. Zhang. On rules. On protocol. Your discovery of the error in your death was a minor inconvenience. Your subsequent actions have elevated that inconvenience to a threat. You cannot be allowed to continue."

Zhang looked at the scroll in his hands. It held the truth—the names, the orders, the conspiracy that had killed him. But it also held something else. At the bottom of the memorandum, beneath the seal of Internal Affairs, was a small footnote.

[NOTE: Original order countersigned by Director of Operations, Celestial Administration]

Director of Operations. Someone at the very top of the celestial hierarchy. Someone who had personally signed off on Zhang's death.

He didn't have time to wonder who it was. The woman raised her hand, and the enforcers moved forward.

Zhang did the only thing he could think of. He activated his jade token and accessed the Core Administrative Interface one more time.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Warning - Unauthorized access detected]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Current Status: Fugitive]
[Warning: Further system manipulation will result in immediate soul termination]

He ignored the warning. He found the thread that represented his current location—the lobby of the Department of Mortal Affairs—and pulled.

The world dissolved into static. The enforcers' hands reached for him, but they passed through empty air. The woman's face twisted in anger as Zhang's form flickered, then vanished.

He reappeared somewhere else. Somewhere cold. Somewhere dark.

Somewhere that smelled like paper and ink.

Zhang opened his eyes and found himself standing in a vast archive. Shelves stretched into infinity, each one loaded with jade scrolls and celestial documents. The air was thick with dust and the faint hum of preserved knowledge.

A sign hung above the entrance:

[ARCHIVES OF DIVINE RECORDS]
[Level 0 - Public Access]
[Authorized Personnel Only Beyond This Point]

Zhang let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding. He was alive. For now.

But the scroll in his hands was heavy. And the footnote at the bottom kept echoing in his mind.

Director of Operations. Celestial Administration.

Someone at the very top had wanted him dead. And now that he knew, they would stop at nothing to make sure he stayed that way.

His jade token chimed. A new message appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Classified Document Detected]
[Title: Memorandum 7845-B]
[Origin: Department of Divine Oversight - Internal Affairs Division]
[Status: Active - Marked for Immediate Destruction]

[ADDENDUM: The Director of Operations has been notified of your unauthorized access. A kill order has been issued. All celestial personnel are authorized to use lethal force.]

[

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Golden Lotus Gambit

The white void flickered.

Zhang pulled his hand back from the sphere as if burned, but the damage was done. Warning notifications cascaded across his vision like falling dominoes:

[ALERT: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[ALERT: Tracing Process...]
[ALERT: Source Identified - Department of Mortal Affairs]
[ALERT: Initiating Countermeasures]

"Not good," Zhang muttered.

He tried to disconnect, but the interface wouldn't let him go. The sphere of gears and runes had begun to spin faster, and the rhythm of its pulsing grew erratic. Lines of golden text appeared on its surface, forming words that Zhang could somehow read even though they were in no language he recognized:

*Intruder detected in the Core Administrative Interface.*
*Requesting immediate lockdown protocol.*
*All access points sealed.*

The white void around him started to solidify, turning from empty space into walls of jade and gold. The room was collapsing, compressing, trying to trap him inside.

Zhang backed away, but there was nowhere to go. The walls were closing in, and the floor beneath his feet was becoming solid. In seconds, he'd be sealed in a box of celestial material with no way out.

Think, Zhang. Think.

He pulled up his interface, scanning through every function he had access to. The [Bypass Protocol] was grayed out—too risky to use again now that the system was watching. [Destiny Modification] was too slow. [Karmic Ledger Viewing] was useless.

But there was one option he hadn't explored yet.

[Timeline Adjustment (Single Thread Only)]

The description was vague, but Zhang had spent enough time in corporate environments to know that "timeline adjustment" was bureaucrat-speak for "time travel." Probably limited, probably dangerous, but right now, it was his only way out.

He selected it.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Timeline Adjustment Initiated]
[Warning: This function is intended for correction of clerical errors only]
[Unauthorized use may result in temporal paradoxes, soul fragmentation, or spontaneous existence failure]
[Proceed?]

Zhang's fingers hovered over the confirmation button. Spontaneous existence failure. That sounded unpleasant.

But the walls were five feet away now and closing fast.

He pressed [Proceed].

The world shattered.

Zhang felt himself being pulled apart and put back together in rapid succession. He saw his own life flash before his eyes—not just the highlights, but every single moment, from birth to death, spread across an infinite canvas of time and space. He saw the moment of his death, the administrative error that caused it, the chain of events that led to his arrival in the Heavenly Bureaucracy.

And then he saw something else.

A golden lotus flower, floating in the void between timelines.

It was beautiful—impossibly so. Its petals radiated light that seemed to contain every color and none at once. And at its center, pulsing like a heartbeat, was a single, perfect pearl.

Zhang felt an overwhelming urge to reach out and touch it.

He did.

The moment his fingers brushed the pearl, he understood.

This was the source of the Heavenly Bureaucracy's power. The golden lotus was the original code—the first line of programming that had created the entire system. And the pearl was the seed, the core, the central command that governed everything.

And it was flawed.

Deep within the pearl, hidden beneath layers of divine encryption, was a crack. A tiny fracture in the foundation of reality itself. Someone had damaged it. Someone had tried to hack the system from the inside.

And whoever it was, they had failed.

The crack was spreading. Slowly, imperceptibly, but inevitably. In a few thousand years, it would reach the surface of the pearl. And when it did, the entire Heavenly Bureaucracy would collapse.

Zhang pulled his hand away.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Timeline Adjustment Complete]
[New Temporal Anchor Set]
[Current Time: 3 Hours Before Initial Access]

Zhang blinked.

He was back in the hallway outside Supervisor Hu's office. The wall clock read 9:47 AM—three hours before he'd first accessed the Core Administrative Interface.

The golden lotus was gone. The white void was gone. The sphere of gears and runes was gone.

But the memory remained.

Zhang leaned against the wall, his heart pounding. He had just seen the end of the universe. And it was caused by a bug in the celestial management system.

"Zhang Wei!"

He looked up. Supervisor Hu was standing in the doorway of his office, face red with anger.

"Why are you loitering in the hallway? Get back to your desk! The quarterly karma reports are due in two hours, and I haven't seen a single completed form from you!"

Zhang straightened up. "Yes, Supervisor Hu. Right away."

He walked past Hu and headed toward his cubicle, mind racing. The crack in the pearl. The golden lotus. The administrative error that killed him. It was all connected.

But how?

He sat down at his desk and stared at the mountain of forms in front of him. Quarterly karma reports. Destiny reconciliation spreadsheets. Reincarnation queue status updates. The same mind-numbing bureaucracy he'd been drowning in since his first day.

But now he saw it differently.

Every form, every approval, every stamp of the celestial seal—it was all part of the system. And the system was broken.

Zhang pulled out his jade token and examined it. The token was his key to the system, his interface with the bureaucracy. But it was also a leash. It tracked his access, monitored his usage, flagged his activities.

If he was going to fix the crack in the pearl, he needed to cut the leash.

He needed to go off-grid.

"Zhang Wei!"

He looked up. A courier immortal was standing at the entrance to the cubicle farm, holding a scroll sealed with golden wax.

"Message from the Department of Divine Audits. You're being summoned for a Karmic Inspection."

The cubicle farm went silent.

Zhang felt every pair of eyes in the room turn to him. Karmic Inspections were bad news. They were random audits of a clerk's work, designed to catch errors and punish incompetence.

But this wasn't random.

Someone had flagged him.

"Now?" Zhang asked.

"Now," the courier confirmed. "The Inspector is waiting in Chamber 7-B."

Chamber 7-B. The interrogation room.

Zhang stood up, ignoring the whispers and stares from his coworkers. He walked past the courier and out of the cubicle farm, heading toward the east wing of the building.

The hallways were empty. Too empty. Normally, there were at least a dozen immortals shuffling between departments, carrying scrolls and forms and celestial paperwork. But now, the corridors were silent.

Zhang's instincts screamed at him. This was a trap.

He reached Chamber 7-B and pushed open the door.

The room was exactly as he'd expected: bare walls, a single table, two chairs. A dim light hung from the ceiling, casting long shadows across the floor.

And sitting at the table, looking at him with cold, calculating eyes, was a woman he'd never seen before.

She was beautiful—impossibly so. Her hair was the color of moonlight, her skin pale as jade, her eyes deep pools of silver that seemed to contain the wisdom of ages. She wore the robes of a high-ranking celestial official, marked with the symbols of the Department of Divine Audits.

"Zhang Wei," she said, her voice smooth as silk. "Please, sit."

Zhang didn't move. "Who are you?"

"I am Inspector Ling. I'm here to conduct your Karmic Inspection."

"That's not what I asked."

A flicker of amusement crossed her face. "You're perceptive. Good. That will make this easier."

She gestured to the chair across from her. "Please. Sit. I promise I won't bite."

Zhang hesitated, then slowly walked to the table and sat down. Inspector Ling watched him with an unreadable expression.

"You accessed the Core Administrative Interface," she said. It wasn't a question.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't lie to me, Zhang Wei. I can see it in your karmic signature. You touched the golden lotus."

Zhang's blood ran cold.

"So you're here to delete my soul," he said.

Inspector Ling laughed—a sound like wind chimes in a storm. "Delete your soul? No. I'm here to offer you a job."

"What?"

She leaned forward, her silver eyes boring into his. "You saw the crack in the pearl. You know the truth. The Heavenly Bureaucracy is dying. And I need someone on the inside to help me fix it."

Zhang stared at her. "You're the one who damaged it."

"I'm the one who discovered the damage," she corrected. "And I've been trying to repair it for three thousand years. But I can't do it alone. The system is too vast, too complex. I need a partner. Someone with mortal ingenuity and celestial access."

"Why me?"

"Because you're the only one who's ever bypassed the system's security. You're the only one who's seen the crack and lived to tell about it. And you're the only one who has a personal stake in seeing this through."

She reached into her robes and pulled out a jade token—identical to his own, but glowing with a faint golden light.

"The Administrative Error that killed you," she said. "It wasn't an accident. It was sabotage. Someone in the higher echelons of the bureaucracy is trying to destabilize the system. And they used your death as a test case."

Zhang's hands clenched into fists. "Who?"

"I don't know yet. But I have a lead." She slid the token across the table. "This will give you access to the sealed records of the Celestial Personnel Department. Everything you need to find the one who ordered your death is in there."

Zhang looked at the token, then back at Inspector Ling. "And what do you get out of this?"

"The crack repaired. The system stabilized. And the satisfaction of watching the arrogant fools who run this place get their comeuppance."

She stood up, her robes flowing around her like water.

"Think about it, Zhang Wei. But don't take too long. The crack is spreading. And once it reaches the surface, it won't just destroy the Heavenly Bureaucracy. It will destroy everything."

She walked toward the door, then paused.

"Oh, and one more thing. The inspection isn't a lie. You really are being audited. I just made sure I was the one assigned to do it."

She smiled—a cold, predatory smile.

"Welcome to the resistance, Zhang Wei. Try not to die."

And then she was gone.

Zhang sat alone in the interrogation room, staring at the golden token on the table.

His hand reached out and picked it up.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Item Acquired]
[Item: Ling's Access Token (Tier 7 - Restricted)]
[Warning: Unauthorized use of this token will flag your soul for immediate termination]
[Proceed?]

Zhang's thumb hovered over the confirmation button.

He didn't have a choice.

He pressed [Proceed].

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Immortal Summons the Janitor

Zhang yanked his hand back from the sphere as if burned.

"System, close connection," he hissed.

The white void dissolved, and he was back in his cubicle, staring at his jade terminal. The screen flickered once before stabilizing into the familiar filing interface. His heart hammered against his ribs.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Terminated]

[Alert Status: Investigation Pending - Soul ID 7845-B]

[Recommended Action: Await Supervisor]

Zhang's mouth went dry. *Await Supervisor.* That wasn't a suggestion. That was a countdown.

He glanced around the office. Nothing had changed—clerks hunched over their terminals, jade tokens clattered against desks, the ambient hum of celestial bureaucracy filled the air. But Zhang felt like every pair of eyes in the room was trained on him. Paranoia, probably. Hopefully.

His fingers flew across the terminal, pulling up the file he'd accessed before the connection dropped. The data was still there, cached in his local memory—a fragment of the Core Administrative Interface, saved before the system could revoke his access.

He scanned the information. Supervisor Hu. Clerk ID 2319. The error that killed him.

But there was more. Hidden beneath the main log, buried in the metadata, was a second file. A ghost file, invisible to the standard search functions. Zhang had only caught it because the Core Interface parsed data differently than the standard terminal.

He opened it.

[CLASSIFIED: Department of Mortal Affairs - Internal Memo]

[Subject: Statistical Anomaly - Reincarnation Queue Batch 374]

[From: Office of the Heavenly Auditor - Third Division]

[To: Supervisor Hu, DMA Floor 7]

[Body:]

[The death of Soul ID 7845-B has been flagged for review. Initial assessment indicates a 0.3% deviation from projected mortality timeline. This is within acceptable variance parameters for the current fiscal quarter. However, due to the proximity of Soul ID 7845-B's reincarnation queue position to [REDACTED], further investigation is warranted. Please provide justification for the error by the next celestial audit cycle.

Failure to comply will result in the reassignment of DMA Floor 7 personnel to [REDACTED].]

Zhang read the memo three times.

*0.3% deviation. Within acceptable variance parameters.*

Someone had known. Someone had looked at the error that killed him and decided it wasn't worth fixing. A rounding error. That's what his life had been—a rounding error in the celestial budget.

And that last line. The threat. *Reassignment to REDACTED.* Whatever that destination was, it was bad enough to keep Supervisor Hu compliant. Bad enough to make him bury the truth.

Zhang's chair creaked as he leaned back. The pieces were starting to fit together, but the picture they formed was worse than he'd imagined. This wasn't just incompetence. This was a cover-up. Someone in the higher ranks of the Heavenly Bureaucracy was manipulating death records, and Zhang's soul had been collateral damage.

"Zhang Wei!"

He nearly jumped out of his skin. Supervisor Hu stood at the entrance to the cubicle maze, his face a mask of controlled fury. Behind him, two figures in white robes hovered—celestial enforcers, their faces blank and featureless, like masks carved from jade.

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said, his voice steady despite the adrenaline flooding his system. "What can I do for you?"

"Don't play dumb." Hu strode forward, his robes billowing behind him. The enforcers followed, their feet never quite touching the ground. "You accessed restricted records. The system flagged your terminal within seconds."

"I don't know what you're talking about," Zhang said. "I was just filing the quarterly destiny reports. Standard procedure."

Hu's eyes narrowed. "The Core Administrative Interface is not standard procedure."

"Core Administrative Interface?" Zhang blinked, feigning confusion. "I don't have access to that. I'm a Tier 1 clerk. I can barely access the break room schedule."

The enforcers stopped behind Hu, their blank faces angled toward Zhang. He could feel them looking at him, even though they had no eyes. It was like being stared at by a wall.

"The system detected an unauthorized connection from your terminal," Hu said. "The connection was traced to the Core Administrative Interface. That requires Tier 2 access or higher."

"Then maybe someone else used my terminal," Zhang said. "You know how it is around here. People borrow each other's desks all the time. I was at lunch."

"You don't take lunch."

"I started today. The cafeteria has a new dish—Soul Noodles with Five-Flavor Broth. Highly recommended."

Hu's jaw tightened. He knew Zhang was lying, but he couldn't prove it. The system had flagged the connection, but it hadn't recorded Zhang's face or voice. The Core Interface didn't work that way—it was all thought and intention, invisible to external monitoring.

But Hu wasn't stupid. He knew Zhang had done something. And he knew that Zhang knew something.

"Step away from your terminal," Hu said.

Zhang didn't move. "Why?"

"That's an order."

"With respect, Supervisor Hu, I'm a union member. You can't order me to leave my workstation without a valid reason."

Hu's eye twitched. "Union? There's no union in the Heavenly Bureaucracy."

"Maybe there should be." Zhang smiled. "I've been thinking about starting one. I hear the celestial workers are unhappy with their compensation packages. No vacation days. No soul-care leave. It's a real issue."

The enforcers shifted, their white robes rustling like paper. Hu held up a hand, stopping them.

"You're playing a dangerous game, Zhang Wei."

"I'm just trying to do my job."

"Your job is to file reports. Not to dig through classified data. Not to access systems you don't understand." Hu leaned in close, his voice dropping to a whisper. "Not to ask questions about things that don't concern you."

"Everything in the Heavenly Bureaucracy concerns me," Zhang said. "I'm a clerk. It's my job to be concerned."

Hu stared at him for a long moment. Then he straightened, his expression shifting from anger to something colder. Something calculated.

"Very well," he said. "If you want to play investigator, I'll give you something to investigate." He pulled a jade scroll from his sleeve and unrolled it. "I'm reassigning you. Effective immediately."

Zhang's stomach dropped. "Reassigning me?"

"To the Department of Karmic Reconciliation. Floor 13."

Zhang knew that floor. Everyone knew that floor. The Department of Karmic Reconciliation was where they sent clerks who had displeased their superiors. It was a punishment posting, a dead-end assignment in a department that dealt exclusively with the most hopeless cases—souls who had racked up so much negative karma that their destinies were permanently broken.

"And to make sure you don't cause any trouble," Hu continued, "I'm placing you on probationary status. No access to restricted records. No independent filing. Everything you do must be approved by your new supervisor."

"Who's my new supervisor?"

Hu's smile was thin and cruel. "You'll find out when you get there."

The enforcers stepped forward, their blank faces now inches from Zhang's. One of them raised a hand, and a chain of golden light materialized from its palm.

"You will come with us," the enforcer said. Its voice was flat, emotionless, like a recording played at the wrong speed.

Zhang looked at the chains, then at Hu, then back at his terminal. The cached file was still there, sitting in his local memory. If he left now, he might never get another chance to access it. But if he resisted, the enforcers would drag him away anyway, and he'd lose everything.

He held up his hands. "Fine. I'll go quietly."

The enforcer wrapped the golden chain around Zhang's wrists. It was surprisingly light—more like a suggestion than a restraint. But when Zhang tried to pull his hands apart, the chain tightened, biting into his skin.

"This way," the enforcer said.

They led him through the office, past the staring clerks, past the rows of cubicles, past the water cooler where two minor deities were arguing about the proper way to fold a prayer scroll. Zhang kept his eyes forward, refusing to meet anyone's gaze.

The elevator at the end of the hall opened as they approached. The enforcer gestured for Zhang to step inside.

"Floor 13," it said.

The elevator began to descend. The numbers on the display flickered past—12, 11, 10—and then the elevator stopped.

But not at 13.

The doors opened onto a floor Zhang had never seen before. The walls were made of black jade, veined with gold. The air was thick with incense, heavy and sweet. At the end of the hall, a massive door stood open, revealing a room filled with scrolls and maps and glowing spheres.

And sitting behind a desk at the center of the room was a woman in flowing red robes. Her hair was white, her eyes were golden, and she was staring directly at Zhang.

"Ah," she said, her voice carrying a weight that made the air vibrate. "You must be the troublemaker."

The enforcers behind Zhang bowed low. "Lady Meng. We have brought the prisoner as requested."

*Lady Meng.* Zhang's mind raced. *Lady Meng? As in Lady Meng Po? The goddess of forgetfulness? The one who serves the tea of oblivion to souls about to be reincarnated?*

She smiled, and there was nothing kind about it. "Leave us."

The enforcers vanished, dissolving into motes of golden light. The doors slammed shut behind them.

Zhang stood alone in the office of a goddess, his hands still bound in golden chains.

"Don't worry," Lady Meng said, rising from her desk. "I'm not here to punish you."

"Then why am I here?"

She walked around the desk, her robes trailing behind her like a river of blood. "Because I've been watching you, Zhang Wei. Ever since you accessed the Core Administrative Interface. Ever since you found that little file."

"You know about that?"

"I know about everything that happens in my domain." She stopped in front of him, close enough that he could smell the jasmine on her breath. "And I know what you're trying to do."

"Then you know someone killed me. An administrative error. A rounding error in the celestial budget."

"Yes."

"And you're okay with that?"

Lady Meng's golden eyes glittered. "I'm not okay with anything. That's why I called you here."

She reached out and touched the golden chains on his wrists. They dissolved instantly.

"I want to help you," she said. "But first, I need to know one thing."

"What?"

She leaned in close, her voice dropping to a whisper. "How far are you willing to go to get your revenge?"

Zhang met her golden gaze. "As far as it takes."

Her smile widened. "Good. Because what you're about to discover will change everything."

She snapped her fingers, and the room dissolved into darkness.

When the light returned, Zhang was standing in a vast chamber filled with floating screens, each one displaying a different scene—battles, births, deaths, weddings, funerals. The screens stretched into infinity, filling the space with a cacophony of light and sound.

"This is the true heart of the Heavenly Bureaucracy," Lady Meng said, appearing beside him. "The Archive of All Things."

Zhang stared at the screens. "Why are you showing me this?"

"Because you're not the only one who

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Red Envelope Protocol

Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the white void around him dissolved into a cascade of screaming numbers. The sphere of gears and runes twisted, its beautiful symmetry collapsing into chaos as warning symbols erupted across its surface.

[SYSTEM ALERT: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Initiating Security Protocol 7-Alpha]
[Lockdown in 3... 2...]

Zhang's fingers flew across the interface, pulling up command lines he'd only glimpsed during his brief training. The celestial bureaucracy had one weakness—it was built by committee. Every system had seventeen backup protocols, but they all shared the same root architecture. Find the root, and you found the bypass.

He typed: `CANCEL_PROTOCOL_7A /FORCE /ORIGIN:INTERNAL_AUDIT`

The countdown stopped at 1.

[PROTOCOL 7-ALPHA CANCELLED]
[Override Authorized: Internal Audit Division]
[Please verify your audit ID]

Zhang's grin turned wolfish. He didn't have an audit ID. But he knew someone who did. He pulled up Supervisor Hu's personnel file, cross-referenced it with the department's compliance division, and found what he was looking for: Hu had been flagged for "excessive paperwork delays" three centuries ago. The audit was still pending.

He copied Hu's old audit case number and appended `_OVERRIDE_REQUEST`.

[VERIFICATION ACCEPTED]
[Audit ID: HX-7843-PENDING]
[Welcome, Internal Audit Representative]

The sphere stabilized, and the warning symbols faded. Zhang let out a breath he didn't realize he'd been holding. That was close. Too close. He'd triggered a security protocol that should have erased his access entirely, and he'd only survived because he'd guessed the right override format.

*Wild guess*, he thought. *Or the system is designed to be exploited.*

He filed that thought away for later and turned his attention back to Hu's file. The error that killed him was right there, logged and timestamped. But something was wrong. The timestamp showed the misrouting occurred at exactly 3:47 AM celestial standard time. Yet Hu's login records showed he hadn't accessed the system until 8:15 AM that morning.

Someone else had logged in under Hu's credentials.

Zhang pulled up the access logs for Clerk ID 2319. The system dutifully displayed a list of login timestamps for the past thousand years. Every single one matched Hu's known schedule—except for the 3:47 AM login. That one had no corresponding biometric confirmation.

And it had been executed from a terminal in Department 7.

Department 7. The one that didn't officially exist.

Zhang's blood ran cold. He'd heard whispers about Department 7 during his brief time in the celestial bureaucracy—rumors about a division that handled "special adjustments" to mortal destinies. Adjustments that didn't follow karmic guidelines. Adjustments that benefited certain immortals at the expense of mortals.

He was supposed to have been one of those adjustments.

But something had gone wrong. Instead of a clean death that would have served some unknown immortal's purposes, Zhang's soul had been dumped into the wrong queue. He'd ended up in the Department of Mortal Affairs instead of whatever fate Department 7 had planned for him.

And now he'd accessed the Core Administrative Interface.

A new notification appeared, this one different from the others. It was a small, red envelope icon pulsing in the corner of his vision.

[INCOMING MESSAGE]
[Source: Unknown]
[Classification: Eyes Only]
[Subject: You've found us]

Zhang hesitated. Every instinct told him not to open it. Red envelopes in celestial bureaucracy meant one thing: a message from someone who knew they shouldn't be contacting you. It was the digital equivalent of a dead drop in a dark alley.

He opened it.

`You're asking the wrong questions, Zhang Wei. The error wasn't in the routing. The error was in the target. You weren't supposed to die that day. You were supposed to live. Someone changed the plan. And now they know you're looking.`

`- A Friend in Low Places`

The message vanished as he read it, leaving no trace. No sender ID, no routing information, no metadata. It was as if it had never existed.

Zhang stared at the empty space where the message had been. *Someone changed the plan.* That meant his death wasn't just a mistake. It was a targeted assassination. But who? And why would anyone want him dead? He was a nobody—a mid-level manager at a tech company, a borderline alcoholic, a man whose greatest achievement was successfully filing his taxes on time for three consecutive years.

Unless it wasn't about him.

He pulled up his mortal file again, searching for connections he might have missed. Family history, work records, financial transactions, medical visits. Nothing unusual. He was boring. Deliberately boring, he realized now. His whole life had been designed to be forgettable.

But there was one thing. Buried in his childhood medical records, under a section marked "Incomplete Observations," was a note: `Subject displays unusual sensitivity to probability fluctuations. Recommend further study.`

Probability fluctuations. That was celestial bureaucracy speak for "can sense when destiny is being manipulated."

Zhang's hands started shaking. He wasn't a random victim. He was a threat. Someone had identified him as a potential problem and tried to eliminate him before he could develop his abilities.

And they'd failed.

He closed the file and looked at the sphere again. The Core Administrative Interface pulsed gently, waiting for his next command. He had access to every destiny in his division, every karmic balance, every timeline adjustment. He could change anything.

But if he used that power, he'd trigger every alarm in the celestial bureaucracy. Department 7 would know exactly where he was. They'd send someone to "correct" the error permanently.

He needed a different approach.

Zhang pulled up the department's staffing roster and searched for one name: Li Mei. The elderly clerk who had been at her desk for a thousand years. The one who never complained, never took breaks, never seemed to notice the chaos around her.

The one who had left a red envelope in his file on his first day.

He found her personnel file. It was suspiciously thin. Name: Li Mei. Position: Senior Clerk. Tenure: Unknown. Performance Reviews: "Satisfactory" repeated for the past three centuries. No promotions, no demotions, no transfers. She had simply existed, unchanging, a constant in the ever-shifting bureaucracy.

Under her current assignment, there was a single line: `Awaiting Instructions from Department 7`.

Zhang's breath caught. Li Mei was Department 7's eyes in the Department of Mortal Affairs. She'd been watching him this whole time. The note in his file, the subtle guidance, the way she always seemed to be nearby when he needed information—it was all part of a larger plan.

But whose plan?

He closed the file and turned away from the sphere. He'd learned enough for one session. Any more, and he risked leaving digital footprints that even his override couldn't hide.

As he prepared to disconnect, another red envelope appeared.

`They're coming for you. Not Department 7. Someone worse. The Celestial Compliance Office. They know about your access. Hu reported you.`

`- Still Your Friend`

The Celestial Compliance Office. The immortal version of internal affairs. They didn't investigate. They didn't interview. They simply deleted. Souls, memories, entire existences—all erased with a single command.

Zhang disconnected from the interface and found himself back in his office chair. The jade token sat on his desk, still warm from use. His hands were shaking, but not from fear.

He was angry.

He'd spent his entire mortal life being pushed around by bosses, by systems, by circumstances he couldn't control. And now, even in death, someone was trying to control him. Hu, Department 7, the Celestial Compliance Office—they all wanted him to be a good little clerk who filed his reports and stayed in his lane.

Well, Zhang Wei had never been good at staying in his lane.

He picked up the jade token and walked out of his office. The main floor was quiet, the night shift clerks working silently at their stations. Hu's office door was closed, but light flickered through the frosted glass. He was still there, probably writing his report on Zhang's unauthorized access.

Zhang walked past Hu's office and stopped at Li Mei's desk. She was there, as always, her ancient fingers moving across a keyboard that seemed to predate the invention of writing itself.

"You knew," Zhang said quietly. "You knew from the beginning."

Li Mei didn't look up. "I know many things, young man. I know that the tea in the break room has gone cold. I know that Supervisor Hu has a gambling problem he thinks no one knows about. I know that the Celestial Compliance Office has already dispatched a team to this building."

She paused and finally raised her eyes to meet his. "And I know that you have exactly seventeen minutes before they arrive."

Seventeen minutes. That wasn't enough time to run. It wasn't enough time to hide. It was barely enough time to make a decision.

"What do you want?" Zhang asked. "Why are you helping me?"

Li Mei's weathered face cracked into something that might have been a smile. "Because I've been waiting for someone like you for eight hundred years. Someone who can see the code behind the curtain. Someone who isn't afraid to break the rules."

She reached into her desk and pulled out a small, white envelope. Unlike the red ones, this one was plain, unmarked, and seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it.

"This is your exit," she said, handing it to him. "Open it when you're ready to leave the building. It will take you somewhere safe."

Zhang took the envelope. It felt heavier than it should have. "And what about you?"

"I'll delay them as long as I can." She turned back to her screen. "Now go. And Zhang Wei?"

He paused at the door.

"Don't trust anyone. Not even me."

He left without another word, the white envelope burning a hole in his pocket. He had seventeen minutes to escape a building full of immortals who wanted him dead, a message from a mysterious friend who might be setting him up, and a destiny that someone had tried to steal from him.

For the first time since he'd died, Zhang Wei felt alive.

He ducked into the stairwell and opened the envelope. Inside was a single piece of paper with a string of numbers: `742.19.88.3.142.7`

An IP address. But not for a computer. For something else. Something that existed between the layers of reality.

He typed the numbers into his jade token, and the world dissolved around him.

When he opened his eyes, he was standing in a library that stretched infinitely in all directions. Bookshelves rose to impossible heights, filled with scrolls and tomes that glowed with inner light. And standing in front of him, holding a cup of tea, was a man he recognized from a single, faded photograph in his mortal file.

The man smiled. "Hello, son. I was wondering when you'd find your way here."

Zhang's father had been dead for fifteen years. But here, in this impossible library, he was very much alive.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Location Discovered]
[The Archives Between Worlds]
[Warning: This location does not exist in any celestial registry]
[Your presence here has been noted by parties unknown]
[Estimated time before discovery: Unknown]

[END OF CHAPTER 15]

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Immortal Who Forgot to Die

Zhang yanked his hand back from the glowing sphere as if burned. The alert flashed in his vision, pulsing red.

[INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL INITIATED]
[Investigating Officer: Unknown]
[Estimated Time Until Contact: 3... 2... 1...]

The white void dissolved. Zhang found himself back in his cubicle, staring at the jade token on his desk. The warning had already faded from his interface, but his heart was still hammering against his ribs.

Someone was coming.

He had seconds. Maybe less.

Zhang grabbed the token and shoved it into his robe pocket. Then he yanked open his desk drawer and pulled out the stack of unresolved destiny reports that Hu had thrown at him earlier. He spread them across his workspace, grabbed a brush, and began writing furiously.

The footsteps approached.

"Zhang Wei."

Zhang looked up to find a woman standing at the entrance to his cubicle. She was tall, with silver hair that cascaded down her back like frozen waterfalls. Her robes were immaculate white, unmarked by any rank insignia. But the way she held herself—perfect posture, eyes that seemed to look through him rather than at him—told Zhang everything he needed to know.

This was not a clerk.

"I'm sorry," Zhang said, forcing confusion into his voice. "Can I help you? I'm very busy. Supervisor Hu needs these reports by—"

"I know who you are." She stepped into the cubicle. The air grew cold. "And I know what you did."

Zhang's brush paused. He kept his expression neutral. "I file reports. I process destiny requests. If you have a complaint about my work, you'll need to take it up with—"

"I'm not here about your work." She leaned down, placing both hands on his desk. Her silver eyes bore into his. "I'm here about the administrative error that killed you."

The words hung in the air like a guillotine blade.

Zhang's mind raced. Options: Deny everything. Feign ignorance. Try to delete the token. Run. None of them would work.

"I don't know what you're talking about," he said.

"Liar." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I've been monitoring the Celestial Management System since the day you accessed those records. You're very clever, Zhang Wei. Clever enough to find the hole. Clever enough to bypass the standard protocols. But not clever enough to hide."

Zhang's hand drifted toward his pocket. "If you're going to report me—"

"I'm not here to report you."

He stopped.

The woman straightened, brushing a strand of silver hair from her face. "My name is Xian Nu. I'm a Senior Auditor in the Department of Celestial Compliance. And I need your help."

Zhang stared at her. "You need my help."

"Yes."

"You want the dead guy who just discovered a bug in the system to help you."

"Correct."

"With what, exactly?"

Xian Nu glanced around the cubicle, as if checking for eavesdroppers. Then she leaned in close and lowered her voice. "There's an immortal who forgot to die."

Zhang blinked. "Come again?"

"His name is Li Feng. Former cultivator. Achieved immortality approximately three hundred years ago. Should have ascended to the Heavenly Court upon his death fifty years ago. But according to our records, he never died."

"Maybe he's still alive?"

"He's not. We tracked his soul signature. It faded from the mortal realm forty-seven years ago. By all accounts, he should have arrived at the Reincarnation Department for processing. But he never showed up."

Zhang considered this. "Maybe he got lost? The bureaucracy isn't exactly user-friendly."

"The Department of Mortal Affairs has a 99.8% success rate for soul retrieval. Souls don't get lost." Xian Nu's eyes narrowed. "Someone intercepted him. Someone with access to the Celestial Management System. Someone who knew how to reroute a soul without leaving a trace."

"And you think I can help you find out who?"

"I think you're the only person in this entire bureaucracy who has both the access and the willingness to break the rules." She pulled a jade slip from her sleeve and placed it on his desk. "This contains everything we know about Li Feng's disappearance. Take it. Study it. And when you're ready to help, find me in the Department of Celestial Compliance. Office 774."

Zhang looked at the jade slip. Then back at Xian Nu. "Why me?"

"Because everyone else I've asked has either refused or reported me to their superiors." Her smile turned bitter. "The Heavenly Bureaucracy is built on loyalty, Zhang Wei. Loyalty to the system. Loyalty to the chain of command. Loyalty to the lie that everything works perfectly. But you've already seen the cracks. You've already touched the truth."

She turned and walked away. Her footsteps echoed through the silent office, fading into the distant hum of celestial machinery.

Zhang stared at the jade slip for a long moment. Then he picked it up.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Item Acquired]
[Item: Li Feng Case File (Encrypted)]
[Source: Xian Nu - Senior Auditor, Department of Celestial Compliance]
[Warning: Unauthorized possession of this file is a Level 7 violation. Penalty: Soul Erasure.]

Soul erasure. That was new.

Zhang pocketed the slip next to the jade token. Two pieces of evidence. Two paths into the heart of the system's corruption. Two chances to get himself killed.

He liked those odds.

The rest of the shift passed without incident. Zhang filed reports, processed destiny requests, and pretended not to notice the way Supervisor Hu kept glancing at him from across the department. The investigation alert had apparently been dealt with—or at least, no one had come to drag him away yet.

At the end of his shift, Zhang logged out and returned to his living quarters. The room was as sparse as he'd left it: a cot, a desk, a single window that looked out onto an endless expanse of clouds. But tonight, it felt different. Tonight, it felt like a prison cell.

He sat down at the desk and pulled out both items: the jade token and the encrypted case file.

Time to work.

He activated the token first. The system interface bloomed before him, displaying the administrative functions he'd unlocked. Destiny modification. Karmic ledger viewing. Timeline adjustment. And the redacted option, still tantalizingly out of reach.

But there was something else now. A new function, hidden in the depths of the menu:

[USER NOTE: Access to Department of Celestial Compliance - Pending Authorization]

She'd already granted him partial access. Smart. That meant she was serious about the partnership.

Zhang closed the token and turned to the case file. The encryption was impressive—he'd need to bypass at least three layers of celestial security to read the contents. But he'd spent his mortal life cracking software protections. This was just a different kind of code.

An hour later, he was in.

The file was a masterpiece of bureaucratic obfuscation. Li Feng, immortal cultivator, had been scheduled for ascension fifty-three years ago. His death had been recorded, his soul extracted, his reincarnation queue assigned. But somewhere between extraction and arrival, the trail went cold.

Zhang scrolled through the records. There were gaps—periods where the monitoring logs simply stopped. Not erased, not corrupted. Just... blank. As if someone had reached into the system and pulled the plug.

He checked the timestamps. The gaps corresponded exactly with the tenure of one particular official: a mid-level administrator in the Department of Mortal Affairs named Zhao Jian.

The same Zhao Jian who had been Supervisor Hu's predecessor.

Zhang's blood ran cold. He pulled up Hu's file. Sure enough, there it was: Hu had been promoted to department head immediately after Zhao Jian's sudden retirement. No explanation. No review. Just a quiet transfer of power.

This wasn't a coincidence. This was a conspiracy.

He reached for the jade token to dig deeper, but his hand froze mid-motion.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Access Detected]
[Source: Your living quarters]
[Action: File 774-B has been opened]
[Alert Level: Critical]

Zhang whirled around.

The door to his room was open.

Standing in the doorway was a man he'd never seen before. He was tall, broad-shouldered, with a face that looked like it had been carved from stone. His robes were black, unmarked by any celestial insignia. And in his hand, he held a staff of pure jade that hummed with barely contained power.

"Zhang Wei," the man said. His voice was flat. Emotionless. "You have been found in possession of classified materials. By order of the Celestial Compliance Department, you are hereby placed under arrest."

Zhang's hand closed around the jade token. "I don't think so."

"I'm not asking." The man took a step forward. The air pressure in the room spiked. "Surrender the token and the file. Come quietly. Or I will be forced to use lethal force."

Lethal force. In the afterlife. That was a joke.

But Zhang wasn't laughing.

He looked at the token in his hand. At the file on his desk. At the man in the doorway. Three heartbeats. Three options.

Run. Fight. Talk.

He chose option four.

"Do you know what's in this file?" Zhang asked.

The man paused. "Irrelevant."

"Irrelevant?" Zhang laughed. "An immortal who disappeared from the system. A department head who retired under suspicious circumstances. A trail of blank logs that leads straight to the current Supervisor of Mortal Affairs. And you're telling me that's irrelevant?"

The man's expression didn't change. But his grip on the staff tightened.

"I'm willing to bet," Zhang continued, "that if we dig a little deeper, we'll find even more. Maybe a few other immortals who went missing. Maybe some souls that were rerouted for personal gain. Maybe even proof that the Heavenly Bureaucracy isn't as perfect as everyone pretends it is."

The man said nothing.

"So here's my question." Zhang stood up slowly, keeping the token in his hand. "Are you here to arrest me because I broke the rules? Or are you here to silence me because I found the truth?"

The room fell silent. The humming of the staff grew louder.

Then the man smiled.

It was not a friendly smile.

"Clever," he said. "Very clever. But cleverness won't save you from what's coming."

He raised the staff.

Zhang activated the token.

[COMMAND: Emergency Protocol - System Override]
[Verification: Soul ID 7845-B]
[Authorization: Bypass Active]
[Initiating: Temporal Stasis Field]

The world froze.

The man in the doorway stopped mid-swing. The staff's glow became motionless. Even the clouds outside the window ceased to move.

Zhang let out a shaky breath. He had seconds—maybe minutes—before the system noticed the unauthorized override and shut it down.

He grabbed the case file, shoved it into his robe, and ran.

Past the frozen man. Through the empty hallways. Down staircases that spiraled into infinity. Past offices filled with statues of immortals caught in mid-motion.

He didn't stop running until he reached a door he'd never seen before.

It was made of black iron, covered in runes that pulsed with a sickly green light. Above it, a sign read:

[DEPARTMENT OF CELESTIAL COMPLIANCE]
[RESTRICTED ACCESS]
[LEVEL 7 CLEARANCE REQUIRED]

Zhang's hand hovered

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Immortal Who Signed His Name Wrong

Zhang barely had time to close the restricted records before a knock came at his cubicle wall.

"Junior Clerk Zhang."

He turned to find a woman he'd never seen before. She was tall, with hair so silver it looked like liquid mercury, and her robes were embroidered with characters that shifted and writhed like living things. Her eyes were the color of old jade, and they regarded him with the cold assessment of a butcher sizing up meat.

"I am Inspector Mei, Department of Internal Affairs." She didn't smile. "You've been flagged for accessing records beyond your clearance level. I'm here to conduct a preliminary interview."

Zhang's mind raced. The system had snitched on him within minutes. Either they had automated monitoring that was surprisingly effective, or someone had been watching him specifically.

"I was just trying to understand my death," Zhang said, keeping his voice steady. "The token showed me there was an error. I wanted to see the details."

Mei's expression didn't change. "The token shows what the system determines you need to see. Nothing more. Nothing less." She produced a scroll from her sleeve and unrolled it with a flick of her wrist. "My records indicate you attempted to access Batch 374's error log. That file is classified Tier 3. You are Tier 2."

"Provisional Tier 2," Zhang corrected.

"Provisional is still Tier 2." Mei rolled the scroll back up. "The standard penalty for unauthorized access is demotion and thirty years of remedial data entry. However, given the circumstances of your... unusual hiring process, Internal Affairs has decided to be lenient."

Zhang didn't trust leniency. In his experience, when bureaucracy offered mercy, it usually meant they wanted something.

"What's the catch?"

Mei's lips twitched. It might have been a smile. "You've been assigned to a special project. The Department of Karmic Balancing has a backlog that requires immediate attention. You'll be working with Senior Archivist Chen."

Zhang recognized the name. Chen was legendary in the department—not for competence, but for having survived eighteen attempts on his life by various deities who were unhappy with his filing decisions. The man had been a clerk for three thousand years and had never been promoted. That took either incredible dedication or incredible incompetence.

"What kind of backlog?"

"Inconsistencies in the Record of Merits and Demerits for the Eastern Azure Dragon lineage." Mei's voice dropped slightly. "There seems to be a discrepancy regarding the current Dragon King's cultivation advancement. Someone may have... accelerated his progress through improper means."

Zhang felt a chill run down his spine. The Eastern Azure Dragon was one of the Four Symbols, a primordial being whose power rivaled the Heavenly Emperor himself. If someone had been messing with his karmic records, that wasn't just a paperwork error—it was a potential celestial war.

"And you want me to investigate?"

"We want you to organize the paperwork so that Senior Archivist Chen can investigate." Mei's tone made it clear that "organize" meant "do all the actual work while Chen takes the credit." "This is a probationary assignment. If you perform well, the unauthorized access will be expunged from your record."

"And if I don't perform well?"

Mei's smile finally appeared. It was not comforting. "The Hell of Unending Data Entry needs a new supervisor. The previous one was recently... reassigned."

She left without another word, her silver hair trailing behind her like a ghost.

Zhang sat in his cubicle for a long moment, processing what had just happened. He'd been caught, but instead of punishment, he'd been given a high-priority assignment that involved one of the most powerful beings in existence. That wasn't mercy. That was a setup.

He was being used as bait.

Someone wanted to see if the Dragon King's records were being tampered with, but they didn't want to risk their own people. So they threw a disposable junior clerk at the problem. If Zhang succeeded, great. If he died, well, he was already dead anyway.

"Typical," Zhang muttered. "Even the afterlife has corporate politics."

He pulled up the personnel directory and searched for Senior Archivist Chen. The file was surprisingly thin for someone who'd been around for three millennia. Born in the Warring States period, died at the age of ninety-two after successfully arguing with a mountain god over land rights. Hired into the Heavenly Bureaucracy as a filing clerk. Never promoted. No commendations. No disciplinary actions. Just... nothing.

Three thousand years of doing the same job, and the man had left absolutely no mark on the system.

That was either the most impressive mediocrity Zhang had ever seen, or it was a deliberate camouflage.

He found Chen in the Archive of Forgotten Prayers, a dusty subsection of the department that no one visited because it contained all the prayers that had been answered but then rescinded. The room was filled with scrolls that wept ink, their contents constantly rewriting themselves as the universe changed its mind.

Chen was a small man with a face like a dried persimmon and the kind of posture that came from a lifetime of hunched-over desk work. He was currently engaged in a staring contest with a scroll that kept trying to slither away from him.

"Ah, the new assistant," Chen said without looking up. "Good. Hold this scroll down. It's being difficult."

Zhang grabbed the scroll. It pulsed with a faint warmth, like a living thing trying to escape. "Inspector Mei said we're investigating the Dragon King's records."

"Mmm." Chen pulled a brush from behind his ear and began making annotations on the scroll. Each stroke of ink glowed briefly before sinking into the paper. "The Eastern Azure Dragon. Fifth generation. Currently at the Ninth Heavenly Tribulation stage. Expected to break through to the Dao Ascension realm within the century."

"That's unusually fast for a Dragon King, isn't it?"

Chen's brush paused. "You know about dragon cultivation?"

"I was a xianxia nerd in my mortal life. Read hundreds of novels." Zhang shrugged. "The Eastern Azure Dragon lineage usually takes millennia to advance through each tribulation stage. The current king is only three thousand years old. He should be at the Sixth or Seventh Tribulation at most."

"Indeed." Chen resumed his writing. "And yet, the records show he has completed eight tribulations in the past five hundred years. A pace that is... unprecedented."

"Impossible."

"Improbable. Not impossible." Chen finally looked up. His eyes were the pale gray of morning mist, and they held a depth that suggested he knew far more than he let on. "There are ways to accelerate cultivation. Some are legitimate—heavenly treasures, fortunate encounters, the blessing of a higher power. Others are... less legitimate."

"You mean the stealing of karmic merit from other beings."

"I mean exactly that." Chen rolled up the now-docile scroll and tucked it into his sleeve. "The Dragon King's records show an abnormal number of 'fortunate encounters' that conveniently occurred right before each tribulation. A lucky pill here, a hidden realm there, an ancient master who happened to leave him his life's work... The pattern is too perfect to be natural."

Zhang's mind was already racing ahead. "So someone's been feeding him opportunities. But who would have the power to arrange that kind of thing?"

"The question is not who. The question is why." Chen stood up and began walking through the stacks of weeping scrolls. "Follow me. There's something I need to show you."

They wound through the Archive of Forgotten Prayers, past scrolls that whispered forgotten promises and tablets that glowed with the faint light of broken oaths. Finally, they reached a section that was cordoned off with red silk ropes.

Chen produced a key made of starlight and unlocked an invisible barrier. The air shimmered, revealing a single scroll floating in the center of the space. It was made of material Zhang had never seen before—something between jade and liquid, constantly flowing and reforming.

"This is the Original Record of Destiny for the Eastern Azure Dragon lineage," Chen said. "It was written at the beginning of time and cannot be altered by any force in the universe."

"Then why is it here?"

"Because someone tried to alter it." Chen pointed to a faint crack running through the scroll's surface. "They failed, of course. But the attempt itself is a crime of the highest order. Tampering with the Original Record is punishable by soul annihilation."

Zhang leaned closer to examine the crack. It pulsed with a faint golden light, and when he focused on it, he could see traces of something that looked like code.

Wait. Code.

"Chen, what's the celestial system built on?"

"What do you mean?"

"The core system. The one that processes all the destinies and karma. What language is it written in?"

Chen frowned. "No one knows. It's always been there. The original architects of the Heavenly Bureaucracy created it before the concept of time existed."

Zhang's heart began to pound. He reached out and touched the crack, and instantly, the code flooded into his mind.

It wasn't ancient. It wasn't divine. It was Python.

Specifically, it was a Python script that had been compiled into heavenly law, with a single line of code that was causing the error:

```
if destiny.faster_cultivation == True:
    log_error("Unauthorized acceleration detected")
    # BUG: Error log not being sent to Internal Affairs
    # FIX: Add send_notification(InternalAffairs, error)
```

Someone had introduced a bug into the very fabric of reality. A deliberate one. And they'd covered their tracks so well that even the Heavenly Bureaucracy hadn't noticed for centuries.

Zhang pulled his hand back as if burned.

"Chen," he said carefully, "I think I know what's happening. But you're not going to believe me."

Chen's mist-gray eyes met his. "Try me."

"The system isn't divine. It's software. And someone has been hacking it."

For a long moment, Chen said nothing. Then he reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small object—a jade token, identical to the one Zhang had received, but covered in scratches and cracks.

"I know," Chen said quietly. "I've been trying to fix it for three thousand years."

The scroll behind them suddenly flared with light. A notification appeared in the air, hovering like a ghost:

[SYSTEM ALERT: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Source: Junior Clerk Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Secondary Source: Senior Archivist Chen - ID 0003-A]
[Response: Immediate Termination Protocol Activated]
[Time Until Execution: 5... 4... 3...]

"Run," Chen said.

Zhang didn't need to be told twice. They bolted through the Archive as alarms began to blare, the very walls of the Heavenly Bureaucracy turning against them.

Behind them, the Original Record of Destiny let out a shriek that sounded like reality tearing apart.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Audit That Wasn't

Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the void around him shattered like glass.

He was back in his office, slumped over his desk, the jade token cold against his chest. The fluorescent lights hummed their eternal, soul-crushing hum. A stack of forms had materialized on his desk overnight—correction, it was still the same night. Time worked differently here. Or maybe it didn't work at all.

His head throbbed. That was new. Dead people weren't supposed to get headaches.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert Neutralized]

[Source: Internal Investigation Division]

[Status: False Positive - Routine Maintenance]

[Your access level has been noted. Continue your duties.]

Zhang stared at the message. False positive. Routine maintenance. The system had flagged him, investigated him, and then... let him go. That was either incredibly lucky or incredibly suspicious.

He checked his access level. Still Tier 2. Still Senior Clerk (Provisional). But something had changed. A new tab had appeared in his interface: [Karmic Ledger - Supervisor Review Pending].

Supervisor Hu.

Zhang's stomach dropped. Hu had seen the alert. Hu knew he'd accessed restricted records. And instead of confronting him, Hu had done nothing. That was worse. That meant Hu was waiting.

"Zhang Wei!"

The voice cracked through the office like a thunderbolt. Zhang looked up to see Supervisor Hu standing in his doorway, face the color of fermented tofu.

"Conference room. Now."

Zhang grabbed his jade token and followed. The other clerks didn't even look up. They'd learned long ago that looking up meant getting volunteered for extra shifts.

The conference room was a cramped space with a table that had seen better centuries. Hu stood at the head, arms crossed, a document floating in the air beside him.

"Close the door."

Zhang complied. The moment it clicked shut, Hu activated a privacy barrier. The runes on the walls glowed once, then faded.

"Sit."

Zhang sat.

"I know what you did."

Zhang kept his face neutral. "I don't know what you're talking about, Supervisor."

"Don't play games with me, boy." Hu's voice dropped to a whisper. "You accessed the Core Administrative Interface. You pulled up the error logs from your own death. You saw the records."

Silence.

"You're not going to deny it?"

"Would it help?"

"No."

"Then why would I bother?"

Hu's eye twitched. For a moment, Zhang thought the old man might actually explode. But instead, Hu did something unexpected. He laughed.

It was a dry, brittle sound, like paper crumbling.

"You know," Hu said, sitting down heavily across from Zhang, "I've been in this department for three thousand years. Three thousand years of filing, processing, auditing. I've seen clerks come and go. Most of them never figure it out. They just... accept."

"Accept what?"

"That the system works." Hu leaned forward. "But you? You figured it out in three days. Three days, Zhang Wei. That's a record."

Zhang's mind raced. This wasn't the confrontation he'd expected. "Figured what out exactly?"

"The flaw." Hu gestured at the document floating beside him. "The administrative error that killed you? It wasn't an accident."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "What?"

"It was a test."

The word hit him like a physical blow. "A test."

"A test for the new system. The Celestial Management System had a vulnerability—a bug in the reincarnation queue processing. The higher-ups wanted to know if it could be exploited. So they identified a target."

"Me."

"You." Hu's voice was flat. "You were chosen because you were disposable. No family. No connections. A mid-level programmer with no one who would notice when you suddenly dropped dead of an aneurysm at your desk."

Zhang's hands were shaking. He forced them still. "And the test?"

"You were supposed to die, enter the queue, and get processed normally. The bug would have been triggered, and the system would have flagged it for patching. But something went wrong."

"Wrong how?"

"The bug wasn't a bug." Hu pulled up another document. "It was a backdoor. Someone had inserted code into the reincarnation queue—code that allowed unauthorized access to the Core Administrative Interface. The test was supposed to be routine. Instead, it revealed that the system had been compromised."

Zhang's mind went to the sphere. The gears. The runes. The access he'd gained. "Someone wanted me to find it."

"Yes."

"Who?"

Hu's face went pale. "I don't know. And that's the problem. The higher-ups think I'm involved. They've assigned an internal investigation to my department. If they find out that a Tier 1 clerk accessed the Core Interface..."

"They'll blame you."

"They'll delete me." Hu's voice cracked. "Three thousand years of service, and they'll delete me like a corrupted file."

Zhang sat back. The pieces were falling into place. Hu wasn't threatening him. Hu was afraid. Afraid of losing his existence because of something Zhang had done.

"What do you want from me?"

"I want you to fix it."

"Fix what?"

"The backdoor. The records. Everything." Hu slid a jade slip across the table. "This contains the original code from the reincarnation queue. The same code that was supposed to process your death. If you can find out who modified it—who inserted the backdoor—we can clear my name."

"And if I refuse?"

Hu's expression hardened. "Then I'll have no choice but to report you for unauthorized system access. The penalty is soul deletion. And I don't think you want to find out what happens when a soul is deleted from existence."

Zhang picked up the jade slip. It was warm, pulsing with stored data. "You're blackmailing me."
