"I'm giving you a choice. Help me, and I'll make sure your position is secured. You'll have access, resources, protection. Refuse..." Hu spread his hands.

"One condition."

"Name it."

"I want to see my file. The real one. Not the censored version. I want to know who I was before I died. Everything."

Hu hesitated. Then he nodded. "Done."

They shook hands. It felt like signing a deal with a demon.

Back at his desk, Zhang inserted the jade slip into his terminal. The screen flickered, then displayed a wall of code. He'd seen programming languages before, but this was something else entirely. Ancient runes mixed with modern syntax, floating in a structure that defied mortal logic.

He started reading.

The reincarnation queue code was elegant, designed with the precision of a master craftsman. But there, buried in the middle, was a section that didn't belong. A block of code written in a language Zhang didn't recognize—something older, darker, pulsing with malicious intent.

He tried to trace it back to its source. The trail led through department after department, each one redirecting to another. Finance. Logistics. Heavenly Records. The Celestial Bureaucracy was a labyrinth, and someone had buried a minotaur in its heart.

Hours passed. The code was a puzzle, and Zhang was good at puzzles. He'd spent his mortal life solving them. This was just another programming challenge, albeit one with existential stakes.

And then he found it.

The backdoor code originated from a single source. A terminal in the Department of Heavenly Justice. Specifically, from a user account that shouldn't exist.

[User: Admin_Void]

[Status: Active]

[Last Access: 3 Days Ago]

[Access Level: Tier 0 - Celestial Administrator]

Three days ago. The same day Zhang had died. The same day the test had been run.

Someone in Heavenly Justice had killed him. Someone with access to the highest level of the Celestial Bureaucracy.

"The Hell of Unending Data Entry was a mercy compared to this, Zhang muttered, staring at the screen. The discovery changed everything. This wasn't just a bureaucratic error. This was a conspiracy.

His terminal pinged. A new message.

[FROM: Internal Investigation Division]

[SUBJECT: Your Test Results]

[Congratulations, Clerk Zhang Wei. You've passed the initial screening. Your next assignment will be delivered shortly. Please remain at your workstation.]

Zhang's blood ran cold. The test. The screening. They knew. They'd been watching the entire time.

He looked up at the clock on the wall. Still midnight. Still the same night. But everything had changed.

His terminal pinged again. A new file had appeared on his desktop. He opened it.

A single image: a photo of Supervisor Hu, taken from a security camera. Beneath it, a timestamp. And beneath that, a single line of text:

[Cause of Termination: Knowledge of Classified Information]

Zhang's hands flew to the jade token. He pulled up Hu's status.

[Status: Terminated]

[Reason: Administrative Discretion]

[Time of Termination: 2 Minutes Ago]

In the time it had taken Zhang to trace the code, Supervisor Hu had been deleted.

Gone. Three thousand years, erased like a typo.

And Zhang was next.

He felt it before he saw it—a shift in the air, a change in the cosmic pressure. The office around him flickered. The other clerks froze mid-motion, their forms becoming transparent.

A figure materialized at the front of the room.

She was tall, dressed in a flowing robe of pure white, her face obscured by a veil of light. She carried a scroll that glowed with the heat of a dying star.

"Clerk Zhang Wei."

Her voice was calm. Final.

"In accordance with Article 47, Section 3 of the Celestial Administrative Code, you have been found guilty of unauthorized system access, conspiracy to undermine bureaucratic protocol, and possession of classified information."

Zhang's mouth went dry. "Who are you?"

"I am the Auditor. And I am here to deliver your verdict."

She unrolled the scroll.

"By order of the Celestial High Court, your soul is hereby sentenced to immediate deletion. All records of your existence will be expunged. Your name will be forgotten. Your deeds will be unmade. You will cease to have ever been."

The runes on the walls began to glow red.

"No appeals," the Auditor continued. "No exceptions. This verdict is final."

Zhang looked at his terminal. At the jade slip. At the code that had condemned him.

He had seconds. Maybe less.

His hand moved to the jade token. To the backdoor. To the code that Admin_Void had used to kill him.

If he was going to be deleted anyway...

He selected the code. Modified it. Redirected it.

The Auditor raised her hand. The runes flared.

"Goodbye, Clerk Zhang Wei."

"I don't think so."

Zhang slammed his palm on the terminal.

The world went white.

And in the void between existence and non-existence, a new message appeared:

[SYSTEM ALERT: Unauthorized Soul Transfer Detected]

[Origin: Department of Mortal Affairs - Terminal ID 7845-B]

[Destination: [REDACTED]]

[Status: In Progress]

[Time to Completion: 3... 2... 1...]

[Transfer Complete]

[Welcome to the Department of Heavenly Justice, Clerk Zhang Wei.]

Zhang opened his eyes.

He was standing in a room made of pure obsidian, surrounded by floating scrolls and burning seals. A desk sat in front of him, carved from the bones of forgotten gods. And on the desk, a single document:

[POSITION: Junior Auditor, Internal Investigation Division]

[START DATE: Immediately]

[PREVIOUS HOLDER: Admin_Void]

[NOTE: This position has been vacant for 3 days

title: The Celestial Performance Review
content:
Zhang Wei pulled his hand back from the glowing sphere as if burned. The system had detected him. Of course it had. Nothing in the Heavenly Bureaucracy went unnoticed for long—that was literally the point of the entire organization.

But the records were already burned into his memory. Supervisor Hu. Clerk ID 2319. The man responsible for Zhang's death was the same man who now held his performance reviews.

"Fascinating, isn't it?"

Zhang spun around. A woman stood in the white void, maybe ten feet away. She wore a traditional Hanfu that shifted colors as she moved—from deep purple to midnight blue to absolute black. Her hair was pulled back in a severe bun, and she held a tablet made of what looked like solid jade.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Identity Detected]
[Name: Li Mei - Senior Auditor, Department of Celestial Compliance]
[Access Level: Tier 5 - Regional Supervisor]
[Relationship: Unknown]

"Senior Auditor Li," Zhang said, bowing slightly. "I was just—"

"Accessing restricted records that your clearance level shouldn't allow." Li Mei's voice was calm, almost bored. "I've been watching you since you bypassed the Death Review Protocol. That was clever. Who taught you?"

"No one. I just—"

"Liar." She took a step closer. "The Celestial Management System runs on principles that take centuries for even gifted immortals to understand. You've been dead for three days. Either you're a once-in-a-millennium genius, or someone's helping you."

Zhang held her gaze. "Maybe I'm just really motivated."

Li Mei's lips twitched—almost a smile. "Motivation doesn't break encryption. But you know what does? A backdoor." She tapped her tablet. "I've been tracking unauthorized access patterns for six months. Every time, the trail leads to the same place: the Department of Mortal Affairs. Your department."

"You think someone in my division is hacking the system?"

"I think someone in your division is *running* the system," Li Mei said. "And I think you were brought here to be their pawn."

The words hit Zhang like a physical blow. A pawn. Was that all he was? Some celestial chess piece moved by forces he couldn't see?

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]
[Quest: The Auditor's Gambit]
[Objective: Help Senior Auditor Li Mei investigate unauthorized system access]
[Reward: Permanent Tier 3 access, Immunity from one administrative violation]
[Warning: Accepting this quest will flag you as a compliance informant. Your relationship with Department of Mortal Affairs will be permanently altered.]

"Don't answer yet." Li Mei held up a hand. "Consider the implications. If you accept, you'll be working against your own department. Your colleagues will become your suspects. Your supervisor will become your target."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then I delete your memory of this conversation and report you for unauthorized system access. You'll be demoted to Heavenly Custodial Services. The toilets don't clean themselves, Clerk Zhang."

Zhang laughed. He couldn't help it. "So either way, I'm screwed?"

"Welcome to the Heavenly Bureaucracy." Li Mei's almost-smile became a real one. "We've been perfecting this system for ten thousand years. We're very good at making sure everyone loses."

He looked at the quest notification. Then he looked at the memories of his death—still fresh, still burning. Supervisor Hu's face when he'd yelled about filing errors. The way Hu had conveniently forgotten to mention the mistake.

"I'll do it," Zhang said.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Quest Accepted]
[New Status: Compliance Informant - Level 1]
[Warning: Your activities are now being monitored by multiple departments]
[Recommendation: Proceed with extreme caution]

"Excellent." Li Mei tapped her tablet, and the white void dissolved around them. Zhang found himself back in his cubicle, sitting in front of his celestial terminal. The clock on the wall had moved—four hours had passed, not the few minutes he'd experienced in the Core Administrative Interface.

Time dilation. Of course.

"Your first task," Li Mei's voice whispered directly into his ear, "is to access Supervisor Hu's personal files. Find out who he's been communicating with outside the department."

"How am I supposed to do that? He's Tier 6. I'm Tier 2."

"Use your initiative. That's why I recruited you."

The connection cut. Zhang stared at his terminal, mind racing. Hu's personal files. That would require access to the Departmental Archive, which was protected by—he checked his system knowledge—a Level 4 encryption protocol. Way beyond his clearance.

But Hu had a terminal in his office. And Zhang had noticed something during their earlier confrontation: Hu never locked his terminal when he left the room. The man was arrogant, confident that no one in his department would dare touch his things.

Arrogance was a vulnerability.

Zhang stood up and walked toward Hu's office. The cubicle farm was relatively quiet—most clerks were at lunch, taking advantage of the one-hour break the system mandated. He passed by Chen's desk; the old clerk was snoring, head pillowed on a stack of destiny reports.

Hu's office door was closed. Zhang knocked.

No answer.

He tried the handle. Unlocked.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Warning]
[You are about to enter a restricted area]
[Action: Unauthorized access to Supervisor Hu's office]
[Penalty if caught: Immediate demotion to Hell of Unending Data Entry]
[Confirm?]

Zhang shoved the notification aside and slipped inside.

The office was a mess. Papers everywhere, half-empty teacups, a jade plant that looked like it hadn't been watered in centuries. Hu's terminal sat on the desk, screen dark but clearly active—the power light was still on.

Zhang sat in Hu's chair. It was uncomfortably warm.

He reached for the terminal, but paused. A thin thread, almost invisible, stretched across the keyboard. A trap. Hu wasn't as careless as he seemed.

Carefully, Zhang pulled out his own jade token—the one he'd used to bypass the Death Review Protocol. He held it close to the thread. The token vibrated, and a message appeared:

[Item: Karmic Tripwire]
[Effect: Alerts owner when unauthorized access is attempted]
[Disarm Method: Requires Tier 4 clearance or specific bypass code]

Tier 4 clearance. He didn't have that.

But he had something better: a backdoor.

Zhang focused on the thread, visualizing the code that made it up. It was simple, really—just a conditional trigger connected to Hu's personal notification system. If he could change the condition...

He reached out with his consciousness, touching the thread's code. It was like playing a piano that only existed in his mind. He found the notification parameter and changed it. Instead of alerting Hu, it would now alert... Zhang smiled. It would alert Li Mei.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Karmic Tripwire Disarmed]
[Alert Redirected to: Senior Auditor Li Mei]
[Warning: This modification has been logged]
[Department of Celestial Compliance will be notified of all future tripwire activations]

"Perfect," Zhang muttered.

He accessed Hu's terminal. The screen lit up with a password prompt. Zhang typed: "SupervisorHu123".

Access granted.

"You have got to be kidding me," Zhang said. "That's the password?"

Hu's personal files were a treasure trove. Budget reports, performance reviews, disciplinary actions. But Zhang was looking for something specific: communications.

He found them in a folder labeled "PRIVATE - ADMIN USE ONLY."

The messages were encrypted, but the jade token made short work of them. Zhang scrolled through, heart pounding.

And then he found it.

A conversation between Hu and someone identified only as "Benefactor." The messages were short, clinical:

[Benefactor: Batch 374 needs adjustment. Remove ID 7845-B from reincarnation queue.]
[Hu: Understood. Will process as administrative error.]
[Benefactor: Ensure the death is clean. No anomalies.]
[Hu: Already arranged. Heart attack during sleep. Very clean.]

Zhang's hands trembled. His death hadn't been a mistake. It had been ordered.

"Who are you?" he whispered to the screen. "Who ordered my death?"

But before he could scroll further, the office door opened.

Supervisor Hu stood in the doorway, a cup of tea in one hand and a look of pure fury on his face.

"What," Hu said slowly, "are you doing in my office?"

Zhang's mind raced. He could try to explain, but what explanation would work? He could run, but where? He could fight, but Hu was an immortal with millennia of experience.

He chose option four: bluff.

"Senior Auditor Li Mei asked me to retrieve some files for a compliance audit," Zhang said, keeping his voice steady. "She said you'd been notified."

Hu's eyes narrowed. "Li Mei? That bureaucratic bloodhound? Why would she send a Tier 2 clerk instead of coming herself?"

"Because she's currently auditing your department's expense reports," Zhang said. "She found some... discrepancies. She asked me to cross-reference your personal files with the department budget."

It was a gamble. A wild, insane gamble. But Zhang had noticed something in Hu's files—a pattern of unexplained expenses that didn't match any official department activity. If Hu was corrupt, that accusation would hit close to home.

Hu's face went pale. "Discrepancies? What discrepancies?"

"I'm not at liberty to say. But she wanted me to check—"

"Get out." Hu's voice was ice. "Get out of my office and tell Li Mei that if she wants my files, she can come herself."

Zhang stood, careful not to show his relief. "Of course, Supervisor Hu. I'll deliver your message."

He walked past Hu, heart hammering. One wrong move and—

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert]
[Supervisor Hu has activated emergency lockdown]
[All exits from Department of Mortal Affairs are sealed]
[Reason: Unauthorized access detected]
[All personnel: Remain at your stations for inspection]

The doors slammed shut. Alarms blared. Clerks screamed and scrambled back to their cubicles.

Hu grabbed Zhang's shoulder. "Actually, Clerk Zhang, I think you should stay. I want to have a conversation with you about unauthorized access to administrative files."

His grip was iron. Zhang couldn't move.

"This way." Hu dragged him toward a door Zhang hadn't noticed before—a door that led down, into darkness. "We're going to have a very long talk about your future here."

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Critical Alert]
[You are being escorted to an undisclosed location]
[Survival probability: 13.7%]
[Recommended action: Escape immediately]

[Quest Updated: The Auditor's Gambit]
[New Objective: Survive Supervisor Hu's interrogation]
[Penalty for Failure: Permanent soul deletion]

The door closed behind them, plunging Zhang into darkness.

"Now," Hu's voice echoed from everywhere and nowhere, "let's start with the obvious question. Who sent you?"

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Clockwork Heart of Heaven

Zhang barely had time to register the alert before the void around him shattered like glass.

He wasn't falling anymore. He was standing in the middle of a celestial office—except this one was different. The desks were arranged in perfect concentric circles, radiating outward from a central pillar of pure white light. Clerks floated between stations, their feet never touching the ground, their faces blank masks of concentration. And in the center, where the pillar touched the ceiling, a massive clockwork heart beat with mechanical precision.

*Tick. Tock. Tick. Tock.*

Each pulse sent ripples through the air, and Zhang felt his own heartbeat synchronize against his will.

[WARNING: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Location: Core Processing Hub - Level 7]
[Security Response: En Route]
[Estimated Time to Arrival: 47 seconds]

Forty-seven seconds. That was almost generous. Zhang had once escaped a burning building with thirty seconds to spare. Of course, that building had only been on fire because someone in the Department of Arson had misfiled a permit, but the principle remained.

He needed a distraction.

His fingers flew across the jade token, pulling up the Core Administrative Interface. The sphere of gears and runes had followed him, floating just behind his right shoulder like a loyal pet. He accessed the Karmic Ledger, searching for something—anything—that would cause chaos.

There. A pending audit on Department Head Wang's expense reports. Someone had been claiming reimbursement for "ceremonial incense" that cost more than Zhang's entire mortal life savings. The audit had been delayed for three months due to "administrative oversight."

Zhang approved it.

The moment he did, alarms blared throughout the hub. Red lights flashed. Clerks scrambled, their serene floating replaced by panicked flailing. A voice boomed from the central pillar: "Unannounced Audit! All personnel, freeze your current tasks! Department Head Wang, report to the Ethics Committee immediately!"

From across the hub, Zhang saw a portly immortal in expensive robes drop his jade cup in shock. Wang's face cycled through seven shades of purple before settling on an unhealthy maroon. "Who approved this?!" he screamed at no one in particular. "I have a lunch meeting with the Director of Destiny Allocation! Do you know how hard it is to get a reservation at the Nectar of Eternity Pavilion?"

Zhang didn't wait to see how that played out. He slipped between desks, keeping his head down, moving toward the exit he'd spotted on his right. A door marked "Authorized Personnel Only - Karmic Temperature Control" beckoned.

*Thirty-two seconds.*

He reached the door and pressed his jade token against the lock. A red light blinked.

[Access Denied: Insufficient Clearance]
[Required: Tier 4 - Supervisor]
[Current: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)]

Of course. The one door he needed was locked. And behind him, he could hear the stomp of celestial boots—the security response had arrived.

*Twenty-five seconds.*

Zhang looked at the floating sphere. "Can you get me through this door?"

The sphere pulsed once, twice. A rune on its surface shifted, and a new notification appeared:

[Core Administrative Interface - Emergency Override Available]
[Warning: Using emergency override will flag your account for immediate review]
[Proceed? Y/N]

He selected Y.

The door clicked open. Zhang slipped through just as the first security officer rounded the corner. He caught a glimpse of golden armor, a face of pure marble, and eyes that burned with heavenly fire. Then the door slammed shut, and Zhang was alone in a corridor of pulsating heat.

The Karmic Temperature Control room was exactly what it sounded like: a massive chamber filled with furnaces that regulated the karmic energy flowing through the system. Each furnace was labeled with a region and time period. Zhang passed by one marked "Ancient Egypt - 1250 BCE" and felt a wave of heat that smelled of sand and ambition. Another read "Industrial Revolution - Europe" and radiated a cold, mechanical chill.

He needed a place to hide. Somewhere the security couldn't find him while he figured out his next move.

That's when he saw it.

In the corner of the room, partially hidden behind a furnace labeled "Ming Dynasty - Economic Fluctuations," was a door that didn't match the others. It was old. Wooden. With a brass handle that looked like it had been pulled from a Victorian mansion. A sign above it read:

[Department of Unresolved Matters]
[Established: Before Time]
[Supervisor: Unknown]
[Warning: Entering this department voids all previous employment contracts and celestial protection]

Zhang stared at the sign for a long moment. "Department of Unresolved Matters." That sounded like the kind of place where things went to be forgotten. The kind of place where a dead man who shouldn't be dead might find answers.

The security team was getting closer. He could hear them shouting orders, their voices echoing through the corridors. They were methodical, efficient. They'd find this room eventually.

He grabbed the handle and pulled.

The door swung open, revealing a staircase that descended into absolute darkness. No light. No sound. Just an endless spiral of steps that seemed to go down forever.

Zhang took a step forward—

And the world flipped upside down.

Suddenly he was standing on the ceiling, the staircase now rising above him. He stumbled, caught himself, and looked up to see the door he'd entered through now hanging above his head like a trapdoor to heaven.

"That's not disorienting at all," he muttered.

A voice answered from the darkness: "First time?"

Zhang spun around. Standing on the stairs—which were now below him, he was very confused about the geometry—was a woman. She was tall, with silver hair that floated around her head like she was underwater. Her eyes were the color of old parchment, and she wore a simple gray robe that had been patched in at least seventeen places.

"Who are you?" Zhang asked.

"Someone who's been waiting for you." She smiled, and it was not a comforting expression. "I'm the Supervisor of Unresolved Matters. You can call me Chen. Everyone does. Well, everyone who knows I exist, which is exactly three people, including you."

"Three? Who's the third?"

Chen's smile widened. "That's one of the unresolved matters, isn't it?"

She turned and began walking down the stairs. After a moment, Zhang followed. The darkness seemed to part around her, revealing a corridor lined with doors. Each door was different—some were grand, covered in gold leaf and intricate carvings; others were plain, barely visible against the walls. One looked like it was made of pure shadow, with a handle that didn't quite exist.

"What is this place?" Zhang asked.

"The basement of Heaven. The place where paperwork goes when no one wants to deal with it. The lost causes, the forgotten errors, the mistakes that everyone pretends never happened." Chen stopped in front of a door that looked suspiciously like the one Zhang had entered through. "Like your death, for example."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "You know about that?"

"I know everything that happens in this department. Which is ironic, because our entire purpose is to handle things that nobody knows about." She pushed open the door, revealing a small office cluttered with scrolls and papers. A desk sat in the center, covered in a mountain of documents that looked like they'd been accumulating for centuries. "Come in. We have a lot to discuss."

Zhang stepped inside, and the door swung shut behind him.

Chen settled into a chair that groaned under her weight. She gestured for Zhang to sit in the one across from her. He did, and immediately regretted it—the chair was clearly designed for someone with significantly less spine.

"You accessed the Core Administrative Interface," Chen said. "That's impressive. Most clerks spend centuries working their way up to that kind of clearance. You did it in what, two days?"

"I had help." Zhang held up the jade token.

"Ah, yes. The bug." Chen's eyes glittered. "That's why I'm interested in you. You see, I've been watching the system for a long time. Longer than most immortals have been alive. And I've noticed something: the system is broken. Not in small ways, but fundamentally. It's designed to maintain itself, not to function properly. Errors get buried, mistakes get swept under the rug, and people like you get killed because someone filed the wrong form."

Zhang leaned forward. "You want to fix it?"

"I want to expose it." Chen's voice dropped to a whisper. "There's a group of immortals—high-ranking ones—who've been manipulating the system for their own benefit. They've been altering destinies, stealing karma, and covering their tracks so well that even the Jade Emperor doesn't know it's happening."

"And you want my help?"

"I want your talent." Chen reached into her desk and pulled out a scroll, unrolling it to reveal a complex diagram. It looked like a map of the celestial bureaucracy, but with pathways and connections that Zhang had never seen before. "There's a backdoor in the system. A way to access the original source code—the first lines of reality that were written when Heaven was created. If I can get there, I can prove what the immortals have been doing. But I can't do it alone. I need someone who can navigate the system from the inside. Someone who's already flagged for monitoring, so their movements are predictable."

Zhang stared at the map. The pathways glowed with an inner light, pulsing in rhythm with the clockwork heart he'd seen earlier.

"What's in it for me?"

Chen smiled. "I can get you a new mortal life. A fresh start. No paperwork, no bureaucracy, no mistakes. You'll be reborn into a family of your choosing, with memories intact if you want them."

It was the most tempting offer Zhang had heard since he died. A second chance. A real one.

But something in Chen's eyes made him hesitate. There was a hunger there, a desperation that didn't match her calm demeanor.

"What aren't you telling me?"

Chen's smile faltered. "The backdoor is protected. By something old. Something that predates the bureaucracy itself. If we go there, there's a chance we might not come back."

"And you're willing to risk that?"

"I've been in this department for ten thousand years, Zhang Wei. I've seen the system eat souls, erase memories, and rewrite history. I'm already dead in every way that matters." She stood up, her chair groaning in relief. "But you? You're still fighting. That's why I need you."

She extended her hand.

"Will you help me bring down Heaven?"

Zhang looked at her hand. Then at the map. Then at the jade token that had started it all.

He thought about Supervisor Hu, who had killed him through negligence. About the system that had tried to sweep his death under the rug. About the clockwork heart he'd seen, beating with the rhythm of a universe that had stopped caring.

He took her hand.

"Let's burn it all down."

The moment he spoke, the jade token erupted with light. A notification appeared, hovering in the air between them:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Unlocked]
[Quest: The Department of Unresolved Matters]
[Objective: Access the Source Code of Reality]
[Reward: ???]
[Warning: This quest cannot be abandoned]
[Warning: This quest may result in permanent soul deletion]
[Warning: The system is aware of your location]

[SECONDARY NOTIFICATION: Security Response Elevated]
[Threat Level: Critical]
[Assigned Enforcer: Celestial Inquisitor

title: The Immortal's Gambit
content:
Zhang didn't wait for the investigation team to arrive. He'd spent enough time in corporate hell to know that when IT flagged you, you had about thirty seconds before your access got locked. He pulled up the interface, fingers flying through menus that shouldn't exist for someone at his tier.

*[Command: Mask Access History]*
*[Status: Failed - Insufficient Clearance]*

Of course. The system wasn't going to let him cover his tracks that easily. But Zhang hadn't survived five years of understaffed tech support without learning a few tricks.

*[Command: Redirect Audit Trail to Supervisor Hu's Terminal]*
*[Status: Processing...]*
*[Warning: This action violates Celestial Protocol 47-B]*
*[Confirm?]*

He confirmed. The system hesitated for a moment—a full half-second that felt like an eternity—then accepted the command. Hu's terminal would show that *he* had been the one poking around restricted records. Let the supervisor explain that to his bosses.

Zhang pulled his hand back from the sphere just as the void around him began to shimmer. Shapes formed in the white expanse—desks, cabinets, the familiar grey cubicle walls of the Department of Mortal Affairs. He was being pulled back to his workstation.

*[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Remote Access Terminated]*
*[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]*
*[Session Duration: 4.7 Seconds]*
*[Data Transferred: 2.3 Petabytes]*

Petabytes. He'd downloaded petabytes of celestial data in under five seconds. His mortal brain should have melted. Instead, he felt... fuller. Like he'd eaten a meal he hadn't realized he was hungry for.

The white void dissolved completely, and Zhang found himself sitting at his desk, the jade token cool in his hands. The office was exactly as he'd left it—fluorescent lights humming, papers rustling, the distant sound of Supervisor Hu yelling at someone in his office.

But everything looked different now.

Zhang blinked, and he could *see* the karmic threads. They wove through the office like invisible spiderwebs, connecting every clerk to every file, every decision to every consequence. Hu's thread was thick and dark, tangled with what looked like thousands of unresolved debts. The other clerks had thinner threads, some bright, some dull.

And his own thread? Zhang looked down at his chest. A thin golden line extended from his soul, winding through the ceiling toward some distant point he couldn't see. It was frayed at the edges, damaged by whatever error had killed him. But it was there.

*[New Ability Unlocked: Karmic Sight - Tier 1]*
*[Description: You can now perceive the karmic connections between souls and events. Use wisely. Some truths are better left unseen.]*

"Zhang Wei!"

He looked up. Clerk Liu was standing at his cubicle entrance, face pale. "Supervisor Hu wants to see you. Now."

Liu's thread flickered with anxiety—bright orange bursts of fear. Zhang could read it like a neon sign. Interesting. The ability wasn't just about seeing connections; it was about reading emotional states too.

"Did he say why?" Zhang asked, standing slowly.

"He's... upset. Someone accessed restricted records from his terminal. He thinks it was you."

Perfect. The misdirection had worked. But it also meant Zhang had maybe five minutes before Hu figured out what really happened. He needed to move fast.

The walk to Hu's office felt different now. Every step revealed more of the celestial machinery behind the mundane facade. The water cooler was a karmic processing node. The broken printer was a destiny distribution terminal that had been improperly configured. The motivational poster about teamwork? It was actually a binding contract that prevented clerks from reporting their supervisors.

Zhang stopped in front of Hu's door. Through the frosted glass, he could see the supervisor's silhouette pacing back and forth. The karmic threads around the office were pulsing faster now, responding to Hu's agitation.

He knocked.

"Enter."

Zhang pushed open the door. Hu was behind his desk, face red, jade token clutched in his hand. The supervisor's office was a mess—papers everywhere, a cracked tea cup on the floor, and a faint smell of burnt incense.

"Sit." Hu pointed to the chair across from him.

Zhang sat. His karmic sight was going haywire now—Hu's thread was so dark it was almost black, and it connected to dozens of other threads that led to... Zhang squinted. Those threads went to the same place. A massive knot of unresolved karma, hidden behind layers of administrative obfuscation.

The same kind of obfuscation that had covered up Zhang's death.

"Someone accessed restricted records during your break," Hu said, voice tight. "From *my* terminal."

"That's concerning." Zhang kept his face neutral. "Do you know who?"

"I have my suspicions." Hu leaned forward, hands flat on the desk. "You were seen near the core administrative interface. You've been acting strange ever since you arrived. And now someone is trying to frame me for accessing records that could get us both demoted to Hell of Endless Meetings."

"Maybe it's someone who has access to your terminal and wants to shift blame." Zhang met Hu's eyes. "Someone who knows the system well enough to fake an access log."

Hu's eye twitched. The thread around him pulsed once, twice—then settled. He was scared. Not angry, not suspicious. Scared.

"What do you want?" Hu asked, voice dropping to a whisper.

"Excuse me?"

"Don't play games with me, Zhang Wei. I've been in this department for three thousand years. I know when someone is making a play." Hu's hands were shaking. "You found something. In the system. Something about your death."

Zhang didn't answer. He let the silence stretch, watching Hu's thread writhe with fear and guilt.

"I can make your life very difficult," Hu continued. "I can have you reassigned to the Karmic Penalty Division. I can have your soul flagged for reincarnation as a cockroach. I can—"

"You can't do any of that." Zhang cut him off. "Because if you do, I'll report what I found. The misrouted soul batch. The unresolved errors. The three thousand years of corruption you've buried in the system."

Hu's face went white.

"How do you..."

"Your thread," Zhang said. "I can see it. All of it. The debts you've accumulated, the souls you've wronged, the destinies you've corrupted. It's all there, Supervisor. And it's all connected to you."

For a long moment, neither of them spoke. The office was silent except for the hum of the celestial machinery hidden behind the walls.

Then Hu laughed.

It wasn't a pleasant laugh. It was the laugh of someone who had just realized they were cornered and had decided to stop caring.

"You think you've won," Hu said, standing up. "You think you've found leverage. But you don't understand how this place works, Zhang Wei. The corruption isn't a bug. It's a feature."

He pressed something on his desk. The walls of the office shimmered, revealing the true nature of the room—a nexus of karmic energy, connected to every department in the Heavenly Bureaucracy.

"The system is designed to be exploited," Hu continued. "That's not a flaw. That's the point. The immortals at the top don't care about justice or destiny. They care about stability. And stability means maintaining the status quo."

He walked around the desk, stopping inches from Zhang. "You found proof that your death was a mistake. Congratulations. Do you know what happens when you try to fix that mistake? The entire system has to acknowledge that it's fallible. That the Celestial Management System can make errors. And that would cause a cascade failure that would destabilize every department in heaven."

"So I should just accept it?"

"I'm saying you should think about the consequences." Hu's voice was almost gentle now. "You expose my corruption, you get your revenge. But you also trigger a celestial audit that will destroy thousands of souls—souls that are currently being processed, souls that are waiting for reincarnation, souls that are balanced on the edge of fate. Is your revenge worth that?"

Zhang's blood ran cold. He looked at the karmic threads again, following them from Hu's office to the rest of the department. The supervisor was right. If Zhang pulled on the wrong thread, the entire tapestry would unravel.

But if he didn't pull, Hu would keep corrupting the system. Keep killing people through negligence. Keep burying the truth.

"Is there another way?" Zhang asked.

Hu smiled. It was a tired, sad smile. "There's always another way. But it's not the way you want."

"What do you mean?"

"You want to expose me. You want justice. But justice isn't what this system rewards." Hu walked back to his desk and sat down. "This system rewards power. Influence. Control. If you want to change things, you don't tear them down. You climb to the top and change them from within."

He pulled out a jade token—different from Zhang's, darker, with more complex runes carved into its surface.

"I can't give you justice," Hu said. "But I can give you a promotion."

*[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]*
*[Title: The Immortal's Gambit]*
*[Description: Supervisor Hu has offered you a choice. Accept his patronage and begin the climb through the Heavenly Bureaucracy, or reject him and face the consequences of your discovery.]*
*[Reward: Unknown]*
*[Penalty: Unknown]*
*[Accept? Y/N]*

Zhang stared at the notification. This was it. The moment that would define his afterlife. Accept corruption and become part of the system he hated, or reject it and risk everything.

"What's the catch?" he asked.

"No catch. Just a simple truth: you can't fix the system from the bottom. You have to be in a position to make changes." Hu extended the token. "Take this. It's a promotion to Tier 3 - Senior Clerk. It comes with access to the Destiny Adjustment Department, a salary of 500 karmic credits per cycle, and the authority to review and modify mortal destinies within your division."

"And in exchange?"

"You forget what you found today. You work for me. And when I need a favor, you repay it."

It was a terrible deal. Zhang knew that. But it was also the only deal on the table.

He reached out and took the token.

*[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Promotion Accepted]*
*[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]*
*[New Rank: Tier 3 - Senior Clerk]*
*[New Department: Destiny Adjustment - East Division]*
*[Warning: Your soul has been flagged for monitoring by the Celestial Audit Bureau]*
*[Additional Warning: Your karmic debt has increased by 10,000 points]*
*[Final Warning: Supervisor Hu now has administrative access to your soul records]*

The token burned in Zhang's hand. Not painfully—it felt like holding a hot coal that had been cooled just enough to be bearable. A reminder of the deal he'd made.

"Good choice," Hu said, settling back into his chair. "Now, get out of my office. You have a new department to report to. And Zhang Wei?"

"Yes?"

"Welcome to the game."

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The Demon King's Missing Stapler

Zhang Wei snatched his hand back from the glowing sphere as if burned. The white void flickered, and for one terrifying second, he thought the whole system was going to collapse around him.

[INCOMING: Investigation Team Delta]
[ETA: 47 seconds]
[Current Location: Floor 37, Celestial Administration Complex]
[Recommendation: Evacuate immediately]

*Forty-seven seconds.* That was almost generous. In his mortal office, Zhang had once cleared his desk, falsified a status report, and made it to the bathroom to hide from his boss in twenty-three seconds flat.

He could work with forty-seven.

Zhang's fingers flew across the interface, pulling up the system architecture. The Core Administrative Interface was beautiful in its complexity—a masterpiece of celestial engineering that had been built over millennia by countless generations of divine coders. It was also, Zhang realized with growing excitement, a complete disaster.

There were shortcuts everywhere. Emergency protocols. Backdoor access routes that had been left behind by lazy developers who didn't want to walk through the proper channels. Entire sections of the code were held together with what looked like digital duct tape.

*This system wasn't designed by gods,* Zhang thought. *It was designed by bureaucrats who got promoted past their competence.*

He found what he needed: a memory dump subroutine, originally intended for archiving old prayers. It would erase his recent access logs while preserving the data he'd extracted. He triggered it just as the first signs of Investigation Team Delta's arrival appeared—a shimmering in the void, like heat waves rising from summer asphalt.

Zhang dove sideways, activating a secondary protocol he'd spotted: [Emergency Reassignment: Temporary Transfer, Department of Divine Logistics].

The world dissolved into numbers again, but this time the journey felt longer. He passed through layer after layer of celestial administration, each department more bizarre than the last. He saw the Department of Inconvenient Coincidences (motto: "Your car keys are exactly where you left them. Probably."), the Bureau of Dramatic Timing ("We make sure your dramatic revelation happens *just* as the door opens"), and what appeared to be an entire floor dedicated to matching socks in alternate dimensions.

Then he slammed to a stop.

Zhang found himself in a supply closet.

Not a metaphorical supply closet. An actual, physical closet filled with brooms, mops, and boxes of something labeled "Ambrosia-Flavored Cleaning Solution (Divine Strength, May Cause Temporary Immortality, Do Not Ingest)."

He pushed open the door and stepped into chaos.

The Department of Divine Logistics was everything the pristine floors of Mortal Affairs were not. Papers were stacked in precarious towers that defied both gravity and common sense. Filing cabinets had been converted into makeshift walls, creating a maze of cubicles that seemed to have been designed by someone who hated straight lines. Every surface was covered in sticky notes, and in the distance, Zhang could hear someone screaming about a missing shipment of lightning bolts.

A clerk rushed past him, carrying an armful of scrolls that were visibly on fire. "Move! The demon lord's quarterly tribute is late again, and if I have to process one more insurance claim for souls that got 'accidentally' damned, I'm going to request a transfer to the Void!"

Zhang blinked. "The Void has a HR department?"

"Everything has a HR department!" the clerk yelled back, already disappearing around a corner. "That's the problem!"

A notification popped up in Zhang's vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Assignment]
[Department: Divine Logistics, Sub-department Q-7]
[Position: Inventory Reconciliation Clerk (Temporary)]
[Supervisor: Li Mei, Senior Logistics Coordinator]
[Current Objective: Survive until end of shift]

*Survive until end of shift.* That was... not reassuring.

"YOU! New meat!"

Zhang turned to find a woman staring at him from across the room. She was tall, with hair that seemed to be made of living flame, and she wore a vest covered in badges that read "Certified in 37 Types of Disaster Management." Her eyes glowed with the kind of intensity that said she had not slept in approximately three centuries.

"Li Mei," she said, not bothering with pleasantries. "You're my new clerk. Good. The last one got promoted to the Department of Ascended Hermits after he accidentally reclassified the Jade Emperor's personal prayer collection as 'archival waste.'"

"That sounds like a punishment," Zhang observed.

"It was supposed to be. He's thriving. Apparently, hermits are very organized." Li Mei shoved a tablet into his hands. "Here. Your first assignment."

Zhang looked down at the screen. It displayed a single line of text:

[INCIDENT REPORT: Missing Divine Artifact]
[Item: Golden Stapler of Supreme Organization]
[Last Known Location: Palace of the Demon King, 7th Hell]
[Current Status: Unconfirmed]
[Priority Level: ABSOLUTE MAXIMUM - This is not a drill]

"A stapler," Zhang said slowly. "You want me to retrieve a stapler from the Demon King."

"It's not *just* a stapler. It's the *Golden Stapler of Supreme Organization*. It was a gift from the Celestial Bureaucracy to the Demon King during the Treaty of Eternal Boredom, signed in 3,402 BCE. It's the only thing keeping the paperwork of Hell from descending into total chaos."

Zhang stared at her. "Hell has paperwork?"

"Of course Hell has paperwork! Where do you think all those 'souls to be processed' forms go? You think demons just *know* who to torture?" Li Mei threw her hands up. "There's a system! A terrible, bloated, soul-crushingly incompetent system, but a system nonetheless!"

"And if the stapler isn't returned?"

Li Mei's flame-hair flickered. "The Treaty of Eternal Boredom collapses. Hell declares open war on Heaven. The mortal realm gets caught in the crossfire. And I have to fill out *so many forms* explaining why we lost the stapler."

Zhang considered his options. He could refuse. He could try to find another way back to his original plan of exposing Supervisor Hu. He could—

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Side Quest Accepted]
[Quest: The Case of the Missing Stapler]
[Objective: Retrieve the Golden Stapler of Supreme Organization from the 7th Hell]
[Time Limit: 3 Hours (Celestial Standard)]
[Reward: Permanent Position in Department of Divine Logistics, +500 Karma, Unlock [REDACTED]]
[Penalty: Disciplinary Review, 90% Chance of Soul Reassignment to Entry-Level Position]

*Entry-level position.* In the bureaucracy, that meant one thing: Hell of Unending Data Entry.

"Fine," Zhang said, surprising himself. "But I need resources. Access to the system. And a way to get there that doesn't involve walking through Hell's front gate."

Li Mei's grin was sharp enough to cut glass. "I was hoping you'd say that."

She led him through the maze of cubicles, past clerks arguing about the proper classification of different types of lightning, past a room where someone was trying to teach a cloud how to use a filing system, and finally to a door marked:

[EMERGENCY TRANSPORT: USE ONLY IN CASE OF EXISTENTIAL THREAT TO THE CELESTIAL ORDER]

"That's always," Zhang noted.

"We had the sign made before the incident," Li Mei said, not explaining what "the incident" was. "The transport system will take you directly to the 7th Hell's administrative district. You'll be registered as a 'Senior Audit Specialist from the Department of Divine Logistics'—that's a real position, by the way, we just never filled it because no one wanted the job."

"Why would no one want—" Zhang stopped. "Let me guess. The last person who held it is dead."

"Define 'dead.'" Li Mei shrugged. "His soul is still processing somewhere. We think. It's hard to tell with these things."

Zhang stepped through the door and found himself in a small room with a single pedestal in the center. The pedestal glowed with the same light as the Core Administrative Interface.

"One more thing," Li Mei said from the doorway. "The Demon King doesn't know the stapler is missing. If he finds out, the treaty is void anyway. So you need to get in, find the stapler, and get out without him knowing."

"Any advice on how to do that?"

Li Mei thought for a moment. "Don't die. The paperwork to replace a temporary clerk is *ridiculous*."

The door closed. The pedestal flared, and Zhang felt himself being pulled apart and reassembled simultaneously.

When his vision cleared, he was standing in a corridor made of obsidian and bone. The air smelled of sulfur and old paper. In the distance, he could hear the screams of the damned—but they were muffled, as if coming through soundproof walls.

A sign on the wall read:

[WELCOME TO THE 7TH HELL ADMINISTRATIVE DISTRICT]
[Please check in with Reception]
[Remember: All demons must be registered with the Department of Infernal Affairs]
[Violators will be subject to Additional Torment Surcharges]

Zhang took a deep breath. He could do this. He'd infiltrated corporate offices, government buildings, and once, memorably, a yoga retreat to steal back his ex-girlfriend's cat. How hard could Hell be?

He turned the corner and came face-to-face with a demon.

The creature was seven feet tall, with skin the color of dried blood and horns that curled like a ram's. It was wearing a business suit and holding a clipboard. Its eyes—yellow, with vertical slits—fixed on Zhang with unsettling intensity.

"Name," the demon said. Its voice sounded like rocks grinding together.

"Zhang Wei," he said, hoping the fake credentials held. "Senior Audit Specialist, Department of Divine Logistics. I'm here for a routine inspection."

The demon's eyes narrowed. It consulted its clipboard, then looked back at Zhang.

"Inspection," it repeated. "No one told us about an inspection."

Zhang forced a smile. "That's the point, isn't it? An announced inspection isn't much of an inspection."

The demon stared at him for a long moment. Then it did something unexpected.

It laughed.

"You know," the demon said, "you're the first auditor who's given me that line. Most of them come in here shaking in their boots, trying to bribe me with holy water or whatever." It shook its head. "Fine. Come on in. The Demon King is in a meeting, so you'll have to deal with me."

It extended a clawed hand. "Name's K'tharr. Chief Administrative Liaison for the 7th Hell."

Zhang shook it, trying not to flinch at the texture. "Nice to meet you."

"Likewise." K'tharr smiled, revealing rows of needle-sharp teeth. "I hope you're ready for a tour. We've got a lot of paperwork to show you."

As the demon led him deeper into Hell's administrative complex, Zhang couldn't shake the feeling that something was very, very wrong.

The demon was being *too* helpful.

And in Zhang's experience, when anyone in a bureaucracy was too helpful, it meant they had something to hide.

He checked his notification log. The quest timer was counting down: 2 hours, 47 minutes remaining.

*Plenty of time,* he thought. *What could possibly go wrong?*

The answer came thirty seconds later, when K'tharr opened a door and Zhang found himself staring at a massive throne room.

Sitting on the throne, flanked by lesser demons holding flaming spears, was the Demon King himself.

And in his hand, he held the

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Soul Forge Incident

The sphere's light dimmed as Zhang pulled his hand away, but the information remained seared into his mind. Supervisor Hu. Clerk ID 2319. The man who had personally processed Zhang's death—and then covered it up.

Zhang's jade token buzzed. A message appeared:

[URGENT: Department of Internal Auditing]
[Subject: Unauthorized System Access]
[Recipient: Zhang Wei - Clerk ID 7845-B]
[Body: Report to Floor 13, Room 1301 immediately. Failure to comply within 5 minutes will result in automatic disciplinary hearing.]

Five minutes. That wasn't enough time to think, let alone plan. Zhang's eyes darted to the glowing sphere. The Core Administrative Interface. It had given him power, but it had also made him visible. The system was watching him now.

He turned and ran.

The white void dissolved around him, and he found himself back in the hallway outside the filing room. The celestial marble floors stretched out before him, lined with identical doors and identical potted plants. Everything looked the same, but nothing felt the same. Zhang had seen the code beneath reality, and now he couldn't unsee it.

He sprinted down the corridor, his sandals slapping against the marble. Numbers flashed past him—room numbers, floor indicators, administrative zones. He needed somewhere to hide, somewhere to think.

Room 404. Storage Closet: Incense and Ritual Offerings.

Zhang skidded to a stop, threw open the door, and dove inside. The closet was cramped, filled with boxes of incense sticks, stacks of prayer paper, and jars of what looked like candied fruit. He pressed himself against the wall and tried to control his breathing.

His jade token pulsed again. Another message:

[TIME REMAINING: 4 minutes, 23 seconds]

"Great," Zhang muttered. "The system's timing me."

He pulled up the administrative panel he'd unlocked. The interface floated before his eyes, a translucent grid of numbers and symbols. Destiny Modification. Karmic Ledger Viewing. Timeline Adjustment. And the redacted one.

Zhang focused on Timeline Adjustment. A list of options appeared: [View Current Thread] [Create Branch] [Merge Threads] [Delete Thread]. That last one made him pause. Delete a timeline? That meant erasing someone's existence entirely. Even the thought of it felt wrong.

He selected [View Current Thread].

The world around him dissolved into a river of light. He saw himself—Zhang Wei, age 28, dead from a heart attack that never should have happened. But beyond that, he saw the threads that connected him to others. Supervisor Hu's thread was a thick, gnarled rope, tangled with hundreds of other souls. And there, at the edge of Zhang's vision, was a thread that glowed brighter than the rest.

Gold. Pure gold.

Zhang reached for it, and the thread wrapped around his finger. A name appeared: [REDACTED] - [REDACTED] - [Connection: Unknown].

"What the—"

The closet door burst open.

Zhang's concentration shattered, and the timeline vision vanished. Standing in the doorway was a woman in a silver robe, her hair pulled back in a severe bun. She carried a clipboard and wore an expression that could curdle milk.

"Zhang Wei," she said. "You are late."

"I was—"

"The Department of Internal Auditing does not accept excuses. Follow me."

Zhang stepped out of the closet, his mind racing. The woman studied him with cold, calculating eyes. She didn't look angry. She looked curious. That was worse.

They walked in silence through the corridors of the Heavenly Bureaucracy. Zhang tried to memorize the route, but the hallways twisted and turned in impossible ways. Left became right. Up became down. The building itself seemed to resist mapping.

Finally, they reached a door marked [Room 1301 - Internal Auditing Division].

The woman pushed it open, and Zhang stepped inside.

The room was a perfect cube, white on all sides. In the center sat a simple wooden desk. Behind the desk sat a man who looked like an accountant from a 1950s insurance commercial—round glasses, thinning hair, a tie that was slightly too tight.

"Sit," the man said.

Zhang sat.

The man folded his hands on the desk. "I am Auditor Chen. You accessed restricted records approximately seven minutes ago. Do you deny this?"

"No," Zhang said.

Auditor Chen's eyebrows rose. "No? You admit it?"

"Would lying help?"

"Unlikely."

"Then no."

Auditor Chen leaned back in his chair. "Most clerks, when confronted with evidence of their violations, attempt to lie. They claim system errors. They blame junior staff. They beg for mercy. You are... unusual."

"I've had a bad week."

"Indeed." Auditor Chen pulled a file from his desk. "Zhang Wei. Born 1995. Died 2023. Cause of death: cardiac arrest. Except..." He opened the file and tapped a line. "According to your medical records, you had no pre-existing heart conditions. Your blood pressure was normal. Your cholesterol was excellent. You died of a heart attack that, statistically speaking, should have been impossible."

Zhang's heart pounded. "I know."

"You know?"

"I accessed the records. My death was an administrative error. Someone in this department routed my soul to the wrong queue."

Auditor Chen's expression didn't change. "That is a serious accusation."

"It's not an accusation. It's a fact."

"Facts require evidence."

Zhang reached into his robe and pulled out his jade token. He held it up, letting the light catch the characters etched into its surface. "The Core Administrative Interface showed me the logs. My death was processed by Clerk ID 2319. That's Supervisor Hu."

For a long moment, Auditor Chen said nothing. Then he laughed.

It was not a friendly laugh.

"Zhang Wei," he said, wiping his eyes. "Do you have any idea who Supervisor Hu reports to?"

"No."

"Supervisor Hu reports to Section Chief Wang. Section Chief Wang reports to Deputy Director Li. Deputy Director Li reports to the Minister of Mortal Affairs herself. And the Minister..." Auditor Chen leaned forward. "The Minister is married to the Celestial Emperor's nephew."

Zhang's blood ran cold.

"You're telling me," Zhang said slowly, "that I can't report this because the person who killed me is connected to the Emperor?"

"I'm telling you that the system is designed to protect itself. Administrative errors happen. They are corrected quietly, without fanfare, and without blame. The alternative—public accountability—would destabilize the entire bureaucracy. Do you understand?"

"No."

Auditor Chen sighed. "Let me put it differently. You can pursue this complaint. You can demand justice. But to do so, you would need to accuse a Section Chief's subordinate of negligence. That Section Chief would then need to investigate. The investigation would reveal that the Deputy Director's office approved the error correction. The Deputy Director would then need to explain to the Minister why her department made a mistake. And the Minister would need to explain to the Emperor's family that her department killed a mortal by accident."

"So?"

"So the Emperor's family doesn't like bad news. They would fire the Minister. The Minister would fire the Deputy Director. The Deputy Director would fire the Section Chief. And the Section Chief—" Auditor Chen smiled thinly. "—would fire Supervisor Hu. But before that happens, every single person in that chain of command would have a very strong incentive to make sure the initial complaint never reached them."

Zhang stared at him. "You're telling me that if I report this, I'll be killed. Again."

"No. If you report this, you will be erased. There's a difference. Death implies an afterlife. Erasure implies nothing."

The weight of Auditor Chen's words settled over Zhang like a shroud. He had thought he was playing a game. He had thought he could hack the system, expose the truth, and achieve immortality. But the system wasn't a machine. It was a living, breathing network of relationships and power. And he was a single thread, easily cut.

"I see," Zhang said quietly.

"Do you?"

"Yes." Zhang stood up. "Thank you for your time, Auditor Chen."

"Sit down."

Zhang hesitated. Auditor Chen's voice had changed. It was no longer friendly. It was no longer threatening. It was... interested.

"I said sit."

Zhang sat.

Auditor Chen removed his glasses and cleaned them with a cloth. "I told you all of that for a reason, Zhang Wei. Not to discourage you. To test you."

"Test me?"

"I have been an auditor for three thousand years. In that time, I have processed exactly seventeen complaints of administrative error. Sixteen of those complainants chose to accept compensation and move on. One chose to pursue the truth."

"What happened to them?"

"Which one?"

"All of them."

Auditor Chen smiled. "The sixteen who accepted compensation now hold comfortable positions in the bureaucracy. They have good jobs, good benefits, good afterlives. The one who pursued the truth..."

He paused, letting the silence stretch.

"She became my superior."

Zhang's jaw dropped. "What?"

"That woman who brought you here? She was the complainant. Seventeen hundred years ago, she discovered that her death was an accident. She chose to fight. She lost everything—her position, her status, her soul. She was consigned to the Hell of Unending Data Entry. But she didn't stop fighting. She learned the system. She mastered it. And eventually, she broke it."

"She's the head of Internal Auditing?"

"She's the head of everything." Auditor Chen's eyes glinted. "The woman you met is not just my superior. She is the second most powerful being in the Heavenly Bureaucracy. She answers only to the Celestial Emperor himself."

Zhang's mind reeled. The woman with the severe bun and the clipboard. The woman who had looked at him like he was a puzzle to be solved. She had started exactly where he was now.

"She wants to meet you," Auditor Chen said. "Properly. Tomorrow, at the Hour of the Ox. The Soul Forge."

"The Soul Forge?"

"You'll find out when you get there." Auditor Chen returned his glasses to his face. "One piece of advice: don't be late. She hates lateness."

Zhang nodded slowly. His jade token pulsed.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]
[Title: The Soul Forge Incident]
[Objective: Meet with [REDACTED] at the Soul Forge]
[Reward: Unknown]
[Penalty for Failure: Permanent Soul Erasure]

Permanent soul erasure. That was new.

Zhang stood, his legs shaky. As he reached the door, Auditor Chen spoke again.

"Oh, and Zhang Wei? One more thing."

"What?"

"The gold thread you saw. The bright one. I wouldn't recommend following it."

Zhang froze. "How did you—"

"I told you. I've been an auditor for three thousand years." Auditor Chen smiled. "I see everything."

Zhang left the room with his heart pounding and his mind racing. The gold thread. The woman in silver. The Soul Forge. Everything was connected, but he couldn't see how.

He walked down the corridor, past the identical doors and identical potted plants, until he reached a window. Outside, the sky of the celestial realm stretched endlessly, a tapestry of stars and clouds.

Somewhere out there was the truth.

And Zhang Wei, Junior Clerk, Department of Mortal Affairs, was going to find it.

Even if it killed him.

Again.

His jade token buzzed one final time:

[REMINDER: Hour of the Ox - 3:00 AM Celestial Standard Time]
[Location: Sector 7 - The Soul Forge

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Immortal Who Forgot His Password

Zhang Wei's hand snapped back from the sphere as if burned. The notification pulsed in his vision, a warm amber warning that felt far too much like a security camera noticing he'd picked the wrong lock.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Process Detected]
[Source: Internal]
[Action: Soul ID 7845-B has accessed restricted records]
[Alert Level: Medium]
[Recommended Action: Investigate and—]

The message cut off mid-sentence.

Zhang stared. The notification flickered, distorted, and then resolved into something else entirely:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Interrupted]
[Override Initiated]
[Source: Unknown]
[New Directive: Ignore previous alert. Do not investigate.]

"What the—"

The white void around him shimmered. For a brief, terrifying moment, Zhang saw something else in the space between the gears and runes: a face. Pale, gaunt, with eyes that burned like twin furnaces. It stared at him from a distance that felt infinite and intimate at the same time.

Then it was gone.

The sphere resumed its gentle pulsing. The notifications vanished. Zhang found himself standing alone in the white void, his heart hammering against his ribs.

Someone else was in the system.

Someone who could override alerts.

Someone who didn't want him investigated.

Zhang backed away from the sphere slowly, his mind racing. He'd assumed the celestial bureaucracy was a top-down hierarchy—Jade Emperor at the top, minor gods in the middle, clerks like him at the bottom. But what if that wasn't the whole picture? What if there were factions within the bureaucracy itself, competing for control of the system?

He'd stumbled into something much bigger than a single administrative error.

The white void dissolved, and Zhang found himself back in the copy room. The photocopier had stopped spewing papers. The room was silent except for the hum of fluorescent lights that had no business existing in an afterlife.

His jade token vibrated.

[New Message]
[From: Admin_Assistant_7]
[Subject: RE: Your Access Level]

Zhang opened it.

[Your provisional Tier 2 access has been approved. Congratulations, Senior Clerk Zhang Wei. Please report to the Records Hall on Floor 3 for your new assignment. Bring your token and a willingness to learn.
—Admin_Assistant_7]
[PS: Don't touch anything in the copy room. Trust me.]

Zhang let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding. Someone had just covered for him. Someone in the system's administration. Which meant he had an ally—or a puppet master.

Either way, he was still alive. Sort of.

He pocketed his token and stepped out of the copy room, past the still-smoking photocopier, and into the hallway. The celestial office building stretched in impossible directions. Corridors that shouldn't connect did. Doors opened into rooms larger than the building itself. Staircases led both up and sideways.

Floor 3 was a short walk away, but in this place, "short" was relative. Zhang counted three left turns, a right, and a straight path through a hallway that seemed to loop back on itself twice before he finally found the Records Hall.

The room was massive—cathedral-like, with ceilings that disappeared into mist. Shelves upon shelves of scrolls and tomes stretched in every direction, organized by a system that Zhang couldn't begin to decipher. Clerks floated between the aisles, their feet barely touching the ground, carrying stacks of documents that shifted and rearranged themselves as they walked.

At the center of the hall stood a desk. On the desk sat a woman.

She looked... normal. That was the first thing Zhang noticed. In a world of glowing immortals and floating clerks, she looked like she'd stepped out of a Beijing office building circa 2019. Blazer, glasses, a cup of tea that was definitely just regular black tea and not some celestial brew. Her nameplate read: "Chen Meiying - Senior Records Administrator."

"Ah, Zhang Wei." She smiled, and it seemed genuine. "I've been expecting you. Supervisor Hu didn't mention you'd been promoted so quickly. Interesting."

Zhang approached the desk cautiously. "I didn't know either. It just... happened."

"Things in the bureaucracy 'just happen' all the time." Chen Meiying set down her tea. "The trick is figuring out who made them happen and why. You're a clever one, aren't you? I can tell. The system doesn't promote dead mortals to Tier 2 without a reason."

She was fishing. Zhang knew it. But he also knew he needed allies.

"There was an error in my death," he said carefully. "I accessed the records. Someone covered for me."

Chen Meiying's smile didn't waver, but her eyes sharpened. "Someone in Administration?"

"I don't know who."

"Hmm." She tapped her fingers on the desk. "You know, Zhang Wei, I've worked in the Records Hall for three thousand years. In that time, I've seen systems fail, gods fall, and clerks rise to positions they had no business holding. The celestial bureaucracy is not a meritocracy. It's not even a theocracy. It's a nightmare of competing interests held together by habit and fear."

She leaned forward.

"And I've noticed something strange lately. The system is... changing. Small things. Records that don't match. Souls that get rerouted to the wrong reincarnation queues. Access permissions that appear and disappear without authorization. Someone is playing games in the administrative core."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "You think it's the same person who covered for me?"

"I think there's a ghost in the machine." Chen Meiying stood up. "And I think you might be able to help me find it."

"Why me?"

"Because you're already flagged for monitoring. Because you're a dead man who shouldn't exist. And because"—she reached into her desk and pulled out a small, unassuming box—"you're the only one who can open this."

She placed the box on the desk. It was featureless, made of some dark wood that seemed to absorb light. No hinges. No locks. No visible seams.

"What is it?"

"A sealed destiny record. From before the system was digitized." Chen Meiying's voice dropped. "It contains the original specifications for the Celestial Management System. The source code, if you will. Before the immortals locked it down and forgot how it worked."

Zhang stared at the box. "And you want me to open it?"

"You're a Tier 2 Senior Clerk with unauthorized access to the administrative core. If anyone can bypass the seal, it's you."

"And if I can't?"

Chen Meiying's smile turned sad. "Then you'll probably never find out why you died."

Zhang reached out and touched the box. Nothing happened. No notification. No glow. No system response.

"Maybe it's locked for a reason," he said.

"Everything in the bureaucracy is locked for a reason. The question is whether that reason serves the immortals—or the truth."

He picked up the box. It was heavier than it looked, and warm, as if something inside was alive. He turned it over in his hands, searching for any kind of opening, any weak point.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Object Detected]
[Name: Primordial Destiny Container]
[Status: Sealed - Authentication Required]
[Access Level Required: Tier 7 - Celestial Administrator]
[Warning: Attempting to open without authorization will trigger immediate soul termination.]

Tier 7. The highest level. Only the most powerful immortals could open this thing.

Zhang looked at Chen Meiying. "I can't open this. I'd be killed instantly."

"Then find someone who can open it for you. Someone with Tier 7 access."

"The only people with Tier 7 access are gods."

"Exactly." Chen Meiying sat back down. "But gods have weaknesses, Zhang Wei. They forget things. They misplace things. They have secrets they'd rather keep hidden."

She slid a small piece of paper across the desk. On it was written a single name:

"Lord Feng - Department of Heavenly Justice"

"Lord Feng oversees karmic audits for the entire Eastern Heavens. He has Tier 7 access, and he has a problem: he lost his login credentials three centuries ago and has been too embarrassed to ask for a reset."

Zhang blinked. "A god... forgot his password?"

"Immortals are not infallible. They're just really good at hiding their mistakes." Chen Meiying's eyes glinted. "Find Lord Feng. Offer to 'recover' his credentials. In exchange, ask him to open the box."

"And if he refuses?"

"Then you'll learn something valuable: some gods don't want the truth to come out."

Zhang pocketed the paper and the box. The weight of both felt heavier than it should have. He was walking a razor's edge between the bureaucracy's rules and whatever chaos was brewing beneath the surface.

But at least he had a direction now.

He turned to leave, then paused. "Chen Meiying. Why are you helping me?"

She picked up her tea and took a long sip. "Because three thousand years is a long time to watch a broken system pretend to work. And because"—she set the cup down—"I've seen what happens when mortals gain power in the heavens. It's never boring."

Zhang left the Records Hall with more questions than answers. Lord Feng, Department of Heavenly Justice. A god who forgot his password. A box that could reveal the system's source code.

And somewhere, watching from the shadows, a pale-faced figure with burning eyes.

He found a quiet corner of the building—an alcove with a window that looked out onto an endless starfield—and pulled out his jade token.

[Query: Locate Lord Feng, Department of Heavenly Justice]

[Processing...]

[Result: Lord Feng is currently presiding over Hearing #7842-B in the Court of Celestial Appeals. Estimated duration: 3 hours.]

[Note: Lord Feng has a known pattern. After hearings, he visits the Celestial Garden's East Pavilion for tea. He is always alone during this time.]

Zhang grinned. Perfect.

Three hours. He had three hours to figure out what he was going to say to a god who had forgotten his own password.

He leaned against the wall and stared out at the stars. Somewhere out there, his former life was continuing without him. His family, his friends, his half-finished novel collecting dust on a hard drive. They didn't know he was dead. They didn't know he was here, in a bureaucratic nightmare, plotting against immortals.

But if his plan worked, he might just get a second chance.

Or he might get his soul deleted.

Either way, it beat data entry.

His token vibrated again.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert]
[Unauthorized access detected in Core Administrative Interface]
[Source: Unknown]
[User ID: [REDACTED]]
[Action: Soul ID 7845-B records have been accessed.]

Someone was looking at his file. Again.

Zhang pushed off the wall and started walking toward the Celestial Garden. He didn't have three hours anymore.

He had to reach Lord Feng before whoever was watching him made their move.

---

The Celestial Garden was a pocket dimension of impossible beauty. Trees with leaves of jade and gold. Rivers of liquid starlight. Flowers that bloomed and wilted in seconds, each petal carrying a tiny universe within it.

Zhang found the East Pavilion easily enough. It was a small structure of white marble, perched on the edge of a cliff that overlooked a sea of clouds. And sitting inside, alone, was a man in flowing blue robes.

Lord Feng.

He looked younger than Zhang expected—perhaps early thirties, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to see everything at once. He

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Divine Performance Review

The white void flickered.

Zhang's hand remained on the sphere, but something was wrong. The numbers streaming through his mind began to stutter, skipping like a scratched disc. The glowing gears of the administrative core ground to a halt, then reversed direction.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Process Detected - Investigation Initiated]

[Source: Internal]

[Action: Soul ID 7845-B has accessed restricted records]

[Alert Level: Medium]

[Recommended Action: Investigate and apprehend]

Zhang pulled his hand back like he'd been burned. The sphere pulsed once, twice, then resumed its normal rotation. But the damage was done. Somewhere upstairs, someone had noticed.

"Think, Zhang." He paced the white void, his footsteps making no sound. "They know you accessed the records. They don't know what you found. Yet."

The jade token in his pocket grew warm. He pulled it out, and a new message flickered across its surface:

[URGENT: Soul ID 7845-B]

[Report to Hall of Celestial Adjudication - Room 404]

[Time: Immediately]

[Failure to comply will result in automatic soul deletion]

Room 404. File not found. How appropriate.

Zhang considered his options. Running was pointless—where would he go? The afterlife was a closed system. He could try to hide in the administrative core, but that would be like hiding in a boss's office during office hours. Eventually, someone would find him.

No. The only way forward was through.

He selected [Accept Summons] on the token.

The white void dissolved, and Zhang found himself standing in a hallway. It was identical to the one outside the Department of Mortal Affairs—same beige walls, same flickering fluorescent lights, same smell of stale tea and desperation. But the doors here were different. They bore golden plaques inscribed with names of celestial judges, and each one seemed to radiate a pressure that made Zhang's soul feel compressed.

Room 404 was at the end of the hall. The door was slightly ajar, and a sliver of golden light spilled out onto the floor.

Zhang pushed it open.

The room inside was enormous—far larger than the hallway suggested. Golden pillars rose to a ceiling that disappeared into mist. At the far end, seated behind a desk that could have doubled as a small airfield, was a woman who looked like she'd been carved from marble and then set on fire.

Her hair was a cascade of golden flames that didn't burn. Her eyes were twin suns that somehow didn't blind him. She wore robes that shifted colors with every breath, and her presence made Zhang feel like an ant standing before a god.

Which, technically, he was.

"Zhang Wei." Her voice was calm, measured, and absolutely terrifying. "Junior Clerk, Department of Mortal Affairs. Currently enjoying a provisional promotion to Senior Clerk due to... irregular circumstances."

Zhang bowed. He didn't know the proper protocol for meeting a celestial judge, but bowing seemed like a safe bet. "That's me. Your Honor."

"I am Judge Qian." She leaned forward, and the temperature in the room rose several degrees. "Do you know why you're here?"

"Because I accessed restricted records?"

"Partially." She gestured, and a scroll materialized in front of her. "Three hours ago, a routine audit of the Department of Mortal Affairs revealed an anomaly. Your death certificate was flagged for review."

Zhang's heart—did souls have hearts?—skipped a beat. "Flagged?"

"Someone in the system noticed that your death was... irregular. They filed a formal complaint." Judge Qian's eyes narrowed. "Do you know who that someone might be?"

Zhang thought fast. Option one: tell the truth, blame Supervisor Hu, and hope the celestial bureaucracy had a whistleblower protection program. Option two: lie, hope she didn't have a truth-detection spell, and probably get his soul obliterated.

"I complained," Zhang said. "I sent a message through the jade token."

Judge Qian's eyebrow rose. "You reported your own death as a clerical error?"

"Is that not what happened?"

"It is what happened." She unrolled the scroll, and Zhang saw his own name written in characters that glowed with an inner fire. "But the protocol for reporting such errors is clear. You should have filed a Form 7-B, Karmic Discrepancy Report, with your immediate supervisor."

"I tried. Supervisor Hu told me to accept my fate."

"Supervisor Hu." Judge Qian's voice was flat, but Zhang caught a flicker of something in her eyes. Annoyance? Recognition? "He has been... known to discourage internal reporting."

This was it. The moment to push.

"Your Honor, I have evidence that Supervisor Hu was directly responsible for the administrative error that caused my death. He processed my reincarnation queue without verifying the soul IDs. And when I confronted him about it, he threatened me with reassignment to the Hell of Unending Data Entry."

Judge Qian was silent for a long moment. The golden flames of her hair flickered, casting dancing shadows across the room.

"Show me this evidence."

Zhang pulled out the jade token and activated the records he'd accessed. The data streamed into the air between them, forming a holographic display of the celestial system's inner workings. There it was: the log entry showing Supervisor Hu's ID number attached to the misrouted soul queue. The timestamp. The lack of verification. The subsequent cover-up.

Judge Qian studied the display with the intensity of a scholar examining a rare manuscript.

"This is... thorough." She turned to Zhang. "For a junior clerk who has been dead for less than a week, you have an impressive grasp of the administrative system."

"I had a lot of free time. Being dead is surprisingly boring."

A ghost of a smile crossed her face. "I imagine it is." She dismissed the display with a wave of her hand. "Very well. I will investigate Supervisor Hu's conduct. If your evidence is accurate, he will face disciplinary action."

"Thank you, Your Honor."

"However." Judge Qian's voice sharpened. "Your unauthorized access of restricted records cannot go unpunished. You bypassed security protocols, accessed information beyond your clearance level, and potentially compromised the integrity of the celestial system."

Zhang's stomach dropped. "I was just trying to prove my case—"

"Intent does not negate consequence." She raised a hand, and a new document appeared before her. "You are hereby reassigned from the Department of Mortal Affairs to the Department of Internal Affairs. Effective immediately."

Internal Affairs. That sounded... actually not terrible.

"Your new position will be Junior Auditor," Judge Qian continued. "You will report to the Office of Administrative Oversight. Your duties will include investigating irregularities in celestial processing, auditing karmic ledgers, and—" she paused, her eyes meeting his— "identifying system vulnerabilities."
