Zhang's breath caught. Identifying system vulnerabilities. That was a fancy way of saying "find bugs."

"I don't understand," he said carefully. "You're promoting me? After I broke the rules?"

"I'm giving you a tool." Judge Qian's voice dropped to barely a whisper. "The celestial bureaucracy has been running for ten thousand years. In that time, it has accumulated layer upon layer of procedure, protocol, and patchwork fixes. No one person understands the whole system. Not the Jade Emperor. Not the Three Pure Ones. Certainly not the middle managers who have been coasting on seniority for centuries."

She leaned forward, and for a moment, Zhang saw something almost human in her eyes.

"I want you to find the cracks, Zhang Wei. Find them, document them, and report them directly to me. In return, I will ensure that your death is corrected, your soul is restored, and you receive a full pension for your time in the celestial bureaucracy."

It was the best offer he'd ever received, dead or alive.

"One question," Zhang said. "Why me?"

"Because you're the first person in three thousand years to find a bug in the system. Everyone else just accepts things as they are. You don't." She smiled, and it was the most terrifying expression he'd ever seen. "You hack your way to the top. I respect that."

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Received]

[Quest: The Auditor's Path]

[Objective: Identify and document 10 system vulnerabilities within 30 days]

[Reward: Soul Restoration, Full Celestial Pension, Promotion to Tier 3]

[Penalty for Failure: Permanent Assignment to Hell of Unending Data Entry]

Zhang accepted the quest. He didn't have a choice.

"Excellent." Judge Qian stood, and the room seemed to shrink around her. "Your first assignment: investigate the Department of Blessings Distribution. There have been reports of blessings being misrouted to the wrong recipients. Someone in that department is running a side business selling heavenly favor to mortals."

"Blessings black market?"

"Precisely." She handed him a new jade token, this one carved from black jade and etched with golden characters. "This will grant you access to all departments except the highest security levels. Use it wisely."

Zhang took the token. It was cold against his palm, and he could feel the power humming within it.

"One more thing," Judge Qian said as he turned to leave. "Supervisor Hu has been... dealt with. You won't have to worry about him."

"What does 'dealt with' mean?"

"It means he's been reassigned to a position where his... talents... can be better utilized. The Celestial Mail Room. Sorting letters from mortals. By hand."

Zhang couldn't help the grin that spread across his face. "That's almost poetic."

"I thought so." Judge Qian sat back down, her attention already on another scroll. "You're dismissed, Auditor Zhang. Don't make me regret this."

Zhang left Room 404 feeling lighter than he had since he died. He had a new job, a new purpose, and a direct line to a celestial judge who seemed to actually want to fix the system.

But as he walked down the hallway, his new jade token pulsed with a warning:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Entity Detected]

[Location: Department of Blessings Distribution]

[Threat Level: High]

[Warning: This entity is not registered in the celestial database]

[Recommendation: Proceed with extreme caution]

Zhang stopped walking.

Not registered in the celestial database. That meant one of two things: either it was a rogue immortal operating outside the system, or it was something that had never been meant to exist in the afterlife at all.

He looked at the black jade token in his hand. Looked at the hallway stretching before him.

His first day as an auditor, and he was already walking into a nightmare.

"Should have asked for hazard pay," Zhang muttered, and kept walking.

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Immortal Who Forgot to Die

Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the void around him shattered like glass.

He was thrown back into his cubicle, gasping as if he'd been holding his breath for hours. The jade token in his hand was warm—too warm. It pulsed with an angry red light that made his desk lamp flicker.

"Breathe, Zhang Wei," he muttered to himself. "You're dead. You don't need to breathe."

But his lungs disagreed, dragging in air that smelled like burnt paperwork and ozone.

The office was silent. Too silent. The usual hum of celestial machinery, the distant clatter of clerks processing fates, the occasional scream from Accounting—all of it had stopped.

Zhang looked up.

Every clerk in his department was frozen. Not metaphorically frozen—literally frozen. Li Wei stood mid-stride, one foot raised, a stack of papers suspended in the air where he'd dropped them. The woman who always argued about karmic interest rates had her mouth open, mid-sentence, her coffee cup hovering inches from her lips.

Only Zhang moved.

"What the—"

A shadow fell over his cubicle.

Zhang turned slowly. Standing behind him was a man in an immaculate white suit, so clean it seemed to glow. He had no face. Not a blank face, but a smooth expanse of pale skin where features should have been. No eyes, no nose, no mouth. Just a featureless oval that somehow conveyed amusement.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Entity Detected]
[Identity: Classified]
[Access Level: ???]
[Warning: Do not engage]

"Too late," Zhang whispered.

The faceless man leaned down, bringing his smooth head close to Zhang's. A voice emanated from everywhere and nowhere at once—deep, resonant, and utterly devoid of emotion.

"Junior Clerk Zhang Wei. You have accessed restricted records without authorization. You have bypassed standard verification protocols. You have flagged your soul for monitoring within twelve minutes of your reassignment."

Zhang swallowed. "In my defense, the system didn't say I couldn't."

"The system said you could not. You chose to ignore it."

"I chose to interpret it creatively."

The faceless man straightened. For a long moment, he simply stood there, his blank face tilted as if examining Zhang like a curious scientist studying a particularly stubborn cockroach.

"I am Inspector Wu," the faceless man said. "Department of Internal Investigations. I handle... anomalies."

"Anomalies?"

"Deviations from expected celestial behavior. Bugs in the system. Clerks who think they're clever." Inspector Wu's voice carried no inflection, but Zhang felt the weight of judgment behind every word. "You have been flagged, Zhang Wei. Not just monitored—flagged. Your access privileges have been suspended pending review."

"That's not fair."

"Fairness is a mortal concept. We deal in protocol."

Zhang's mind raced. He'd barely touched the system, barely scratched the surface of what it could do, and already they were coming for him. This was worse than his old job. At least there, he'd had to screw up for three weeks before HR noticed.

"I want to speak to my supervisor," Zhang said.

"Supervisor Hu is currently being interviewed regarding the circumstances of your death. He will be unavailable for the foreseeable future."

That got Zhang's attention. "Interviewed? By who?"

"Internal Investigations. Your file has raised several questions. The misrouting of your soul, the delayed correction, the conveniently lost paperwork—these patterns suggest a systemic issue rather than a simple error."

Zhang felt a cold satisfaction settle in his chest. Hu was in trouble. The man who'd tried to bury his mistake was finally facing consequences.

But then Inspector Wu continued.

"However, the investigation has revealed another anomaly. A discrepancy in the records that predates your arrival."

"What kind of discrepancy?"

"A soul that should not exist."

Inspector Wu raised his hand—a perfectly manicured hand, Zhang noticed, with nails that looked like polished marble. A screen materialized in the air between them.

[RECORD: Soul ID 2319]
[Status: Deceased - Reincarnated - Cycle Complete]
[Current Location: Mortal Realm - City of Shanghai]
[Discrepancy: Soul ID 2319 is currently active in Celestial Bureaucracy as Clerk ID 2319]
[Note: The same individual is also alive in the mortal realm]

Zhang stared at the screen. "That's... that shouldn't be possible. You can't be dead and alive at the same time."

"And yet, here we are." Inspector Wu's blank face somehow conveyed a smile. "Clerk ID 2319 is the one who processed your death, Zhang Wei. The one who misrouted your soul. The one who has been working in this department for three years."

"Three years?"

"Three years. Simultaneously living a mortal life in Shanghai. Working a job. Eating food. Sleeping. Dreaming. All while processing fates in the afterlife."

Zhang felt his brain struggling to process the implications. "So you're saying the guy who killed me is... also alive? He's not dead?"

"Correct."

"And he's been working here for three years without anyone noticing?"

"The system did not flag the discrepancy until you accessed the restricted records. Your actions triggered a cross-reference that revealed the inconsistency." Inspector Wu paused. "You have, in effect, exposed a security breach that has existed since before your arrival."

Zhang didn't know whether to feel proud or terrified. He settled on both.

"What happens now?"

"Now, we find Clerk ID 2319. We ask him how he managed to cheat death—and the bureaucracy that manages it."

Inspector Wu snapped his fingers.

Time resumed.

Li Wei stumbled forward, catching himself on a desk. The arguing woman spilled her coffee. The office erupted into its usual chaos of noise and motion.

And in the center of it all, Zhang sat frozen, staring at the spot where Inspector Wu had been.

A new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Temporary Assignment]
[You have been assigned to Department of Internal Investigations]
[Role: Consultant - Soul Discrepancy Analysis]
[Duration: Until resolution of Case #2319-D]
[Reward: Full access restoration + Promotion to Tier 3]
[Penalty for Failure: Soul Deletion]

[Accept?] [Decline?]

Zhang stared at the options. Accept meant working with the faceless horror of Internal Investigations. Decline meant soul deletion. It wasn't much of a choice.

He selected [Accept].

The screen dissolved into a new interface:

[CASE FILE: #2319-D]
[Subject: Clerk ID 2319 - Name: Chen Ming]
[Current Status: Unknown - Last seen at desk 7B]
[Threat Level: Low (Mortal with system access)]
[Notes: Subject is aware of investigation. Has gone missing.]

Missing.

Zhang looked across the office to desk 7B. It was empty. The chair was pushed back, a half-empty cup of celestial tea still steaming on the desk. A single piece of paper lay on the keyboard, covered in handwriting.

Zhang walked over and picked it up.

The note read:

*"Zhang Wei—*

*You're probably reading this. Which means you found the records. Which means you're working with Internal Investigations now.*

*I want you to know: I didn't mean to kill you. It was an accident. But when I realized what I'd done, I had a choice: report it and face deletion, or bury it and hope no one noticed.*

*I chose wrong. I know that now.*

*But here's the thing, Zhang Wei. I've been working here for three years. I've seen things. I've learned things. And I know that the system doesn't work the way they tell us it does.*

*The immortals didn't build this bureaucracy. They found it. They inherited it. And they don't understand it any better than we do.*

*If you want to know the truth—the real truth—find me in the Mortal Realm. Shanghai. The noodle shop on Huangpi Road. Ask for the owner's son.*

*Come alone.*

*Don't tell Internal Investigations.*

*—Chen Ming"*

Zhang read the note twice. Then three times.

The immortals didn't build this bureaucracy. They found it.

That changed everything.

He folded the note and slipped it into his pocket. Then he looked up at the ceiling, toward the distant glow of the Core Administrative Interface, and smiled.

"Looks like I'm going back to Shanghai."

His jade token pulsed with new information:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Portal Access Granted]
[Destination: Mortal Realm - Shanghai, China]
[Duration: 72 hours (Extended leave)]
[Note: You will appear as a mortal. Do not reveal your celestial status. Do not use administrative functions in the mortal realm. Do not get killed again.]

[Proceed?] [Yes] [No]

Zhang selected [Yes].

The world dissolved into light.

When he opened his eyes, he was standing on a busy Shanghai street. The smell of exhaust fumes and street food hit him like a wall. A bicycle nearly ran him over. Someone yelled at him in Shanghainese.

He was alive.

Or at least, he looked alive.

Zhang checked his reflection in a shop window. Same face. Same tired eyes. Same cheap haircut he'd had when he died. But there was something different—a faint golden glow in his irises that hadn't been there before.

The mark of the bureaucracy.

He turned and found himself staring at a small noodle shop. The sign was faded, the paint peeling. Inside, a few customers sat at worn tables, slurping soup.

Huangpi Road. The noodle shop.

Zhang pushed open the door.

A bell chimed. An old woman behind the counter looked up, surprised.

"We're closing soon," she said in Mandarin.

"I'm looking for the owner's son."

The woman's face went pale. She glanced toward the back of the shop, where a curtain hung over a doorway.

"He's not here," she said quickly. Too quickly.

"Ma'am, I really need to—"

The curtain rustled. A young man stepped out. He was maybe twenty-five, with messy black hair and glasses. He looked like any other Shanghai university student.

But when he met Zhang's eyes, he froze.

"You're him," Chen Ming whispered. "You're the dead guy."

"And you're the guy who killed me."

The shop went silent. The customers stopped eating. The old woman's hands shook.

Chen Ming took a step forward. "I can explain."

"You wrote a note telling me to find you. So explain."

"Not here." Chen Ming glanced around nervously. "They're watching. Internal Investigations, I mean. They have agents everywhere."

"They don't know I'm here."

"Yet." Chen Ming grabbed Zhang's arm. "Come on. I know a place where we can talk."

He pulled Zhang through the curtain, into a cramped storage room filled with boxes of dried noodles and spices. A trapdoor sat in the corner, barely visible under a pile of sacks.

Chen Ming lifted the trapdoor, revealing a ladder leading down into darkness.

"After you," he said.

Zhang looked at the ladder. He looked at Chen Ming. He thought about the faceless Inspector Wu. He thought about the system that wanted him dead.

Then he climbed down.

The ladder led to a small basement. A single lightbulb hung from the ceiling, illuminating a desk covered in papers and computer monitors. The walls were lined with shelves, each one stuffed with binders and scrolls.

Chen Ming closed the trapdoor above them and descended.

"This is my real office," he said. "Everything I've learned in three years. All the secrets the bureaucracy doesn't want you to know."

Zhang picked up a binder. The label read: "Orig

title: The Heavenly Compliance Officer
content:
Zhang Wei stared at the alert notification, his heart pounding against his ribs like a trapped bird. The system knew. After all his careful maneuvering, all his clever bypasses and subtle data manipulation, the Celestial Management System had detected his unauthorized access.

*Investigate and what?* he wondered, reading the truncated message. *Investigate and delete? Investigate and demote to Hell of Endless Spreadsheets?*

The white void around him flickered. The massive sphere of gears and runes pulsed once, twice, then faded into transparency. Zhang found himself back in his cubicle, still clutching the jade token that had grown warm in his hand.

"Zhang Wei!"

Supervisor Hu's voice cut through the ambient hum of celestial office equipment. Zhang shoved the token into his robes just as Hu rounded the corner, his face a shade of red that suggested his blood pressure was reaching Cultivation levels.

"Sir?" Zhang said, keeping his voice neutral.

"Come with me. Now." Hu didn't wait for a response. He turned and marched down the corridor with the stiff-legged urgency of a man who had just received very bad news.

Zhang followed, his mind racing. The alert. Hu must have received a copy. Or maybe someone higher up had sent a directive down the chain. Either way, this wasn't going to be a pleasant conversation.

They passed through the Department of Mortal Affairs—rows of clerks at identical desks, filing cabinets stretching to a ceiling that existed in a different dimension, the ever-present smell of incense and ozone. A few clerks glanced up as Zhang passed, their expressions ranging from curiosity to pity. Word traveled fast in the afterlife.

Hu led him to a small conference room that Zhang hadn't noticed before. It was bare except for a wooden table and two chairs. A single window showed a view of clouds and distant golden pagodas, but the image seemed static, like a screensaver.

"Sit," Hu said.

Zhang sat.

Hu didn't. He paced back and forth, his hands clasped behind his back, his robe swishing with each agitated step. For a long moment, the only sound was the soft rustle of celestial silk.

"I received a notification," Hu finally said. "From the System Integrity Division."

Zhang kept his face blank. "Sir?"

"The SID. They're the ones who monitor unauthorized access to the Core Administrative Interface. They're also the ones who investigate potential system breaches." Hu stopped pacing and fixed Zhang with a glare. "And they've flagged your soul ID for a Level Two investigation."

*Level Two,* Zhang thought. *Not Level One. That means they're suspicious but not certain. I still have time.*

"I don't understand, sir," Zhang said, injecting confusion into his voice. "I've been following all protocols. Filing reports. Processing karma disputes. I haven't done anything—"

"Don't lie to me, boy." Hu slammed his hand on the table. The wood didn't crack, but the sound echoed like thunder. "I know you accessed restricted records. I know you've been poking around in places a junior clerk has no business being."

Zhang met his gaze. "And how do you know that, Supervisor Hu?"

The question hung in the air. Hu's eyes widened slightly, then narrowed. He had been caught—not in a lie, but in an implication. If Hu knew about Zhang's unauthorized access, it meant either he had his own backdoor into the system, or he was the one who had flagged Zhang in the first place.

Neither option was good.

"Watch your tone," Hu said, but his voice had lost some of its authority. "I've been in this department for three centuries. I know when something is wrong."

"Then you also know that my death was a mistake," Zhang said quietly.

The silence that followed was deafening. Hu's face cycled through several emotions—anger, shock, fear, and finally, resignation. He sank into the chair opposite Zhang, suddenly looking older than his immortal appearance suggested.

"How did you find out?" Hu asked.

"The jade token. The records in the Core System. I saw the error log, Supervisor. I saw that my soul was misrouted. I saw that the correction was assigned to you." Zhang leaned forward. "And I saw that you never processed it."

Hu rubbed his face with both hands. When he spoke, his voice was tired. "You think I wanted this? You think I enjoy being the one who has to clean up the department's mistakes?"

"Then why didn't you fix it?"

"Because I couldn't!" Hu's fist hit the table again, but this time the strike was weak, desperate. "The error log requires three levels of approval to process a correction. My supervisor denied it. His supervisor denied it. By the time I could escalate, the batch had been finalized, and your body had been cremated."

Zhang stared at him. "So you just... let it go?"

"What was I supposed to do? Declare war on the entire Department of Mortal Affairs? File a formal complaint that would take centuries to process?" Hu laughed bitterly. "This is the Heavenly Bureaucracy, Zhang Wei. We don't fix problems. We file them away until they become someone else's responsibility."

The words hit Zhang like a physical blow. He had expected corruption, incompetence, maybe even malice. But this... this was something worse. This was a system designed to perpetuate itself, to absorb mistakes and turn them into procedures, to make everyone complicit in its failures.

"So I'm stuck here forever," Zhang said.

"Unless you can find a way to reverse the process." Hu met his eyes. "Which is impossible. The reincarnation queue is final. Once a soul is processed, it cannot be unprocessed."

"Then what's the point of the error log?"

"The point is to make people feel better. To give them the illusion that accountability exists." Hu stood up. "I'm telling you this as a warning, Zhang Wei. Stop digging. The SID investigation will find nothing if you give them nothing. Keep your head down, do your work, and in a few centuries, you'll forget about your mortal life."

"Is that what you did?"

Hu didn't answer. He walked to the door, then paused. "The SID investigator will arrive tomorrow morning. Her name is Li Mei. She's... thorough. Don't give her a reason to look deeper."

He left, closing the door behind him.

Zhang sat alone in the conference room, staring at the fake clouds through the fake window. The simulation was perfect—too perfect. Every wisp of vapor, every golden ray of light, every detail designed to make immortals feel at home in their endless existence.

But Zhang wasn't an immortal. He was a dead man who had been cheated out of his second chance.

*Stop digging,* Hu had said. *Keep your head down.*

But Zhang had never been good at following orders.

---

The next morning, a commotion rippled through the Department of Mortal Affairs. Clerks whispered behind their hands, filing cabinets slammed with unusual force, and even the ambient humming seemed to grow tense.

Zhang didn't have to wait long to understand why.

A woman walked through the main entrance, and the entire department fell silent. She was tall, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to cut through everything they looked at. Her robes were not the standard gray of junior clerks or the blue of supervisors, but a deep crimson that marked her as someone with authority—and blood on her hands.

"System Integrity Division," someone whispered. "She's the investigator."

Li Mei stopped in the center of the room and surveyed the clerks like a hawk scanning for prey. Her gaze passed over Zhang, lingered for a fraction of a second, then moved on.

"Supervisor Hu," she said, her voice carrying effortlessly across the vast space. "I require access to your department's records. All of them."

Hu appeared from his office, his face pale. "Of course, Investigator Li. Right this way."

"I also require an interview with the clerk who triggered the alert. Clerk ID 7845-B. Zhang Wei."

The room went quiet. Every eye turned to Zhang.

He stood up from his desk, his heart pounding but his face calm. "That's me."

Li Mei studied him for a long moment, her expression unreadable. "Come with me."

She didn't wait for an answer. She turned and walked toward a door that hadn't been there a moment ago—a door made of black jade, inscribed with characters that seemed to writhe and shift in the light.

Zhang followed, feeling the weight of a hundred stares on his back.

The door closed behind them, and they were in a room that existed outside of space. There were no walls, no floor, no ceiling—just infinite darkness punctuated by floating screens of data, each one displaying information about Zhang's life, death, and afterlife.

"Impressive," Zhang said.

"Necessary." Li Mei circled him slowly, her footsteps echoing in the void. "I've reviewed your file, Clerk Zhang. It's quite remarkable. In less than a week, you've managed to bypass security protocols, access restricted records, and discover the truth about your own death."

"I was curious."

"Curiosity is a virtue in mortals. In the afterlife, it's a liability." She stopped in front of him, close enough that he could see the faint silver threads woven into her crimson robes. "Tell me what you found."

Zhang considered his options. Lie, and risk her discovering the truth through the system. Tell the truth, and risk immediate deletion. Or...

"I found a bug," he said.

Li Mei's eyes narrowed. "A bug?"

"In the Core Administrative Interface. A flaw in the code that allows someone with the right access to modify records without authorization." Zhang met her gaze. "I think someone's been using it. Not just to reroute souls, but to change destinies. To alter karma balances. To—"

"That's enough." Li Mei's hand shot out and grabbed his wrist. Her grip was like iron, and he felt a pulse of energy travel up his arm. "You're telling the truth. But you're also hiding something."

"I'm a dead man who was murdered by bureaucracy. I'm hiding a lot of things."

To his surprise, Li Mei smiled. It was a cold smile, sharp as a blade, but there was something almost human in it.

"I'm not here to punish you, Zhang Wei. I'm here to offer you a job."

Zhang blinked. "What?"

"The bug you found—I've been tracking it for months. It's not a flaw in the system. It's a backdoor, intentionally created by someone within the Heavenly Bureaucracy." She released his wrist. "Someone is manipulating the system from the inside. And I need someone who can think like a mortal to help me catch them."

"Why me?"

"Because you have nothing to lose. Because you've already proven you can bypass the system's defenses. And because..." She paused. "Because the person I'm hunting is the one who authorized your death."

The words hit Zhang like a hammer.

"Who?" he demanded.

Li Mei shook her head. "Not yet. First, I need to know if you're willing to work with me. Officially, you'll be under investigation. Unofficially, you'll be my operative."

"You're asking me to become a spy."

"I'm asking you to help me clean up the corruption in the Heavenly Bureaucracy." Her smile sharpened. "Isn't that what you wanted? Justice?"

Zhang thought about his cramped apartment. His unpaid bills. The job that had slowly drained the life out of him years before his actual death.

"Fine," he said. "I'm in."

Li Mei nodded. "Good. Then let's begin."

She touched the void, and a new screen materialized—a list of names, each one

title: The Immortal's Secret Tab
content:
Zhang Wei stared at the alert, his heart pounding against his ribs like a prisoner demanding escape. *Investigate and*—what? The notification cut off, leaving him dangling in uncertainty. He'd triggered something. That much was clear. But whether it was a simple system flag or a full-scale celestial manhunt, he had no way of knowing.

He closed the interface. The white void dissolved around him, and he found himself back in his cramped office cubicle, surrounded by stacks of destiny reports and the soft hum of divine machinery. The jade token sat on his desk, innocuous and silent, as if it hadn't just shown him the secrets of the universe.

"Zhang Wei!"

He jumped. Supervisor Hu stood in the doorway, his face a mask of controlled fury. Behind him, two figures in gold-trimmed robes stood at attention—Celestial Security, if Zhang had to guess. Their eyes glowed with the pale light of divine judgment, scanning the room as if looking for something hidden.

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said, forcing his voice steady. "What a pleasant surprise."

"Don't play games with me." Hu stepped forward, his robes swishing against the floor. "The Core Administrative Interface registered an unauthorized access from your terminal. Explain yourself."

Zhang's mind raced. Denial was pointless—they had logs. Blaming it on a system error was possible, but risky. The truth was out of the question. That left only one option: misdirection.

"I was filing the overdue destiny reports," Zhang said, pointing at the stack on his desk. "You know, the ones you told me to prioritize. But the system kept throwing errors. Something about a corrupted index in the Reincarnation Queue."

Hu's eyes narrowed. "The Reincarnation Queue?"

"Yes. Specifically, Batch 374." Zhang leaned forward, lowering his voice conspiratorially. "I think someone's been tampering with the records. The system flagged an anomaly—a misrouted soul. But every time I tried to access the file, it redirected me to an error screen."

He watched Hu's face carefully. The supervisor's left eye twitched—barely noticeable, but Zhang caught it. *Bingo.*

"That's... concerning," Hu said slowly. "I'll have the technical division look into it."

"Should I continue filing the reports?"

"No." Hu turned to leave, then stopped. "Actually, yes. But stay away from the administrative interface. Consider it a direct order."

"Understood."

The Celestial Security guards followed Hu out, but one of them paused at the door. He was young—younger than Zhang, even—with a face that looked like it had never known a day of hardship. His eyes met Zhang's, and for a moment, something passed between them. Recognition? Curiosity? Zhang couldn't tell.

Then the guard was gone, and Zhang was alone.

He let out a breath he didn't realize he'd been holding. That was close. Too close. Hu knew something, but he wasn't ready to act on it yet. That gave Zhang time. Not much, but enough.

He pulled up the jade token again, careful to keep the interface minimized. The Core Administrative Interface was too risky to access directly now, but there were other tools at his disposal. Lesser functions, hidden in the bureaucracy's labyrinthine code.

*Karmic Ledger Viewing.*

The token pulsed, and a new window appeared. It was simpler than the sphere interface—just lines of text, names, and numbers. Debts and credits, measured in karmic units. Every action a soul took in life, recorded and quantified.

Zhang scrolled through the ledger, searching for familiar names. There was Li Xia, his former neighbor, who had a positive balance of 3,742 karma for feeding stray cats. There was the landlord who had evicted him, sitting at -8,912 karma for "habitual exploitation of vulnerable tenants." Good. That one would come due eventually.

And then he found it: *Hu Zhong, Supervisor, Department of Mortal Affairs.*

The number made Zhang's blood run cold.

*Karmic Balance: -247,891*

That wasn't just bad. That was catastrophic. For context, serial killers usually topped out at around -50,000. Genghis Khan had clocked in at -120,000. Hu had managed to accumulate nearly twice that, all while working in the *heavenly bureaucracy*.

Zhang scanned the entries, looking for the source. Most of it was standard corruption: falsified reports, suppressed errors, redirected karmic credit. But buried deep in the list was something else. A single line item that read:

*[REDACTED - Clearance Level: Tier 5 Required]*

Tier 5. That was Immortal rank. Supervisor Hu, a mid-level clerk, had accessed information meant for the highest echelons of celestial power. And whatever he'd done with it, it had cost him nearly a quarter million karma.

Zhang's finger hovered over the entry. Opening it would trigger another alert, of that he was certain. But the information inside could be the key to everything—to his death, to Hu's corruption, to the very nature of the heavenly bureaucracy itself.

He made a decision.

He tapped the entry.

The token screamed—a high-pitched whine that drilled into his skull. The interface flickered, warped, and then stabilized. A single message appeared:

[ACCESS GRANTED]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Override: Temporary Tier 5 Clearance (1 Use Remaining)]

Below it, a file opened.

It was a video log, timestamped from three months before Zhang's death. The footage showed Hu standing in a darkened room, facing a figure shrouded in shadow. The figure spoke in a voice that sounded like grinding stones and breaking glass.

"Are you certain this will work?"

Hu nodded, his face slick with sweat. "The test subject is ideal. No family, no connections, no outstanding karmic debts. The system won't flag the loss."

"And the replacement?"

"Already prepared. A soul from the overflow queue, slated for recycling. I've redirected it to take the subject's place in the reincarnation cycle."

The shadowy figure leaned forward, and Zhang caught a glimpse of pale skin and eyes like dying stars. "Excellent. Then proceed. The Celestial Management System must be tested. If we can bypass its safeguards, the rest will fall into place."

"Yes, Master."

The footage ended.

Zhang stared at the blank screen, his mind reeling. *Test subject.* That was him. He hadn't died by accident. He'd been *selected*. Chosen as a guinea pig for some experiment designed to break the celestial system itself.

And Hu was just a pawn. The real mastermind was still out there, hiding in the shadows.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[Source: Internal - ID 7845-B]
[Alert Level: Critical]
[Action: Celestial Security has been dispatched to your location]
[Estimated Time of Arrival: 3 Minutes]

Zhang cursed. Three minutes. He had three minutes to figure out what to do. He could run, but where would he go? The heavenly bureaucracy was infinite, but also inescapable. He could fight, but Celestial Security was armed with weapons that could erase a soul from existence.

Or he could do something they wouldn't expect.

He opened the jade token's interface one last time and typed a single command:

[Initiate: Emergency Protocol - Administrative Transfer]

A new window appeared:

[To: Department of Internal Affairs - Celestial Bureaucracy]
[From: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B (Senior Clerk, Provisional)]
[Subject: Urgent Report of Malfeasance]

Zhang's fingers flew across the interface, copying the contents of the video log and Hu's karmic ledger into the message. He attached the original file, timestamp and all. Then he added a final line:

*I have evidence of a conspiracy that threatens the stability of the heavenly bureaucracy. My life is in danger. Please investigate immediately.*

He hit send.

The token went dark. A second later, the door to his office slammed open. Three Celestial Security officers stood in the doorway, their weapons drawn and glowing.

"Zhang Wei," the lead officer said, her voice flat and cold. "You are under arrest for unauthorized access to restricted celestial records. Any resistance will result in immediate soul deletion."

Zhang raised his hands slowly. "I understand. I'll cooperate."

The officer's eyes narrowed. "That's surprisingly reasonable."

"I've learned that fighting the system is pointless." Zhang smiled—a thin, humorless smile. "But I've also learned that the system has cracks. And cracks can be exploited."

He didn't resist as they bound his hands in chains of light. He didn't struggle as they led him out of the office and through the endless corridors of the heavenly bureaucracy. He just walked, counting the steps, memorizing the turns.

They passed other clerks, other departments. Some stared. Most pretended not to see. The bureaucracy was a machine, and machines didn't concern themselves with broken parts.

They reached a door made of black jade, carved with symbols that writhed and shifted when Zhang tried to focus on them.

"The Holding Cells," the officer said. "You'll remain here until your trial."

"And when is that?"

"Whenever the system schedules it."

Zhang nodded. That was fine. He had time. And while he waited, he would watch. He would listen. He would learn.

Because somewhere in this infinite, Kafkaesque nightmare of an afterlife, there was a man with eyes like dying stars who had killed him for an experiment. And Zhang Wei intended to return the favor.

The door opened, and he stepped into the darkness.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Objective Unlocked]
[Objective: Survive Celestial Security Detention]
[Reward: Access to Hidden Faction - The Defragmented]
[Warning: This objective requires you to make contact with other dissidents within the system. Trust no one.]

The door slammed shut, plunging Zhang into absolute blackness.

But he wasn't alone.

A voice whispered from the shadows, soft and amused: "So. You're the one who broke into Hu's files."

Zhang tensed, his chains rattling. "Who's there?"

"Someone who's been waiting a long time for someone like you." A figure emerged from the darkness—a woman with silver hair and eyes that glowed like embers. She wore the robes of a Celestial Archivist, but they were torn and stained, as if she'd been in a fight. "I'm Lin Mei. And I think we have a mutual enemy."

She held out her hand.

Zhang looked at it, then at the darkness around them. Trust no one, the system had said. But the system was the one that had let him die.

He took her hand.

"Show me what you know."

Lin Mei smiled. "Gladly."

[CHAPTER END]

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Chapter 11 Complete]
[Karma Earned: +500 (Risk-Taking)] [Karma Earned: +1,200 (Conspiracy Discovery)] [Karma Deducted: -300 (Unauthorized Access)]

[Total Karmic Balance: 1,400]

[Next Chapter Available In: Unspecified]

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Demon King's Tax Return

Zhang Wei stared at the alert notification, his hand still touching the massive sphere of interlocking gears and runes. The Core Administrative Interface hummed beneath his fingertips, pulsing with the rhythm of reality itself.

*Alert Level: Medium. Recommended Action: Investigate.*

"No shit," Zhang muttered. "They're going to investigate."

He pulled his hand back, but the damage was done. Somewhere in the celestial bureaucracy, alarms were probably blaring. Supervisor Hu was probably sweating through his robes. And Zhang Wei, the dead accountant who should have been reincarnated as a wealthy playboy, was now sitting on top of the most dangerous information in existence.

He needed a plan.

First: access the Karmic Ledger. Find out exactly who Clerk ID 2319 was. Then: figure out how to use his new Destiny Modification powers without getting caught. Finally: survive long enough to make someone pay.

The sphere pulsed again, and Zhang noticed something strange. The runes that covered its surface weren't static—they were shifting, rearranging themselves into new patterns. Almost like they were responding to his presence.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Core Interface Adaptation Detected]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Note: The Core responds to user intent. Focus your will to access desired functions.]

Zhang closed his eyes and focused. *Show me Clerk ID 2319.*

The sphere's gears spun faster. Runes lit up in sequence, and a stream of information flowed into Zhang's mind.

[Clerk ID 2319]
[Name: Wang Tao]
[Department: Mortal Affairs - Reincarnation Queue Management]
[Current Status: Terminated]
[Reason: Termination following Karmic Audit of Batch 374]
[Additional Details: Soul reassigned to Hell of Unending Data Entry. Appeal pending.]

Terminated. They'd fired Wang Tao. No—they'd destroyed him. The Hell of Unending Data Entry wasn't a punishment. It was a death sentence for whatever remained of his soul.

But wait. The appeal was still pending. Which meant someone was fighting to get Wang Tao out. Or someone was *pretending* to fight while actually making sure he stayed trapped.

Zhang opened his eyes. "Supervisor Hu."

The old man had been the one to handle Zhang's case. The old man had conveniently "forgotten" to mention the error. And now, the clerk who actually caused the problem was conveniently "terminated" to Hell.

It was a cover-up. A perfect bureaucratic cover-up.

Zhang smiled. He'd spent thirty years as an accountant. He knew a fudged ledger when he saw one.

He was about to dig deeper into the system when a new sensation washed over him. Cold. Heavy. Like someone had dropped a frozen blanket over his soul.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Celestial Inspection Team Detected]
[Estimated Time of Arrival: 30 Seconds]
[Recommendation: Disconnect from Core Interface immediately]

"Thirty seconds?" Zhang's eyes went wide. "That's nothing."

He pulled away from the sphere, but as he did, something caught his attention. A file. Nestled in a subdirectory so obscure that even the system seemed to have forgotten it existed.

[FILE: Ancient Records - Pre-Bureaucracy Era]
[Subject: The Revenant Protocol]
[Status: Archived - Read Only]
[Warning: Accessing this file without proper authorization may result in immediate soul dissolution.]

The Revenant Protocol. The name sent a chill through Zhang's spectral form. He didn't know what it was, but he knew one thing: anything hidden this deep in the system was important.

Another cold wave washed over him. The Inspection Team was getting closer.

Zhang made a decision. He reached out and touched the file.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[File: The Revenant Protocol]
[Accessing...]
[ACCESS DENIED]
[Security Override Initiated]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Status: Flagged for Immediate Termination]

The sphere let out a deafening screech. Runes flared red. The white void around Zhang began to crack, fissures of golden light splitting through the nothingness.

And then everything went black.

---

Zhang woke up on the floor of the filing room.

His head throbbed. His soul ached. And standing over him, looking down with a mixture of concern and barely concealed anger, was Fairy Lin.

"Get up," she said. "You're in trouble."

"What else is new?" Zhang groaned, pushing himself to his feet. The filing room was exactly as he'd left it—rows upon rows of scrolls and tablets, the faint hum of celestial energy in the air. But something was different. The air felt heavier. Charged.

"The Inspection Team is here," Fairy Lin said. "They're looking for you."

"Me? Why me?"

"I don't know. But Supervisor Hu is stalling them. He says you're out on a supply run."

Zhang blinked. "Hu is covering for me?"

"Hu is covering for himself. If they find out you accessed the Core Interface without authorization, he's responsible. And the punishment for negligence is the Hell of Unending Data Entry."

"Great. So I'm the fall guy."

"You're the *only* guy," Fairy Lin said. She grabbed his arm and pulled him toward the door. "Come on. I know a place we can hide until they leave."

They moved through the celestial bureaucracy's corridors like ghosts—Fairy Lin leading, Zhang stumbling behind her. The halls were eerily empty. No clerks shuffling papers. No gods shouting orders. Just the oppressive silence of a building holding its breath.

"Where is everyone?" Zhang whispered.

"Gathered in the main hall. The Inspection Team is giving a lecture on proper Core Interface usage."

"Convenient."

Fairy Lin shot him a glance. "What do you mean?"

"I mean it's convenient that everyone's in one place while the Team searches for me. Almost like someone planned it."

They reached a door at the end of the corridor. It was plain, unmarked, and looked like it hadn't been opened in centuries.

"This is the Archive Annex," Fairy Lin said. "Records that are too old or too dangerous to keep in the main system. No one comes here. Not even the Inspection Team."

She pushed the door open, and Zhang stepped inside.

The Archive Annex was a cavernous space filled with towering shelves that stretched up into darkness. Scrolls and tablets lined every surface, some so old they were crumbling to dust. The air smelled of ancient paper and faded magic.

"You can stay here until the Team leaves," Fairy Lin said. "I'll come get you when it's safe."

"Wait." Zhang grabbed her arm. "Why are you helping me?"

Fairy Lin's expression softened. "Because you're the first interesting thing to happen in this place in five hundred years. And because I think you're right."

"Right about what?"

"About the system being broken." She looked away. "I've seen things, Zhang Wei. Souls that should have been reincarnated but weren't. Destinies that were changed for no reason. Karma balances that don't add up. Something is wrong with the Celestial Bureaucracy. And I think you're the one who can fix it."

She left before he could respond, the door sliding shut behind her.

Zhang stood alone in the Archive Annex, surrounded by the accumulated knowledge of an entire civilization. And somewhere in this maze of forgotten records was the answer.

He started searching.

Hours passed. Or maybe minutes. Time moved differently in the celestial realm. Zhang pulled scroll after scroll from the shelves, scanning them for anything useful. Most were administrative records—tax assessments from the Tang Dynasty, marriage certificates from ancient Rome, death warrants from the Mongol Empire.

And then he found it.

A scroll, wrapped in golden silk, sealed with a symbol he didn't recognize. The symbol of a flame consuming a scroll.

He unrolled it carefully.

[RECORD: The Revenant Protocol - Draft Version 1]
[Author: Unknown]
[Date: Pre-Bureaucracy Era]

*The Revenant Protocol is a contingency measure designed for extreme circumstances. Should the Celestial Bureaucracy become compromised by external forces, the Protocol allows a single mortal soul to bypass the reincarnation cycle and ascend directly to administrative authority.*

*However, the Protocol carries significant risks:*
*- The soul selected will retain its mortal memories and personality*
*- The soul will not be bound by celestial law*
*- The soul will have the power to override any administrative decision*

*Note: The Protocol was deemed too dangerous for implementation and was sealed by order of the Jade Emperor himself.*

Zhang's hands trembled. This was it. The ultimate loophole. A way to become an immortal without playing by the system's rules.

But the scroll was only a draft. The actual Protocol—if it existed—was hidden somewhere else.

He was about to search further when a voice echoed through the Annex.

"Zhang Wei."

The voice was cold. Ancient. And it came from everywhere at once.

Zhang turned slowly.

A figure stood in the doorway. Tall. Regal. Dressed in robes of pure white that seemed to absorb light. His face was ageless, his eyes like twin stars burning with celestial fire.

"I am Inspector Zhao," the figure said. "And you have been flagged for immediate termination."

Zhang's heart stopped. "Termination? But I haven't—"

"You accessed restricted records. You bypassed security protocols. You touched the Core Interface without authorization." Inspector Zhao's voice was flat, devoid of emotion. "These are capital offenses in the Celestial Bureaucracy."

"I was just—"

"You were trying to hack the system." Inspector Zhao took a step forward. "And you almost succeeded. But we have protocols for such things. We always have."

He raised his hand, and golden light began to gather in his palm.

Zhang's mind raced. He couldn't fight. He couldn't run. He was trapped in a room full of ancient records, about to be destroyed by a celestial inspector.

And then he remembered the scroll.

"What if I told you I know about the Revenant Protocol?" Zhang said quickly.

Inspector Zhao's hand stopped. His eyes widened—just a fraction, but it was enough.

"How do you know about that?"

"I found it. In the system. Before you caught me." Zhang held up the scroll. "I know it exists. And I know you've been hiding it."

The inspector's composure cracked. For the first time, Zhang saw something like fear in his eyes.

"You don't understand what you've found," Inspector Zhao said. "That Protocol isn't just dangerous. It's *forbidden*. The Jade Emperor himself ordered it destroyed."

"Then why is it still here?"

Silence.

"Because someone wanted it to be found," Zhang said slowly. "Someone left it here on purpose. Someone wanted a mortal to find it and use it."

Inspector Zhao's face went pale. "That's not possible."

"Isn't it?" Zhang smiled. "You know, I used to work in accounting. And in accounting, there's one thing I learned: everything is connected. Every number tells a story. And the story this system is telling me is that someone inside the Celestial Bureaucracy is trying to tear it down."

"You're insane."

"Maybe. But I'm also right."

The golden light in Inspector Zhao's hand flickered. Died.

"Leave," he said quietly. "Leave this place and never speak of the Protocol again. Forget what you've found."

"And if I don't?"

"Then I'll have no choice but to report you to the Jade Emperor himself. And even he won't be able to save you from what comes next."

Zhang met the inspector's gaze. Two souls, locked in a battle

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Demon King's Resume

Zhang Wei's hand froze over the glowing sphere as the alert flashed across his vision. *Investigate and what?* The notification cut off mid-sentence, replaced by spinning runes that indicated some kind of system lag.

"Come on, come on," he muttered, watching the loading icon spin.

The white void around him flickered. For a brief second, Zhang saw something else—a massive desk covered in papers, a tea cup with steam rising, and an old man in traditional robes squinting at a scroll. Then it vanished, replaced by the endless white.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Alert Resolved]
[Source: Unknown]
[Status: False Positive - Karmic Echo]

Zhang blinked. False positive? He'd literally just accessed restricted records. The system should be screaming for his soul on a silver platter. Instead, it had dismissed the alert like a spam email.

*Someone's covering for me.*

The thought sent a chill down his spine. He hadn't been in the Celestial Bureaucracy long enough to make allies. The only people who knew he existed were Supervisor Hu, the receptionist who'd given him the jade token, and the grumpy clerks who ignored him in the hallways.

Unless... the system itself was helping him.

No. That was paranoid. Systems didn't have agendas. They just processed data.

But this was a celestial system. And Zhang had already seen how much of it ran on blind faith and outdated protocols.

He dismissed the thought and focused on the file he'd uncovered. The records of his death—no, his *murder by incompetence*. The error log was detailed, timestamped, and damning. Clerk ID 2319 had processed the wrong Zhang Wei. There were two of them in the same city, same age, same district. One died of a heart attack at his desk. The other was supposed to live another forty-three years.

Clerk ID 2319 clicked on the wrong name.

And Supervisor Hu had buried the correction request.

Zhang pulled up the current status of the correction request. It was still pending, buried under seventeen layers of "under review" subcategories. The estimated resolution time: 127 years.

"One hundred and twenty-seven years," Zhang said out loud. "I've been dead for a week and they're telling me the fix will take a century."

The sphere pulsed, almost sympathetically.

He needed leverage. Something that would make Supervisor Hu sit up and pay attention. Something that would make the entire department afraid of what Zhang Wei, Junior Clerk, knew.

He started digging.

The system was vast—an ocean of data stretching across timelines, dimensions, and afterlives. Zhang navigated through the Karmic Ledger, skimming through entries that detailed the moral accounts of every soul in his assigned division. Most were boring: minor debts, small virtues, the usual balance of good and bad that made up a mortal life.

But then he found something interesting.

A ledger entry from three hundred years ago. A soul named Li Feng had accumulated massive negative karma—murder, betrayal, corruption. The kind of soul that got sent straight to the deepest hells for ten thousand years of purification.

Except Li Feng hadn't been sent to hell.

Zhang checked the disposition record. *Reassigned to Department of Strategic Initiatives.*

That was odd. Souls with high negative karma weren't reassigned. They were punished. That was the whole point of the karmic system—you reap what you sow.

He pulled up the Department of Strategic Initiatives. The records were sparse, filled with redacted sections and "classification pending" notes. But he found one thing: the department's current director.

Li Feng.

The same Li Feng who should have been burning in hell for the past three centuries was now a department director in the Celestial Bureaucracy.

Zhang leaned back, processing this. Someone had pulled strings. Big strings. The kind of strings that only a high-ranking immortal could pull.

He checked Li Feng's current karma balance. It was pristine—almost suspiciously clean. Every negative entry had been expunged, replaced with virtues and merits that looked manufactured.

The system flagged the entries as "verified by Celestial Audit Department."

*Of course it did.*

Zhang made a copy of the records and moved on. If Li Feng was this high up, there had to be others. Other souls who'd been given second chances that violated every rule in the celestial handbook.

He found seventeen more cases in the next thirty minutes. All high-karma debtors. All reassigned to cushy bureaucratic positions. All with their records sanitized and approved by the Audit Department.

But the most interesting case was the most recent: a soul named Kang Min-ho, processed just six months ago. Karma debt: -873,000 points. That was enough to earn a soul a one-way ticket to the Hell of Endless Paper Cuts.

Instead, Kang Min-ho had been assigned to the Department of Mortal Affairs as a Senior Clerk. Same department as Zhang.

Same department as Supervisor Hu.

Zhang pulled up Kang Min-ho's file and felt his blood run cold.

*Name: Kang Min-ho*
*Mortal Identity: CEO, Yongin Heavy Industries*
*Cause of Death: Stabbed 47 times by factory workers*
*Past Life: Demon King of the Third Hell (Term expired)*

Zhang read the last line three times, hoping it would change.

*Past Life: Demon King of the Third Hell (Term expired)*

The Celestial Bureaucracy had employed a *demon king*. Not just any demon king—the ruler of an entire hell realm. And they'd given him a desk job processing mortal paperwork.

This wasn't just corruption. This was a security breach of cosmic proportions.

Zhang's mind raced. If he reported this, he'd be a hero. Or he'd be dead. Probably both, in that order. The people who'd orchestrated this wouldn't want some junior clerk exposing their little arrangement.

He needed to be smart about this.

He started copying all the files he could find, creating a backup in the administrative interface's hidden memory banks. If he died—again—someone needed to know the truth.

The sphere pulsed, and a new notification appeared.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Request]
[Source: Department of Strategic Initiatives]
[Recipient: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Subject: Transfer Offer]

Zhang stared at the message. He hadn't applied for a transfer. He hadn't even told anyone he was investigating. How did the Department of Strategic Initiatives know he existed?

He opened the request.

*Dear Clerk Zhang Wei,*

*Your performance in the Department of Mortal Affairs has been noted by our recruitment team. We believe your skills would be better utilized in our department, which handles high-priority strategic initiatives for the Celestial Bureaucracy.*

*Benefits include:*
*- Increased salary (3,000 karma credits/month)*
*- Private office with window (views of the Celestial Gardens)*
*- Access to restricted administrative functions*
*- Immunity from standard performance reviews*

*Please report to the Department of Strategic Initiatives main office by the end of the current celestial cycle for onboarding.*

*Signed,*
*Li Feng, Director*

Zhang's hands trembled. This was either an incredible opportunity or a trap. Probably both.

He checked the timestamp on the transfer request. It had been generated exactly three minutes after he'd accessed Kang Min-ho's file.

Someone was watching him. Someone very high up.

And they wanted him close.

He had two choices: accept the transfer and walk into the lion's den, or refuse and hope the demon king didn't decide to "handle" the junior clerk who'd been snooping through his file.

Zhang thought about the Hell of Unending Data Entry that had been his original punishment. He thought about Supervisor Hu, who'd let his death go uncorrected. He thought about the seventeen other souls who'd been given cushy afterlife jobs while real sinners burned.

He selected *Accept Transfer*.

The sphere pulsed once, and the white void around him dissolved into a new scene. Zhang found himself standing in an office. Not the gray cubicle farm of Mortal Affairs, but a real office—polished wooden desk, a window overlooking a garden of glowing flowers, walls lined with scrolls of ancient calligraphy.

And behind the desk sat a man.

He was handsome in an unsettling way. Perfect features, sharp eyes, and a smile that didn't quite reach his gaze. He wore modern business attire instead of robes, which made him stand out in the celestial setting.

"Welcome, Zhang Wei," the man said, his voice smooth as silk. "I'm Director Li. Please, have a seat."

Zhang didn't sit. "You knew I'd find the files."

Li's smile widened. "I was counting on it. Do you know how long I've been looking for someone who could navigate the administrative interface without triggering a full system lockdown? Three hundred years. And then you show up, some dead mortal who can't even file paperwork properly, and you walk through security like it's nothing."

"You wanted me to find Kang Min-ho's file."

"I wanted you to find *all* the files. The system needs an outsider, Zhang. Someone who isn't bound by celestial protocol. Someone who can see the cracks in the foundation." Li leaned forward, his eyes glinting. "The Celestial Bureaucracy is broken. Has been for millennia. And I've been waiting for the right person to help me fix it."

Zhang's jaw tightened. "Help you fix it, or help you control it?"

Li laughed—a warm, genuine sound that didn't match the coldness in his eyes. "Smart. Very smart. That's exactly why I need you."

He reached into his desk and pulled out a scroll, unrolling it across the polished wood. Zhang stepped closer and saw it was a map. Not of the physical world, but of the Celestial Bureaucracy itself—every department, every level, every hidden pathway between realms.

"This," Li said, tapping the map, "is the real power structure. Not the one they show new recruits. The one that actually runs things."

Zhang studied the map. At the center, marked in gold ink, was a single word: *Jade Emperor's Throne Room.*

"Your job," Li continued, "is to help me get there."

The office door slammed open. Standing in the doorway was a woman in flowing white robes, her face twisted with fury. Golden light radiated from her body, and the temperature in the room dropped twenty degrees.

"Li Feng," she hissed. "You're recruiting mortals now? How far you've fallen."

Li didn't flinch. "Lady Meng, how delightful to see you. I thought you were busy with the reincarnation tea."

The woman—Lady Meng, the Goddess of Forgetfulness—stepped into the room, her eyes fixed on Zhang. "This one. He's been flagged by the monitoring system. Accessing restricted records. Tampering with the administrative interface."

"Allegedly," Li said smoothly.

"I'm taking him to the Celestial Audit Department for questioning." Lady Meng reached for Zhang's arm.

Li moved faster. He stepped between them, his smile never wavering. "I'm afraid I can't let you do that. Zhang Wei is a valued member of my department now. And he has... immunity from standard review processes."

Lady Meng's eyes narrowed. "Since when does your department grant immunities?"

"Since I started keeping records of everyone who's ever taken a bribe, altered a karma balance, or reassigned a demon king to a clerical position." Li's voice was pleasant, but there was steel underneath. "Would you like me to read your file aloud, Lady Meng? It's quite detailed."

The goddess's face went pale. She stared at Li for a long moment, then turned and swept out of the room without another word.

Zhang let out a breath he didn't realize he

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Celestial Budget Committee

Zhang Wei's fingers still tingled from touching the administrative sphere. The white void around him flickered as warning notifications cascaded across his vision.

[ALERT: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[ALERT: Security Protocol 7-B Activated]
[ALERT: Your location has been logged]

He needed to move. Fast.

"Exit," Zhang commanded.

The void shattered like glass, and Zhang found himself back at his desk. The jade token sat innocuously in his palm, warm to the touch. His screen still displayed the office supply requisition form. Everything looked normal.

Everything except the new icon that had appeared in the corner of his vision: a tiny gear with an infinity symbol at its center. The Core Administrative Interface. Permanent access.

Zhang quickly minimized it before anyone could see.

"Zhang Wei!"

He nearly jumped out of his chair. Supervisor Hu stood at his cubicle entrance, face redder than a demon's backside. Three other clerks crowded behind him, clutching scrolls and tablets.

"Yes, Supervisor Hu?"

"Explain this." Hu thrust a scroll at Zhang. It was covered in glowing red stamps. REJECTED. DENIED. FLAGGED FOR REVIEW.

Zhang scanned the contents. Office supply requisitions. All of them. The forms he'd spent the last three hours processing.

"I don't understand," Zhang said carefully. "I followed standard protocol. Each requisition was verified against departmental budgets and celestial allocation guidelines."

"Exactly!" Hu's voice cracked. "You rejected twenty-three requisitions from the Department of Heavenly Feasts!"

"They requested three thousand jade chopsticks," Zhang replied. "Their budget only allows for twelve hundred. The variance requires approval from the Celestial Finance Committee, which requires a minimum fourteen-day review period."

Silence. Then someone in the back started laughing.

Hu spun around. "Who did that?"

"I did." A woman pushed through the crowd. She wore a simple white robe with silver embroidery—no rank insignia. Her hair was pulled back in a severe bun, and her glasses had chains that dangled like tiny nooses.

Every clerk in sight immediately straightened their postures.

"Director Zhao," Hu stammered. "I didn't know you were—"

"Obviously not." Director Zhao walked past him and stopped at Zhang's cubicle. She studied him with the intensity of a tax auditor examining a suspicious deduction.

"You're the new dead clerk," she said.

"That's... one way to describe it."

"I've been reading your files. Particularly the ones you accessed ten minutes ago."

Zhang's blood went cold. The Core Administrative Interface. The restricted records. She knew.

Director Zhao smiled. It was not a pleasant expression. "Relax, Junior Clerk Zhang. If I wanted you deleted, you'd already be processing reincarnation paperwork. No, I'm here because you just made my life significantly more difficult."

"I'm sorry?"

"You rejected requisitions from Heavenly Feasts. That department is overseen by Minister Pu. Minister Pu owes me seventeen favors, and now he's going to cash them all in to expedite those approvals." She tapped the scroll. "Do you know what happens when favors get cashed in?"

"Work gets done?"

"Chaos happens." Director Zhao plucked the scroll from Zhang's desk. "Protocols get bypassed. Records get lost. And people like me—people who keep this bureaucracy running—we have to clean up the mess."

She leaned closer. "But chaos also creates opportunities. Tell me, Zhang Wei, do you play chess?"

"I've been known to lose spectacularly."

"Good. Losers understand consequences." She straightened up. "You're being transferred. Special assignment. My office, effective immediately."

"But my probation—"

"Approved." Director Zhao snapped her fingers. A clerk appeared at her side, holding a document that was already stamped with official seals. "Sign here."

Zhang looked at Hu. The supervisor's face had gone from red to an unhealthy shade of purple. "Director, with all due respect, Junior Clerk Zhang has only been with us for—"

"Three days. I know." Director Zhao's smile widened. "And in those three days, he's discovered a system error that your department has been ignoring for six months, hacked into a restricted administrative interface, and angered one of the most powerful immortals in the Heavenly Court."

She turned back to Zhang. "You're either the most incompetent clerk I've ever met, or the most dangerous. Either way, I want you on my team."

Zhang signed.

---

Director Zhao's office was nothing like the cramped cubicle farm Zhang had been working in. It occupied an entire floating island, connected to the main complex by a bridge made of pure light. The walls were lined with scrolls that moved on their own, their characters shifting and rearranging like living things.

"Sit." Director Zhao gestured to a chair that looked like it was made of compressed clouds. Zhang sat. It was surprisingly comfortable.

"I'm going to tell you something that should get me demoted to janitorial duty," she said, settling behind her desk. "The celestial bureaucracy is broken."

"I noticed."

"Everyone notices. But no one's done anything about it because the people in power benefit from the chaos." She pulled up a holographic display. Zhang recognized the Core Administrative Interface—the same sphere he'd touched in the white void.

"This is the Celestial Management System. CMS for short. It was designed three thousand years ago by the Jade Emperor's chief architect. It was supposed to automate destiny processing, karma accounting, and reincarnation logistics."

"Sounds ambitious."

"It was revolutionary. But it was also flawed. The original architect was executed for treason before he could finish the final patch."

Zhang raised an eyebrow. "Executed?"

"The official story says he tried to seize power. The unofficial story—" Director Zhao paused. "The unofficial story is that he discovered something in the system's code that the Jade Emperor didn't want anyone to find."

"What did he discover?"

Director Zhao's eyes met his. "That the celestial bureaucracy is running on borrowed time. Literally. The system is consuming immortal essence faster than the Heavenly Court can produce it. In approximately six hundred years, the entire system will crash. Every soul in existence—mortal, immortal, demonic—will be recycled into raw cosmic energy."

Zhang stared at her. "That's..."

"Apocalyptic. Yes. And the Jade Emperor knows. He's been trying to fix it for centuries, but every solution requires him to relinquish power. So instead, he's buried the problem under so much bureaucracy that no one can find it."

"Except you."

"Except me." Director Zhao leaned forward. "And now, except you."

The holographic display shifted, showing a complex web of connections. At its center was a single node, pulsing with red light.

"This is the source of the error that killed you," Director Zhao said. "Your death wasn't a mistake. It was intentional."

Zhang felt his hands clench. "Who?"

"Someone who's been systematically eliminating clerks who get too close to the truth. You're the seventh one in the last year."

"Then why am I still alive?"

"Because you're useful." Director Zhao smiled again. "And because the person who ordered your death doesn't know you've accessed the Core Interface. They think you're just another incompetent clerk who'll drink himself to death within a century."

"Comforting."

"It should be. It means you have time." She stood up. "But not much. The next Celestial Budget Committee meeting is in three days. During that meeting, I'm going to propose a system-wide audit. If it passes, we'll have access to every corner of the CMS."

"And if it doesn't?"

"Then the person who killed you will know someone's investigating. And they'll come for us both."

Zhang processed this. "So I need to make sure the audit passes."

"You need to do more than that." Director Zhao pulled up another file. "The Budget Committee is controlled by three immortals: Minister Pu of Heavenly Feasts, General Feng of Heavenly Defense, and Lady Xiu of Heavenly Records. Each of them has veto power."

"Can we convince them?"

"Minister Pu is angry about the chopstick requisitions. General Feng only cares about military spending. And Lady Xiu—" Director Zhao hesitated. "Lady Xiu hasn't spoken to anyone in three hundred years."

"Why?"

"No one knows. She sealed herself in the Heavenly Records Archive and hasn't left since. Her staff say she's been reviewing something. Something that's made her... afraid."

A notification appeared in Zhang's vision.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]
[Title: The Budget Gambit]
[Objective: Secure approval for System-wide Audit]
[Time Limit: 72 Hours]
[Rewards: Director Zhao's Trust, Full CMS Access, +5000 Karma Points]
[Penalty for Failure: Soul Deletion (Irreversible)]

Zhang looked at the quest. Then at Director Zhao. Then at the pulsing red node that represented the person who had murdered him.

"I'm in," he said. "But I need something first."

"Name it."

"Access to the Heavenly Records Archive. If Lady Xiu knows something, I want to know what it is."

Director Zhao's expression flickered—surprise, then calculation. "The archive is sealed. Even I can't—"

"I can hack it."

"Excuse me?"

Zhang pulled up the Core Administrative Interface. The sphere of gears and runes materialized above his palm. "The CMS has a backdoor. The original architect built it in case he needed emergency access. It's hidden in the soul-processing algorithms."

Director Zhao's eyes widened. "How do you know that?"

"Because I accessed it when I first touched the Core. It was buried so deep even the system didn't know it was there." Zhang grinned. "Whoever designed this system was a genius. But they were also paranoid. They left themselves a way in."

"And you're going to use it to break into the archive."

"I'm going to use it to save reality." Zhang stood up. "But first, I need to find a way past the archive's security. Any suggestions?"

Director Zhao was silent for a long moment. Then she reached into her robe and pulled out a small jade key. It glowed with an inner light.

"This is a Celestial Skeleton Key," she said. "It can unlock any door in the Heavenly Court. Once."

"Why haven't you used it?"

"Because once it's used, it's gone. And I've been saving it for something that matters." She held it out to him. "Convince me this matters."

Zhang took the key. "If Lady Xiu has been hiding in that archive for three centuries, she's found something. Something that scared her enough to abandon her duties. Something that the person who killed me doesn't want anyone to find."

He closed his fingers around the key. "I'm going to find out what it is."

Director Zhao nodded slowly. "Then I expect results, Junior Clerk Zhang. Or we'll both be processing reincarnation paperwork in the Hell of Unending Data Entry."

"I'd rather not."

"Neither would I."

Zhang turned to leave, but paused at the door. "Director Zhao?"

"Yes?"

"Thank you. For trusting me."

"Don't thank me yet." Her voice was cold. "Trust is a currency in the celestial bureaucracy. And you just spent a very large portion of mine."

Zhang stepped out of the office and into the bridge of light. Below him, the Heavenly Court stretched out in all its bureaucratic glory—towers of ledgers, rivers of ink, clouds made of pending requests.

Somewhere in that mess was the Heavenly Records Archive. And somewhere in that archive was the truth about his death.

He looked at the jade key in his hand.

Three days. Seventy-two hours. And a system that was literally falling

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Ghost in the Server Farm

Zhang Wei didn't wait for the system to finish its investigation. He pulled his hand away from the sphere and the white void dissolved around him, depositing him back in the cramped office cubicle that smelled like stale tea and desperation.

His jade token buzzed. Three messages.

[From: Internal Affairs Division - Alert Level: Yellow]
[Subject: Unauthorized Access]
[Body: Clerk Zhang Wei, your terminal has been flagged for accessing restricted records. Please report to Department of Internal Auditing, Floor 47, Room 4703 for mandatory re-education. Failure to appear within one celestial cycle will result in automatic demotion.]

Zhang deleted the message without reading the rest.

The second was from Supervisor Hu.

[From: Supervisor Hu]
[Subject: Your Performance]
[Body: I heard you've been poking around where you shouldn't. Come to my office. Now.]

The third message had no sender name. No subject line. Just a single line of text:

*They're watching the watchers. Meet me in Server Farm 7-C. Come alone.*

Zhang stared at the message for a long moment. Server Farm 7-C was in the basement levels—the kind of place where they sent clerks who'd been "administratively reorganized" into paper-pushing roles in departments that didn't officially exist. It was the kind of place where people went and never came back.

But someone had sent him a message without routing through the celestial messaging system. That meant they had access levels even higher than his newly acquired Tier 2 clearance. Or they were a ghost in the system.

Both options were equally terrifying and equally intriguing.

He grabbed his jade token and stood. The other clerks in his department watched him with expressions that ranged from pity to barely concealed glee. They knew. They always knew when someone was about to get crushed by the celestial machinery.

"Zhang." A hand grabbed his arm. It was Old Chen, the seventy-year-old accountant who'd been filing the same reports for three thousand years. His eyes were bloodshot and his breath smelled of celestial wine. "Don't go."

"I have to."

"No." Old Chen's grip tightened. His voice dropped to a whisper. "I've been here longer than anyone. I've seen clerks like you before. Ambitious. Clever. They always end up in the Error Correction Department."

"What's the Error Correction Department?"

Old Chen's face went pale. "It's where they send souls that need to be... corrected. Permanently."

Zhang pulled his arm free. "Then I'll be careful."

"You don't understand." Old Chen glanced around frantically, then leaned closer. "The system isn't broken. It's designed that way. The errors aren't mistakes—they're features. The deaths, the misrouted souls, the karmic imbalances—they're all intentional. Someone built this system to fail on purpose."

The words hit Zhang like a physical blow. "What?"

"I don't know who. I don't know why. But I know that every time someone gets close to the truth, they disappear." Old Chen pressed a small jade chip into Zhang's hand. "This is the key to Server Farm 7-C's auxiliary access. The same person who sent you that message gave it to me three thousand years ago, and told me to give it to the next one who asked the right questions."

"Next one?"

"Every five hundred years, someone stumbles onto the truth. Every five hundred years, they go to that server farm." Old Chen's eyes were wet. "Every five hundred years, they never come back."

Zhang looked at the chip. It hummed with a frequency he could feel in his teeth. Three thousand years. That meant Old Chen had been waiting for this moment for three thousand years.

And he'd never had the courage to go himself.

"Thank you," Zhang said.

Old Chen shook his head. "Don't thank me. Curse me. I should have gone myself. I should have done something. But I was afraid." He looked at Zhang with hollow eyes. "I'm still afraid. But maybe you're different."

Zhang pocketed the chip and walked toward the elevator. The doors opened immediately, as if the system was expecting him. He stepped inside and pressed the button for basement level 7.

The elevator descended for a long time. Longer than seven floors should take. The numbers on the display ticked past 7, then 8, then 9, then kept going. The air grew cold and thin. The lights flickered.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unregistered Elevator Movement Detected]
[Please present valid authorization]
[Warning: Unauthorized personnel are not permitted below Administrative Level 6]
[Warning: Biometric scan initiated]

Zhang's heart stopped. A beam of golden light swept across the elevator, scanning his face, his hands, his soul signature.

[Scan Complete]
[Identity: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Status: ALERT - Soul Tagged for Monitoring]
[Access: DENIED]

The elevator shuddered to a halt. The doors remained closed.

[Please remain stationary. Security personnel have been dispatched.]

"No," Zhang muttered. He pulled out the jade chip Old Chen had given him and pressed it against the control panel. The chip glowed, then merged with the panel, dissolving into the metal.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the elevator lurched and began descending again. Faster this time. The numbers blurred past—20, 30, 40, 50. The temperature dropped below freezing. Zhang's breath misted in front of his face.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Offline Route Activated]
[Warning: This route is not registered in the Celestial Transport Network]
[Proceed at your own risk]

The elevator finally stopped. The doors slid open, revealing a corridor made of raw, pulsing data. Walls of green text streamed past—lines of code, equations, administrative records. The floor hummed with electrical energy.

Zhang stepped out. The corridor stretched endlessly in both directions, curving into darkness.

A figure emerged from the data-streams. It was a woman wearing a clerk's uniform identical to Zhang's, but her face was obscured by a mask of shifting numbers. She spoke, and her voice came from everywhere at once.

"You came."

"Who are you?"

"I'm what happens when a clerk survives the Error Correction Department." She raised her hand, and the numbers on her face resolved into a face—young, tired, with eyes that had seen too much. "My name was Li Mei. I died in 1987. Car accident. They brought me here and I started asking questions."

"What did you find?"

"Everything." Li Mei gestured at the corridor. "This is the true heart of the Celestial Bureaucracy. Not the offices upstairs, not the departments, not the gods. This. The code. The system that runs everything."

"It's broken," Zhang said.

"It's not broken." Li Mei's voice hardened. "It's sabotaged. Someone inserted a recursive loop into the core administrative protocol three thousand years ago. It causes cascading errors in the mortal destiny processing system. The deaths, the misrouted souls, the karmic chaos—it's all by design."

"Who?"

"We don't know. The code is signed with an ancient celestial signature, but the identity has been redacted. Even the highest-ranking immortals can't access it."

Zhang's mind raced. "Supervisor Hu. He knows something. He was the one who processed my death. He's been covering it up."

"Hu is a pawn. He doesn't know who he's working for." Li Mei stepped closer. "But I know where the answer is. The core server. It's in the deepest level of this facility, protected by a firewall that hasn't been breached in three thousand years."

"Can you get me in?"

"I can get you close." Li Mei handed him a data-slate covered in glowing runes. "This is a backdoor I've been building for five hundred years. It will bypass the outer security layers, but the inner ones... you'll have to figure those out yourself."

Zhang took the slate. It was warm, almost alive. "Why are you helping me?"

"Because I'm tired." Li Mei's face flickered, the numbers threatening to reclaim it. "I've been hiding in this server farm for a thousand years. I've watched clerks come and go. I've seen the system consume everyone who tried to fight it. But you're different."

"How?"
