"You're not afraid of the system. You see it for what it is—a machine. And machines can be hacked." She smiled, but it was a sad smile. "I can't go with you. I've integrated too deeply with the server farm's code. If I leave, I'll dissolve."

"Then how do I find the core server?"

"Follow the red lines." Li Mei pointed at the floor. Thin lines of crimson light pulsed through the data-streams, tracing a path into the darkness. "They'll take you to the central chamber. But be careful. The deeper you go, the more the system will fight back. And there are things down here that aren't part of the bureaucracy anymore."

"What things?"

Li Mei's face went blank. For a moment, she was just a silhouette against the glowing walls. Then she spoke, her voice barely a whisper.

"Ghosts. The souls of clerks who tried to escape. They've been absorbed into the system, turned into guardians. They don't remember who they were. They only know their purpose: stop anyone who tries to reach the core."

Zhang looked at the path ahead. The red lines pulsed, beckoning him forward.

"One more thing," Li Mei said. "The person who sabotaged the system. They're still here. Somewhere. Watching."

"How do you know?"

"Because three thousand years ago, someone tried to destroy the Celestial Bureaucracy from within. They failed. But they didn't die. They became something else." She pointed at Zhang's jade token. "Check your notification history. Search for the name 'The First Clerk.'"

Zhang pulled up his token. He searched.

[Search Result: No Records Found]
[Search Result: Access Denied]
[Search Result: Identity: ████████ - Status: ACTIVE]
[Location: ████████]
[Warning: This entity is not bound by celestial law]

The token went dark.

Li Mei stepped backward, dissolving into the data-streams. "The First Clerk built this system. Then they tried to tear it down. They're the reason the bureaucracy is broken. And they're the reason you're dead."

"Where are they?" Zhang called out.

But Li Mei was gone, swallowed by the endless code.

Zhang stood alone in the corridor, holding the data-slate, listening to the hum of the celestial machinery. The red lines pulsed at his feet, leading into the darkness.

He took a step forward.

Then another.

And then the corridor exploded with light.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Security Breach Detected]
[Location: Server Farm 7-C - Restricted Zone]
[Deploying: Error Correction Protocol - Level Omega]
[Target: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Classification: Hostile Entity - Terminate Immediately]

The walls shifted. The data-streams congealed into shapes—humanoid figures made of pure information, their faces twisted into screams of eternal agony. The ghosts Li Mei had warned him about.

They turned toward Zhang with hollow eyes.

And they charged.

Zhang ran.

Behind him, the ghosts screamed with voices that weren't voices, their code-based bodies leaving trails of corrupted data. The red lines beneath his feet pulsed faster, guiding him through a maze of corridors that shifted and changed as he moved.

He rounded a corner and found himself in a vast chamber. At its center, suspended in a

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Protocol That Shouldn't Exist

Zhang barely had time to process the alert before the sphere in front of him flickered. Warning symbols bloomed across its surface like bruises, red characters bleeding into the space around him.

[UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS DETECTED]
[INITIATING COUNTERMEASURES]
[ESTIMATED TIME UNTIL SYSTEM PURGE: 47 SECONDS]

"Of course," Zhang muttered, already moving. He didn't know what a system purge looked like, but he had a strong suspicion it wasn't the kind you survived. His fingers flew across the interface, calling up the commands he'd memorized from the forbidden database. "Cancel countermeasures. Authorization: Zhang Wei, ID 7845-B, Senior Clerk Provisional."

[INVALID AUTHORIZATION]

"Override with emergency protocol."

[INVALID PROTOCOL]

"Override with—" Zhang stopped. The sphere was pulsing faster now, the red consuming more of its surface. He had seconds left. And then he remembered something. Something he'd seen in the database but dismissed because it made no sense.

He typed: [PROTOCOL 000: HEAVENLY RESET]

The sphere went dark.

For three agonizing seconds, Zhang floated in absolute silence and blackness. The kind of silence that pressed against your ears like physical weight. The kind of darkness that made you question whether you still had eyes.

Then the sphere lit up again, but different. The gears now spun in reverse. The runes glowed with a soft blue light instead of harsh gold. And a new message appeared:

[PROTOCOL 000 ACCEPTED]
[SYSTEM RUNNING IN LEGACY MODE]
[TRACE PROTOCOLS: DISABLED]
[ALERT SYSTEM: OFFLINE]
[Welcome back, Administrator]

Administrator.

The word hit Zhang like a physical blow. He stared at it, waiting for the punchline, waiting for another notification to correct the "mistake." But nothing came.

He was now an Administrator.

Not a Junior Clerk. Not a Senior Clerk. Not even a Supervisor or Department Head. An Administrator. The kind of being that made gods nervous.

"How?" Zhang whispered.

The sphere pulsed once, and a new interface appeared. It was different from the bureaucratic nightmare he'd been navigating. This one was clean, intuitive, almost friendly. It showed the entire structure of the Celestial Bureaucracy as a series of interconnected nodes, each one representing a department, a function, a god.

And at the very top, above even the Celestial Emperor, was a single node labeled:

[ORIGINAL ADMINISTRATOR]
STATUS: VACANT

Zhang's blood ran cold. Original Administrator. Vacant. As in, the position was empty. As in, someone had been there once, and now they weren't.

The sphere pulsed again, and more data flowed into his mind. The truth. The real truth behind the Celestial Bureaucracy.

It hadn't always been this way. Once, the system was simple. Souls lived, souls died, souls reincarnated. No karma points. No destiny reports. No audits. Just a clean cycle of existence.

Then the Original Administrator disappeared.

And the gods, left without guidance, built their own system. Layer upon layer of rules, regulations, and redundancies. They created the bureaucracy to fill the void left by their absent creator. But they didn't understand the original code. They just kept adding patches and workarounds, building a tower of celestial red tape that grew more unstable with each passing millennium.

The "bugs" Zhang had found weren't bugs. They were features of the original system, buried under centuries of bureaucratic garbage.

And now the system had recognized him as an Administrator. Why? Because he'd accessed the original protocol. Because he'd proven he could see through the layers of obfuscation. Because—

A new notification appeared. This one wasn't from the system. It was from the jade token, which was now glowing with frantic urgency.

[URGENT MESSAGE - Supervisor Hu]
[Zhang. My office. NOW.]

Zhang closed the sphere interface. The white void dissolved around him, and he found himself back at his desk, the jade token still warm in his hand. Li Feng was staring at him with wide eyes.

"Where did you just go?" she asked. "You were gone for fifteen minutes. I was about to report you missing."

"Went to the bathroom," Zhang said.

"Your body was still here."

Damn.

"Meditation technique," Zhang said. "Very advanced. You wouldn't understand."

Li Feng's eyes narrowed, but before she could press further, a shadow fell over them both. Supervisor Hu stood there, his face a mask of barely controlled fury.

"Office. Now." He didn't wait for a response, just turned and walked away.

Zhang followed.

Hu's office was exactly what you'd expect from a mid-level celestial bureaucrat: functional, drab, and covered in motivational posters that said things like "SYNERGY IS KEY" and "REPORT ERRORS PROMPTLY." The only personal touch was a small jade statue of a three-legged toad sitting on a pile of gold coins.

"Sit," Hu said, gesturing to a chair.

Zhang sat.

Hu circled around his desk, not sitting himself. He just stood there, staring at Zhang with an intensity that would have made lesser men sweat. Zhang had dealt with worse managers. Once, his boss in life had made him work through Christmas while she went on a cruise. Hu's silent treatment was amateur hour.

"I've been reviewing your file," Hu said finally. "Interesting reading. Went from entry-level clerk to Senior Clerk Provisional in less than a week. That's... unprecedented."

"I'm a fast learner."

"Indeed." Hu picked up a scroll from his desk. "And you've also accessed seventeen restricted databases, modified twenty-three destiny threads, and somehow convinced the celestial ledger to show a zero balance for your entire department's karmic deficit."

Zhang kept his face neutral. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't." Hu slammed the scroll down. "Don't play stupid with me. I've been in this department for three thousand years. I know when someone is gaming the system. And you, Zhang Wei, are gaming the system."

"Prove it."

Hu's eye twitched. "I don't need to prove it. I just need to report it. And I have. The Celestial Compliance Division is opening an investigation. They'll be here within the hour."

Now Zhang felt a chill. Celestial Compliance Division. He'd seen references to them in the database. They were the immortals you called when you wanted someone to stop existing. Not just die. Stop existing, permanently, across all timelines.

"They won't find anything," Zhang said.

"Oh, I'm sure they won't. You're clearly competent enough to cover your tracks." Hu's smile was thin and predatory. "But the investigation itself will take time. And resources. And during that time, your access will be revoked. Your provisional status will be suspended. And you'll be reassigned to the department of your... predecessor."

"Predecessor?"

Hu picked up a small token from his desk. It was identical to the one Zhang had received on his first day, except this one was cracked down the middle. "The last clerk who tried what you're trying. Clerk ID 7844. Your predecessor."

Zhang's mind raced. "What happened to him?"

"He was reassigned." Hu's smile widened. "To the Department of Non-Existent Souls. It's a very small department. Only one employee. And the employee doesn't exist."

"You're lying."

"Am I?" Hu tossed the token to Zhang. "Check for yourself. Access the personnel records. If you still can."

Zhang caught the token. It was cold in his hand, colder than it should have been. He focused his will, trying to access the system, but something was blocking him. The interface that had been so clear just minutes ago was now fuzzy, unresponsive.

[ACCESS RESTRICTED]
[USER: ZHANG WEI]
[STATUS: PENDING INVESTIGATION]
[ALL PRIVILEGES: SUSPENDED]

Zhang looked up at Hu. The supervisor was watching him with the satisfied expression of a cat that had cornered a mouse.

"You see," Hu said, "I've been doing this for three thousand years. I've seen a hundred ambitious clerks try to climb the ladder. And I've seen every single one of them fall. The Celestial Bureaucracy doesn't reward ambition. It rewards patience. It rewards conformity. And most of all, it rewards loyalty."

"Loyalty to what?" Zhang asked. "A system that's broken? A system that kills people because of clerical errors and then covers it up?"

Hu's expression hardened. "Loyalty to the system itself. The system is perfect. The system is eternal. The system is the will of Heaven made manifest. And you, Zhang Wei, have been trying to hack it. Which means you are a threat."

"I found a way to fix it."

"You found a way to break it." Hu leaned forward, his face inches from Zhang's. "And now you'll be unmade. That's the price of ambition in Heaven."

Zhang stared into Hu's eyes. He could see the truth there. Hu wasn't just a bureaucrat. He was a gatekeeper. A guardian of the status quo. And he'd done this before. Probably many times.

But Hu had made one mistake.

He'd given Zhang the cracked token.

Even with his access suspended, Zhang could still feel the faint pulse of Administrator-level code running through his soul. The protocol he'd activated had changed him at a fundamental level. The system might have locked his user account, but it couldn't lock what he'd become.

Zhang smiled.

"What's so funny?" Hu asked.

"Nothing," Zhang said. "I was just thinking about how long it took you to notice something."

"Notice what?"

"That I've already done more damage than you can imagine."

Hu's eyes widened. He turned to his desk, frantically pulling up interfaces. "What did you do? What did you—"

The first explosion shook the office.

It wasn't a physical explosion. It was a karmic explosion. A sudden, violent release of accumulated spiritual debt that rippled through the Celestial Bureaucracy like a shockwave. Alarms began wailing. Red lights flashed.

[SYSTEM ALERT]
[MASSIVE KARMIC IMBALANCE DETECTED]
[SOURCE: DEPARTMENT OF MORTAL AFFAIRS - DIVISION 7]
[MULTIPLE DESTINY THREADS: CORRUPTED]
[RECOMMENDED ACTION: EVACUATE]

"What have you done?" Hu screamed.

Zhang stood up slowly. "I didn't do anything. The system did it. Or rather, the system finally showed what's been there all along."

He reached for his jade token. It was still locked, still restricted. But now he could feel something new. A connection. A thread leading from his token to the core of the system itself.

The Protocol 000 activation hadn't just given him Administrator access. It had made him part of the original code. A living key to the system's true functions.

And that meant he could unlock anything.

Zhang focused, and the token flared to life. Not with the golden light of the bureaucracy, but with the blue light of the original system. The light of the missing Administrator.

[ACCESS RESTORED]
[USER: ZHANG WEI]
[STATUS: ADMINISTRATOR (ACTING)]
[ALL PRIVILEGES: UNLOCKED]

Hu stumbled backward, his face pale. "That's not possible. That's not—"

"It is possible," Zhang said.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Immortal Who Forgot Password

The sphere pulsed once, twice, then went dark.

Zhang blinked. The white void around him flickered like a dying fluorescent light. Somewhere in the distance, he heard a sound he hadn't expected: the ringing of a telephone.

Not a celestial chime. Not a system notification chime. An actual, mortal-world telephone ring.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Incoming Communication - External Line]

[Source: Unknown]

[Warning: External communications are restricted to Tier 5 personnel and above]

[Your current access level: Tier 2]

[Would you like to:]

[Accept Call]

[Ignore]

[Trace Origin]

Zhang stared at the options. External line. That meant someone outside the Celestial Bureaucracy was calling him. But who? He'd been dead for less than a week. His mother didn't even know how to use a smartphone, let alone call the afterlife.

He selected [Trace Origin].

The sphere flickered back to life, but this time it showed a different image: a messy desk covered in stacks of paper, empty tea cups, and what looked like a half-eaten baozi. Behind the desk sat a woman in her thirties, wearing a rumpled business suit and glasses that were clearly taped together at the bridge.

She was frantically typing on a keyboard that had several keys missing.

[Connection Established]

[User: Li Mei - ID 2319]

[Status: Alive]

Zhang's blood ran cold.

ID 2319. The clerk whose error had killed him.

"Hello?" Li Mei's voice crackled through the void. "Hello? Is this working? Stupid piece of—" There was a thud, presumably her hitting the keyboard. "Can you hear me?"

Zhang hesitated. Every instinct told him to hang up, to sever the connection and bury himself in administrative protocols until he figured out what was going on. But Li Mei was alive. She was still in the mortal world, still working in whatever office had caused his death.

And she was calling him.

"I can hear you," Zhang said.

"Oh thank the heavens." Li Mei's voice trembled with relief. "Listen, I don't have much time. They're monitoring my terminal now. But I had to warn you."

"Warn me about what?"

"About Supervisor Hu. He's not just covering up the error that killed you. He's been doing this for years. Hundreds of souls, maybe thousands. He reroutes destinies to meet his quotas, and when there's a mistake, he buries it."

Zhang's grip tightened on the sphere. "Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I'm next." Li Mei's voice dropped to a whisper. "I found the records. All of them. He's been manipulating the system to make it look like his department is the most efficient in the Heavenly Bureaucracy. But the real numbers? They're catastrophic. Souls getting sent to wrong reincarnation queues. People dying decades early. And the worst part?"

"What?"

"He's been selling access. Rich mortals, powerful families, they pay him to alter their karmic destinies. He's been doing it for three hundred years."

Three hundred years. Zhang's mind raced. That meant Hu had been alive long before he joined the Celestial Bureaucracy. He wasn't just a corrupt official—he was an immortal who had found a way to game the system.

"Where are you now?" Zhang asked.

"Underground parking garage, level three. There's a dead zone in the celestial monitoring here. I've been hiding for two days." A pause. "They found my apartment this morning. Burned it down. Made it look like a gas leak."

"Who's 'they'?"

"I don't know. But they're not regular clerks. They have access to the Core Administrative Interface. They can—" A crash echoed through the line. Li Mei gasped. "Oh no. They found me."

"Li Mei—"

"Listen to me. There's a backup. I stored everything on a—" The line went dead.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Connection Lost]

[Source: External Line Terminated]

[Recommended Action: File Incident Report with Department of Inter-Planar Communications]

Zhang stared at the dark sphere. His hands were shaking. Not from fear—from anger. Supervisor Hu had been running a criminal operation for centuries, using the Celestial Bureaucracy's own systems to cover his tracks. And now Li Mei, the woman whose mistake had killed him, was probably dead too.

But the backup. She mentioned a backup.

He accessed the system logs, searching for any trace of Li Mei's work. The records were there, buried under layers of encryption and redaction. But Zhang had something Hu didn't: a Tier 2 access level that let him see the administrative metadata.

There. A file transfer. Destination: unknown. Time stamp: three hours ago.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unauthorized Data Exfiltration Detected]

[Source: Clerk ID 2319 - Li Mei]

[Destination: [REDACTED]]

[File Size: 847 GB]

[Status: Transfer Incomplete - 43% Complete]

Forty-three percent. That was better than nothing. But where had she sent it?

Zhang pulled up the routing logs. The data had been sent through a series of proxy servers, bouncing between mortal and celestial networks until it reached... a cloud storage service.

A mortal cloud storage service.

Zhang almost laughed. Li Mei had hidden the evidence of a three-hundred-year-old celestial conspiracy in Dropbox.

He accessed the account credentials from the metadata. Email: limei.notdead@gmail.com. Password: ********

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Warning - Accessing Mortal Networks is Prohibited Without Proper Authorization]

[Violation of: Celestial-Mortal Interface Treaty, Article 7, Section 4]

[Penalty: Soul Deletion, Memory Wipe, or Reassignment to Hell of Unending Data Entry]

[Proceed?]

Zhang selected [Yes].

The white void around him dissolved, replaced by a blur of colors and sounds. He felt himself falling again, but this time it was different. He wasn't moving through space—he was moving through the barriers between worlds. The mortal realm and the celestial bureaucracy were supposed to be separate, governed by ancient treaties and cosmic laws.

But someone had built a backdoor.

The Dropbox interface appeared in front of him, rendered in the same glowing runes as the celestial system. There was only one file: [EVIDENCE_ARCHIVE.zip].

He downloaded it.

The file opened to reveal thousands of documents. Financial records. Karmic ledgers. Destiny modification logs. Names of wealthy mortals who had paid for better reincarnations. Names of souls who had been sacrificed to cover Hu's tracks.

And at the very bottom, a single text file: [CONFESSION.txt]

Zhang opened it.

*My name is Li Mei. I've been a clerk in the Department of Mortal Affairs for twelve years. When I first discovered the discrepancies in the reincarnation queues, I thought it was a bug. I tried to report it through proper channels. But the channels were controlled by Supervisor Hu.*

*He offered me a choice: keep quiet and receive a promotion, or speak out and be "reassigned." I chose to keep quiet. I've been complicit in his crimes for eleven years.*

*But I can't do it anymore. Not after what happened to Zhang Wei.*

*He was supposed to live. He was supposed to invent something that would save millions of lives. But Hu needed a quota filler, and Zhang's destiny was "flexible." So Hu altered the records, made it look like Zhang was supposed to die in that accident.*

*I processed the order. I sent him to the Reincarnation Void.*

*I'm sorry, Zhang Wei. I'm so sorry.*

*The backup of all evidence is attached. Use it wisely. They'll come for me soon, but maybe this will make things right.*

*—Li Mei*

Zhang sat in silence, the file burning into his vision.

She was sorry. She was sorry for killing him, for covering up the truth, for letting Hu continue his operations for over a decade. And now she was probably dead.

But she'd given him the weapons he needed.

He accessed the celestial system again, pulling up Supervisor Hu's file. Current status: Active. Location: Department of Mortal Affairs, Floor 7, Office 712.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Available]

[Quest: Expose Corruption]

[Objective: Deliver evidence of Supervisor Hu's crimes to the Celestial Audit Bureau]

[Reward: Promotion to Tier 3, Full Soul Restoration, Reincarnation Priority]

[Warning: Supervisor Hu has connections at the highest levels of the Heavenly Bureaucracy. Pursuing this quest may result in:]

- Soul Deletion

- Eternal Damnation

- Reassignment to Hell of Unending Data Entry

- [REDACTED]

[Accept?]

Zhang's finger hovered over the option. He thought about his mother, never knowing what happened to him. He thought about the invention he was supposed to create, the lives he was supposed to save. He thought about Li Mei, hiding in a parking garage, trying to do the right thing at the end.

And he thought about Supervisor Hu, sitting in his office, probably drinking tea and filing lies.

He selected [Accept].

The sphere exploded with light.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Quest Accepted]

[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]

[Current Objective: Deliver Evidence to Celestial Audit Bureau]

[Location: Floor 42, Tower of Heavenly Justice]

[Time Limit: 24 Hours]

[Warning: Supervisor Hu has been notified of your actions]

[His enforcers are en route to your location]

The white void dissolved. Zhang found himself back in the storage room, surrounded by filing cabinets and the faint hum of celestial machinery. The door burst open.

Three figures stood in the doorway, their faces obscured by glowing white masks. They wore identical black suits, each with a badge that read: [Internal Affairs - Special Operations Division].

"Zhang Wei," the lead figure said, his voice flat and mechanical. "You are under arrest for violation of Celestial-Mortal Interface Treaty, unauthorized access to restricted records, and tampering with administrative protocols."

Zhang slowly raised his hands. "I have the evidence."

"We know." The figure stepped forward. "And we're going to make sure it never reaches the Audit Bureau."

The other two moved to flank him. Zhang's mind raced. He had twenty-four hours, but they'd already found him. How was he supposed to deliver the evidence if they were watching his every move?

Then he noticed something.

The lead figure's badge was crooked.

Not a lot, just slightly off-center. But in a bureaucracy where everything was supposed to be perfect, a crooked badge was a sign of something wrong. These weren't real enforcers. They were impostors.

And impostors could be outsmarted.

"You know," Zhang said, forcing a calm smile, "you guys really should work on your attention to detail."

The lead figure tilted his head. "What?"

Zhang pointed at the crooked badge. "That. It's going to get you flagged in your next performance review."

The figure looked down, reflexively reaching to adjust it.

That was all Zhang needed.

He dove to the side, rolling behind a filing cabinet as the impostors drew weapons—not celestial enforcement wands, but mortal-made pistols. Bullets tore through the cabinets, sending papers flying into the air.

Zhang scrambled through the chaos, grabbing a jade token from a fallen clerk's desk. He didn't know where it would take him, but anywhere was better than here.

He smashed the token against the floor.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Emergency Transport Activated]

[Destination: Random]

[Warning: Destination may be

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Immortal Who Knew Too Much

Zhang barely had time to withdraw his hand from the sphere before the void around him shattered like glass.

He was back in his cubicle, gasping, the jade token cold against his chest. The familiar hum of celestial machinery filled his ears, but something was different. The air felt wrong. Charged. Like the moment before a thunderstorm.

A shadow fell across his desk.

"Zhang Wei."

The voice was smooth, cultured, and utterly devoid of warmth. Zhang looked up to find a woman standing before him. She was tall, with silver hair that flowed like liquid mercury and eyes that shifted between colors—gold, blue, crimson, gold again. She wore a pristine white robe embroidered with characters that hurt to look at directly.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Detected Entity - Immortal Li Mei]
[Position: Auditor, Department of Internal Investigations]
[Warning: Do not attempt deception. Auditor Li Mei has 10,000+ years of experience detecting lies.]

Zhang's mouth went dry. Internal Investigations. That was the celestial equivalent of the IRS, the FBI, and the Spanish Inquisition all rolled into one.

"Auditor Li Mei," he said, standing and bowing. "What an honor. Can I help you with something?"

Li Mei's eyes settled on a pale green—which Zhang had learned meant she was amused, not angry. Yet.

"You accessed restricted records seventeen minutes ago," she said. "Records pertaining to your own death. Would you like to explain why?"

Seventeen minutes. That meant the system had flagged him almost immediately. Zhang's mind raced through possibilities. Denial? Pointless. She had the logs. Deflection? Risky. She'd probably seen every excuse ever invented. Truth? Maybe. But not all of it.

"I was curious," Zhang said. "I died unexpectedly. I wanted to understand what happened."

Li Mei's lips twitched. "Curiosity. A mortal trait. One we usually correct within the first century of service."

"Then I guess I'm a slow learner."

She laughed. It was a pleasant sound, like wind chimes, but it made Zhang's skin crawl. "You're either very brave or very stupid, Clerk Zhang. I haven't decided which yet."

"Could it be both?"

"Possibly." She reached into her sleeve and produced a scroll. "I've been reviewing your file. Interesting reading. You've only been here a few weeks, and already you've bypassed several security protocols, accessed restricted data, and modified your own access level."

"Creative problem-solving?"

"Unauthorized system manipulation."

Zhang held his breath. This was it. The moment where she either deleted his soul or dragged him to some celestial prison for the rest of eternity.

But instead of condemning him, Li Mei unrolled the scroll and handed it to him.

"Read it."

Zhang took the scroll cautiously. The characters were written in gold ink that seemed to move as he looked at them. It took him a moment to understand what he was reading.

It was a list. Names. Hundreds of them. All mortal souls who had died due to administrative errors in the last five years.

And none of them had been reincarnated.

"They're still here?" Zhang asked, his voice barely a whisper.

"Somewhere," Li Mei said. "The system doesn't delete them. It... redistributes them. To various departments. Labor, mostly. Data processing. Maintenance of the celestial infrastructure."

Zhang thought of the Hell of Unending Data Entry. The warning he'd received. The token that had shown him the truth.

"They're slaves," he said.

"Indentured servants," Li Mei corrected. "There's a difference. In theory."

"In theory."

She smiled again, but this time there was no amusement in it. "I've been investigating these discrepancies for three centuries. Every time I get close to the source, something happens. Leads vanish. Witnesses are reassigned. Evidence gets lost in the system."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you found something I couldn't." Li Mei leaned closer, her eyes fixed on his. "The record you accessed—the log of the error that killed you. It mentioned a specific responsible party. Clerk ID 2319."

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said.

"No." Li Mei shook her head. "That ID doesn't belong to Hu. It belongs to someone much higher in the hierarchy. Someone who's been covering their tracks for millennia."

Zhang's blood ran cold. "Who?"

"I don't know. The ID is encrypted with a level of security I can't bypass. But you..." She studied him like a specimen. "You have a unique ability. You think like a mortal. You see the system not as sacred code, but as a tool to be manipulated. That perspective is valuable."

"So you want me to hack the celestial bureaucracy."

"I want you to find out who's been killing mortals and enslaving their souls." Li Mei's eyes shifted to a deep, angry red. "And I want you to bring them to justice."

---

The next three days passed in a blur of data analysis and system navigation. Zhang barely slept. He barely ate. The celestial cafeteria served ambrosia that tasted like whatever you wanted most, but he couldn't be bothered to enjoy it.

He had work to do.

Li Mei had given him access to certain backdoor protocols—off-the-books pathways through the celestial network that allowed him to move undetected. In exchange, he sent her regular reports on his findings.

And he was finding a lot.

The pattern was subtle, buried under layers of bureaucratic noise. But once Zhang knew what to look for, it became obvious. Souls were being misrouted at an alarming rate. Always the same type: mortals who died young, with high karmic potential. Creative minds. Problem-solvers. Innovators.

People like him.

"Why?" Zhang muttered, staring at his screen. "Why target specific types of souls?"

He cross-referenced the data with Li Mei's files. The souls that were taken—the ones never reincarnated—were all being sent to a single location. A department that didn't officially exist.

Department 0.

Zhang tried to access its records. Nothing. Tried to find its location. Nothing. It was as if the department had been erased from the system entirely.

But nothing was ever truly deleted. Not in a bureaucracy. There were always traces. Backups. Memos that someone forgot to shred.

He found one such memo buried in a file from three thousand years ago.

[INTERNAL MEMO - EYES ONLY]
[FROM: Director of Special Projects]
[TO: Department 0 Personnel]
[SUBJECT: Soul Harvesting Protocol Update]

The memo was brief, but it confirmed everything Zhang suspected. Department 0 existed. It was harvesting souls. And it was using them for something called the "Great Work."

Whatever that meant.

Zhang was about to dig deeper when his jade token pulsed with a message.

[URGENT: Your presence is requested at the Celestial Tribunal]
[Reason: Review of Unauthorized System Access]
[Time: Immediately]
[Note: Failure to appear will result in automatic soul deletion]

Zhang's heart stopped.

The Celestial Tribunal. That was the highest court in the heavenly bureaucracy. The place where even immortals feared to tread.

He'd been caught.

---

The Celestial Tribunal was nothing like Zhang expected.

Instead of a grand hall with marble pillars and golden thrones, it was a small, cramped room that looked exactly like a municipal courthouse. Fluorescent lights buzzed overhead. The air smelled of stale coffee and desperation.

And behind the judge's bench sat a man who looked like he'd been born in a suit.

"Zhang Wei," the judge said, his voice tired. "You've been accused of unauthorized system manipulation, accessing restricted records, and conspiracy to undermine celestial governance. How do you plead?"

Zhang glanced around the room. No jury. No audience. Just him and the judge.

And one other person.

Supervisor Hu sat in the corner, arms crossed, smirking.

"Not guilty," Zhang said.

The judge sighed. "Of course you are. They always are." He shuffled through papers. "Let's see what we have here. Evidence of tampering. Logs of unauthorized access. Testimony from Auditor Li Mei—"

"Li Mei?" Zhang's head snapped up.

"Who confirmed that you accessed the system without proper authorization," the judge continued. "And that you attempted to access Department 0 records."

Li Mei. She'd set him up.

"Wait," Zhang said. "She's the one who told me to investigate."

"She told you?" The judge raised an eyebrow. "So you admit to the investigation?"

"No, I—" Zhang stopped. Realization hit him like a brick. "She set a trap. She wanted me to access the records so she could report me."

"Auditor Li Mei has been investigating corruption in the celestial bureaucracy for three centuries," the judge said. "She's one of our most trusted agents. Why would she set a trap for a junior clerk?"

Because she's the one behind it all.

The thought hit Zhang like a lightning bolt. Li Mei wasn't trying to find the culprit. She was protecting them. She'd used him to find out how much a mortal could discover—and when he got too close, she'd thrown him under the celestial bus.

"Supervisor Hu," the judge said, "you have something to add?"

Hu stood, brushing off his robes. "Yes, Your Honor. I have evidence that the defendant was planning to hack the Core Administrative Interface and modify his own destiny."

"That's a lie," Zhang said.

"Is it?" Hu pulled out a jade token. "I have logs showing that Zhang Wei attempted to access destiny modification protocols. The same protocols that, if used improperly, could allow a mortal soul to ascend to immortality."

The judge's eyes narrowed. "Is this true?"

"It's not what it looks like," Zhang said. "I was investigating—"

"Investigation is not your job, Clerk Zhang," the judge interrupted. "Your job is to file paperwork. To process destiny reports. To follow the rules." He leaned forward. "The rules exist for a reason. They maintain order. They prevent chaos. And when someone breaks them, they must face the consequences."

Zhang opened his mouth to argue, but a notification appeared before him.

[TRIBUNAL VERDICT: Guilty]
[Charge: High Treason Against Celestial Order]
[Punishment: Eternal Indentured Servitude - Department 0]

Department 0.

The place he'd been trying to find.

The place where souls went to disappear forever.

"No," Zhang whispered.

"Take him away," the judge said.

Two celestial guards materialized at Zhang's sides, grabbing his arms. He struggled, but their grip was like iron.

"Wait," he shouted. "I have proof. I have evidence that Li Mei—"

But the guards were already dragging him away. The last thing Zhang saw before the doors closed was Li Mei standing in the hallway, watching him with those shifting, unreadable eyes.

She smiled.

And then the world went dark.

---

Zhang woke up in chains.

He was in a massive cavern, lit by flickering green crystals. Thousands of souls were chained to workstations, their fingers flying across interfaces made of pure light. The air hummed with the sound of data processing.

And in the center of it all stood a massive machine—a sphere of interlocking gears and runes, just like the one he'd seen in the Core Administrative Interface.

But this one was different.

This one was bleeding.

Crimson energy dripped from its gears, pooling on the floor below. The souls working nearby seemed to be drawing power from it, their chains pulsing with the same red light.

"Welcome to Department 0," a voice said.

Zhang turned. Li Mei stood behind him,

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Immortal's Secret Side Hustle

The white void shimmered around Zhang as he pulled his hand back from the celestial sphere. The notification about the detected process still hung in the air, pulsing with an urgency that made his dead heart—or whatever passed for it in the afterlife—skip a beat.

*Investigate.* Right. That meant someone was coming.

Zhang's fingers flew across the interface, navigating through layers of cascading menus and subroutines that materialized before him like origami made of light. He needed to cover his tracks. The system was old, bloated with millennia of accumulated code and celestial patches. If there was one thing Zhang had learned in his mortal years of IT support, it was that old systems had blind spots.

He found one.

[SYSTEM COMMAND: Execute Purge Protocol Gamma-7]
[Target: Access Logs - User ID 7845-B]
[Timeframe: Last 15 Minutes]
[Status: Executing... Complete]

The notification about the detected process flickered and vanished. Zhang let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding. But he wasn't done yet. He had Supervisor Hu's name. He had the record of the error that killed him. Now he needed evidence.

He searched the database for Clerk ID 2319.

[RECORD: Clerk ID 2319]
[Name: Li Ming]
[Status: Transferred - Department of Celestial Archives (Cycle 847, Day 123)]
[Notes: Exemplary service record. No disciplinary actions.]

Transferred. Of course. Hu had moved the responsible clerk to another department, probably to bury the evidence. But the archives were still accessible. And if Li Ming was still alive—or whatever passed for alive up here—he might have a story to tell.

Another notification popped up, but this one was different. It wasn't a system alert. It was a personal message.

[INCOMING MESSAGE: User ID 2319]
[Subject: You found me]

Zhang's blood ran cold. *Impossible.* He had just accessed the record. How could—

The message expanded automatically.

*"I've been waiting for someone to dig into Batch 374. Meet me in the Archive Repository, Section 7-F. Come alone. If Supervisor Hu finds out, we're both deleted."*

Zhang stared at the message. A trap? Probably. But if Li Ming was willing to talk, it might be his only chance to get the full story.

He closed the interface and stepped back from the celestial sphere. The white void dissolved around him, and he found himself standing in the hallway outside the Department of Mortal Affairs, exactly where he had been before his unauthorized trip to the Core Administrative Interface.

The door to the archives was at the end of the hall, guarded by a bored-looking immortal in a crumpled uniform. Zhang approached, his mind racing through possible excuses.

"Stop," the guard said, not looking up from the scroll he was reading. "Section 7-F is restricted. Authorized personnel only."

Zhang reached into his pocket and pulled out his jade token. "I have clearance. Senior Clerk Zhang Wei, Department of Mortal Affairs."

The guard finally looked up. His eyes narrowed. "That's a Tier 2 token. Section 7-F requires Tier 4."

"I'm on official business from Supervisor Hu." Zhang kept his voice steady. "He sent me to retrieve a file."

The guard snorted. "Hu never sends anyone. He comes himself."

*Damn.* Zhang had hoped the immortal would be too lazy to question a direct order. Time for Plan B.

"Look," Zhang said, lowering his voice conspiratorially. "Between you and me, Hu's been acting strange lately. I think he's hiding something. And I think it's in Section 7-F."

The guard's expression flickered with interest. "What kind of something?"

"I don't know yet. But if I find it, and you helped me, there might be a promotion in it for both of us."

The guard studied him for a long moment, then shrugged. "Fine. But if Hu asks, you never saw me."

He stepped aside, and the door to the archives slid open with a groan of ancient machinery.

Section 7-F was a labyrinth of towering shelves filled with scrolls, tablets, and crystalline data shards. The air was thick with dust and the faint hum of stored information. Zhang made his way through the narrow aisles, following the directions embedded in Li Ming's message.

At the end of the seventh row, he found a small reading desk illuminated by a single floating orb of light. Behind the desk sat a nervous-looking immortal with thinning hair and a perpetual frown etched onto his face.

"You're Zhang Wei," the immortal said. It wasn't a question.

"Li Ming?"

The immortal nodded. "I've been watching the system logs since I was transferred. I knew someone would eventually find the error. I just didn't think it would take this long."

"How long has it been?"

"By mortal reckoning? About three years. But time moves differently here. You were in the reincarnation queue for six months before the error was discovered."

Six months. Zhang had been dead for six months before someone noticed he wasn't supposed to be. And in that time, Supervisor Hu had done nothing.

"Why did you agree to process my death?" Zhang asked, his voice cold.

Li Ming's face crumpled with guilt. "I didn't know it was an error. The system flagged your soul for reincarnation. I just entered the data. That's my job. I process thousands of souls every cycle. I don't check every single one for mistakes."

"But there was a mistake. And you knew about it."

"I found out later, when the system generated an error report. But by then, Supervisor Hu had already sealed the file. He told me to forget about it. Said it was a minor glitch that would be corrected in the next system update."

"And you believed him?"

Li Ming's laugh was bitter. "He's a Supervisor. I'm a Clerk. What was I supposed to do? File a formal complaint? Go over his head? You've been here long enough to know how that works. The system protects itself."

Zhang sat down across from him. "Then why contact me now?"

"Because I can't live with it." Li Ming's hands trembled as he reached into his robe and pulled out a data crystal. "I kept a copy of the original records. The ones that show Supervisor Hu personally modified the destiny queue to divert your soul into the error state."

Zhang took the crystal. It pulsed with a warm light, heavy with the weight of truth. "Why would he do that?"

"That's what I've been trying to figure out. And I think I found something." Li Ming leaned forward, his voice dropping to a whisper. "Check the Karmic Ledger for Cycle 847, Day 112. The day before the error occurred."

Zhang inserted the crystal into his jade token. A holographic display materialized, showing a complex web of karmic balances and destiny threads. He navigated to the specified date.

And then he saw it.

[KARMIC TRANSACTION: Cycle 847, Day 112]
[Source: Supervisor Hu - ID 2317]
[Recipient: Unknown Entity - ID [REDACTED]]
[Amount: 50,000 Positive Karma Units]
[Purpose: Debt Settlement]
[Status: Completed]

Zhang's breath caught. "He traded karma. Fifty thousand units. That's enough to buy a minor godhood."

"Exactly," Li Ming said. "And look at the recipient ID."

Zhang tried to access the redacted information, but the system blocked him. [Access Denied: Tier 8 Clearance Required]

"I can't see it."

"Neither can I. But I did some digging. That ID pattern matches an entity operating outside the Celestial Bureaucracy. Someone who isn't bound by the same rules."

Zhang's mind raced. Supervisor Hu, a mid-level bureaucrat in the Department of Mortal Affairs, had somehow acquired enough karma to pay off a debt to an entity outside the system. And to get that karma, he needed to create a deficit somewhere. A deficit that had to be filled by a soul.

Zhang's soul.

"He killed me to pay off a debt," Zhang said, the words tasting like ash.

Li Ming nodded slowly. "I think so. But I don't know who he owed, or why. That's where you come in."

"Me?"

"You're the victim. You have standing to file a formal grievance with the Celestial Oversight Committee. If you can prove Supervisor Hu tampered with the system, he'll face judgment. And you might get your life back."

Zhang stared at the crystal in his hand. A chance to return to the mortal world. To live again. But something nagged at him. "If the Oversight Committee is so powerful, why hasn't anyone noticed the system bugs? Why is the whole bureaucracy held together with divine paperclips?"

Li Ming's face paled. "Because... the committee doesn't know. The bugs are being hidden. By someone high up. Very high up."

"How high?"

Before Li Ming could answer, the archive doors slammed open. Heavy footsteps echoed through the rows of shelves.

"Someone's coming," Li Ming hissed. "Hide the crystal. If they find us together, we're both done for."

Zhang shoved the crystal into his robe just as a figure emerged from between the shelves. Supervisor Hu.

His face was a mask of cold fury. "Li Ming. I should have known."

"Supervisor Hu," Li Ming stammered. "I was just—"

"Saving your pathetic excuse for a soul?" Hu's eyes locked onto Zhang. "And you. The dead man who won't stay dead. I warned you, Zhang Wei. I told you to focus on your work."

"You killed me," Zhang said flatly.

"I corrected an administrative error. It happens every day. Thousands of souls are misplaced, misrouted, or simply deleted. You're not special."

"I have proof."

Hu's expression didn't change. "Do you?"

Zhang reached for the crystal, but his hand stopped mid-motion. The pocket was empty. He patted his robe frantically, but the crystal was gone.

"Looking for this?" Hu held up the data crystal, its light pulsing mockingly. "Did you really think I wouldn't notice someone accessing my personal records? I've been monitoring the system since the moment you arrived, Zhang Wei. I knew you'd find Li Ming eventually. I just let you do the work for me."

Li Ming's face went ashen. "You used me as bait."

"Of course I did. You were always too guilty to keep your mouth shut. Now, both of you are going to come with me. We're going to have a lovely chat about unauthorized system access, tampering with celestial records, and conspiracy to undermine the Heavenly Bureaucracy."

Hu snapped his fingers, and the archive walls began to dissolve into a void of swirling numbers. The floor beneath Zhang's feet turned to code, crumbling away into nothing.

And then Li Ming did something unexpected. He lunged at Hu, grabbing the data crystal from his hand.

"Run!" Li Ming shouted, his body already beginning to dissolve as the void consumed him. "Take the crystal! Expose him!"

The crystal flew through the air, spinning end over end. Zhang caught it just as the floor beneath him gave way completely.

He fell.

Fell through an infinite sea of code, through layers of reality and bureaucracy, past the departments of mortal affairs and immortal oversight, past the archives and the administrative cores, falling, falling, falling until—

He landed hard on a cold marble floor.

Zhang groaned, pushing himself upright. He was in a vast hall filled with towering statues of ancient immortals. The ceiling was lost in darkness above, and the only light

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Immortal Janitor's Secret

Zhang pulled his hand back from the sphere as if burned. The notification blinked ominously in his vision.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Process Detected]
[Alert Level: Medium]

He had maybe thirty seconds before someone came to investigate. Maybe less.

"Think, Zhang. Think." He paced in the white void, his footsteps echoing strangely. The sphere pulsed behind him, a constant reminder of the power—and danger—now at his fingertips.

Option one: Run. Disconnect from the interface and pretend this never happened. But that would leave him exactly where he started, a dead man in a dead-end job, waiting for the ax to fall.

Option two: Stay and lie. Claim he accidentally stumbled into the interface. Blame it on system error. Hope Supervisor Hu was too lazy to investigate.

Option three: Do what he did best.

Zhang cracked his knuckles and dove back into the code.

The sphere's surface rippled as he touched it, and he felt the system's architecture spread out before him like a city seen from above. Traffic flows of destiny, buildings of karmic debt, highways of fate converging and diverging. And somewhere in the middle, a security protocol was waking up.

[ALERT: Unauthorized Access Detected in Sector 7-G]
[Countermeasures Initializing]

"Not today, firewall." Zhang found the security protocol's root and began rewriting its permissions. In his mortal life, he'd spent three years as an IT support specialist before getting fired for "excessive system modification." That job had taught him one thing: all systems had blind spots.

The celestial bureaucracy's blind spot was its own complexity.

He found what he was looking for in a subroutine labeled [ARCHIVE: CLEANUP ROUTINES 0.1]. It was buried so deep that even most immortals probably didn't know it existed—an old protocol from when the system was first built, designed to let maintenance personnel access restricted areas without triggering alarms.

Zhang copied the protocol's signature and wrapped himself in it.

[ALERT: Countermeasures Disengaged]
[Reason: Authorized Maintenance Access Detected]
[User: Janitorial Staff - ID 0000-GENERIC]

He let out a breath he didn't realize he'd been holding. "Janitorial staff. Perfect."

The white void dissolved, and Zhang found himself standing in a hallway. Not the gleaming marble corridors of the main bureaucracy, but something older. The walls were rough-hewn stone, covered in runes that glowed with a sickly green light. The air smelled of ozone and old paper.

A door to his left creaked open.

"Ah. I wondered when someone would find this place."

The voice was old, crackling like dry leaves. Zhang turned to see a figure emerging from the darkness. He was short, stooped, wearing the same white and gold robes as every other celestial official, but his were patched and frayed. In his hands, he carried a mop.

An actual mop.

"You're—" Zhang started.

"The janitor. Yes." The old man's eyes twinkled. "Also the founder of this mess you call a bureaucracy. But mostly the janitor now."

Zhang's mouth opened and closed. "The founder? But that was—the legends say the First Emperor of Heaven built this place."

"He did. I showed him where to put the filing cabinets." The janitor chuckled, a dry, rasping sound. "Name's Li. Just Li. I've been cleaning up after immortals for five thousand years. You'd think they'd learn not to make such a mess."

A suspicious silence fell between them. Zhang studied the old man's face—wrinkled, weathered, but with eyes that held the weight of millennia.

"You're not actually a janitor," Zhang said slowly.

"Neither are you, son." Li leaned on his mop. "You're a dead man who stumbled into the administrative core and somehow managed to bypass security protocols that have stumped gods and demons alike. That takes a special kind of stupid. Or a special kind of clever."

"I prefer clever."

"Most clever people do." Li shuffled closer, and Zhang noticed something odd about his feet. They didn't quite touch the ground. "I've been watching you, Zhang Wei. Ever since you bypassed the reincarnation queue. Ever since you found the bug."

"You knew about the bug?"

"Son, I wrote the bug." Li's grin revealed teeth that were too white, too perfect. "Every system needs a backdoor. Even heaven's. Especially heaven's."

Zhang's mind raced. "Why? Why would you sabotage your own creation?"

"Sabotage?" Li laughed. "Boy, I didn't sabotage anything. I left an exit. For people like you. People who don't fit the neat little boxes the immortals want to stuff everyone into." He gestured at the hallway. "This whole place—the bureaucracy, the departments, the endless paperwork—it's all a cage. A beautiful, elaborate cage designed to keep the universe running smoothly. But cages need keys."

"You're the key."

"I'm the master key." Li's eyes gleamed. "And you, Zhang Wei, are the lock I've been waiting five thousand years to open."

Before Zhang could respond, the hallway shook. Dust rained from the ceiling, and the green runes flickered.

[ALERT: Sector 7-G Emergency Lockdown]
[Source: Supervisor Hu]
[Reason: Security Breach - Priority: Maximum]

"Hu found us," Zhang said.

"Hu found you." Li corrected. "I'm technically not supposed to exist. But don't worry. I have a plan."

"Let me guess. Run?"

"Partially." Li reached into his robes and pulled out a key. It was made of pure light, shaped like a dragon coiled around a filing cabinet. "This key can open any door in the heavenly bureaucracy. Including the one to the original system logs. The ones that show exactly who killed you and why."

Zhang's hand twitched toward it. "And what do you want in return?"

"Nothing much." Li's smile was ancient and knowing. "Just help me burn the whole thing down."

The ground shook again, harder this time. Cracks spiderwebbed across the stone walls, and Zhang heard shouts in the distance. Immortal voices, filled with anger and panic.

[ALERT: Security Teams En Route]
[Estimated Arrival: 45 seconds]

"Decision time, son." Li held out the key. "Take it and become the biggest threat to the heavenly bureaucracy since the Monkey King rebelled. Or don't, and spend eternity filing reports about people who actually lived."

Zhang thought about Supervisor Hu's smug face. About the clerk who killed him through incompetence. About the system that treated human lives like data points to be processed and forgotten.

He took the key.

The moment his fingers touched it, knowledge flooded into him. Not just the system's secrets, but its weaknesses, its fractures, its hidden passages. He saw the bureaucracy for what it really was: a house of cards built on ancient lies and convenient omissions.

"The original logs," Zhang said, the words tasting strange on his tongue. "Where are they?"

"Department of Historical Records. Sub-basement seven. Behind the water cooler that nobody drinks from." Li's form was beginning to flicker. "I've kept them hidden for millennia. Too dangerous for anyone to find. But you—you're dangerous enough to use them."

The hallway's end exploded inward. Five immortals in golden armor stood in the breach, their weapons drawn and glowing with celestial fire.

[ALERT: Security Team Omega Arrived]
[Recommendation: Surrender Immediately]

"Zhang Wei!" One of the immortals stepped forward, and Zhang recognized the voice. Supervisor Hu. "By order of the Celestial Administration, you are charged with unauthorized system access, destiny manipulation, and—" Hu's eyes fell on Li, and his face went pale. "And consorting with a banned entity."

Li winked at Zhang. "Told you I didn't officially exist."

"Seize them!" Hu pointed, and the immortals lunged forward.

Zhang raised the key. It blazed with light, and the world twisted around him. He felt reality bend, space folding like paper, and then he was somewhere else.

A small office. Filing cabinets lining the walls. A water cooler in the corner.

Sub-basement seven.

He was alone.

Zhang let out a shaky breath and looked at the key in his hand. It pulsed gently, warm against his palm. Somewhere above, thousands of immortals were probably mobilizing to hunt him down.

But he had the key. He had the access. And soon, he would have the truth.

He walked to the water cooler and pushed it aside. Behind it, carved into the stone wall, was a door that shouldn't exist. A door that only opened with one key.

Zhang inserted the key. The door swung open, revealing a room filled with scrolls, each one glowing with the light of a thousand lives.

And at the center of the room, sitting in a chair made of pure jade, was a woman. She was beautiful in the way that mountains are beautiful—ancient, terrible, and completely indifferent.

"I was wondering when you'd get here," she said. "I'm the original Supervisor of Mortal Affairs. The one who built this department from nothing. And I need your help."

Behind her, the scrolls rustled, and Zhang saw names on them. Thousands of names. Millions.

All of them people who had died because of "administrative errors."

All of them people who should still be alive.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: New Quest Unlocked]
[Quest: The Truth of Department 7]
[Objective: Survive the Immortal Hunters]
[Secondary Objective: Learn the Original Supervisor's Secret]
[Warning: This quest cannot be abandoned]

The woman smiled, and it was not a kind smile.

"Welcome to the real bureaucracy, Zhang Wei. I hope you're ready for the paperwork."
